### [28] [Comparative Evaluation of Convolutional and Transformer-Based Detectors for Automated Weed Detection in Precision Agriculture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00908)
*Alcides Toledo Espinosa,Gerardo Antonio Álvarez Hernández,Ángel Eduardo Zamora-Suárez,Miguel Bolaños,Juan Irving Vásquez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper compares CNN-based (YOLOv6-nano) and transformer-based (RTDETR, RF-DETR) object detectors for early weed detection, finding CNNs offer better efficiency while transformers provide superior global context at higher computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate and compare convolutional and transformer-based object detection architectures for early weed detection in realistic precision agriculture scenarios.

Method: Comparative evaluation of YOLOv6-nano (CNN), RTDETR, and RF-DETR (transformer) on the GROUNDBASED_WEED dataset, measuring precision, recall, average precision, and inference speed.

Result: CNN-based detectors achieve high performance at lower computational cost; transformer-based approaches offer better global context capture but require higher resource demands.

Conclusion: There is a clear trade-off between efficiency and contextual modeling, providing practical criteria for model selection in precision agriculture.

Abstract: This paper presents a comparative evaluation of convolutional and transformer-based object detection architectures for early weed detection in realistic scenarios. Representative models from each paradigm are considered, including YOLOv26-nano, a recent variant of the YOLO family, and transformer-based approaches such as RTDETR and RF-DETR. Experiments were conducted on the GROUNDBASED_ WEED dataset, allowing performance to be evaluated in terms of detection accuracy and computational efficiency using metrics such as precision, recall, average precision, and inference speed. The results highlight a clear trade-off between efficiency and contextual modeling: CNN-based detectors achieve high performance at a lower computational cost, while transformer-based approaches offer better global context capture at the expense of higher resource demands. These results provide practical criteria for model selection in precision agriculture applications.

</details>


### [29] [When Good OCR Is Not Enough: Benchmarking OCR Robustness for Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00911)
*Lin Sun,Wang Dexian,Jingang Huang,Linglin Zhang,Change Jia,Zhengwei Cheng,Xiangzheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces InduOCRBench, a new OCR benchmark for industrial RAG systems covering 11 challenging document types, finding that high OCR accuracy doesn't guarantee strong RAG performance due to structural and semantic errors that cause retrieval and generation failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OCR benchmarks rely on character-level metrics (WER/CER) that inadequately measure downstream RAG effectiveness in real-world industrial conditions.

Method: The authors created a benchmark covering 11 challenging document types (extreme layouts, high-resolution pages, complex backgrounds, historical documents, etc.) and evaluated SOTA OCR models under a controlled OCR-first RAG pipeline.

Result: High OCR accuracy does not necessarily translate to strong downstream RAG performance; structural and semantic errors cause substantial retrieval failures even when WER/CER remains low. This mismatch is category-dependent and stable across pipeline choices.

Conclusion: Character-level metrics are insufficient for evaluating OCR in RAG contexts; the benchmark is publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Industrial Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems depend on optical character recognition (OCR) to transform visual documents into text. Existing OCR benchmarks rely on character-level metrics, which inadequately measure downstream RAG effectiveness under real-world conditions. We introduce an OCR benchmark for industrial RAG systems covering 11 challenging document types, including extreme layouts, high-resolution pages, complex or watermarked backgrounds, historical documents with non-standard reading orders, visually decorated text, and documents containing tables and mathematical formulas. Evaluating recent SOTA OCR models under a controlled OCR-first RAG pipeline shows clear performance degradation on realistic industrial documents despite strong conventional benchmark scores. We find that high OCR accuracy does not necessarily translate into strong downstream RAG performance: structural and semantic errors can cause substantial retrieval failures even when WER/CER remains low. Further analysis shows that this mismatch is category-dependent, arises through both retrieval-side and downstream generation-side failures, and remains stable across representative OCR-first pipeline choices. The benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/Qihoo360/InduOCRBench.

</details>


### [30] [Object-Level Explanations for Image Geolocation Models: a GeoGuessr use-case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00912)
*Emilie Durrieu,Christophe Hurter,Philippe Muller,Victor Boutin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an object-centric pipeline to analyze what visual cues geolocation models use, showing that attribution maps can be decomposed into interpretable object-like elements that retain more predictive information than random regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attribution methods like Grad-CAM only highlight diffuse regions rather than coherent objects, making it hard to determine whether geolocation models rely on interpretable visual cues (e.g., road markings, vegetation) similar to human reasoning.

Method: The authors extract salient regions from attribution maps, segment them into object-like elements, and evaluate predictive relevance via deletion/insertion tests comparing attribution-guided crops to random crops.

Result: Attribution-guided crops consistently retain more information for model predictions than random crops of similar coverage on a three-country benchmark.

Conclusion: Attribution maps can be decomposed into interpretable, perceptible elements, enabling object-level analysis of geolocation models.

Abstract: When humans play geolocation games such as GeoGuessr, they rely on concrete visual cues, such as road markings, vegetation, or architectural details, to infer where an image was captured. Whether image geolocation models rely on similar object-level evidence remains difficult to determine, as attribution methods like Grad-CAM typically highlight diffuse regions rather than coherent visual entities, making it difficult to link model predictions to specific objects or perceptible patterns. In this work, we propose an object-centric analysis pipeline to investigate the visual evidence used by geolocation models. Starting from attribution maps, we extract salient regions and segment them into object-like elements. We evaluate their predictive relevance through deletion and insertion tests, comparing attributionguided crops to randomly selected regions with similar coverage. Experiments on a three-country benchmark show that attribution-guided crops consistently retain more information for the model's prediction than random crops. These results suggest that attribution maps can be decomposed into interpretable, perceptible elements, providing a step toward object-level analysis of geolocation models.

</details>


### [31] [Leveraging Imperfect Medical Data: A Manifold-Consistent Spatio-Temporal Network for Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00913)
*Jiangtao Fan,Anish Jindal,Amir Atapour-Abarghouei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Manifold-Consistent Spatio-Temporal Network (MCSTN) for robust Human Activity Recognition under imperfect sensing conditions (missing data, sensor failures, noise) by using dual-level corruption modeling and a dual-stream spatio-temporal architecture, achieving competitive results on PAMAP2, Opportunity, and WISDM datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world wearable IoMT signals are often corrupted by missing measurements, sensor failures, and environmental noise, which significantly degrade conventional deep learning models that assume clean and complete inputs.

Method: Proposes MCSTN with (1) dual-level corruption modeling (physical-level + diffusion-driven continuous corruption) and (2) dual-stream spatio-temporal architecture that decouples temporal dynamics and spatial correlation learning, enforcing representation consistency across multiple corrupted views.

Result: Achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on three HAR benchmarks (PAMAP2, Opportunity, WISDM), particularly under imperfect sensing conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed MCSTN framework demonstrates effectiveness and robustness for real-world wearable IoMT sensing applications under imperfect conditions.

Abstract: Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has attracted increasing attention in medical and healthcare monitoring, particularly with the growth of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT). However, in real-world wearable sensing scenarios, IoMT signals are often corrupted by missing measurements, sensor failures, and environmental noise, which significantly degrade the performance of conventional deep learning models that assume clean and complete inputs. To address this challenge, we propose a Manifold-Consistent Spatio-Temporal Network (MCSTN) for robust HAR under imperfect sensing conditions. The proposed framework introduces a dual-level corruption modeling mechanism that simulates realistic sensor imperfections through both physical-level corruption and diffusion-driven continuous corruption. By enforcing representation consistency across multiple corrupted views, the model learns stable and corruption-invariant semantic representations. Furthermore, we design a dual-stream spatio-temporal architecture that explicitly decouples temporal dynamics modeling and spatial correlation learning. The temporal stream captures long-term activity dynamics, while the spatial stream models inter-sensor relationships, enabling more effective spatio-temporal representation learning. Extensive experiments on three widely used HAR benchmark datasets, PAMAP2, Opportunity, and WISDM, demonstrate that the proposed MCSTN achieves competitive performance compared with existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly under imperfect sensing conditions. These results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework for real-world wearable IoMT sensing applications.

</details>


### [32] [Rethink MAE with Linear Time-Invariant Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00915)
*Zice Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SSMProbe uses State Space Models as permutation-sensitive probes to reveal that token ordering critically affects frozen visual representations' performance, with learned soft permutations significantly outperforming fixed scans on localized patch features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard probing methods (GAP, CLS tokens) treat patch representations as unstructured bag-of-words, ignoring token order. The authors challenge this by showing token order is a critical, exploitable dimension in frozen visual representations.

Method: SSMProbe uses a State Space Model (discrete LTI dynamical system) as a permutation-sensitive probe. It formulates token ordering as an information scheduling problem, comparing fixed scan heuristics against a learned differentiable soft permutation (Sinkhorn-based) from downstream supervision.

Result: Fixed scans fail on highly localized patch features, while learned soft permutations achieve strong performance. Pre-training objectives shape token structure: DINOv2 concentrates global semantics in CLS tokens, MAE preserves distributed representations, ViT is CLS-dominated, BEiT is moderate. The heterogeneity is order-dependent, not merely topological.

Conclusion: Token ordering is a critical dimension in frozen visual representations that standard probing ignores. SSMProbe's learned routing effectively discovers and exploits token heterogeneity, offering a powerful diagnostic lens for visual representation analysis.

Abstract: Standard representation probing for visual models relies on mathematically permutation-invariant operations like Global Average Pooling (GAP) or CLS tokens, treating patch representations as an unstructured bag-of-words. We challenge this paradigm by demonstrating that token order is a critical, exploitable dimension in frozen visual representations (e.g., MAE, BEiT, DINOv2, and ViT as CLS-ablation extreme). We propose SSMProbe, a probing framework driven by a State Space Model (SSM). Operating as discrete Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) dynamical systems, SSMs act as permutation-sensitive probes where sequence order strictly dictates the final state due to inherent memory decay. Formulating token ordering as an information scheduling problem, we compare fixed scan heuristics against a differentiable soft permutation (Sinkhorn-based) learned from downstream supervision. Evaluations on standard and fine-grained classification benchmarks reveal a striking order gap: while fixed scans fail dramatically on highly localized patch features, our learned soft permutation successfully extracts highly competitive performance from otherwise heavily localized patch sequences. We find that pre-training objectives fundamentally shape token structure: DINOv2 concentrates global semantics in optimized CLS tokens leaving patches hyperspecialized, pure MAE preserves distributed representations with heterogeneous patch informativeness, and ViT represents a supervised CLS-dominated extreme. BEiT occupies middle ground. This heterogeneity is order-dependent -- meaning the SSM probe's performance depends critically on which tokens are placed at which temporal positions -- and is not merely a topological property of the spatial grid. SSMProbe's learned routing effectively discovers and exploits this heterogeneity, offering a powerful new diagnostic lens for visual representation analysis.

</details>


### [33] [SAMamba3D: adapting Segment Anything for generalizable 3D segmentation of multiphase pore-scale images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00916)
*Rui Zhang,Xianzhi Song,Linqi Zhu,Branko Bijeljic,Gensheng Li,Martin J. Blunt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAMamba3D adapts the frozen SAM encoder with Mamba-based volumetric context and cross-scale feature interaction for generalizable 3D pore-scale segmentation of multiphase rock images, reducing dataset-specific retraining needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D segmentation methods for multiphase pore-scale X-ray images are dataset-specific, requiring retraining when rock type, fluid pattern, or scanning conditions change. Foundation models like SAM provide strong 2D priors but aren't directly applicable to 3D data.

Method: SAMamba3D combines a largely frozen SAM encoder (2D boundary priors) with Mamba-based volumetric context modeling and progressive cross-scale feature interaction for 3D segmentation.

Result: Matches or outperforms current 3D baselines on sandstone and carbonate datasets with different fluids, wettability, and scanning conditions, while reducing case-specific retraining. Preserves physically meaningful descriptors like fluid saturation, connectivity, and interface morphology.

Conclusion: SAMamba3D enables more reliable and rapid analysis of large 3D multiphase images by providing generalizable segmentation without extensive dataset-specific tuning.

Abstract: Reliable segmentation of multiphase pore-scale X-ray images of rocks is necessary to quantify fluid saturation, connectivity, and interfacial geometry. However, current 3D segmentation methods are typically dataset-specific, requiring retraining or extensive fine-tuning whenever rock type, fluid pattern, scanner, or acquisition conditions change. Foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) provide strong 2D boundary priors, but they are not directly applicable to 3D data.
  We present SAMamba3D, a parameter-efficient framework that adapts a largely frozen SAM encoder to generalizable 3D pore-scale segmentation by coupling it with Mamba-based volumetric context modeling and progressive cross-scale feature interaction. For sandstone and carbonate datasets, with different fluids, wettability, and scanning conditions, SAMamba3D matches or outperforms current 3D baselines while reducing the need for case-specific retraining. The resulting segmented images preserve physically meaningful descriptors, including fluid saturation, connectivity, and interface morphology, enabling more reliable and rapid analysis of large 3D multiphase images.

</details>


### [34] [Energy-Based Constraint Networks: Learning Structural Coherence Across Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00960)
*Chirag Shinde*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces energy-based constraint networks — a modality-agnostic architecture that learns structural coherence from contrastive pairs, achieving strong text corruption detection (93.4%/87.2%) and deepfake detection (0.959 AUC) with composable, encoder-agnostic branches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a single architecture that can learn structural coherence across different modalities (text, vision) by detecting violations of learned constraints, without requiring task-specific architectural changes.

Method: Uses frozen encoder embeddings processed through a state-space model with dual-head attention, producing global energy scores and per-position energy localizations. Multiple independently trained branches detect different violation types and compose at inference.

Result: Text: 93.4% accuracy on trained corruption types, 87.2% on 9 unseen types (7.4M params). Vision: 0.959 AUC on FaceForensics++ Deepfakes, 0.870 AUC on Celeb-DF zero-shot (3.6M params per branch).

Conclusion: First architecture to learn within-modality structural coherence as an explicit energy landscape with per-position decomposition, demonstrating cross-modal transfer via corruption respecification alone.

Abstract: We introduce energy-based constraint networks -- a modality-agnostic architecture that learns structural coherence from contrastive pairs. The system processes frozen encoder embeddings through a state-space model with dual-head attention, producing a scalar energy measuring structural consistency alongside per-position energy scores that localize violations. Multiple independently trained branches detect different violation types and compose at inference without interference.
  We demonstrate the framework in two domains. In text, the system achieves 93.4% accuracy on trained corruption types and 87.2% on 9 unseen types, using frozen BERT and 7.4M trainable parameters. In vision, the same architecture achieves competitive deepfake detection: 0.959 AUC on FaceForensics++ Deepfakes and 0.870 on Celeb-DF without any Celeb-DF training data, using frozen DINOv2 and 3.6M parameters per branch.
  The framework supports flexible training: branches learn from designer-specified corruptions, real-world paired data, or both. Composable branches require representation compatibility -- a finding validated through extensive experimentation where five incompatible approaches failed before the compatible one succeeded. The architecture is encoder-agnostic and domain-agnostic: changing the domain requires only new corruption strategies; changing the encoder requires only a new input projection layer. To our knowledge, this is the first architecture to learn within-modality structural coherence as an explicit energy landscape with per-position decomposition, and to demonstrate that the same architecture transfers across modalities via corruption respecification alone.

</details>


### [35] [Democratizing the medieval English legal tradition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00977)
*Michael Zhang,Elise Wang,Charlotte Whatley,Seth Strickland,Dylan Bannon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper builds a dataset of 4,029 handwritten medieval Latin legal text lines and trains an open-source pipeline for transcription, achieving up to 88% word accuracy using neural networks, n-gram language models, and LLM-based post-correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most early Anglo-American legal records exist only in handwritten medieval Latin, readable by only a few dozen scholars worldwide, creating a major accessibility barrier for legal scholars, medievalists, and students.

Method: The authors constructed a dataset of 4,029 lines from 193 medieval cases, trained R-Blla and CNN+LSTM with CTC decoding for line segmentation and handwriting recognition, applied n-gram LM post-processing, used Gemini Pro 3 for error correction, and compared with TrOCR transformer architecture.

Result: Base model achieved 79% word accuracy; n-gram LM post-processing improved to 82%; Gemini Pro 3 correction boosted to 88%. TrOCR showed comparable word accuracy but worse character accuracy due to over-guessing.

Conclusion: The pipeline successfully opens medieval English legal texts to broader audiences via a web portal (glyphmachina.com), combining traditional HTR with modern LLM correction for practical transcription.

Abstract: The record of the beginning of the most widespread legal system in the world is contained in millions of pages of handwritten text. Most of the records of the first centuries of the Anglo-American legal system are hand-written in a highly abbreviated form of medieval Latin which only a few dozen scholars in the world are trained to read. In this interdisciplinary project, we construct a dataset of 4029 lines of text across 193 medieval criminal and civil cases. We then use the dataset to train an open-source end-to-end pipeline for transcribing these manuscripts. We first train standard neural network architectures for line segmentation and handwriting recognition (R-Blla and CNN+LSTM with CTC decoding, respectively) and show that they can already achieve 79% word accuracy, despite the relatively small training set and the challenge of expanding abbreviations. We then demonstrate that simple post-processing significantly boosts accuracy: adding an n-gram language model to the CTC decoder improves word accuracy to 82%, while asking Gemini Pro 3 to correct mistakes boosts accuracy to 88%. Finally, we compare the CNN+LSTM architecture with TrOCR, a transformer-based OCR architecture, demonstrating that TrOCR shows comparable word accuracy but worse character accuracy due to its over-willingness to guess, making it harder for humans to infer the correct reading. We incorporated our pipeline into a web portal (glyphmachina.com), opening up the English legal tradition to legal scholars, medievalists, and students.

</details>


### [36] [WildTableBench: Benchmarking Multimodal Foundation Models on Table Understanding In the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01018)
*Junzhe Huang,Xiaoxiao Sun,Yan Yang,Yuxuan Hou,Ruotian Zhang,Sirui Li,Hehe Fan,Serena Yeung-Levy,Xin Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WildTableBench is a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal foundation models on real-world table images, showing that current models struggle significantly—only one model exceeds 50% accuracy, with most performing poorly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of table image understanding rely on structured-text tables or clean rendered images, failing to capture the visual complexity of in-the-wild table images with varied layouts and diverse domains, which require sophisticated structural perception and numerical reasoning.

Method: The authors collected 402 high-information-density table images from online forums and websites across diverse domains, with 928 manually annotated and verified questions spanning 17 subtypes across five categories. They evaluated 21 frontier proprietary and open-source multimodal foundation models on this benchmark.

Result: Only one model exceeded 50% accuracy; all remaining models ranged from 4.1% to 49.9%. Diagnostic analyses revealed persistent weaknesses in structural perception and reasoning across models.

Conclusion: WildTableBench serves as a valuable diagnostic benchmark for table image understanding, highlighting current model limitations and providing insights into areas needing improvement.

Abstract: Using multimodal foundation models to analyze table images is a high-value yet challenging application in consumer and enterprise scenarios. Despite its importance, current evaluations rely largely on structured-text tables or clean rendered images, leaving the visual complexity of in-the-wild table images underexplored. Such images feature varied layouts and diverse domains that demand sophisticated structural perception and numerical reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce WildTableBench, the first question-answering benchmark for naturally occurring table images from real-world settings. WildTableBench comprises 402 high-information-density table images collected from online forums and websites across diverse domains, together with 928 manually annotated and verified questions spanning 17 subtypes across five categories. We evaluate 21 frontier proprietary and open-source multimodal foundation models on this benchmark. Only one model exceeds 50% accuracy, while all remaining models range from 4.1% to 49.9%. We further conduct diagnostic analyses to characterize model failures and reveal persistent weaknesses in structural perception and reasoning. These results and analyses provide useful insights into current model capabilities and establish WildTableBench as a valuable diagnostic benchmark for table image understanding.

</details>


### [37] [EmoMM: Benchmarking and Steering MLLM for Multimodal Emotion Recognition under Conflict and Missingness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01024)
*Yueru Sun,Yimeng Zhang,Haoyu Gu,Nuo Chen,Dong She,Xianrong Yao,Yang Gao,Zhanpeng Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces EmoMM, a benchmark for Multimodal Emotion Recognition, discovers a Video Contribution Collapse phenomenon in MLLMs, and proposes CHASE, a lightweight attention steering mechanism to mitigate decision bias under modality conflicts and missingness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically investigate how Multimodal Large Language Models make decisions under modality conflict and missingness in emotion recognition, which remains unexplored.

Method: The authors built the EmoMM benchmark (with modality-aligned, conflict, and missing subsets) and proposed CHASE (Conflict-aware Head-level Attention Steering), a lightweight inference-time mechanism that detects conflicts and steers attention without retraining.

Result: CHASE consistently improves performance across various settings, effectively mitigating decision bias in complex affective scenarios.

Conclusion: The Video Contribution Collapse phenomenon exists in MLLMs for MER, and CHASE can effectively address this bias, enhancing model reliability.

Abstract: Multimodal Emotion Recognition (MER) is critical for interpreting real-world interactions. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM) have shown promise in MER, their internal decision-making mechanisms under modality conflict and missingness remain largely underexplored. In this paper, to systematically investigate these behaviors, we introduce EmoMM, a comprehensive benchmark featuring modality-aligned, conflict, and missing subsets. Through extensive evaluation, we uncover a Video Contribution Collapse (VCC) phenomenon, where MLLM marginalize video evidence due to high token redundancy and modality preferences. To address this, we propose Conflict-aware Head-level Attention Steering (CHASE), a lightweight mechanism that detects modality conflicts and performs inference-time attention steering, effectively mitigating decision bias without retraining the backbone. Experimental results demonstrate that CHASE consistently improves performance across various settings, significantly enhancing the reliability of MLLM in complex affective scenarios.

</details>


### [38] [InterPhys: Physics-aware Human Motion Synthesis in a Dynamic Scene](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01036)
*Chaoyue Xing,Wei Mao,Miaomiao Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a physics-aware human motion generation framework that models all human-related forces (human-object, human-scene, and internal body dynamics) using soft physical constraints and a continuous distance-based force model, achieving more physically plausible motion synthesis in dynamic scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing physics-aware human motion synthesis methods generate physically unrealistic motions due to limited contact modeling, typically restricted to hands only. There is a need for comprehensive force modeling to ensure physically grounded motion generation.

Method: The framework (1) explicitly models the full spectrum of forces (human-object, human-scene, internal body dynamics), (2) imposes soft physical constraints for force/torque balance, and (3) proposes a continuous distance-based force model that generalizes contact modeling to arbitrary surfaces, handling both static environments and dynamic moving objects.

Result: The approach significantly improves physical plausibility, generalizes well to complex scenes, and sets a new benchmark for physically consistent human motion generation.

Conclusion: Full-spectrum force modeling with continuous distance-based contact representation is key to achieving physically realistic human motion synthesis in dynamic environments.

Abstract: This paper tackles the problem of physics-aware human motion synthesis in a dynamic scene. Unlike existing works which mainly tend to generate physically unrealistic motions due to limited contact modeling, typically restricted to hands, in this paper, we introduce a physics-aware human motion generation framework that explicitly models the full spectrum of human-related forces, including human-object, human-scene, and internal body dynamics.~Our method imposes soft physical constraints to maintain force and torque balance, ensuring physically grounded motion synthesis. We further propose a novel continuous distance-based force model that generalizes contact modeling to arbitrary surfaces, capturing interactions not only with static environments but also with dynamic, moving objects. Extensive experiments show that our approach significantly improves physical plausibility and generalizes well to complex scenes, setting a new benchmark for physically consistent human motion generation.

</details>


### [39] [Neighbor2Inverse: Self-Supervised Denoising for Low-Dose Region-of-Interest Phase Contrast CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01075)
*Johannes B. Thalhammer,Lorenzo D'Amico,Lucy Costello,Sebastian Peterhansl,Daniel Frey,Tina Dorosti,Florian Schaff,Jannis Ahlers,Ronan Smith,Marcus Kitchen,Franz Pfeiffer,Martin Donnelley,Daniela Pfeiffer,Kaye S. Morgan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Neighbor2Inverse, a self-supervised denoising framework for low-dose propagation-based X-ray phase-contrast CT that generalizes to clinical CT. It leverages subsampling of noisy projections to create independent noise realizations for training a denoising network in the image domain, achieving superior noise suppression and structural detail preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Propagation-based X-ray phase-contrast imaging (PBI) has high medical potential but requires substantial radiation dose reduction for clinical translation, leading to increased image noise. Existing supervised denoising methods rely on paired low- and high-dose datasets which are rarely available, while most self-supervised methods are not well adapted to the PBI-CT inverse problem.

Method: Neighbor2Inverse builds on the Neighbor2Neighbor principle: each noisy projection is subsampled into two variants preserving structural information but containing independent noise realizations. These are reconstructed separately, and the resulting pairs are used to train a denoising network directly in the image domain.

Result: In region-of-interest PBI CT experiments, Neighbor2Inverse achieves superior noise suppression while preserving fine structural details, demonstrated by improved contrast-to-noise ratio, spatial resolution, and composite image quality metrics. Competitive performance is also shown on clinical CT data under simulated low-dose conditions.

Conclusion: Neighbor2Inverse offers a practical self-supervised denoising solution for low-dose PBI-CT that generalizes to clinical CT, overcoming the key limitation of requiring paired low- and high-dose datasets.

Abstract: Propagation-based X-ray phase-contrast imaging (PBI) enables high-contrast visualization of lung structures and holds strong medical potential. However, safe translation to the clinic will require a substantial radiation dose reduction, which inevitably increases image noise. Supervised convolutional-neural-network-based denoising can restore image quality but depends on paired low- and high-dose datasets, which are rarely available in practice. Self-supervised methods avoid this limitation, yet most are not well adapted to the inverse problem of PBI computed tomography (CT). We introduce Neighbor2Inverse, a self-supervised denoising framework designed for low-dose PBI-CT that generalizes to clinical CT. Building on the Neighbor2Neighbor principle, each noisy projection is subsampled into two variants that preserve structural information but contain independent noise realizations. These are reconstructed separately, and the resulting pairs are used to train a denoising network directly in the image domain. We benchmark the proposed method against established analytical and self-supervised denoising approaches. In region-of-interest PBI CT experiments, Neighbor2Inverse achieves superior noise suppression while preserving fine structural details, as demonstrated by improved contrast-to-noise ratio, spatial resolution, and composite image quality metrics. Competitive performance is also observed on clinical CT data under simulated low-dose conditions.
  This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication. Copyright may be transferred without notice, after which this version may no longer be accessible.
  Code, data, and interactive figures are available at https://github.com/J-3TO/Neighbor2Inverse.

</details>


### [40] [WILD SAM: A Simulated-and-Real Data Augmentation for Autonomous Driving Perception under Challenging Weather](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01081)
*Hamed Khatounabadi,Xiaohu Lu,Hayder Radha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes WILD and WILD SAM frameworks to denoise pseudo-labels and combine denoising with simulation-based training, improving object detection under adverse weather by up to 13% AP on the Four Seasons dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art object detectors fail under adverse weather conditions, causing a domain shift safety issue for autonomous vehicles. Existing solutions rely on synthetic data with limited real-world applicability, and pseudo-labeling methods haven't been exploited for weather-based domain adaptation due to noisy labels under harsh conditions.

Method: 1) WILD (Weather-Induced pseudo Label Denoising) framework that filters noisy pseudo labels from real adverse weather data. 2) WILD SAM, a hybrid training methodology combining pseudo-label denoising with simulation-based training using real data from the target harsh-weather domain.

Result: Validated on the Four Seasons dataset across rainy and snowy scenarios; improves Average Precision (AP) up to 13% and significantly reduces the weather-induced performance gap relative to baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed WILD and WILD SAM frameworks effectively mitigate weather-induced domain shift for object detection, outperforming baseline methods and showing promise for real-world autonomous driving under adverse weather.

Abstract: The performance of state-of-the-art object detectors degrades significantly under adverse weather, causing a safety-critical domain shift problem for autonomous vehicles. Recent efforts address this problem by relying on synthetic data to train the object detectors, which limits their real-world applicability. Meanwhile, pseudo-labeling is widely used for cross-dataset domain adaptation problems. However, these methods have not been exploited by weather-based domain adaptation approaches due to the noisy nature of such labels generated under harsh weather conditions. In this paper, we propose two new approaches to mitigate this weather-induced domain shift. First, we propose a Weather-Induced pseudo Label Denoising (WILD) framework that filters noisy pseudo labels generated by real data captured under adverse weather conditions. Second, we develop a novel hybrid training methodology, WILD SAM, that exploits both pseudo-label denoising and simulation-based training solutions while using real-data from the target harsh-weather domain. We validate both proposed approaches, WILD and WILD SAM, on the recently released Four Seasons dataset across rainy and snowy scenarios. Experiments show that the proposed frameworks improve Average Precision (AP) up to 13\% and significantly reduce the weather-induced performance gap relative to the baseline. The code is available at: https://github.com/Kh-Hamed/WILD-SAM

</details>


### [41] [Patient-Specific Optimization for Mandibular Reconstruction Planning with Enhanced Bone Union](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01084)
*Hamidreza Aftabi,John E. Lloyd,Amanda Ding,Benedikt Sagl,Eitan Prisman,Antony Hodgson,Sidney Fels*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OsteoOpt++ is an image-to-decision planning loop for mandibular reconstruction that optimizes cut-plane and donor-positioning variables to improve bone union, achieving up to 29 percentage points (329% relative) improvement in donor-mandible apposition over conventional approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mandibular reconstruction with vascularized bone grafts suffers from donor-host nonunion, and existing virtual surgical planning produces geometric plans rather than configurations that explicitly promote bone union.

Method: The method converts pre-operative CT into a personalized digital twin via registration and CT-derived parameter updates, then uses Bayesian optimization with expected-improvement-plus acquisition to search six clinically controllable cut-plane and donor-positioning variables under an apposition-driven objective.

Result: Cycle-averaged donor-mandible apposition improved by up to 29 percentage points (329% relative) in generic cases and up to 26 percentage points in patient-specific cases. Sensitivity analysis showed ≤4% objective change, and longitudinal validation showed Dice overlap of 0.70-0.76 between predicted apposition and year-1 bone formation.

Conclusion: OsteoOpt++ provides surgeons with a pre-operative, image-driven recommendation for cut-plane orientation and donor placement that improves predicted union conditions over current operating room configurations.

Abstract: Mandibular reconstruction with vascularized bone grafts is complicated by donor-host nonunion, and current virtual surgical planning produces a geometric plan rather than a configuration that explicitly promotes bone union. We present OsteoOpt++, an image-to-decision planning loop for patient-specific mandibular reconstruction. A pre-operative computed tomography (CT) is converted into a personalized digital twin through template-to-patient registration and CT-derived updates of the muscle and temporomandibular-joint parameters. Bayesian optimization with an expected-improvement-plus acquisition rule then searches six clinically controllable cut-plane and donor-positioning variables under an apposition-driven objective and a safety-factor-regularized variant. The workflow was evaluated on three generic defects (body, symphysis, and ramus-body) and a total of 3+1 patient-specific cases, with 3 used for optimization and 1 for validation. In the generic cases, against a common surgical approach, cycle-averaged donor-mandible apposition increased by up to 29 percentage points (329% relative); in the patient-specific cases, against the surgeon-implemented day-5 post-operative configuration, by up to 26 percentage points. A 10% sensitivity analysis over eleven modeling parameters capped the change in the apposition-driven objective at 3% for generic cases and 4% for patient-specific cases, and the longitudinal case showed Dice overlap of 0.70 and 0.76 between predicted apposition and year-1 bone formation. Clinically, this provides surgeons with a pre-operative, image-driven recommendation for cut-plane orientation and donor placement that is predicted to improve union conditions over the configurations currently delivered in the operating room. The optimization and patient-specific modeling code is open source at https://github.com/hamidreza-aftabi/OsteoOpt.

</details>


### [42] [Disciplined Diffusion: Text-to-Image Diffusion Model against NSFW Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01113)
*Chi Zhang,Changjia Zhu,Xiaowen Li,Yao Liu,Zhuo Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DDiffusion counters NSFW generation in text-to-image diffusion models by using semantic retrieval to detect malicious prompt embeddings and localized editing to sanitize only harmful image regions, avoiding binary blocking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety filters use binary allow/block signals, making them vulnerable to adversarial keyword attacks and causing high false-alarm rates that degrade user experience.

Method: DDiffusion uses (1) semantic retrieval mechanism to evaluate prompts against concept distributions for detecting malicious semantics, and (2) localization method during diffusion to selectively edit harmful regions while preserving benign content.

Result: DDiffusion suppresses malicious content while preserving generation fidelity for benign prompts, without providing the binary allow-deny signal that probing attacks exploit.

Conclusion: DDiffusion offers a more robust and user-friendly safety approach by returning locally sanitized images instead of uniform blocking, addressing both adversarial vulnerability and false-alarm issues.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models have the ability to build high-quality pictures from text prompts, but they pose safety concerns because they can generate offensive or disturbing imagery when provided with harmful inputs. Existing safety filters typically rely on text-based classifiers or image-based checkers that completely block the output upon detecting a threat, issuing an explicit allow/block feedback signal to the user. This binary strategy leaves models vulnerable to adversarial attacks that alter keywords to bypass detection, and it causes high false-alarm rates that degrade the experience for benign users. To address such vulnerabilities, we propose Disciplined Diffusion (DDiffusion), a novel robust text-to-image diffusion that counters Not Safe For Work (NSFW) generation by uncovering implicit malicious semantics in prompt embeddings. DDiffusion leverages a semantic retrieval mechanism to evaluate prompts against concept distributions rather than relying on brittle pairwise similarity. Furthermore, it employs a localization method during the diffusion process to selectively edit only the harmful regions of the generated image. By returning locally sanitized images instead of applying uniform blocking, DDiffusion suppresses malicious content while preserving generation fidelity for benign prompts and avoiding the binary allow-deny signal on which existing probing attacks rely.

</details>


### [43] [ScribbleEdit: Synthetic Data for Image Editing with Scribbles and Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01135)
*Anya Ji,George Ma,Téa Wright,Yiming Zhang,David Chan,Alane Suhr,Somayeh Sojoudi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScribbleEdit introduces a large-scale synthetic dataset combining freehand scribbles with text instructions to improve controllable image editing, enabling unified models to handle both spatial and semantic control more effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models cannot jointly interpret abstract scribbles alongside text instructions due to lack of specialized training data, making precise user control over both spatial layouts and semantic details difficult.

Method: A synthetic pipeline automatically generates source-target image pairs via inpainting, paired with human-drawn scribbles and VLM-generated text instructions to create the ScribbleEdit dataset. Models are then finetuned on this dataset.

Result: Off-the-shelf models struggle with abstract scribble inputs, but finetuning on ScribbleEdit significantly improves spatial alignment and semantic consistency of edits.

Conclusion: ScribbleEdit bridges the gap between spatial (scribble) and semantic (text) control modalities, enabling more accurate and controllable image editing with unified multimodal models.

Abstract: Recent progress in generative models has significantly advanced image editing capabilities, yet precise and intuitive user control remains difficult. Specifically, users often struggle to communicate both exact spatial layouts and specific semantic details simultaneously. While natural language instructions effectively convey high-level semantics like texture and color, they lack spatial specificity. Conversely, freehand scribbles provide rough spatial boundaries but cannot express detailed visual attributes. Consequently, achieving precise control requires combining both modalities. However, existing models struggle to jointly interpret abstract scribbles alongside text due to a lack of specialized training data.
  In this work, we introduce ScribbleEdit, a large-scale synthetic dataset designed to bridge this gap by combining natural language instructions with freehand scribble inputs for more accurate, controllable edits. We construct this dataset through a synthetic pipeline that automatically generates source-target image pairs via inpainting, which are then paired with human-drawn scribbles and VLM-generated text instructions. Using ScribbleEdit, we evaluate and finetune both diffusion-based and autoregressive unified multimodal image editing models. Our experiments reveal that while off-the-shelf models struggle with abstract scribble inputs, finetuning on our synthetic dataset significantly improves their ability to generate spatially aligned and semantically consistent edits.

</details>


### [44] [Semantic Context-aware mOdality fUsion Transformer (SCOUT): A Context-Aware Multimodal Transformer for Concept-Grounded Pathology Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01144)
*Suryakant Singh,Saarthak Kapse,Joel Saltz,Prateek Prasanna*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCOUT is a multimodal framework for pathology report generation that integrates local histology, whole-slide context, and expert-curated semantic descriptors via progressive contextual conditioning, achieving state-of-the-art BLEU and METEOR scores on TCGA-BRCA, MICCAI REG, and HistAI datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pathology foundation models can generate fluent reports but lack clinical grounding, failing to accurately represent diagnostic concepts and relationships due to difficulty integrating heterogeneous visual evidence across cellular, tissue, and diagnostic levels while maintaining interpretability.

Method: SCOUT uses a context-aware concept-grounded multimodal framework with depth-aware contextual modulation and adaptive multimodal fusion. It progressively conditions image representations using global slide information and explicit diagnostic concepts, integrating local histological patterns, whole-slide context, and expert-curated semantic descriptors in a unified learning paradigm.

Result: SCOUT achieves best BLEU-1 to BLEU-4 and METEOR on all datasets (TCGA-BRCA, MICCAI REG, HistAI), plus best ROUGE-L on TCGA-BRCA and MICCAI REG. On TCGA-BRCA: BLEU-1/2/3/4 = 0.436/0.303/0.202/0.156, METEOR=0.204. On REG 2025: BLEU-1/2/3/4 = 0.865/0.834/0.805/0.780, METEOR=0.568.

Conclusion: Results support that progressive contextual conditioning is effective for grounded pathology report generation, enabling clinically coherent reports while preserving complementarity across representational scales.

Abstract: Whole-slide images (WSIs) present a fundamental challenge for computational pathology due to their extreme resolution, multi-scale heterogeneity, and the requirement for clinically reliable interpretation. Although recent pathology foundation models have enabled fluent report generation, they often lack clinical grounding, failing to accurately represent key diagnostic concepts and relationships observed by pathologists. This limitation arises from the difficulty of integrating heterogeneous visual evidence spanning fine-grained cellular patterns, slide-level tissue architecture, and high-level diagnostic concepts, while maintaining interpretability and clinical coherence. Here we present SCOUT: Semantic Context-aware mOdality fUsion Transformer, a context-aware concept-grounded multimodal framework for pathology report generation that enables progressive conditioning of image representations by global slide information and explicit diagnostic concepts. The method integrates local histological patterns, whole-slide context, and expert-curated semantic descriptors within a unified learning paradigm, allowing visual features to be dynamically refined throughout the encoding process. By combining depth-aware contextual modulation with adaptive multimodal fusion during text generation, the framework produces clinically coherent reports while preserving complementarity across representational scales. Using CONCH1.5 features, we evaluate SCOUT against WSI-Caption, HistGen, and BiGen on TCGA-BRCA, MICCAI REG, and HistAI. SCOUT achieves the best BLEU-1 to BLEU-4 and METEOR scores on all datasets, plus the best ROUGE-L on TCGA-BRCA and MICCAI REG. On TCGA-BRCA, it reaches 0.436/0.303/0.202/0.156 BLEU-1/2/3/4 and 0.204 METEOR; on REG 2025, it achieves 0.865/0.834/0.805/0.780 and 0.568. These results support progressive contextual conditioning for grounded pathology report generation.

</details>


### [45] [CEZSAR: A Contrastive Embedding Method for Zero-Shot Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01165)
*Valter Estevam,Rayson Laroca,Helio Pedrini,David Menotti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A contrastive learning-based zero-shot action recognition method using joint embedding space with automatic negative sampling, achieving SOTA on UCF-101 and Kinetics-400.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address semantic gap (mismatch between textual and visual representations) and domain shift (differences between training and test sets) in zero-shot action recognition.

Method: Proposes a joint embedding space that encodes videos and sentences, trained by aligning videos with natural-language descriptions, plus an automatic negative sampling procedure to generate unpaired data for augmentation.

Result: State-of-the-art results on UCF-101 and Kinetics-400 datasets under several split configurations.

Conclusion: Contrastive learning with joint embedding and automatic negative sampling effectively mitigates semantic gap and domain shift in zero-shot action recognition.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel Zero-Shot Action Recognition~(ZSAR) method based on contrastive learning. In ZSAR, we aim to classify examples from classes that were missing during training. Two well-known problems remain in ZSAR: the semantic gap and the domain shift. A semantic gap occurs because label representations come from the textual domain (i.e., language models) and must be associated with visual representations (i.e., CNNs, RNNs, transformer-based). This multimodal nature implies that the semantic properties of the two spaces are not identical. On the other hand, the domain shift arises from differences between the training and test sets and is inherent to ZSAR once the test set is unknown. One of the most promising methods to address both issues is learning joint embedding spaces. Therefore, we propose a new model that encodes videos and sentences in a joint embedding space, trained by aligning videos with their natural-language descriptions. We design an automatic negative sampling procedure to augment the training dataset and generate unpaired data, i.e., visual appearance and unrelated descriptions. Our results are state-of-the-art on the UCF-101 and Kinetics-400 datasets under several split configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/valterlej/cezsar.

</details>


### [46] [CADFit: Precise Mesh-to-CAD Program Generation with Hybrid Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01171)
*Ghadi Nehme,Eamon Whalen,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CADFit is a hybrid optimization-based framework that reconstructs editable parametric CAD construction sequences from meshes by incrementally fitting operations (extrusions, revolutions, fillets, chamfers) using IoU-driven optimization, outperforming prior methods in accuracy and reducing invalid outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CAD reconstruction methods are limited to difficult-to-edit formats (meshes/Breps) or oversimplified sketch-and-extrude pipelines with low-complexity datasets. Recovering full editable parametric CAD sequences from geometric inputs remains a major challenge for design and manufacturing.

Method: CADFit formulates reconstruction as an IoU-driven optimization over structured CAD programs, using incremental fitting with geometric feedback to validate parametric operations. It supports extrusions, revolutions, fillets, and chamfers. A multimodal pipeline also enables reconstruction from images by combining image-based geometry reconstruction with CADFit.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art mesh-to-CAD methods in volumetric IoU and Chamfer Distance across multiple benchmarks, with substantially reduced Invalid Ratio of reconstructed CAD programs, especially for complex designs.

Conclusion: CADFit enables accurate reconstruction of higher-complexity CAD models, providing a practical foundation for generating richer datasets and advancing future learning-based CAD reverse engineering approaches.

Abstract: Despite recent progress, recovering parametric CAD construction sequences from geometric input, such as meshes or point clouds, is a key challenge for design and manufacturing, as existing CAD reconstruction and generation methods are largely restricted to difficult-to-edit formats like meshes or Breps or editable simple sketch-and-extrude pipelines and low-complexity datasets. We introduce CADFit, a hybrid optimization-based CAD reconstruction framework that recovers complex, editable CAD construction sequences from meshes by incrementally fitting and validating parametric operations using geometric feedback. Our approach is distinguished by formulating reconstruction as an IoU-driven optimization over structured CAD programs and supporting a rich set of operations, including extrusions, revolutions, fillets, and chamfers. Experiments on multiple CAD benchmarks show that CADFit outperforms state-of-the-art mesh-to-CAD methods in volumetric Intersection-over-Union and Chamfer Distance, while substantially reducing the Invalid Ratio of reconstructed CAD programs, particularly for complex designs. We further present a multimodal pipeline that enables end-to-end reconstruction of CAD construction sequences from images by combining image-based geometry reconstruction with CADFit. By enabling accurate reconstruction of higher-complexity CAD models, CADFit provides a practical foundation for generating richer datasets and advancing future learning-based approaches to CAD reverse engineering.

</details>


### [47] [Phase-map synthesis from magnitude-only MR images using conditional score-based diffusion models with application in training of accelerated MRI reconstruction models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01185)
*M. Berk Sahin,Dilek Yalcinkaya,Abolfazl Hashemi,Behzad Sharif*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes using conditional score-based diffusion models (SBDMs) to synthesize realistic phase maps from magnitude-only MR images, enabling generation of large k-space datasets to train deep learning models for accelerated MRI reconstruction, outperforming naive and GAN-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw k-space data is often discarded in clinical MRI due to storage and privacy concerns, while magnitude-only images are abundant. This scarcity of training data limits the development of generalizable deep learning-based MRI reconstruction models.

Method: A conditional score-based diffusion model (SBDM) is used to synthesize realistic phase maps from magnitude-only MR images. These phase maps are combined with magnitude-only images to generate k-space data, which is then used to train a DL model for accelerated MRI reconstruction.

Result: The DL model trained on SBDM-synthesized k-space data outperforms models trained on naive smooth-phase data and GAN-synthesized data in both quantitative metrics and qualitative reconstruction fidelity, though ground-truth k-space data still performs best.

Conclusion: SBDM-based phase synthesis is a promising approach to leverage abundant magnitude-only image datasets for training high-quality DL-based MRI reconstruction models, reducing reliance on scarce raw k-space data.

Abstract: Accelerated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) enabled by the training of deep learning (DL)-based image recon. models requires large and diverse raw k-space datasets. In most clinical MRI applications, due to storage and patient privacy concerns, raw k-space data is discarded and magnitude-only images are the only component saved. Consequently, a large portion of the DL-based MRI recon. literature has either relied on small training datasets or has used one of the few available open-source k-space datasets. At the same time, the growing number of anonymized magnitude-only image registries/databases motivates the development of techniques that can use them as training datasets for generalizable DL-based recon. models. Here we propose to address this challenge by employing a generative approach based on conditional score-based diffusion models (SBDMs): given a magnitude-only MR image, it synthesizes a phase map (in the image domain) that realistically corresponds to the magnitude-only image. We evaluate its generative capabilities in a downstream DL-based recon. task whereby a large k-space dataset is generated by combining the SBDM-synthesized phase-maps and the corresponding magnitude-only images, and this k-space dataset is then used to train a DL model for accelerated MRI recon. We compare the performance of the resulting DL model versus those trained according to (a) a naive approach that uses smooth phase, (b) a k-space training dataset generated using synthesized phase maps derived from a generative adversarial network, and (c) the ground truth k-space data. Our results suggest that the DL model trained from SBDM-synthesized k-space data outperforms the other approaches in terms of quantitative metrics as well as qualitatively observed recon. fidelity, i.e., whether the reconstructed images include erroneous or hallucinated features that could adversely impact diagnostic accuracy.

</details>


### [48] [Asymmetric Invertible Threat: Learning Reversible Privacy Defense for Face Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01217)
*Jiabei Zhang,Ziyuan Yang,Andrew Beng Jin Teoh,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARFP is a privacy protection framework for face images that integrates keyed reversible obfuscation, adversarial training against restoration attacks, and tamper detection, improving resistance to inverse purification while preserving authorized recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial face privacy methods use input-space perturbations that can be partially inverted by adversaries who learn restoration or purification mappings, because current defense paradigms do not control reversibility.

Method: ARFP has three components: (1) Key-Conditioned Manifold Binding (ties protection to a user key), (2) Adversarial Restoration-Aware Training (trains against a surrogate restoration adversary), (3) Authorized Reversible Restoration (enables key-based recovery with nonce-based tamper indication).

Result: Experiments show ARFP improves resistance against evaluated restoration attacks while preserving authorized recovery utility, with empirical evidence of key-sensitive recovery and tamper awareness.

Conclusion: ARFP effectively addresses the asymmetric adversarial attack setting by controlling reversibility through key-conditioned protection and adversarial training, offering a more robust face privacy solution with built-in recovery and tamper indication.

Abstract: Face Recognition systems are widely deployed in real-world applications, but they also raise privacy concerns due to unauthorized collection and misuse of facial data. Existing adversarial privacy protection methods rely on input-space perturbations to obfuscate identity information, yet their protection can degrade when adversaries learn restoration or purification mappings that partially invert the transformation. We study this setting as an asymmetric adversarial attack, in which reverse manipulation becomes feasible because existing defense paradigms do not control reversibility. To address this problem, we propose Asymmetric Reversible Face Protection (ARFP), a restoration-aware extension of personalized face cloaking that integrates privacy protection, keyed recovery, and tamper indication in a single framework. ARFP consists of three components: Key-Conditioned Manifold Binding, which ties the protection transformation to a user-provided key; Adversarial Restoration-Aware Training, which introduces a surrogate restoration adversary during training to improve robustness against evaluated inverse purification attacks; and Authorized Reversible Restoration, which supports recovery with the correct key while providing nonce-based tamper indication. Extensive experiments under the threat models considered in this work show that ARFP improves resistance to the evaluated restoration attacks while preserving authorized recovery utility. These results provide empirical evidence of key-sensitive recovery behavior and tamper awareness in the tested settings.

</details>


### [49] [Visual Implicit Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01220)
*Pengfei Jiang,Jixiang Luo,Luxi Lin,Zhaohong Huang,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VIAR introduces an implicit equilibrium layer into next-scale autoregressive visual generation, achieving constant training memory and controllable inference compute, outperforming VAR with fewer parameters and better efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VAR relies on explicit deep stacks with fixed per-scale computation and high memory usage at high resolutions, limiting efficiency and deployability.

Method: VIAR embeds an implicit equilibrium layer (trained with Jacobian-Free Backpropagation) between shallow pre/post blocks, enabling constant training memory and a per-scale iteration knob for inference compute control.

Result: On ImageNet 256x256, VIAR achieves FID 2.16 and sFID 8.07 with only 38.4% parameters of VAR, reduces peak memory from 19.24 GB to 8.53 GB, and doubles throughput to 32.08 images/s.

Conclusion: VIAR validates that implicit equilibria with per-scale compute control can deliver high-quality, efficient, and deployable visual generation, outperforming VAR across quality-efficiency trade-offs.

Abstract: Visual Autoregressive Modeling (VAR) based on next-scale prediction achieves strong generation quality, but their explicit deep stacks fix the amount of computation per scale and inflate memory at high resolutions. We introduce Visual Implicit Autoregressive Modeling (VIAR), a next-scale autoregressive generator that embeds an implicit equilibrium layer between shallow pre/post blocks. The implicit layer is trained with Jacobian-Free Backpropagation, yielding constant training memory, while inference exposes a per-scale iteration knob that enables compute control. On ImageNet 256x256 benchmark, VIAR attains FID 2.16, and sFID 8.07 with only 38.4% parameters of VAR, matching or surpassing strong AR baselines and remaining competitive with large diffusion models. By controlling the per-scale knob, VIAR can reduce peak memory from 19.24 GB to 8.53 GB and doubles throughput from 15.16 to 32.08 images/s on a single RTX 4090, without retraining. Ablations show that fewer steps are sufficient for fixed-point iterations to converge and that VIAR consistently dominates VAR across quality efficiency operating points. In zero shot in-painting and class-conditional editing, VIAR produces sharper details and smoother boundaries while preserving global structure, validating the benefits of implicit equilibria and per-scale compute control for practical, deployable visual generation.

</details>


### [50] [TT4D: A Pipeline and Dataset for Table Tennis 4D Reconstruction From Monocular Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01234)
*Nima Rahmanian,Daniel Kienzle,Thomas Gossard,Dvij Kalaria,Rainer Lienhart,Shankar Sastry*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TT4D is a large-scale table tennis dataset with 140+ hours of reconstructed gameplay from monocular videos, featuring multimodal annotations including 3D ball trajectories, spin, camera calibrations, and human meshes. It uses a novel "lift-first" pipeline that reconstructs 3D ball trajectories before segmentation, overcoming limitations of prior 2D-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for reconstructing table tennis gameplay rely on 2D-based time segmentation of ball tracks, which fails under occlusion and varied camera viewpoints, preventing reliable reconstruction from broadcast monocular videos.

Method: A "lift-first" pipeline that first uses a learned neural network to lift the entire unsegmented 2D ball track to 3D, then performs reliable time segmentation on the 3D trajectory. The network also infers ball spin, handles unreliable detections, and reconstructs trajectories under high occlusion.

Result: The pipeline is the only method capable of reconstructing table tennis gameplay from general-view broadcast monocular videos. The dataset's fidelity is demonstrated through downstream tasks: racket pose/velocity estimation at impact and generative modeling of competitive rallies.

Conclusion: TT4D provides a new foundation for virtual replay, player analysis, and robot learning in table tennis, enabled by the scale and precision achieved through the lift-first reconstruction paradigm.

Abstract: We present TT4D, a large-scale, high-fidelity table tennis dataset. It provides $140+$ hours of reconstructed singles and doubles gameplay from monocular broadcast videos, featuring multimodal annotations like high-quality camera calibrations, precise 3D ball positions, ball spin, time segmentation, and 3D human meshes over time. This rich data provides a new foundation for virtual replay, in-depth player analysis, and robot learning. The dataset's combination of scale and precision is achieved through a novel reconstruction pipeline. Prior methods first partition a game sequence into individual shot segments based on the 2D ball track, and only then attempt reconstruction. However, 2D-based time segmentation collapses under occlusion and varied camera viewpoints, preventing reliable reconstruction. We invert this paradigm by first lifting the entire unsegmented 2D ball track to 3D through a learned lifting network. This 3D trajectory then allows us to reliably perform time segmentation. The learned lifting network also infers the ball's spin, handles unreliable ball detections, and successfully reconstructs the ball trajectory in cases of high occlusion. This lift-first design is necessary, as our pipeline is the only method capable of reconstructing table tennis gameplay from general-view broadcast monocular videos. We demonstrate the dataset's fidelity through two downstream tasks: estimating the racket's pose \& velocity at impact, and training a generative model of competitive rallies.

</details>


### [51] [Degradation-Aware Adaptive Context Gating for Unified Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01236)
*Lei He,Jielei Chu,Fengmao Lv,Weide Liu,Tianrui Li,Jun Cheng,Yuming Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DACG-IR proposes a degradation-aware adaptive context gating model for unified image restoration, using lightweight multi-scale degradation perception to dynamically modulate features, achieving SOTA across multiple degradation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unified image restoration models suffer from task interference when handling diverse degradations, as different degradation types require distinct feature processing strategies.

Method: DACG-IR uses a lightweight multi-scale degradation-aware module to extract coarse degradation info and generate layer-wise prompts, which guide attention temperature and output gating in encoder/decoder blocks. A spatial-channel dual-gated adaptive fusion mechanism refines encoder features to suppress noise propagation.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods in single-task, all-in-one, adverse weather removal, and composite degradation settings.

Conclusion: Explicit degradation perception with adaptive context gating effectively reduces task interference and improves unified image restoration performance.

Abstract: Unified image restoration using a single model often faces task interference due to diverse degradations. To address this, we propose DACG-IR (Degradation-Aware Adaptive Context Gating), which enables explicit perception of degradation characteristics to dynamically modulate feature representations. Our method constructs degradation-aware contextual representations from the input to modulate attention distribution, frequency-domain features, and feature aggregation. Specifically, a lightweight multi-scale degradation-aware module extracts coarse degradation information and generates layer-wise prompts. These prompts guide attention temperature and output gating in encoder and decoder blocks for adaptive feature extraction. Additionally, a spatial-channel dual-gated adaptive fusion mechanism refines encoder features, suppressing noise propagation from shallow to deep layers. This design effectively suppresses degradation-induced noise while preserving informative structures. Experiments show DACG-IR outperforms state-of-the-art methods in single-task, all-in-one, adverse weather removal, and composite degradation settings. Code: https://github.com/HlHomes/DACG-IR-code

</details>


### [52] [Exploring Prompt Alignment with Clinical Factors in Zero-Shot Segmentation VLMs for NSCLC Tumor Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01266)
*Suraj Pai,Thibault Heintz,Cosmin Ciausu,Marion Tonneau,Hugo Aerts,Raymond Mak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates how different prompt dimensions (diagnosis, demographics, staging, anatomy, etc.) affect VoxTell's spatial attention for lung cancer tumor delineation. Anatomical location is the dominant driver, while histology and stage matter little. VoxTell achieves zero-shot DSC of 0.613, statistically on par with fine-tuned nnUNet (0.690) and Ahmed et al. (0.675), significantly beating other zero-shot models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand which prompt dimensions govern spatial behavior in zero-shot VLMs for medical image segmentation, since prompt dimensions remain poorly understood despite their practical importance.

Method: Probing VoxTell via sub-prompt decomposition (diagnosis, demographic, staging, anatomical, generic, irrelevant controls), attribute-wise perturbation robustness, specificity ladders, and cross-case prompt swaps, evaluated with DSC and statistical tests.

Result: Anatomical location dominates (63.4% of location perturbations cause catastrophic drops); histology/stage have minimal effect; VoxTell's zero-shot DSC (0.613) is statistically indistinguishable from nnUNet (0.690) and Ahmed et al. (0.675), and outperforms other zero-shot models.

Conclusion: Segmentation VLMs should be evaluated not only by Dice but also by the prompt dimensions they align to; the model prioritizes "where to look" over "what to look for."

Abstract: Zero-shot vision-language models (VLMs) offer a promptable alternative to task-specific training for gross tumor volume (GTV) delineation in non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC), but the prompt dimensions that govern their spatial behavior remain poorly understood. We study this question by probing alignment directions in VoxTell on a held-out internal NSCLC tumor dataset through sub-prompt decomposition into diagnosis, demographic, staging, anatomical, generic, and irrelevant controls; attribute-wise perturbation robustness; specificity ladders; and cross-case prompt swaps, while benchmarking against fine-tuned and zero-shot baselines using the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) with Wilcoxon signed-rank tests and Benjamini-Hochberg correction. Alignment analyses revealed that anatomical location is the dominant driver of VoxTell's spatial attention: 63.4 percent of location perturbations caused catastrophic drops, prompt specificity improved from generic to full descriptions except for diagnosis-only prompts, irrelevant prompts correctly yielded zero segmentation, and cross-case prompt swaps confirmed patient-specific conditioning (matched DSC 0.906 vs. mismatched 0.406). Histology and stage substitutions had minimal effect, indicating that the model prioritizes "where to look" over "what to look for." In this context, VoxTell, operating fully zero-shot, achieved a mean DSC of 0.613, statistically indistinguishable from nnUNet (0.690, adjusted p = 0.156) and Ahmed et al. (0.675, adjusted p = 0.679), while significantly outperforming all other zero-shot models. Together, these findings argue that segmentation VLMs should be evaluated not only by Dice, but also by the prompt dimensions to which they align.

</details>


### [53] [CNN-based Multi-In-Multi-Out Model for Efficient Spatiotemporal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01277)
*Hyeonseok Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MIMO-ESP, a CNN-based model with Transformer-like architecture for efficient spatiotemporal prediction, achieving competitive efficiency and outperforming existing models on video, traffic, and precipitation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of CNN-based models (local receptive fields, time-channel axis mixing) and Transformer-based models (high complexity, long training time) in spatiotemporal prediction.

Method: MIMO-ESP: CNN-based Multi-In-Multi-Out model for Efficient Spatiotemporal Prediction. Uses a Transformer architecture built on CNN to consider global information, treats time axis independently, and applies dilation for joint spatiotemporal consideration.

Result: Achieves competitive efficiency while outperforming existing models on three benchmark datasets covering video, traffic, and precipitation prediction tasks. Ablation study confirms the usefulness of each component.

Conclusion: MIMO-ESP effectively balances performance and efficiency, demonstrating strong potential for spatiotemporal prediction tasks across diverse domains.

Abstract: Recently, Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) or Transformer architecture based models have been proposed to overcome the limitations of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) based models in spatiotemporal prediction. These models prevent the inefficiency of parallelization limitation due to the sequential properties and stacked error due to the recursive method, and show high performance. Novertheless, there are still some challengies. First, CNN based models have difficulty considering global information due to the local properties of the kernel, and their performance is limited. In addition, information is mixed because the time axis is combined with the channel axis of the image for processing. Models based on Transformer architecture have high complexity due to the self-attention calcuation and take a long training time. In this paper, we propose a new structure model called CNN-based Multi-In-Multi-Out model for Efficient Spatiotemporal Prediction (MIMO-ESP) to overcome these limitations. MIMO-ESP considers global information and significantly improves complexity by configuring a Transformer architecture based on CNN. In addition, it treats the time axis as an independent axis without combining it, and effectively considers spatiotemporal information together by applying dilation. This structure makes MIMO-ESP efficient and high performance. Extensive experiment results on three promising benchmark datasets which including video, traffic, and precipitation prediction tasks demonstrate that the usefulness of MIMO-ESP due to the achieved competitive efficiency while outperforming existing models. Furthermore, the ablation study results demonstrate the usefulness of the components of MIMO-ESP, emphasizing the potential of the proposed approaches.

</details>


### [54] [Developing a Strong Pre-Trained Base Model for Plant Leaf Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01283)
*David J. Richter*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This thesis addresses plant leaf disease classification by identifying open datasets and models, conducting benchmarks, building a new dataset, and training a DenseNet201-based Base Model that outperforms baselines and enables faster, more robust Transfer Learning with less data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plant diseases cause significant crop losses yearly. Manual disease detection is labor-intensive and time-consuming, motivating the need for automated ML-based solutions. However, there is a gap between available public datasets and what is needed to train sufficiently capable models.

Method: First, open datasets and models for plant leaf disease classification were identified and benchmarked. Then, a new dataset was constructed based on benchmark findings and an augmentation applicability study. Finally, a Base Model using DenseNet201 architecture was trained on this new dataset and evaluated via Transfer-Learning experiments.

Result: The proposed DenseNet201-based Base Model outperformed the baseline on the new dataset and on domain-specific Transfer-Learning experiments. It enables faster, more robust, and more stable training with less data compared to general models.

Conclusion: The new Base Model overcomes key issues in plant leaf disease classification, including data scarcity and training instability, by providing an improved foundation for Transfer Learning in this domain.
