Beyond reproducing known convergence rates, we report two empirical observations: (1) the Jacobian eigenvalue spectrum of the learned velocity field stiffens sharply near t=1, explaining why the adaptive Dormand-Prince solver automatically concentrates its step budget at the end of the trajectory; (2) the quality gap between low-order and high-order solvers widens for undertrained and smaller models, indicating that solver choice matters most when the model is imperfect. Code and all experiment scripts are publicly available.

</details>


### [217] [Fast Log-Domain Sinkhorn Optimal Transport with Warp-Level GPU Reductions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00837)
*Hao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FastSinkhorn is a native CUDA implementation of the log-domain Sinkhorn algorithm that achieves 12x speedup over POT and 5.9x over PyTorch baselines on dense OT problems (n=m=8192) while using only 256 MB GPU memory and supporting very small regularization parameters (ε=10^{-4}).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Sinkhorn implementations suffer from numerical instability for small regularization parameters or high overhead from deep learning frameworks, motivating a lightweight, numerically stable CUDA solver.

Method: Warp-level shuffle reductions + shared-memory tiling for high GPU utilization; operates entirely in log-domain for numerical stability.

Result: 12x speedup over POT, 5.9x speedup over PyTorch GPU baselines on n=m=8192 dense OT; handles ε=10^{-4} robustly; 256 MB memory footprint.

Conclusion: Native CUDA kernels with careful numerical treatment provide a practical and efficient foundation for large-scale optimal transport computation.

Abstract: Entropic regularized optimal transport (OT) via the Sinkhorn algorithm has become a fundamental tool in machine learning, yet existing implementations either suffer from numerical instability for small regularization parameters or incur significant overhead from deep learning frameworks. We present FastSinkhorn, a lightweight, native CUDA implementation of the log-domain Sinkhorn algorithm that combines warp-level shuffle reductions with shared-memory tiling to achieve high GPU utilization without sacrificing numerical stability. Our solver operates entirely in the log-domain, enabling robust computation for regularization parameters as small as epsilon = 10^{-4} where standard-domain methods fail. On dense OT problems with n = m = 8192, our implementation achieves 12x speedup over the widely-used POT library and 5.9x speedup over GPU-accelerated PyTorch baselines, while consuming only 256 MB of GPU memory. We validate our solver on image color transfer, 3D point cloud matching, and convergence analysis, demonstrating that native CUDA kernels with careful numerical treatment provide a practical and efficient foundation for large-scale optimal transport computation.

</details>


### [218] [GAZE: Grounded Agentic Zero-shot Evaluation with Viewer-Level Tools and Literature Retrieval on Rare Brain MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00876)
*Duaa Alim,Mogtaba Alim,Liam Chalcroft*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAZE introduces an agentic framework that lets medical VLMs iteratively inspect images using viewer and retrieval tools (PubMed, Open-i), achieving 58.2 mAP@0.3 for lesion localization and 34.9% Top-1 diagnostic accuracy on rare brain MRI conditions, with tool use disproportionately benefiting rare pathologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radiologists inspect images iteratively and consult literature, but current VLMs process images in a single forward pass. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling VLMs to operate in an agentic, tool-augmented manner for more accurate and auditable medical image analysis.

Method: GAZE equips a medical VLM with viewer-level tools (zoom, windowing, contrast, edge detection) and two retrieval tools (PubMed for literature, Open-i for radiological images). Structured prompting with schema-validated outputs ensures consistency, and full tool-call traces are recorded for auditability.

Result: On the NOVA benchmark (906 brain MRI cases, 281 rare neurological conditions), GAZE achieves 58.2 mAP@0.3 for lesion localization and 34.9% Top-1 diagnostic accuracy without task-specific fine-tuning. Rare pathologies benefit disproportionately: IoU > 0.3 rises from 17% to 58% for rare (≤3 examples) vs. 25% to 68% for common (≥10 cases) conditions. Retrieval ablations reveal model-dependent trade-offs between diagnosis and localization gains.

Conclusion: Framework design (structured prompting, schema validation) is itself an experimental variable that improves performance even without tool use. Tool use particularly aids rare disease diagnosis, but retrieval benefits are model-dependent and can trade off localization for diagnostic accuracy, underscoring the need for joint evaluation of multiple tasks in medical VLMs.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) read an image and produce text in a single forward pass, whereas radiologists typically inspect an image several times and consult the literature before writing a report. We introduce GAZE (Grounded Agentic Zero-shot Evaluation), a framework that lets a medical VLM work in this iterative way by calling viewer-level tools (zoom, windowing, contrast, edge detection) and two retrieval tools backed by the U.S. National Library of Medicine (PubMed for medical literature, Open-i for radiological images), with structured outputs validated against a schema and full tool-call traces recorded for auditability. On NOVA, a benchmark of 906 brain MRI cases covering 281 rare neurological conditions, GAZE reaches 58.2 mean average precision (mAP) at intersection-over-union (IoU) 0.3 for lesion localisation and 34.9% Top-1 diagnostic accuracy under a joint protocol that scores captioning, diagnosis, and localisation from the image alone, without task-specific fine-tuning. Before any tool is used, structured prompting and schema-validated outputs already improve over the published Gemini 2.0 Flash baseline (20.2 to 29.4 mAP@0.3), so framework design is itself an experimental variable. Tool use helps rare pathologies disproportionately: the fraction of cases with IoU > 0.3 rises from 17% to 58% for diagnoses with three or fewer examples versus 25% to 68% for common conditions ($\geq$10 cases), with gains tracking engagement (Gemini 3 Flash: Cohen's d = 0.79, 11.8 tool calls per case; Gemini 2.0 Flash: tools used in 8.2% of cases, no significant benefit). Retrieval ablations additionally reveal a model-dependent trade-off in which gains in diagnosis can coincide with losses in localisation, reinforcing the case for joint evaluation of diagnosis, localisation, and captioning in medical VLMs.

</details>


### [219] [StyleShield: Exposing the Fragility of AIGC Detectors through Continuous Controllable Style Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00924)
*Guantian Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reveals that AIGC detectors are fundamentally unreliable due to the blurring boundary between human and AI text, and proposes StyleShield, a flow matching framework for text style transfer that achieves 94.6% evasion against trained detectors and ≥99% against unseen ones, alongside RateAudit which demonstrates detection-rate verdicts can be arbitrarily manipulated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expose the paradox that AIGC detectors become inherently unreliable as language models improve (since they are trained on human data), and to question the commercial ecosystem where detection and "de-AIification" tools share supply chains, shifting focus from content quality to origin judgment.

Method: StyleShield: a flow matching framework for conditional text style transfer operating in continuous token embedding space using a DiT backbone with zero-initialized cross-attention adapters conditioned on frozen Qwen-7B representations. At inference, it adapts the SDEdit paradigm from image synthesis to text embeddings with a single parameter gamma controlling the evasion-preservation trade-off. RateAudit: a document-level scheduling algorithm that demonstrates detection-rate verdicts can be arbitrarily set.

Result: StyleShield achieves 94.6% evasion against the training detector and ≥99% against three unseen detectors on a multi-domain Chinese benchmark, while maintaining 0.928 semantic similarity. RateAudit shows detection-rate verdicts can be set to arbitrary values.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that current AIGC detection systems are fundamentally unreliable, as evasion techniques can achieve near-perfect success rates while preserving semantic content, and detection verdicts can be arbitrarily manipulated, questioning the validity of score-based evaluation in high-stakes settings like academic integrity.

Abstract: AI-generated content (AIGC) detectors are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as academic integrity screening, yet their reliability rests on a fundamental paradox: as language models are trained on human-written corpora, the statistical boundary between AI and human writing will inevitably dissolve as models improve. Commercial incentives have further distorted this landscape -- detection services and "de-AIification" tools often operate within the same supply chain, replacing evaluation of content quality with judgment of content origin. We present StyleShield, the first flow matching framework for conditional text style transfer, operating directly in continuous token embedding space via a DiT backbone with zero-initialized cross-attention adapters conditioned on frozen Qwen-7B representations. At inference, we adapt the SDEdit paradigm from image synthesis to text embeddings, with a single parameter gamma providing smooth continuous control over the evasion-preservation trade-off. On a multi-domain Chinese benchmark, StyleShield achieves 94.6% evasion against the training detector and >=99% against three unseen detectors, maintaining 0.928 semantic similarity. We further introduce RateAudit, a document-level scheduling algorithm that demonstrates detection-rate verdicts can be set to arbitrary values, directly questioning the reliability of score-based evaluation.

</details>


### [220] [Linking spatial biology and clinical histology via Haiku](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00925)
*Yan Cui,Jacob S. Leiby,Wenhui Lei,Dokyoon Kim,Yanxiang Deng,Aaron T. Mayer,Zhenqin Wu,Alexandro E. Trevino,Zhi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Haiku is a tri-modal contrastive learning model that aligns spatial proteomics (mIF), H&E histology, and clinical metadata in a shared embedding space, enabling cross-modal retrieval, improved classification/survival prediction, zero-shot biomarker inference, and counterfactual analysis for biological exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrating molecular, morphological, and clinical data is essential but systematic frameworks for jointly modeling these modalities remain limited, motivating the need for a unified tri-modal approach.

Method: Haiku is a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on 26.7M spatial proteomics patches from 3,218 tissue sections (1,606 patients, 11 organ types), with matched H&E histology and clinical metadata aligned in a shared embedding space.

Result: Outperforms competing approaches: cross-modal retrieval (Recall@50 up to 0.611 vs near-zero baseline), survival prediction (C-index 0.737, +7.91% relative improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson correlation 0.718 across 52 biomarkers). Counterfactual analysis recovers niche-specific shifts (e.g., increased CD8, granzyme B; reduced PD-L1, Ki67) consistent with favorable outcomes.

Conclusion: Tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context for biological exploration and hypothesis generation.

Abstract: Integrating molecular, morphological, and clinical data is essential for basic and translational biomedical research, yet systematic frameworks for jointly modeling these modalities remain limited. Here we present Haiku, a tri-modal contrastive learning model trained on multiplexed immunofluorescence (mIF). It comprises 26.7 million spatial proteomics patches from 3,218 tissue sections across 1,606 patients spanning 11 organ types, with matched hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) histology and clinical metadata aligned in a shared embedding space. Haiku enables three-way cross-modal retrieval, improves downstream classification and clinical prediction tasks over unimodal baselines, and supports zero-shot biomarker inference through fusion retrieval conditioned on clinical metadata-only text descriptions. Across tasks, Haiku outperforms competing approaches, achieving cross-modal retrieval (Recall@50 up to 0.611 versus near-zero baseline), survival prediction (C-index 0.737, +7.91% relative improvement), and zero-shot biomarker inference (mean Pearson correlation 0.718 across 52 biomarkers). Furthermore, we introduce a counterfactual prediction framework in which modifying only clinical metadata while fixing tissue morphology surfaces niche-specific molecular shifts associated with breast cancer stage progression and lung cancer survival outcomes. In a lung adenocarcinoma case study, the counterfactual analysis recovers niche-specific shifts characterized by increased CD8 and granzyme B, reduced PD-L1, and decreased Ki67, broadly consistent with patterns reported for favorable outcomes. We present these counterfactual results as exploratory, hypothesis-generating signals rather than mechanistic claims. These capabilities demonstrate that tri-modal alignment via Haiku enables integrative analysis of spatial biology, bridging molecular measurements with clinical context for biological exploration.

</details>


### [221] [A Review of the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve and a Proof About the Area Beneath It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00926)
*Steven Redolfi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes the probabilistic interpretation of the ROC curve's area under the curve (AUC) as the probability that a classifier ranks a random positive above a random negative, provides a bound on the error when assumptions are violated, and includes a literature review.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and bound the well-known but often loosely stated probabilistic interpretation of the AUC.

Method: Theoretical formalization of the AUC's probabilistic interpretation, derivation of error bounds under hypothesis violations, and a literature review.

Result: A formal proof of the AUC's probabilistic meaning and a bound on its deviation when the underlying hypothesis is not satisfied.

Conclusion: The AUC's probabilistic interpretation holds under certain conditions, with quantifiable deviations when assumptions are unmet.

Abstract: The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve of a binary classifier has often been utilized to measure the performance of the classifier. The area beneath this curve is used in particular because of its quoted probabilistic interpretation as being equal to the probability that the classifier will rank a random positive observation above a random negative observation. This paper formalizes this claim, produces a bound on how far away from the truth it is if a hypothesis is not met, and gives a small literature review of the ROC curve.

</details>


### [222] [PhaseNet++: Phase-Aware Frequency-Domain Anomaly Detection for Industrial Control Systems via Phase Coherence Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00929)
*Raviteja Bommireddy,Varshith Bandaru,Lohith Pakala,Pradeep Kumar B*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PhaseNet++ is a frequency-domain autoencoder for ICS multivariate time series anomaly detection that leverages phase information from STFT (overlooked by prior methods). It uses a Phase Coherence Index (PCI) to build phase-synchronized sensor graphs and jointly reconstructs magnitude and phase spectra, achieving strong results on SWaT benchmark (F1=90.98%) while being the first systematic study of phase-domain anomaly detection in ICS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ICS anomaly detection methods model inter-sensor relationships from raw time-domain amplitude values using GNNs and Transformers, but they discard the phase spectrum from time-frequency transformations. The authors argue that phase information is a complementary and previously overlooked detection modality that could improve anomaly detection performance.

Method: PhaseNet++ is a frequency-domain autoencoder operating on STFT of sliding sensor windows. It introduces a Phase Coherence Index (PCI) inspired by neuroscience's Phase Locking Value to summarize pairwise phase consistency into a continuous adjacency matrix. This guides a graph attention network for phase-synchronized sensor propagation, a sensor-token Transformer encoder for system-wide structure, and a dual-head decoder reconstructing magnitude and phase jointly via circular and coherence-aware objectives.

Result: On the SWaT benchmark, PhaseNet++ achieves F1-score of 90.98%, ROC-AUC of 95.66%, and average precision of 91.51%. The phase-aware front-end and PCI graph module add only 264,816 parameters, demonstrating lightweight phase inductive bias. However, its absolute F1-score is second best compared to recent raw-value methods under different evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: This work positions itself as the first systematic study of phase-domain anomaly detection for ICS. While not achieving state-of-the-art F1-score, it demonstrates that phase information provides a complementary and lightweight detection modality worth further investigation.

Abstract: Multivariate time series anomaly detection in ICS has attracted growing attention due to the increasing threat of cyber-physical attacks on critical infrastructure. State-of-the-art methods model inter-sensor relationships from raw time-domain amplitude values, using graph neural networks, Transformers. However, these methods discard the phase spectrum produced by time frequency transformations, We argue that phase information constitutes a complementary and previously overlooked detection modality for ICS anomaly detection. We present PhaseNet++, a frequency-domain autoencoder that operates on the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of sliding sensor windows, retaining both magnitude and phase spectra. A Phase Coherence Index (PCI), inspired by the Phase Locking Value from neuroscience, summarizes pairwise phase consistency across frequency bins into a continuous adjacency matrix. This matrix guides a graph attention network that propagates information preferentially among phase-synchronized sensors. A sensor-token Transformer encoder captures system-wide structure, and a dual-head decoder reconstructs magnitude and phase jointly via circular and coherence-aware objectives. Evaluated on the Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) benchmark, PhaseNet++ achieves an F1-score of 90.98%, ROC-AUC of 95.66%, and average precision of 91.51%. Ablation studies show that the phase-aware front-end and PCI graph module together add only 264,816 parameters, demonstrating that the phase inductive bias is lightweight. While the absolute F1-score is second best than that of all recent raw-value methods evaluated under different protocols, we position this work as the first systematic study of phase-domain anomaly detection for ICS.

</details>


### [223] [Hierarchical Federated Learning for Networked AI: From Communication Saving to Architecture-Aware Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00931)
*Seyed Mohammad Azimi-Abarghouyi,Mehdi Bennis,Leandros Tassiulas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that hierarchical federated learning should be viewed as an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI, organized around three design axes: architectural parameters, layer-wise optimization decomposition, and layer-wise communication realization, showing that convergence becomes architecture-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move hierarchical federated learning beyond its common framing as a communication-saving protocol and instead establish it as an architecture-aware design framework for distributed optimization over real multi-tier networks.

Method: The paper develops a viewpoint around three coupled design axes (architectural parameters, layer-wise optimization decomposition, layer-wise communication realization) and provides comparative analysis of flat FL, two-tier HFL, and deep HFL, with a regime-oriented design map using wireless edge intelligence as a flagship setting.

Result: Convergence in HFL is architecture-dependent, directly shaped by hierarchy choice, optimization roles, and communication mechanisms. The paper provides a design map and comparative perspective across different HFL architectures.

Conclusion: Hierarchical federated learning should be positioned as a practical methodology for designing future networked AI systems, not merely as a communication-saving protocol.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is fundamentally a distributed optimization problem executed by communicating agents with local data, local computation, and partial system visibility. Once FL is viewed through that lens, hierarchy is not merely a scalability mechanism. It becomes the natural place to rethink how distributed optimization should be organized over real multi-tier networks. This article argues that hierarchical federated learning (HFL) should move beyond its common framing as a communication-saving protocol and instead be viewed as an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI. The framework is organized around three coupled design axes: architectural parameters, layer-wise optimization decomposition, and layer-wise communication realization. The first axis determines the coordination geometry of learning through hierarchy depth, layer asymmetry, and layered connectivity. The second determines how the global FL objective is decomposed across layers and highlights modular multi-layer optimization as a major opportunity beyond one dominant method everywhere. The third determines how the distributed optimization is physically realized under heterogeneous communication regimes, from interference-limited lower tiers to reliable upper tiers. A central message is that, in HFL, convergence becomes architecture-dependent: it is directly shaped by the chosen hierarchy, the assigned optimization roles, and the communication mechanisms that connect them. We develop this viewpoint using large-scale wireless edge intelligence as a flagship networked AI setting, then provide a comparative perspective on flat FL, two-tier HFL, and deep HFL together with a regime-oriented design map. The resulting perspective positions HFL as a practical methodology for designing future networked AI systems.

</details>


### [224] [CGM-JEPA: Learning Consistent Continuous Glucose Monitor Representations via Predictive Self-Supervised Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00933)
*Hada Melino Muhammad,Zechen Li,Flora Salim,Ahmed A. Metwally*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CGM-JEPA and X-CGM-JEPA are self-supervised frameworks that learn view-agnostic representations from CGM data by predicting masked latent features, enabling robust transfer across modalities (venous OGTT, Glucodensity summaries) and settings (cohort generalization, modality shift, home CGM). X-CGM-JEPA ranks top on AUROC across regimes, reduces ethnic disparity gaps by 25-54%, and improves clustering by ~40%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CGM-based metabolic subphenotyping faces two coupled problems: (1) single-view representations fail under modality/setting shifts, and (2) baselines perform inconsistently across these shifts, necessitating view-agnostic representations.

Method: CGM-JEPA uses self-supervised learning with masked latent prediction (not raw value prediction). X-CGM-JEPA adds a masked Glucodensity cross-view objective. Pretrained on ~389k unlabeled CGM readings from 228 subjects, evaluated on two cohorts (N=27, N=17) across three regimes.

Result: X-CGM-JEPA ranks first/second on AUROC across all three regimes; exceeds strongest baseline by +6.5pp (cohort generalization) and +3.6pp (venous-to-CGM transfer, p<0.001). Reduces ethnicity AUROC gap by 25-54%; improves ARI +39%, NMI +40%.

Conclusion: Masked latent prediction with cross-view distributional objectives yields robust, transferable, and equitable representations for metabolic subphenotyping from CGM data.

Abstract: Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) can detect early metabolic subphenotypes (insulin resistance, IR; $β$-cell dysfunction), but population-scale deployment faces two coupled problems. First, the same physiological state appears through multiple views (CGM time series, venous OGTT, Glucodensity summaries), so single-view representations fail to transfer when deployment shifts the modality or setting. Second, baselines perform inconsistently across these shifts. Both problems point to one remedy: representations that abstract away from any single view to capture higher-level temporal and distributional structure. We propose CGM-JEPA, a self-supervised pretraining framework which predicts masked latent representations rather than raw values, yielding abstraction that transfers across modalities. X-CGM-JEPA adds a masked Glucodensity cross-view objective for complementary distributional information. We pretrain on $\sim$389k unlabeled CGM readings from 228 subjects and evaluate on two clinical cohorts ($N=27$ and $N=17$ public-release subsets) across three regimes (cohort generalization, venous-to-CGM transfer, home CGM) under 20-iteration $\times$ 2-fold cross-validation. X-CGM-JEPA ranks first or second on AUROC for both endpoints across all three regimes while no baseline does, exceeding the strongest baseline by up to $+6.5$ pp in cohort generalization and $+3.6$ pp in venous-to-CGM transfer (paired Wilcoxon, $p<0.001$). Under modality shift, it matches mean AUROC while redistributing toward weaker subgroups (ethnicity AUROC gap shrinks 25-54%); on sparse in-domain venous data, the distributional view lifts label-aware clustering (ARI $+39\%$, NMI $+40\%$). Code and weights: https://github.com/cruiseresearchgroup/CGM-JEPA

</details>


### [225] [GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02086)
*Baobing Zhang,Wanxin Sui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GETA-3DGS is the first end-to-end joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3D Gaussian splatting, achieving ~5x storage reduction without hand-tuned heuristics by introducing a 3DGS-aware dependency graph, render-aware saliency, and heterogeneous mixed-precision allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS compression methods treat pruning, quantization, and entropy coding as separate stages with hand-tuned heuristics (opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, SH truncation), which limits cross-scene generalization and prevents users from specifying target rate/quality budgets. A unified end-to-end framework is needed.

Method: (i) A 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph (QADG) treating each Gaussian as a group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (ii) a render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage; (iii) heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision co-optimized with structural sparsity under PPSG descent.

Result: ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, with no per-scene thresholds. Uniform 6-bit cap costs up to -6.74 dB vs. heterogeneous allocation. Complementary to downstream entropy coding (HAC++, CompGS).

Conclusion: GETA-3DGS provides a threshold-free, end-to-end automatic compression framework for 3DGS that generalizes across scenes and can be composed with existing entropy codecs for further gains.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) is a state-of-the-art representation for real-time photorealistic novel-view synthesis, yet a single high-fidelity scene typically occupies hundreds of megabytes to several gigabytes, exceeding the budgets of mobile, immersive, and volumetric video platforms. Existing 3DGS compression methods (e.g., HAC++, FlexGaussian, LP-3DGS) treat pruning, quantization, and entropy coding as separate stages and rely on hand-tuned heuristics (opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, SH truncation), limiting cross-scene generalization and preventing users from specifying a target rate or quality budget. We propose GETA-3DGS, to our knowledge the first end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3DGS. Building on GETA for joint pruning-quantization of deep networks, we contribute: (i) a 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph (QADG) treating each Gaussian primitive as a group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (ii) a render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage into a Gaussian-level importance score; and (iii) a heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision scheme co-optimized with structural sparsity under a projected partial saliency-guided (PPSG) descent guarantee. On Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, GETA-3DGS operates directly on raw Gaussian primitives rather than a post-hoc anchor representation, delivering ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS with no per-scene thresholds. Bit-width policy is the dominant rate-distortion lever: a uniform 6-bit cap costs up to -6.74 dB on view-dependent scenes versus our heterogeneous allocation, matching an information-theoretic reverse-water-filling analysis we develop. GETA-3DGS is complementary to existing codecs: entropy coding (HAC++, CompGS) is downstream, so the two can be composed.

</details>


### [226] [Structured Analytic Coherent Point Drift for Non-Rigid Point Set Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00934)
*Wei Feng,Haiyong Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytic-CPD replaces the kernel-based deformation field in Coherent Point Drift with a structured analytic mapping (truncated multivariate Taylor series), achieving faster convergence and lower errors in non-rigid point set registration, especially under large deformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard CPD's kernel-based displacement field scales with the number of points (M-by-M system), making it computationally heavy and less interpretable. The authors seek a compact, interpretable, and efficient alternative.

Method: Retains CPD's posterior correspondence layer (GMM-based), but replaces the Gaussian-kernel displacement-field M-step with a finite-dimensional structured analytic mapping estimator. Uses barycentric condensation to convert soft correspondences into weighted soft targets, and represents deformation via a truncated multivariate Taylor mapping. A degree-continuation strategy progressively activates higher-order analytic modes for stable large-deformation registration.

Result: On 2D analytic deformations and 3D smooth non-analytic deformations, Analytic-CPD achieves lower final errors and faster convergence than standard CPD in large-deformation settings.

Conclusion: CPD-style probabilistic correspondences combined with structured analytic mappings offer a compact, interpretable alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration.

Abstract: We introduce Analytic-CPD, a structured analytic variant of coherent point drift for non-rigid point set registration. The method retains the CPD posterior correspondence layer, but replaces the point-indexed Gaussian-kernel displacement-field M-step with a finite-dimensional structured analytic mapping estimator. Posterior probabilities from the Gaussian mixture model are condensed through a barycentric identity into weighted soft target points, converting the CPD pairwise soft-correspondence objective into a weighted analytic fitting problem. The deformation is represented by a truncated multivariate Taylor mapping of a vector-valued function, so the number of deformation parameters is controlled by the ambient dimension and the analytic order rather than by an M-by-M kernel system over the moving points. A degree-continuation strategy is further introduced to stabilize large-deformation registration by progressively activating higher-order analytic modes. Experiments on two-dimensional analytic deformations and three-dimensional smooth non-analytic deformations show that Analytic-CPD achieves lower final errors and faster convergence than standard CPD in representative large-deformation settings. The results suggest that CPD-style probabilistic correspondences and structured analytic mappings provide a compact and interpretable alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration. Code is available at https://github.com/monge-ampere/Analytic-CPD.

</details>


### [227] [Watch Your Step: Information Injection in Diffusion Models via Shadow Timestep Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00935)
*An Huang,Junggab Son,Zuobin Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), a mechanism that exploits diffusion models' timestep embedding space as a side channel for injecting malicious information, revealing security vulnerabilities in the temporal dimension of diffusion pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Timestep embeddings in diffusion models, despite being crucial for denoising networks to adapt across noise levels, remain underexplored in terms of security risks and reliable provenance. The authors aim to fill this gap by investigating how this underutilized temporal space can be exploited.

Method: The authors propose Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), which encodes side-channel information into the timestep embedding space. They provide theoretical analysis treating timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings and derive a mutual coherence evaluation to explain separability of disjoint timestep intervals.

Result: Different timesteps exhibit distinct representational capabilities that can encode side-channel information. This encoded information can be utilized for both attack and defense purposes through the scheduler interface. The timestep embedding space is shown to be a powerful side channel.

Conclusion: The diffusion model's timestep serves as a powerful side channel for carrying dedicated information, motivating new directions for adversarial generative modeling by understanding the temporal dimension.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become the foundation of modern generative systems, with most research focusing primarily on improving generation efficiency and output quality. The timestep embedding component is a crucial part of the diffusion pipeline, which provides a temporal conditioning signal to the denoising network, enabling it to adapt its predictions across different noise levels throughout the process. Despite their potential to contain substantial information, timestep embeddings remain underexplored in current research, especially for security risks and reliable provenance. To fill this gap, we introduce Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), a novel mechanism that investigates the underutilized temporal space for malicious information injection into diffusion models. In particular, when zooming in on the timestep embedding space, we find that different timesteps exhibit distinct representational capabilities that can encode side-channel information. Moreover, such encoded information can be utilized for attack and defense purposes through the scheduler interface. We present a theoretical analysis of timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings and derive a mutual coherence evaluation that explains the separability of disjoint timestep intervals. Our findings reveal the diffusion model's timestep as a powerful side channel for carrying dedicated information, motivating new directions for adversarial generative modeling by understanding the temporal dimension.

</details>


### [228] [EventADL: Open-Box Anomaly Detection and Localization Framework for Events in Cloud-Based Service Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00936)
*Luan Pham,Victor Nicolet,Joey Dodds,Hui Guan,Daniel Kroening*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EventADL is the first open-box event-based anomaly detection and localization framework for cloud systems, using Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs) and Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs) to detect anomalies and an Intervention Graph to localize root causes, achieving ≥90% F1 for detection and 100% top-3 accuracy for localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ADL methods focus on metric and log data, leaving event data unexplored. The authors analyze 520 real-world incidents to understand how anomalies manifest through events, motivating the need for an event-based framework.

Method: Three-phase framework: (1) offline training learns Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs) for normal entity interactions and Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs) for normal frequencies; (2) online detection flags deviations from either pattern as anomalous; (3) root cause localization uses an Intervention Graph to model relationships between interactions and anomalies.

Result: Evaluated on three real cloud service systems and two real-world incidents. EventADL achieves ≥90% F1-score for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy for root cause localization, outperforming existing methods.

Conclusion: EventADL effectively fills the gap in event-based ADL, operating with unlabeled data and producing interpretable anomalies with identified root causes.

Abstract: Anomaly detection and localization (ADL) is critical for maintaining reliability and availability in cloud systems. Recent ADL developments focus on metric and log data, leaving event data unexplored. To address this gap, we propose EventADL, the first open-box event-based ADL framework for cloud-based service systems. To motivate the design of our framework, we conduct a systematic analysis on 520 real-world incidents, and provide insights into how anomalies and their root causes manifest through event data. EventADL has three phases: offline training, online anomaly detection, and root cause localization. During the training phase, EventADL first learns Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs), which capture normal interactions between system entities using historical event data, and then learns Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs), which capture the normal frequency of known ESPs. In the online anomaly detection phase, any data in the event stream that deviates significantly from either pattern is identified as anomalous. For localization, EventADL constructs an Intervention Graph that models the relationships between recent system interactions and the detected anomalies for automatic root cause localization. The framework is designed to operate efficiently with unlabeled data and to produce interpretable anomalies with their corresponding root causes. Our evaluation on three real cloud service systems and two real-world incidents demonstrates that EventADL outperforms existing methods, achieving F1-scores of at least 90% for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy in root cause localization.

</details>


### [229] [Fusing Urban Structure and Semantics: A Conditional Diffusion Model for Cross-City OD Matrix Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00938)
*Bin Chen,Zhuoya Meng,Fang Yang,Runkang Guo,Jingtao Ding,Yin Zhang,Chuan Ai,Zhengqiu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEDAN is a structure-enhanced diffusion model that generates commuting Origin-Destination matrices by jointly modeling regional semantic attributes and spatial constraints (adjacency + distance), achieving 7.38% RMSE improvement over the state-of-the-art on real-world U.S. city data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Commuting flow modeling suffers from heterogeneity across cities due to the complex interplay of individual intentions, geographic constraints, and social dynamics. Existing models struggle to generalize across different urban environments.

Method: SEDAN models a city as an attributed graph (regions=nodes with demographic/POI features; flows=weighted edges). It uses a fusion mechanism with graph-transformer-based node interactions for semantic information, while injecting adjacency (guiding attention weights) and distance matrices (as diffusion conditions) as explicit spatial constraints.

Result: On real-world U.S. city OD datasets, SEDAN achieves 7.38% improvement in RMSE over WEDAN, and remains robust across heterogeneous urban scenarios and varying structural patterns.

Conclusion: The fusion of urban semantics and spatial constraints enables generalizable OD matrix generation that is both behaviorally plausible and geographically coherent. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of commuting flows is important for urban governance, traffic planning, and resource allocation. However, the combined influence of individual intentions, geographic constraints, and social dynamics leads to considerable heterogeneity in commuting patterns, making it difficult to develop generation models that generalize across cities. To address this issue, we propose SEDAN, a Structure-Enhanced Diffusion model conditioned on Attributed Nodes for generalizable OD matrix generation. SEDAN models a city as an attributed graph. Each region is treated as a node with demographic and point-of-interest features, and commuting flows are modeled as weighted edges. Adjacency and distance matrices are incorporated to characterize spatial structure. Based on this representation, we design a fusion mechanism within SEDAN to jointly model semantic information and spatial information. Regional semantic attributes are used to model latent travel demand through graph-transformer-based node interactions, while spatial structure is injected into the generation process as explicit constraints. The adjacency matrix guides attention weights to strengthen interactions between neighboring regions. Meanwhile, the distance matrix serves as a diffusion condition to capture spatial proximity and travel impedance. The fusion of urban semantics and spatial constraints enables SEDAN to generate OD matrices that are both behaviorally plausible and geographically coherent. Experiments on real-world OD datasets from U.S. cities show that SEDAN achieves a 7.38\% improvement in RMSE over the state-of-the-art baseline, WEDAN. It also remains robust across heterogeneous urban scenarios and varying structural patterns. Our work provides an effective and generalizable solution for commuting OD matrix generation. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SEDAN.

</details>


### [230] [From Flat Facts to Sharp Hallucinations: Detecting Stubborn Errors via Gradient Sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00939)
*Yee Zhing Liew,Andrew Huey Ping Tan,Anwar P. P Abdul Majeed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EPGS detects stubborn LLM hallucinations (confident wrong answers) by measuring gradient sensitivity to embedding perturbations, leveraging the geometric insight that robust facts lie in flat minima while hallucinations sit in sharp minima.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional hallucination detection fails on "Stubborn Hallucinations" where LLMs are confidently wrong, requiring a new approach.

Method: Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS): perturbs input embeddings with Gaussian noise and measures gradient magnitude spikes as a proxy for Hessian spectrum to distinguish flat (robust) vs. sharp (hallucinated) minima.

Result: EPGS significantly outperforms entropy-based and representation-based baselines for detecting high-confidence factual errors.

Conclusion: Geometric properties (flat vs. sharp minima) provide a robust signal for identifying stubborn hallucinations where LLMs are confidently wrong.

Abstract: Traditional hallucination detection fails on "Stubborn Hallucinations" -- errors where LLMs are confidently wrong. We propose a geometric solution: Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS). We hypothesize that while robust facts reside in flat minima, stubborn hallucinations sit in sharp minima, supported by brittle memorization. EPGS detects this sharpness by perturbing input embeddings with Gaussian noise and measuring the resulting spike in gradient magnitude. This acts as an efficient proxy for the Hessian spectrum, differentiating stable knowledge from unstable memorization. Our experiments show that EPGS significantly outperforms entropy-based and representation-based baselines, providing a robust signal for detecting high-confidence factual errors.

</details>


### [231] [Interpretable experiential learning based on state history and global feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00940)
*Anton Kolonin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new interpretable experiential learning model using state history and global feedback learns behavioral transition graphs with utility and evidence, performing comparably to neural networks on Atari Breakout in resource-constrained settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create an interpretable reinforcement learning model suitable for resource-constrained environments, as an alternative to resource-heavy neural network approaches.

Method: The model learns a behavioral transition graph between state sets, where transitions are attributed with utility and evidence count, using state history and global feedback.

Result: Evaluated on OpenAI Gym Atari Breakout, the model demonstrated performance comparable to known neural network-based solutions.

Conclusion: The interpretable model offers a viable alternative for reinforcement learning in resource-constrained environments with competitive performance.

Abstract: A new interpretable experiential learning model based on state history and global feedback is presented. It is capable of learning a behavioral model represented by a transition graph between sets of states, with transitions attributed with utility and evidence count. This model is expected to be suitable for solving reinforcement learning problem in resource-constrained environments. The model was thoroughly evaluated on the OpenAI Gym Atari Breakout benchmark, demonstrating performance comparable to some known neural network-based solutions.

</details>


### [232] [Divergence is Uncertainty: A Closed-Form Posterior Covariance for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00941)
*Jiarui Xing,Song Wang,Jian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that the divergence of a pre-trained flow matching velocity field directly equals the posterior covariance trace (up to a known factor), enabling exact uncertainty quantification in a single forward pass without retraining or ensembles, achieving ~10,000x compute savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantifying sample uncertainty in flow matching models is an open problem. Existing methods require retraining, costly ensembles, or multi-step variance propagation, introducing trade-offs between training cost, inference cost, and accuracy.

Method: The authors prove the "divergence-uncertainty identity": for any pre-trained flow matching velocity field, the trace of posterior covariance equals the velocity field's divergence times a known time-dependent prefactor plus an additive constant. The matrix-level form uses the velocity Jacobian. This is post-hoc and requires no architectural changes.

Result: On MNIST, per-pixel uncertainty maps focus on digit boundaries (high inter-sample variation). Scalar uncertainty scores track prediction error. The method achieves ~10,000x less total compute than ensembling or Monte Carlo dropout.

Conclusion: None of the existing trade-offs (training cost, inference cost, accuracy) are necessary. The identity provides exact, closed-form posterior covariance for any pre-trained flow matching model at minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Flow matching has become a leading framework for generative modeling, but quantifying the uncertainty of its samples remains an open problem. Existing approaches retrain the model with auxiliary variance heads, maintain costly ensembles, or propagate approximate covariance through many integration steps, trading off training cost, inference cost, or accuracy. We show that none of these trade-offs is necessary. We prove that, for any pre-trained flow matching velocity field, the trace of the posterior covariance over the clean data given the current state equals, in closed form, the divergence of the velocity field, up to a known time-dependent prefactor and an additive constant. We call this the \emph{divergence-uncertainty identity} for flow matching. The matrix-level form of the identity is similarly closed-form, depending solely on the velocity Jacobian. Because the identity is exact and post-hoc, it is computable on any pre-trained flow matching model, with no retraining and no architectural modification. For one-step generators such as MeanFlow, the same identity yields the exact end-to-end generation uncertainty in a single forward pass, eliminating the multi-step variance propagation required by all prior methods. Experiments on MNIST confirm that the resulting per-pixel uncertainty maps are semantically meaningful, concentrating on digit boundaries where inter-sample variation is highest, and that the scalar uncertainty score tracks actual prediction error, all at roughly 10,000$\times$ less total compute than ensembling or Monte Carlo dropout.

</details>


### [233] [Graph Rewiring in GNNs to Mitigate Over-Squashing and Over-Smoothing: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00951)
*Hugo Attali,Nathalie Pernelle,Davide Buscaldi,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This survey reviews graph rewiring techniques that modify graph topology to address over-squashing and over-smoothing in GNNs, covering theoretical foundations, implementations, and performance trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs suffer from over-squashing (excessive compression of distant information) and over-smoothing (loss of node distinguishability), both degrading information flow and limiting performance.

Method: The paper provides a comprehensive survey of graph rewiring techniques — methods that modify input graph topology to improve information propagation, examining their theoretical foundations and practical implementations.

Result: A systematic review and comparison of state-of-the-art graph rewiring approaches, including their theoretical underpinnings and performance trade-offs.

Conclusion: Graph rewiring is a promising class of methods to mitigate over-squashing and over-smoothing in GNNs, with various approaches offering different trade-offs between effectiveness and computational cost.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks are powerful models for learning from graph-structured data, yet their effectiveness is often limited by two critical challenges: over-squashing, where information from distant nodes is excessively compressed, and over-smoothing, where repeated propagation makes node representations indistinguishable. Both phenomena stem from the interaction between message passing and the input topology, ultimately degrading information flow and limiting the performance of GNNs. In this survey, we examine graph rewiring techniques, a class of methods designed to modify the graph topology to enhance information propagation in GNNs. We provide a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art rewiring approaches, delving into their theoretical underpinnings, practical implementations, and performance trade-offs.

</details>


### [234] [Robust volatility updates for Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00966)
*Christoph Mathys,Nicolas Legrand,Peter Thestrup Waade,Nace Mikus,Lilian Aline Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper fixes a mathematical flaw in Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering (HGF) where volatility coupling could produce negative posterior precision (impossible). The authors introduce a modified quadratic approximation using interpolation between two expansions, employing the Lambert W function, resulting in robust update equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original HGF update equations for volatility-coupled nodes can produce negative posterior precision in certain parameter regions, which is logically impossible and causes the algorithm to crash. The authors aim to fix this fundamental mathematical issue.

Method: The authors modify the quadratic approximation of the variational energy for volatility-coupled nodes by interpolating between two quadratic expansions: one at the prior prediction and another at a second mode whose location is derived in closed form using the Lambert W function.

Result: The proposed update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even for large prediction errors, avoiding the negative precision issue entirely.

Conclusion: The modified approach successfully resolves the negative posterior precision problem in HGF networks with volatility coupling, ensuring stable and mathematically valid belief updating across all parameter regimes.

Abstract: Hierarchical Gaussian Filtering (HGF) networks allow for efficient updating of posterior distributions (beliefs) about hidden states of an agent's environment. HGF parent nodes can target the mean or variance of their children. New information entering at input nodes leads to a cascade of belief updates across the network according to one-step update equations for each node's mean and precision (inverse variance). However, the original form of the update equations for variance-targeting parents(volatility coupling) can in some regions of parameter space lead to negative posterior precision, a logical impossibility which causes the updating algorithm to terminate with an error. In this report, we introduce a modified quadratic approximation to the variational energy of volatility-coupled nodes that avoids negative posterior precision. The key idea is to interpolate between two quadratic expansions of the variational energy: one at the prior prediction and one at a second mode whose location is obtained in closed form via the Lambert W function. The resulting update equations are robust across the entire parameter space and faithfully track the variational posterior even for large prediction errors.

</details>


### [235] [Physiology-Aware Masked Cross-Modal Reconstruction for Biosignal Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00973)
*Hao Zhou,Simon A. Lee,Cyrus Tanade,Keum San Chun,Juhyeon Lee,Migyeong Gwak,Megha Thukral,Justin Sung,Eugene Hwang,Mehrab Bin Morshed,Li Zhu,Viswam Nathan,Md Mahbubur Rahman,Subramaniam Venkatraman,Sharanya Arcot Desai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: xMAE is a biosignal pretraining framework that uses masked cross-modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals (e.g., ECG and PPG) to learn physiologically meaningful timing representations, outperforming baselines on 15/19 downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-supervised learning methods treat biosignals from different body locations as interchangeable views, ignoring the directional temporal dynamics (e.g., ECG precedes PPG in a heartbeat). This oversight limits the physiological meaningfulness of learned representations.

Method: xMAE employs masked cross-modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals as a training-time constraint, forcing the model to capture the directional timing structure between signals like ECG (electrical activation) and PPG (peripheral pulse delay).

Result: xMAE outperforms both unimodal and multimodal baselines on 15 of 19 downstream tasks (cardiovascular outcome prediction, abnormal lab detection, sleep staging, demographic inference) and generalizes across devices, body locations, and acquisition settings. ECG-PPG timing structure is reflected in learned PPG representations.

Conclusion: Incorporating temporal structure into multimodal pretraining is effective when signals observe different stages of a shared underlying physiological process, as demonstrated by xMAE's superior performance across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Biosignals acquired from different locations on the body often provide temporally ordered views of the same underlying physiological process. However, most existing self supervised learning methods treat these signals as interchangeable views, overlooking the directional temporal dynamics that link them. A canonical example is the relationship between electrocardiography (ECG), which captures the electrical activation initiating each heartbeat, and photoplethysmography (PPG), which records the resulting peripheral pulse delayed by vascular dynamics. To capture this structured relationship, we introduce xMAE, a biosignal pretraining framework that leverages masked cross modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals as a training time constraint to encourage physiologically meaningful timing structure in the learned representations. We show that pretraining with xMAE yields representations that outperform both unimodal and multimodal baselines on 15 of 19 downstream tasks, including cardiovascular outcome prediction, abnormal laboratory test detection, sleep staging, and demographic inference, while generalizing across devices, body locations, and acquisition settings. Further analysis suggests that the ECG PPG timing structure is reflected in the learned PPG representations. More broadly, xMAE demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating temporal structure into multimodal pretraining when signals observe different stages of a shared underlying process. Code is available at https://github.com/hzhou3/xMAE.

</details>


### [236] [Continual Learning of Feedback-based Molecular Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01020)
*Siddhant Setia,Junichi Suzuki,Tadashi Nakano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using continual learning algorithms to improve performance estimation in feedback-based molecular communication protocols through sequential simulation experiments, achieving better accuracy than baseline neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve performance estimation in molecular communication protocols by addressing the challenge of learning from sequential simulation experiments without forgetting previously learned knowledge.

Method: The method uses continual learning algorithms with regularization and replay strategies in the loss function, built on a standard neural network architecture, to incrementally learn from a continuous stream of simulation results.

Result: The proposed CL-based estimators effectively learn from continuous simulation streams and enhance baseline neural network accuracy at various computational costs.

Conclusion: This paper establishes the implications and effectiveness of continual learning in the field of molecular communication.

Abstract: This paper proposes and evaluates a new performance estimation method that leverages continual learning (CL) algorithms to carry out sequential simulation experiments for a feedback-based molecular communication protocol. As the protocol is sequentially examined in various experimental settings, the proposed CL-based performance estimators incrementally learn a series of unexperienced estimation tasks without compromising those that have been learned in the past. They are designed to work on a standard neural network architecture by customizing regularization and replay strategies in the loss function. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed estimators can effectively learn on a continuous stream of simulation results and enhance the baseline neural network by improving estimation accuracy at a variety of computational costs. This paper's contribution is to establish the implications of CL in the field of molecular communication.

</details>


### [237] [Finite-Sample Analysis of Elimination in Active Hypothesis Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01039)
*Ziyuan Lin,Hoang Ngoc Nguyen,Jie Xu,Ivan Ruchkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm for fixed-confidence active hypothesis testing, where hypothesis sets are progressively pruned and sensing effort is reallocated. The method achieves tighter finite-sample bounds on expected stopping time by reducing non-leading terms, and introduces an aggressiveness parameter to trade off elimination speed vs. confidence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active hypothesis testing with fixed confidence arises in safety-critical applications where reliable decisions must be made with minimal samples. Existing methods may waste sampling effort on clearly inferior hypotheses, motivating a principled elimination mechanism to accelerate stopping.

Method: The authors augment the Track-and-Stop algorithm with hypothesis elimination: champion-specific active-opponent sets are progressively pruned, and sensing effort is reallocated to surviving alternatives. A non-asymptotic upper bound on expected stopping time is derived. An aggressiveness parameter controls the speed-confidence trade-off.

Result: The theoretical analysis shows elimination improves the finite-sample bound at the scale of non-leading terms (tighter tracking and concentration constants). Synthetic Gaussian experiments confirm the predicted gains.

Conclusion: Hypothesis elimination yields meaningful finite-sample improvements in active hypothesis testing without sacrificing asymptotic optimality, with a tunable trade-off between aggressiveness and confidence.

Abstract: A fixed-confidence, finite-sample problem of active hypothesis testing arises in many safety-critical applications. Situated in the context of sequential hypothesis testing, this paper studies the effect of hypothesis elimination on the stopping time. We introduce an elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm, in which champion-specific active-opponent sets are progressively pruned, and sensing effort is reallocated toward the surviving alternatives. Our analysis derives a non-asymptotic upper bound on the expected stopping time. The gain in finite-sample from elimination appears on the scale of the non-leading term, resulting from tighter tracking and concentration constants on the reduced hypothesis set. Furthermore, we introduce an aggressiveness parameter to modulate the trade-off between faster elimination and weaker confidence guarantee. An experimental study on synthetic Gaussian instances confirms the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [238] [Linear-Readout Floors and Threshold Recovery in Computation in Superposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01192)
*Hector Borobia,Elies Seguí-Mas,Guillermina Tormo-Carbó*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reconciles two seemingly contradictory results on computational capacity in superposition by showing they maintain different interface invariants. It derives a Welch-type lower bound for biorthogonal linear readouts and explains that the $d^{3/2}$ scale is a compatibility threshold for a specific template, not a universal bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the apparent contradiction between two recent results that claim different recursive capacity regimes ($\tilde{O}(d^{3/2})$ vs. near-quadratic) for computation in superposition, by showing they are compatible under different interface invariants.

Method: The paper provides a conceptual argument formalizing the distinction between the two approaches' interface invariants, derives a rank-trace Welch-type lower bound for biorthogonal linear readouts, and analyzes random-support threshold recovery versus linear readout performance at quadratic feature load.

Result: For $F \gg d$, worst-case off-diagonal cross-talk of any unit-diagonal linear readout is $\Omega(d^{-1/2})$, tight for unit-norm tight frames. At $F=d^2$, threshold recovery succeeds for sparsities $s=O(d/\log d)$, while linear readouts incur $\Omega(s/d)$ error. The $d^{3/2}$ scale matches the Welch floor against the Hänni correction layer's tolerance.

Conclusion: The $d^{3/2}$ bound is a compatibility threshold for a specific approximate-linear template, not a universal upper bound. Robust nonlinear reset beyond that template remains an open problem.

Abstract: Two recent approaches to computation in superposition reach different recursive capacity regimes: Hänni et al. certify $\tilde{O}(d^{3/2})$ computable features in width $d$ via an approximate-linear recursive template, while Adler and Shavit reach near-quadratic capacity (up to logarithmic factors) using thresholded Boolean recovery. The main contribution of this paper is conceptual: we argue these results are not contradictory because they maintain different interface invariants, and we formalize the distinction.
  As a tool, we record a rank-trace Welch-type lower bound for biorthogonal linear readouts: for $F \gg d$, the worst-case off-diagonal cross-talk of any unit-diagonal linear readout is $Ω(d^{-1/2})$, and the bound is tight on average for unit-norm tight frames. At quadratic feature load $F=d^2$, random-support threshold recovery succeeds for sparsities $s=O(d/\log d)$, while linear readouts still incur $Ω(s/d)$ average per-coordinate squared error on Bernoulli sparse states. Matching the Welch floor against the published tolerance of the Hänni correction layer explains the $d^{3/2}$ scale as a compatibility threshold for that template, not a universal upper bound. Robust nonlinear reset beyond the Hänni template is left open.

</details>


### [239] [Learning in the Fisher Subspace: A Guided Initialization for LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01046)
*Zhi-Quan Feng,Ying-Jia Lin,Hung-Yu Kao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Fisher-guided, data-aware initialization strategy for LoRA that selects adaptation subspaces based on task-relevant sensitivity (curvature from downstream data) rather than weight-only magnitude, consistently improving downstream performance across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LoRA initialization strategies rely on intrinsic properties of pre-trained weights (e.g., weight magnitude), assuming weight geometry alone reflects task relevance. However, this overlooks how the model interacts with downstream data, leading to poor initialization that allocates capacity to task-irrelevant directions.

Method: The authors formulate LoRA initialization as identifying parameter-space directions with the highest impact under the target data distribution. They propose a Fisher-guided framework that uses curvature information (Fisher information) induced by downstream data to characterize how parameter perturbations influence predictions, yielding a task-dependent criterion for selecting LoRA directions.

Result: Empirical results across diverse tasks and modalities demonstrate that the proposed data-aware initialization consistently and significantly improves downstream performance over existing approaches.

Conclusion: Data-aware sensitivity, rather than weight-only magnitude, should govern LoRA subspace selection. The Fisher-guided framework provides a principled way to align adaptation with the target objective, leading to better downstream performance.

Abstract: LoRA adapts large language models (LLMs) by restricting updates to low-rank subspaces of pre-trained weights. While this substantially reduces training cost, the effectiveness of adaptation critically depends on which subspace is chosen at initialization: a poor initialization that allocates capacity to task-irrelevant directions can severely hinder downstream performance. Existing initialization strategies primarily rely on the intrinsic properties of pre-trained weights, implicitly assuming that weight geometry alone reflects task relevance. However, such criteria overlook how the model interacts with the downstream data distribution. In this work, we formulate LoRA initialization as identifying the degree of impact of directions in parameter space under the target data distribution. We argue that data-aware sensitivity, rather than weight-only magnitude, should govern the choice of adaptation subspaces. Building on this perspective, we propose a Fisher-guided framework that leverages curvature information induced by downstream data to characterize how parameter perturbations influence model predictions. This perspective yields a principled, task-dependent criterion for selecting LoRA directions that better align adaptation with the target objective. Empirical results across diverse tasks and modalities demonstrate that data-aware initialization consistently and significantly improves downstream performance over existing approaches.

</details>


### [240] [LEAP: Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining for Efficient Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01058)
*Shashank Kapadia,Deep Naryan Mishra,Sujal Reddy Alugubelli,Haoan Wang,Saipraveen Vabbilisetty,Rishi Bhatia,Anupriya Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEAP introduces an auxiliary training objective that resolves the incompatibility between layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit, achieving 1.61× speedup on distilled models where standard distillation yields zero speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper identifies that standard layer-aligned distillation suppresses representational convergence across layers, which is the very signal that early-exit mechanisms rely on to make early termination decisions. This incompatibility renders early-exit mechanisms ineffective on distilled models.

Method: LEAP (Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining) augments standard distillation with an auxiliary objective that constrains intermediate layers to approximate final-layer representations, without requiring architectural modifications.

Result: LEAP-MiniLM achieves 1.61× measured wall-clock speedup (batch=1, NVIDIA L4) at θ=0.95, with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7 and 1.80× theoretical layer reduction, while standard distilled models achieve zero effective speedup. Validated on STS-B (0.760 ± 0.006) and BEIR benchmarks.

Conclusion: LEAP effectively reconciles distillation and early exit paradigms, providing a practical solution with operational deployment guidance including latency measurements, decision thresholds, and deployment criteria.

Abstract: Layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit represent two predominant computational efficiency paradigms for transformer inference; yet we establish that they exhibit systematic incompatibility under standard deployment conditions for convergence-based early exit. Distillation objectives that align intermediate student layers to teacher representations suppress the representational convergence that early-exit mechanisms exploit, rendering such mechanisms ineffective on distilled models. We introduce LEAP (Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining), an auxiliary training objective that reconciles this incompatibility. LEAP requires no architectural modifications; it augments standard distillation with a single constraint ensuring intermediate layers approximate final-layer representations. LEAP-MiniLM achieves 1.61$\times$ measured wall-clock speedup (batch=1, NVIDIA L4) at $θ$=0.95, with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7 and 1.80$\times$ theoretical layer reduction, where standard distilled models achieve zero effective speedup. We validate across sentence similarity (STS-B: 0.760 $\pm$ 0.006) and retrieval benchmarks (BEIR), providing operational guidance including latency measurements, decision thresholds, and deployment criteria.

</details>


### [241] [GEODE: Angle-Adaptive OOD Detection with Universal Scorer Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01063)
*Bruno Abrahao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GEODE (GEometry-preserving DEtection) is a new OOD detection method that replicates the benefits of Outlier Exposure through a synthetic angle-adaptive norm loss, achieving strong performance across all standard scorers without requiring curated auxiliary data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outlier Exposure shows strong OOD detection but has scorer-dependent tradeoffs (strong on MSP, weak on KNN) and requires curated auxiliary data. The authors first analyze why OE works—finding it's boundary calibration rather than OOD coverage—then propose a method that replicates this calibration synthetically.

Method: GEODE uses an angle-adaptive norm loss where targets scale per-sample with cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, preserving feature geometry where boundary structure matters. The design is grounded in four theorems based on neural collapse.

Result: GEODE works across all seven standard scorers on CIFAR-10 (near-OOD AUROC 89.0-92.3, far-OOD 93.05). Combined with OE, GEODE reaches 95.0 MSP / 94.8 KNN on CIFAR-10, beats OE on every scorer for CIFAR-100, and shows gains on WRN-28-10 (+4.5 Energy).

Conclusion: GEODE preserves geometry that distance-based scorers depend on, unlike methods pushing OOD into classifier null space (e.g., PFS achieves 14.38 KNN AUROC, worse than random). It outperforms vanilla CE at matched epoch counts and avoids catastrophic failure on any scorer.

Abstract: Outlier Exposure (OE) is among the strongest training-based OOD detectors on standard benchmarks but exhibits scorer-dependent tradeoffs (e.g., strong on MSP, weak on KNN) and requires curated auxiliary data. We show why OE works: its features sit at the same geometric locus as real near-OOD data, with the boundary-adjacent quartile driving nearly all of OE's gain. OE is boundary calibration, not OOD coverage. GEODE (GEOmetry-preserving DEtection) replicates this calibration synthetically through an angle-adaptive norm loss in which targets scale per-sample with cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, preserving feature geometry where boundary structure matters. Four theorems grounded in neural collapse justify the design. GEODE works across all seven standard scorers on CIFAR-10 (near-OOD AUROC 89.0-92.3, far-OOD reaching 93.05; no catastrophic failure on any scorer). Since the OOD regime is unknown at deployment, this is the test that matters. GEODE outperforms vanilla CE at matched epoch counts. Combined with OE, GEODE reaches 95.0 MSP / 94.8 KNN on CIFAR-10 and beats OE on every scorer on CIFAR-100. The gains hold on WRN-28-10 (+4.5 Energy, 3 seeds). Unlike methods that push OOD into the classifier null space (e.g., PFS, 14.38 KNN AUROC, worse than random), GEODE's adaptive target preserves the geometry that distance-based scorers depend on.

</details>


### [242] [A dimensional R2 regression metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01066)
*Jaesung Yoo,Stefan Lemke,Jian Zhong Guo,Kanaka Rajan,Adam Hantman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dim-R2, an extension of the R2 score that handles multi-dimensional regression outputs, provides a multidimensional accuracy profile, and reduces noise sensitivity, offering a more interpretable and flexible evaluation metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: R2 is the standard regression metric but has three key limitations: it only works with up to 2D inputs, reduces accuracy to a single scalar (hiding rich prediction patterns), and is highly sensitive to low-variance noise channels, producing uninterpretable negative values.

Method: The authors propose Dim-R2, a simple extension of R2 that accepts arbitrary dimensionality, provides a multidimensional view of accuracy, and reduces sensitivity to noise. It is demonstrated on synthetic sinusoidal data and three multidimensional regression datasets.

Result: Dim-R2 successfully handles arbitrary-dimensional outputs, reveals patterns in prediction accuracy across dimensions, and shows reduced sensitivity to noise compared to standard R2. Its advantages are demonstrated on both synthetic and real multidimensional regression datasets.

Conclusion: Dim-R2 provides a more interpretable and flexible metric for regression evaluation, highlighting accuracy patterns across dimensions and guiding regression modeling, while overcoming the dimensionality, scalar-summary, and noise-sensitivity limitations of standard R2.

Abstract: R2 score is the standard metric for evaluating regression tasks, offering a normalized magnitude-agnostic measure of accuracy that captures variance. However, R2 has three key limitations: it is limited to at most two dimensional inputs, it reduces the score to a single scalar that hides rich patterns of prediction accuracy, and it is sensitive to low-variance noise channels which can yield large, uninterpretable negative values. We introduce the Dimensional R2 score (Dim-R2), a simple extension of R2 that accepts data of arbitrary dimensionality, provides a multidimensional view of accuracy, and reduces sensitivity to noise. We demonstrate its advantages on both synthetic sinusoidal data and three multidimensional regression datasets. Dim-R2 offers an interpretable and flexible metric that highlights patterns in regression accuracy, guiding regression modeling.

</details>


### [243] [Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01067)
*James Butterworth,Gevik Grigorian,Alejandro DiazDelaO*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DVISR extends Deep Symbolic Regression to a variational Bayesian approach, enabling posterior inference over both symbolic expression trees and their constants, and shows improved uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic regression methods like DSR identify a single best-fitting expression but fail to quantify uncertainty, which is critical when data is noisy or limited. A Bayesian approach is needed for probabilistic inference over candidate expressions.

Method: DVISR replaces the original reward in DSR with the ELBO integrand and extends the network architecture to output distributions over constants within expressions, enabling variational Bayesian inference over both expression trees and constants.

Result: DVISR can recover the true posterior in simple settings both with and without constant tokens, and its performance under varying expression space sizes is examined.

Conclusion: DVISR is positioned as a scalable step toward Bayesian symbolic regression with full uncertainty quantification over symbolic models.

Abstract: Symbolic regression discovers explicit, interpretable equations without assuming a functional form in advance. A Bayesian approach strengthens this through probability distributions over candidate expressions, thus quantifying uncertainty in the presence of noisy and limited data. Deep Symbolic Regression (DSR) uses a neural network to generate symbolic expressions, but it is designed to identify a single best-fitting expression rather than infer a posterior distribution over models. We introduce Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression (DVISR), a variational Bayesian extension of DSR. DVISR replaces the original reward with the integrand of the evidence lower bound.
