We introduce STABLEVAL, a disagreement-aware evaluation framework that models latent item correctness and annotator-specific confusion patterns to produce posterior expected item credit and calibrated agent-level scores. Unlike label-denoising approaches such as Dawid-Skene, STABLEVAL is explicitly designed for stable and uncertainty-aware system evaluation rather than hard label recovery. We formalize ranking stability as a first-class evaluation objective and analyze how aggregation methods preserve or distort underlying annotator behavior. Across controlled synthetic experiments and multiple real-world human-annotated benchmarks, majority vote exhibits increasing score error and ranking instability under annotator heterogeneity and adversarial noise, while STABLEVAL yields more stable and statistically grounded system rankings. These results demonstrate that modeling disagreement is essential for robust and reproducible AI evaluation.

</details>


### [355] [Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02124)
*Reza Rastegar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that the zero-temperature limit of softmax-routed mixture-of-experts is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces (boundary mass), not the full input space, with quantitative risk bounds and Γ-convergence results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the singular behavior of softmax-routed MoE models as temperature approaches zero, particularly near routing ties, and to establish rigorous theoretical foundations for how soft routing approximates hard routing.

Method: Coarea/tube estimates at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression, proving boundary mass is linear in slab width with leading constant given by surface integrals over routing interfaces; Γ-convergence analysis; teacher-student landscape-transfer principle; Gaussian two-expert calculation.

Result: Boundary mass scales linearly with slab width; soft-to-hard risk bounds are quantitative; soft objectives Γ-converge to hard-routing objective under compactness and uniform margin control; the zero-temperature limit is governed by routing interface layers; conditional landscape-transfer principle holds.

Conclusion: The geometry of routing interfaces, not the full input space, controls the soft-to-hard routing transition, enabling approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior in teacher-student settings, with local symmetry-breaking aligned with the teacher separator.

Abstract: Softmax-routed mixture-of-experts models approach hard routing as the temperature tends to zero, but this limit is singular near routing ties. This paper studies that singularity at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression. The central object is the \emph{boundary mass}, namely the probability that the top two router scores are separated by only a small margin. Under smoothness and transversality assumptions on the router and input law, we prove coarea/tube estimates showing that this mass is linear in the slab width, with leading constant given by a surface integral over the routing interface in the binary case. These estimates yield quantitative soft-to-hard risk bounds and, under compactness and uniform margin control, $Γ$-convergence of the soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. The main conclusion is that the zero-temperature limit is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces, not by the full input space. We then use this geometric core in two more model-dependent directions. In a teacher--student setting, we prove a conditional landscape-transfer principle showing that, when the profiled hard-routing problem has favorable identifiability and curvature and the relevant derivatives transfer at boundary-layer scale, small-temperature soft routing inherits approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior away from teacher-equivalent partitions. We also give a reduced two-expert Gaussian calculation that illustrates a local symmetry-breaking mechanism aligned with the teacher separator.

</details>


### [356] [LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02133)
*Hongwei Jin,Keunju Song,Zeeshan Memon,Yijiang Li,Stefano Fenu,Hongseok Kim,Liang Zhao,Kibaek Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LUMINA-Bench is a benchmark suite for evaluating learning-based ACOPF surrogates across multiple network topologies, with unified metrics and open-source tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based ACOPF surrogates often fail to generalize across different network topologies, limiting their deployment on unseen grids and operational what-if studies.

Method: The benchmark covers multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation; evaluates homogeneous/heterogeneous architectures under single/multi-topology settings; compares constraint-aware training objectives (MSE, augmented Lagrangian, violation-based Lagrangian).

Result: Provides unified metrics capturing both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations; open-sources data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks as the LUMINA suite.

Conclusion: LUMINA-Bench enables reproducible, feasibility-aware evaluation of OPF surrogates, accelerating research on topology-generalizable power grid models.

Abstract: AC optimal power flow (ACOPF) is foundational yet computationally expensive in power grid operations, driving learning-based surrogates for large-scale grid analysis. These surrogates, however, often fail to generalize across network topologies, a critical gap for deployment on grids not seen during training and for routine operational what-if studies. We introduce LUMINA-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark suite for ACOPF surrogate learning covering multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation. The benchmark evaluates homogeneous and heterogeneous architectures under single- and multi-topology learning settings using unified metrics that capture both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations. We additionally compare constraint-aware training objectives, including MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses, to characterize accuracy-robustness trade-offs across settings. Data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks are open-sourced as the LUMINA suite to support reproducibility and accelerate future research on feasibility-aware OPF surrogates.

</details>


### [357] [On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02141)
*Kaixuan Ji,Qiwei Di,Heyang Zhao,Qingyue Zhao,Quanquan Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a nearly complete characterization of sample complexity for KL-regularized offline multi-armed bandits, proving matching upper and lower bounds across all regularization strengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KL regularization is widely used in offline decision-making, but the exact sample complexity of KL-regularized offline learning has not been fully characterized.

Method: The authors analyze the KL-PCB algorithm (Zhao et al., 2026) in multi-armed bandits, deriving upper bounds under large ($η=\tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$) and small ($η=\tilde{Ω}(ε^{-1})$) regularization, and provide matching lower bounds.

Result: Sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε)$ under large regularization and $\tilde{Ω}(SAC^{π^*}/ε^2)$ under small regularization; matching lower bounds are provided across the entire regularization range.

Conclusion: The results provide a nearly complete characterization of offline multi-armed bandits with KL regularization.

Abstract: Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization is widely used in offline decision-making and offers several benefits, motivating recent work on the sample complexity of offline learning with respect to KL-regularized performance metrics. Nevertheless, the exact sample complexity of KL-regularized offline learning remains largely from fully characterized. In this paper, we study this question in the setting of multi-armed bandits (MABs). We provide a sharp analysis of KL-PCB (Zhao et al., 2026), showing that it achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε)$ under large regularization $η= \tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$, and a sample complexity of $\tildeΩ(SAC^{π^*}/ε^2)$ under small regularization $η= \tildeΩ(ε^{-1})$, where $η$ is the regularization parameter, $S$ is the number of contexts, $A$ is the number of arms, $C^{π^*}$ policy coverage coefficient at the optimal policy $π^*$, $ε$ is the desired sub-optimality, and $\tilde{O}$ and $\tildeΩ$ hide all poly-logarithmic factors. We further provide a pair of sharper sample complexity lower bounds, which matches the upper bounds over the entire range of regularization strengths. Overall, our results provide a nearly complete characterization of offline multi-armed bandits with KL regularization.

</details>


### [358] [Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02143)
*Dongwon Kim,Gyuejeong Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: pFLAlign is a gradient alignment framework for personalized federated learning that preserves client-specific information by reducing variance during local training and realigning the global model during aggregation, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pFL methods fail to reliably preserve personalized information due to high-variance gradients from limited heterogeneous client data and aggregation distortions that corrupt client-specific optimization directions.

Method: pFLAlign introduces two complementary mechanisms: (1) adaptive local gradient direction adjustment to reduce variance during client-side optimization, and (2) realignment of the global model with each client's personalized direction to mitigate aggregation-induced distortion. The framework is derived from PAC Bayesian analysis.

Result: Consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state-of-the-art results across experiments and ablation studies.

Conclusion: Gradient alignment is an effective approach for preserving client-specific information in personalized federated learning, and pFLAlign provides both theoretical grounding and practical improvement over existing methods.

Abstract: Personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to adapt models to client specific data distributions, yet it often fails to reliably preserve personalized information. Local training is hindered by high variance gradients induced by limited and heterogeneous client data, while aggregation further distorts client specific optimization directions. To address these challenges, we propose pFLAlign, a gradient alignment framework to maintain client specific information during both local training and aggregation. pFLAlign consists of two complementary mechanisms: one adapts local gradient directions to reduce variance during client side optimization, and the other mitigates aggregation induced distortion by realigning the global model with each client's personalized direction. Theoretically, we derive pFLAlign from a PAC Bayesian analysis, which reveals how personalized gradient alignment preserves client specific information. Our experiments and ablation studies show that pFLAlign consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state of the art results.

</details>


### [359] [Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02144)
*Debarshi Kundu,Archisman Ghosh,Swaroop Ghosh,Vasant Honavar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gaussian Kernel Attention (GKA) replaces dot-product self-attention with an RBF kernel-based similarity operator, removing learned Q/K/V projections and learning only a bandwidth parameter per head. It achieves competitive performance with far fewer parameters and FLOPs in language modeling, offering an interpretable, locality-aware attention mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether learned linear projections (Q, K, V) in Transformer self-attention are necessary or can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator, linking modern Transformers to classical kernel smoothing methods.

Method: GKA replaces dot-product attention with a Gaussian RBF kernel applied directly to per-head token features, learning only a bandwidth parameter σ_h per head. It uses causal masking and sliding-window constraints via kernel renormalization for language modeling, with a single output projection W_O.

Result: At depth 20, GKA uses 0.42× parameters and 0.49× training FLOPs of standard attention, trains stably with near-zero train-validation gap, and shows competitive benchmark performance with higher BPB at this scale.

Conclusion: GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with explicit locality control, offering a new dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design.

Abstract: Self-attention in Transformers is typically implemented as $\mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d})V$, where $Q=XW_Q$, $K=XW_K$, and $V=XW_V$ are learned linear projections of the input $X$. We ask whether these learned projections are necessary, or whether they can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator. We introduce \textbf{Gaussian Kernel Attention} (GKA), a drop-in replacement for dot-product attention that computes token affinities directly using a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel applied to per-head token features. Each head learns only a bandwidth parameter $σ_h$, while a single output projection $W_O$ preserves compatibility with the standard Transformer interface. GKA can be interpreted as normalized kernel regression over tokens, linking modern Transformer architectures to classical non-local filtering and kernel smoothing methods. We evaluate GKA in both vision and language modeling settings. For autoregressive language modeling within the \texttt{nanochat} framework, we implement causal masking and sliding-window constraints by masking and renormalizing the Gaussian kernel. At depth 20, a GKA model with $0.42\times$ the parameters and $0.49\times$ the total training FLOPs of a standard attention baseline trains stably, exhibits a near-zero train-validation gap, and demonstrates competitive behavior on standard benchmarks, albeit with higher bits-per-byte (BPB) at this compute scale. Overall, GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with an explicit locality scale, offering a dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design.

</details>


### [360] [Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02159)
*Ujjwal Patil,Javad Ghofrani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A PRISMA-guided systematic review of 15 empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in DRL, finding that positive transfer hinges on source-target similarity and gating/alignment mechanisms, ensembles show promise but lack breadth, and compute-matched comparisons are rare.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DRL suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer; reusing pretrained knowledge via transfer learning, distillation, ensembles, or federated training is a common response, but the literature is fragmented and comparisons are inconsistent.

Method: PRISMA-guided systematic review: 589 records from IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing → 570 unique records screened → 89 full texts assessed → 15 empirical studies included; qualitative analysis across source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and fairness of from-scratch baselines.

Result: Three patterns: (1) positive results when source-target tasks share structure or include gating/alignment; (2) ensemble and federated aggregation evidence is promising but sparse and narrow; (3) compute-matched comparisons are rare, weakening efficiency claims.

Conclusion: Contributes a narrow, internally consistent review scope, a study-level synthesis, and a provisional independence spectrum as a hypothesis for future benchmarking.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has delivered strong results in domains such as Atari and Go, but it still suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer beyond the training setting. A common response is to reuse information from previously trained models through transfer, distillation, ensemble methods, or federated training instead of learning each target task from random initialization. The literature on these mechanisms is fragmented, and published comparisons are hard to interpret because tasks, baselines, and compute budgets differ.
  This paper presents a PRISMA-guided systematic review of empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in DRL. Starting from 589 records retrieved from IEEE Xplore, the ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing, we screened 570 unique records and assessed 89 full texts. After applying the final eligibility criteria, 15 empirical studies remained in the main synthesis. We analyzed them qualitatively across three factors: source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and the fairness of comparisons against from-scratch baselines.
  Three patterns recur across the surviving corpus. First, positive results are concentrated in settings where source and target tasks share substantial structure or where the method includes an explicit gating or alignment mechanism. Second, evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and mostly limited to narrow settings. Third, compute-matched comparisons are rare, which weakens claims about efficiency gains over stronger single-agent baselines. The paper contributes a narrower and internally consistent review scope, a study-level synthesis of empirical evidence, and a provisional independence spectrum that should be treated as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric.

</details>


### [361] [Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02165)
*Qinwei Huang,Rui Zuo,Simon Khan,Qinru Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EC-HFRL shows that in experience-constrained federated reinforcement learning for UAV teams, increasing learner participation does not improve performance; instead, learning is driven by experience reuse strategy and gradient transition experiences within clusters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the conventional federated learning assumption that greater participation always improves performance, specifically in hazardous UAV environments where experience generation is constrained by safety, energy, and mission duration.

Method: Proposes Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning (EC-HFRL), where clusters act as FL agents and intra-cluster learners share an experience pool, with analysis of minibatch size and reuse strategy.

Result: Increasing participation does not improve learning; performance is tied to experience reuse strategy and gradient transition experiences. Minibatch size determines replay exposure, and higher participation increases reuse level.

Conclusion: Learner participation plays a limited and secondary role in experience-constrained FRL; performance regimes are associated with learning signal structure rather than federated aggregation effects.

Abstract: Conventional federated learning assumes that greater learner participation improves training performance, by leveraging abundant, independently generated local data. However, in federated reinforcement learning (FRL) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) teams in hazardous environments where experience generation is severely constrained by safety considerations, energy limitations, and mission duration, this assumption may break. This work introduces Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning (EC-HFRL), a framework in which clusters act as federated learning agents, while multiple intra-cluster learners represent parallel learning resources that reuse a shared experience pool. We show that increasing participation does not necessarily improve learning performance. Instead, learning performance is strongly associated with experience reuse strategy and the dominance of key analytically identified gradient transition experiences within a cluster. In particular, minibatch size primarily determines effective replay exposure, while higher intra-cluster participation increases reuse level. Empirical results demonstrate that the performance regimes are strongly associated with the structure of the learning signal, rather than federated aggregation effects, clarifying the limited and secondary role of learner participation in experience-constrained FRL.

</details>


### [362] [Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02167)
*Soyeon Kim,Seongwoo Lim,Kyowoon Lee,Jaesik Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MA-GIG improves Integrated Gradients by constructing attribution paths in a VAE's latent space rather than input space, reducing off-manifold noise and producing more faithful explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IG explanations can be unreliable when integration paths pass through noisy gradient regions, and Guided IG still produces intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold.

Method: MA-GIG constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained VAE, decoding intermediate latent states to bias the path toward the learned generative manifold.

Result: MA-GIG reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers in qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

Conclusion: Manifold-aligned path construction produces more faithful feature attributions by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input.

Abstract: Feature attribution is central to diagnosing and trusting deep neural networks, and Integrated Gradients (IG) is widely used due to its axiomatic properties. However, IG can yield unreliable explanations when the integration path between a baseline and the input passes through regions with noisy gradients. While Guided Integrated Gradients reduces this sensitivity by adaptively updating low-gradient-magnitude features, input-space guidance still produces intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold. To address this limitation, we propose \emph{Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients} (MA-GIG), which constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder. By decoding intermediate latent states, MA-GIG biases the path toward the learned generative manifold and reduces exposure to implausible input-space regions. Through qualitative and quantitative evaluations, we demonstrate that MA-GIG produces faithful explanations by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input. Consequently, our method reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers. Our code is available at https://github.com/leekwoon/ma-gig/.

</details>


### [363] [Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02183)
*Guanmeng Xian,Ning Yang,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCAT improves adversarial robustness on long-tailed datasets by constraining adversarial examples to class-conditional manifolds and using ETF-inspired regularization for balanced class separation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial training works well on balanced datasets but fails under long-tailed class distributions, where tail classes suffer high robust error and unstable decision boundaries.

Method: MCAT penalizes deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space and promotes balanced geometric separation via ETF-inspired regularization.

Result: Consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness on standard long-tailed benchmarks.

Conclusion: Manifold-constrained adversarial risk upper-bounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions, and geometric separation is linked to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins.

Abstract: Adversarial training is effective on balanced datasets, but its robustness degrades under longtailed class distributions, where tail classes suffer high robust error and unstable decision boundaries. We propose Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training (MCAT), a unified framework that enforces the semantic validity of adversarial examples by penalizing deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space, while promoting balanced geometric separation across classes via an ETF-inspired regularization. We provide theoretical results that link geometric separation to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins, and show that manifold-constrained adversarial risk upperbounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions. Extensive experiments on standard longtailed benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [364] [KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02190)
*James Bagrow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KANs suffer from high-curvature oscillations in activations, and the authors propose a basis-agnostic curvature penalty that smooths activations without sacrificing accuracy, along with theoretical bounds linking the penalty to full model curvature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KANs produce pathologically high-curvature oscillations in their learnable activations, which undermines interpretability, and standard regularization fails to fix this.

Method: Deriving a basis-agnostic curvature penalty and proving an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the penalty, while accounting for how function composition shapes curvature.

Result: Penalized models maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations.

Conclusion: This interpretability improvement without sacrificing accuracy strengthens KANs as practical tools for prediction and scientific insight.

Abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) offer a potent combination of accuracy and interpretability, thanks to their compositions of learnable univariate activation functions. However, the activations of well-fitting KANs tend to exhibit pathologically high-curvature oscillations, making them difficult to interpret, and standard regularization penalties do not prevent this. Here we derive a basis-agnostic curvature penalty and show that penalized models can maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations. Accounting for how function composition shapes curvature, we prove an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the curvature penalty, and use this to motivate richer forms of penalties. Scientific machine learning is increasingly bottlenecked by the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability. Results such as ours that improve interpretability without sacrificing accuracy will further strengthen KANs as a practical tool for both prediction and insight.

</details>


### [365] [DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02196)
*Abdullah Ahmad Khan,Ferdous Sohel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs deployed at INT4 precision can recover forgotten content from machine unlearning, and the proposed DURABLEUN-SAF method is the only one that achieves robust forgetting across BF16, INT8, and INT4 precisions simultaneously.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine unlearning evaluations ignore that production LLMs are typically deployed at low-bit precision (INT4), where quantization can restore supposedly forgotten data even when models pass bfloat16 compliance audits.

Method: The paper introduces the Quantization Recovery Attack (QRA), systematically evaluates unlearning robustness under NF4+LoRA INT4 quantization across seven methods, and proposes DURABLEUN-SAF—a quantization-aware forgetting objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding.

Result: INT4 quantization induces up to 22x recovery of forgotten content. A trilemma exists (FA-RA-Q-INT4): no method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method achieving a stable (0.047, {BF16, INT8, INT4})-durability certificate.

Conclusion: The paper advocates for adopting Q-INT4 as a standard evaluation metric alongside FA and RA, as current compliance audits at BF16 are insufficient for guaranteeing unlearning robustness under realistic INT4 deployment.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove specified training data to satisfy privacy regulations such as GDPR. However, existing evaluations assume identical precision at unlearning and deployment, overlooking that production LLMs are deployed at low-bit precision. We show that INT4 quantization systematically restores forgotten content even when models pass compliance audits at bfloat16 (BF16), we term this the quantization recovery attack (QRA). We conduct the first systematic study of unlearning robustness under adapter-space INT4 quantization in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluating seven methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. INT8 is benign; INT4 induces recovery of up to 22x, worsening with dataset difficulty. We identify the FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma: no method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness. A dense Pareto sweep reveals a sharp phase transition once robustness is achieved, retaining accuracy collapses regardless of further tuning. To address this, we propose DURABLEUN-SAF (Sharpness-Aware Forgetting), a quantization-aware objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to achieve a stable empirical (0.047, {BF16, INT8, INT4})- durability certificate: Q-INT4= 0.043 +- 0.002, cert rate= 3/3, versus SalUn's cert rate= 1/3 at its own published hyperparameters. We call for Q-INT4 to be adopted as a standard evaluation metric alongside FA and RA.

</details>


### [366] [Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02245)
*S M Asif Hossain,Shruti Kshirsagar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a two-stage training strategy (pretrain on full population, then fine-tune on demographic subgroups) for sleep stage classification, achieving better accuracy than a single generalized model across 35 of 37 configurations tested on the DREAMT dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard automated sleep staging uses a single population-agnostic model, ignoring known demographic variations in sleep architecture across gender, age, and OSA severity, leading to suboptimal performance for diverse clinical populations.

Method: A two-stage strategy: (1) pretrain a convolutional recurrent model on the full population, (2) fine-tune independently for subgroups defined by gender, age, and AHI severity (AASM standard). Evaluated 37 configurations (single-axis and two-way combinations) on the DREAMT dataset (100 subjects, 7 PSG channels).

Result: 35 out of 37 fine-tuned models outperformed the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9% to 12.9%.

Conclusion: Stratified fine-tuning tailored to patient demographics yields more accurate sleep staging than a one-size-fits-all model, providing a clinically grounded paradigm for personalized sleep assessment.

Abstract: Automated sleep stage classification typically employs a single population-agnostic model, disregarding established demographic variations in sleep architecture. Sleep patterns, however, differ substantially across gender, age, and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) severity, indicating that a onesize-fits all approach may be suboptimal for diverse clinical populations. In this paper, we propose a two stage training strategy based on demographic stratification and transfer learning framework. We first pretrains a convolutional recurrent model on the full population and then fine tunes it independently for demographic subgroups defined by gender, age, and Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) severity according to the AASM clinical standard. Using the DREAMT dataset comprising 100 clinical subjects and 7 PSG channels, we evaluate 37 fine-tuned configurations across single-axis and two-way demographic combinations. Results demonstrate that 35 of the 37 fine-tuned models outperform the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9 to 12.9%. These findings indicate that stratified fine tuning tailored to specific patient demographics yields substantially more accurate sleep staging than a single generalized model, offering a practical and clinically grounded paradigm for personalized sleep assessment.

</details>


### [367] [Break the Block: Dynamic-size Reasoning Blocks for Diffusion Large Language Models via Monotonic Entropy Descent with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02263)
*Yan Jiang,Ruihong Qiu,Zi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes b1, a post-training framework for diffusion LLMs that learns dynamic-size reasoning blocks via Monotonic Entropy Descent with reinforcement learning, replacing fixed-size block generation to improve reasoning coherence and effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-size block generation in diffusion LLMs is a bottleneck: (1) different reasoning tasks need different optimal block sizes, making "one-size-fits-all" ineffective; (2) rigid block partitioning breaks logical flow and reduces reasoning coherence even within a single task.

Method: The paper proposes b1, a post-training framework that uses a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective with reinforcement learning to learn dynamic-size reasoning blocks. It is a plug-and-play module compatible with existing dLLM post-training algorithms.

Result: Empirical observations show that incorrect reasoning exhibits fluctuating/unsteady block-wise entropy trends, while correct reasoning follows a consistent descending trend. b1 achieves consistent improvement over fixed-size block baselines across various reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Dynamic-size reasoning blocks learned via monotonic entropy descent outperform fixed-size block approaches in diffusion LLMs, improving reasoning coherence and effectiveness across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Recent diffusion large language models (dLLMs) have demonstrated both effectiveness and efficiency in reasoning via a block-based semi-autoregressive generation paradigm. Despite their progress, the fixed-size block generations remain a critical bottleneck for effective and coherent reasoning. 1. From a global perspective, different reasoning tasks would correspond to different optimal decoding block sizes, which makes a ``one-size-fits-all'' assumption ineffective. 2. Even within a single reasoning task, the rigid block partitioning would break the logical flow and reduce reasoning coherence. Through empirical observations, we reveal that for block-wise entropy, incorrect reasoning exhibits a fluctuating and unsteady trend between blocks, whereas the correctly generated tasks follow a consistent descending trend. Therefore, this paper proposes b1, a novel post-training framework for dLLMs that learns dynamic-size reasoning blocks via a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective with reinforcement learning to enhance reasoning coherence.b1 integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module with existing dLLM's post-training algorithms. Extensive experiments across various reasoning benchmarks showcase b1's consistent improvement over existing fixed-size block baselines. Our code has been released at https://github.com/YanJiangJerry/Block-R1.

</details>


### [368] [HELIX: Hybrid Encoding with Learnable Identity and Cross-dimensional Synthesis for Time Series Imputation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02278)
*Fengming Zhang,Wenjie Du,Huan Zhang,Ke Yu,Shen Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HELIX introduces learnable feature identities for persistent cross-feature representations in time series imputation, achieving SOTA across 16 baselines and 5 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attention-based methods rediscover feature relationships at each layer without persistent anchors, and graph-based methods rely on predefined topology assuming homogeneous spatial relationships, both limiting consistent representation learning.

Method: HELIX assigns each feature a learnable feature identity (persistent embedding) capturing intrinsic semantics throughout the network, learns arbitrary feature dependencies end-to-end from temporal co-variation, and integrates hybrid temporal-feature attention.

Result: Surpasses all 16 baselines on 5 public datasets across 21 experimental settings. Mechanistic analysis shows alignment of learned feature identities with latent physical/semantic structure progressively across layers.

Conclusion: HELIX effectively translates cross-feature structure into imputation accuracy through persistent feature identities, outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: Time series imputation benefits from leveraging cross-feature correlations, yet existing attention-based methods re-discover feature relationships at each layer, lacking persistent anchors to maintain consistent representations. To address this, we propose HELIX, which assigns each feature a learnable feature identity, a persistent embedding that captures intrinsic semantic properties throughout the network. Unlike graph-based methods that rely on predefined topology and assume homogeneous spatial relationships, HELIX learns arbitrary feature dependencies end-to-end from temporal co-variation, naturally handling datasets where features mix spatial locations with semantic variables. Integrated with hybrid temporal-feature attention, HELIX achieves the state-of-the-art performance, surpassing all 16 baselines on 5 public datasets across 21 experimental settings in our evaluation. Furthermore, our mechanistic analysis reveals that HELIX aligns learned feature identities and dependencies with latent physical and semantic structure progressively across layers, demonstrating that it more effectively translates cross-feature structure into imputation accuracy.

</details>


### [369] [Variational Matrix-Learning Fourier Networks for Parametric Multiphysics Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02280)
*Xinyu Li,Jianhua Zhang,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VMLFN is a physics-informed neural network that enables fast parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling for chiplet design by converting PDE solving into a linear matrix problem via variational weak forms, achieving substantial speedup over conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphysics simulation is essential for system-technology co-optimization in chiplet-based design, but repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems are computationally expensive during parametric design exploration.

Method: VMLFN uses a log-space sine neural representation with random spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regulation, and embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions. It converts physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem by reformulating PDEs into variational weak forms and enforcing stationarity conditions, requiring only first-order derivatives. A heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm selects a problem-adaptive maximum frequency.

Result: Validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems across five benchmarks, VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional PINNs and repeated finite-element simulations.

Conclusion: VMLFN provides an efficient and accurate parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling approach for chiplet-based STCO, avoiding high-order automatic differentiation and penalty-coefficient tuning.

Abstract: Multiphysics simulation is critical for system-technology co-optimization (STCO) in chiplet-based design, but repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems are computationally expensive in parametric design exploration. This paper proposes a variational matrix-learning Fourier network (VMLFN) for efficient parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling. VMLFN constructs a log-space sine neural representation with randomly sampled spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regulation, and embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions. With fixed hidden-layer parameters, the output-layer weights are determined by reformulating the governing PDEs into variational weak forms and enforcing the stationarity condition of the resulting energy functional. This converts physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem, requiring only first-order derivatives and avoiding both high-order automatic differentiation and penalty-coefficient tuning. A heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm is further introduced to select a problem-adaptive maximum frequency that covers the dominant spectral range of the target problem. The proposed method is validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems. Results from five benchmark cases demonstrate that VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional physics-informed neural networks and repeated finite-element simulations.

</details>


### [370] [Graph Federated Unlearning for Privacy Preservation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02297)
*Ruotong Ma,Wentao Yu,Qizhou Wang,Jie Yang,Chen Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a machine unlearning approach for graph federated learning that removes user information upon withdrawal while preserving model performance, using orthogonal unlearning updates and virtual clients to maintain graph topology without leaking removed data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GFL methods fail to address privacy leakage when users withdraw from the system; user information can persist and propagate even after deletion, violating GDPR/CCPA rights. Classical machine unlearning degrades performance, and this problem is worsened in GFL due to local message passing and global model collaboration.

Method: Two key adjustments: (1) Ensuring unlearning updates are orthogonal to gradients from learning other data to minimize performance impact; (2) Introducing virtual clients maintained by the central server to preserve graph topology and global embeddings without recovering information of removed entities.

Result: Comprehensive experiments under user-withdrawal scenarios with a novel membership inference framework show the approach is effective and surpasses seven state-of-the-art baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully enables privacy-preserving user withdrawal in GFL while maintaining model performance, addressing a critical gap in decentralized graph learning.

Abstract: Graph federated learning (GFL) facilitates decentralized training on distributed graph data while keeping sensitive user information local, aligning with policies such as GDPR and CCPA that grant users the right to freely join or withdraw from learning systems. However, even decentralized, user information can persist after quitting, potentially propagating to central servers and then redistributing to malicious clients. This privacy leakage during user withdrawal, despite its importance, has received seldom attention in GFL. To fill the gap, we explore the potential of machine unlearning (MU) to thoroughly remove user information. However, classical MU methods are known to degrade overall performance, a problem that is exacerbated in GFL due to local message passing and global model collaboration. To this end, we make two adjustments to mitigate this challenge for GFL. First, we ensure unlearning updates that minimally affect overall performance, steering them in directions orthogonal to the gradients from learning other data. Second, we introduce virtual clients, maintained by the central server, to preserve graph topology and global embeddings without recovering information of removed entities. We conduct comprehensive experiments under a representative user-withdrawal scenario and propose a novel membership inference framework to rigorously evaluate and validate the reliability of our privacy preservation. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, which also surpasses the performance of seven state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [371] [A Meta Reinforcement Learning Approach to Goals-Based Wealth Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02300)
*Sanjiv R. Das,Harshad Khadilkar,Sukrit Mittal,Daniel Ostrov,Deep Srivastav,Hungjen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A meta-reinforcement learning approach (MetaRL) pre-trained on thousands of goals-based wealth management problems can produce near-optimal portfolio and goal-fulfillment strategies in milliseconds, achieving 97.8% of optimal expected utility with strong robustness to market regime changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optimization methods like Dynamic Programming become computationally infeasible for large state spaces, and require separate training for each new investor problem. The authors seek a faster, more scalable solution that generalizes across diverse GBWM problems.

Method: The authors apply zero-shot meta-learning and foundation model pre-training concepts to develop a MetaRL model trained on thousands of GBWM problems. Each problem involves multi-year scenarios with optimal portfolio choices and goal-fulfillment decisions to maximize expected investor utility.

Result: MetaRL delivers near-optimal strategies in hundredths of a second, achieving on average 97.8% of optimal expected utility. Results are robust to capital market regime changes even when trained on a single regime. The approach scales to larger state spaces where DP becomes infeasible.

Conclusion: MetaRL is an effective, fast, and scalable alternative to Dynamic Programming for goals-based wealth management, offering near-optimal solutions with strong generalization and robustness.

Abstract: Applying concepts related to zero-shot meta-learning and pre-training of foundation models, we develop a meta reinforcement learning approach (denoted MetaRL) that is pre-trained on thousands of goals-based wealth management (GBWM) problems. Each GBWM problem involves a multiple year scenario over which the investor looks to optimally choose an investment portfolio each year and choose to fulfill all, some, or none of the different financial goals that arise each year. These choices seek to maximize the expected total investor utility obtained from the fulfilled financial goals. By eliminating separate training and optimization for each new investor problem, the MetaRL model in inference mode produces near-optimal dynamic investment portfolio and goal-fulfilling strategies for a new GBWM problem within a few hundredths of a second. This delivers expected utilities that are, on average, 97.8% of the optimal expected utilities (determined via Dynamic Programming). These results are remarkably robust to capital market regime changes, even when training uses only one capital market regime. Further, the MetaRL approach can enable solving problems with larger state spaces where Dynamic Programming becomes computationally infeasible.

</details>


### [372] [Differentiable Kernel Ridge Regression for Deep Learning Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02313)
*Jean-Marc Mercier,Gabriele Santin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Sparse Kernels (SKs), a differentiable, localized variant of kernel ridge regression that can be integrated as modular layers into deep learning pipelines (PyTorch), enabling training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel methods remain underexplored at scale compared to deep neural networks; the authors aim to revisit kernel methods as drop-in components for standard deep learning pipelines rather than treating them as a separate paradigm.

Method: The authors propose Sparse Kernels (SKs) — a differentiable, localized, lazy variant of KRR that defers training to inference time and solves small local systems. SKs are integrated as modular PyTorch layers with three learnable parameter sets: feature representations, target values, and evaluation points.

Result: SK-based modules match the performance of trained neural readouts with less training in some settings, and augment existing models to improve performance in others, tested across CNNs, vision transformers, and reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: Kernel methods, when made scalable and differentiable, can be readily integrated with deep learning rather than treated as a separate paradigm.

Abstract: Deep neural networks dominate modern machine learning, while alternative function approximators remain comparatively underexplored at scale. In this work, we revisit kernel methods as drop-in components for standard deep learning pipelines. We introduce \emph{Sparse Kernels} (SKs), a differentiable, localized, and lazy variant of kernel ridge regression (KRR) that defers training to inference time and reduces to the solution of small local systems. We integrate SKs into PyTorch as modular layers that preserve end-to-end trainability, and we show that they expose three distinct sets of parameters -- feature representations, target values, and evaluation points -- each of which can be fixed or learned. This decomposition broadens the design space available to practitioners, enabling, in particular, training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural models. Across convolutional networks, vision transformers, and reinforcement learning, SK-based modules serve two complementary roles: in some settings, they match the performance of trained neural readouts with substantially less training; in others, they augment existing models and improve their performance when used as additional components. Our results suggest that kernel methods, once made scalable and differentiable, can be readily integrated with deep learning rather than treated as a separate paradigm.

</details>


### [373] [When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02323)
*Niklas Houba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Under additive superposition (where multiple components contribute to every observation), attention-based models suffer from "slot collapse" where multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while ignoring weaker ones. The authors identify that attention is memoryless with respect to explained evidence, and propose "evidence depletion" - combining multiplicative depletion with attention bias - which consistently prevents collapse across synthetic benchmarks, audio mixtures (FUSS), and gravitational-wave source inference for the LISA mission.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the structural failure mode of "slot collapse" in compositional inference under additive superposition, where attention-based models fail to enforce non-redundant allocation of latent components because attention lacks memory of what has already been explained.

Method: The authors introduce residual evidence modeling via "evidence depletion" - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias that adds residual state to sequential attention, allowing slots to track what has already been explained.

Result: Evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude across synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), and enables multi-source posterior estimation for gravitational-wave source inference on the LISA mission where standard attention fails completely under identical architectures, data, and losses.

Conclusion: Under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is the operative ingredient for preventing slot collapse and enabling compositional inference; parallel attention, sequential processing alone, and loss-based regularization are insufficient without explicit evidence depletion.

Abstract: Compositional inference - the decomposition of observations into an unknown number of latent components - is central to perception and scientific data analysis. Attention-based models perform well when components are approximately separable, as in object-centric vision. Under additive superposition, however - where multiple components contribute to every observation - we identify a structural failure mode we term slot collapse: multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while weaker ones remain unrepresented. We trace this to a general limitation: attention is memoryless with respect to explained evidence. All slots repeatedly operate on the same input without accounting for what has already been explained, so gradients are dominated by the strongest component, inducing shared fixed points across slots. As a result, attention fails to enforce non-redundant allocation under additive superposition. We address this by introducing residual evidence modeling, instantiated via evidence depletion - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias. Controlled ablations show that parallel attention, sequential processing alone, and loss-based regularization fail to resolve collapse; evidence depletion, which adds residual state to sequential attention, consistently succeeds. Across synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude, generalizing beyond synthetic settings. On gravitational-wave source inference for the ESA/NASA LISA mission, under identical architectures, data, and losses, standard attention fails while evidence depletion prevents collapse and enables multi-source posterior estimation. These results show that under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is the operative ingredient for preventing collapse and enabling compositional inference.

</details>


### [374] [A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02350)
*Tim Sinen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on the hypercube under uniform distribution with coordinate flips, showing L^1 polynomial regression achieves ~Õ(n^{O(log(1/ε)/σ)}) complexity and proving a nearly matching SQ lower bound of n^{Ω(log(1+σ/ε²)/σ)}.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the complexity of learning halfspaces under smoothed distributions on the discrete hypercube, complementing recent continuous (Gaussian) results by [DK26].

Method: The paper uses L^1 polynomial regression for the upper bound and Statistical Query (SQ) lower bound techniques to prove near-optimal hardness.

Result: Upper bound: ~Õ(n^{O(log(1/ε)/σ)}) via L^1 regression; Lower bound: n^{Ω(log(1+σ/ε²)/σ)} in SQ model, showing near-matching complexity.

Conclusion: Smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on the hypercube has complexity that scales polynomially in n with exponent depending on σ and ε, analogous to the continuous Gaussian case.

Abstract: We study the complexity of smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on $\{\pm 1\}^n$ under the uniform distribution in the model of \citet{KM25} where each input coordinate is independently flipped with probability $σ\in (0, {1}/{2})$. We show that $L^1$ polynomial regression achieves complexity $\tilde{O}(n^{O(\log(1/\varepsilon)/σ)})$, and prove a nearly matching Statistical Query complexity lower bound of $n^{Ω(\log(1+σ/\varepsilon ^2)/σ)}$. This complements the recent work of \citet{DK26}, which established analogous bounds in the continuous setting under Gaussian marginals.

</details>


### [375] [ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02356)
*Xianli Zhu,Jia Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZNO is a causal neural operator with stable low-rank rational filters in the z-plane, designed for discrete-time system identification. It excels on stable rational systems with near-unit-circle poles and long memory, achieving competitive results on public benchmarks but not outperforming specialized methods universally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing operator learning methods are mainly tailored for continuous-time problems, while many system-identification problems are intrinsically discrete-time. The authors aim to address this gap by designing a neural operator directly in the z-domain with explicit stability constraints.

Method: ZNO combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization via unit-disk constraint, causal recurrence, and an optional short FIR branch into a single z-domain rational recurrent layer. Poles are parameterized directly in the z-plane, making stability readable and learnable.

Result: ZNO achieves the lowest mean error on controlled discrete tasks with lightly damped near-unit-circle poles and across a five-bin memory difficulty sweep (10–200 steps). On five public nonlinear benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving lowest error on benchmarks matching stable rational dynamics.

Conclusion: ZNO is a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but is not a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

Abstract: We introduce the Z-Domain Neural Operator (ZNO), a causal neural operator whose layers are stable low-rank multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rational filters parameterized directly in the $z$-plane. ZNO addresses a limitation of existing operator learning methods, many of which are primarily tailored for continuous-time problems, while a large class of system-identification problems is intrinsically discrete-time. The $z$-domain form expresses stability as a unit-disk pole constraint and makes learned discrete-time poles directly readable. The model combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization, causal recurrence, and an optional short finite impulse response (FIR) branch in a single $z$-domain rational recurrent layer. Across controlled discrete system-identification experiments, ZNO's advantage is most evident when the target dynamics are stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle. Under matched parameter budgets, ZNO is not uniformly dominant; however, with validation-selected configurations, the same architecture can achieve the lowest mean error across the controlled tasks. A five-bin difficulty sweep over near-unit-circle / long-memory dynamics shows that ZNO has the lowest mean error across memory regimes, from short (approximately 10 steps) to long (approximately 100-200 steps). On five public nonlinear system-identification benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving the lowest mean error on benchmarks whose dynamics align with stable rational discrete-time filters, while classical or state-space baselines remain preferable on some systems. These results position ZNO as a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not as a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

</details>


### [376] [Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02358)
*Sarvagya Somvanshi,Mohan Xu,Rakhi Chadalavada,Nathan Canera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes ViralityNET, an architecture that predicts Reddit post virality by combining internal platform features (title, body, metadata, subreddit embeddings) with exogenous temporal signals from Wikipedia pageview spikes, achieving improved AUC-PR and AUC-ROC scores over text-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current models for predicting social media virality rely heavily on static textual and structural features, effectively ignoring the highly dynamic nature of trend signals. The authors study whether real-world attention signals can improve prediction beyond what post text alone reveals.

Method: ViralityNET combines four post-level streams (title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, learned subreddit embeddings) with a cross-attention block that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix encoding the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms from the preceding seven days. Virality is framed as binary classification using the 90th percentile per-subreddit engagement with a minimum absolute score threshold.

Result: Incorporating external attention signals yields consistent gains, outperforming text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieving an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836.

Conclusion: Incorporating external attention signals yields measurable improvements over text-only baselines, highlighting the importance of real-world dynamics in shaping online virality.

Abstract: Current models for predicting social media virality rely heavily on static textual and structural features, effectively ignoring the highly dynamic nature of trend signals. We study whether real-world attention signals can improve the prediction of social-media virality beyond what post text alone reveals. We introduce \model{}, an architecture that predicts Reddit post virality by fusing internal platform representations with exogenous temporal signals derived from Wikipedia pageview spikes. We frame virality as a binary classification task that accounts for differences in subreddit scale, labeling posts as viral if they exceed the 90th percentile of per-subreddit engagement and a minimum absolute score threshold.
  We introduce ViralityNET combines four post-level streams: title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, and learned subreddit embeddings with a cross-attention block that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix encoding the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms from the preceding seven days. Empirical results suggest that incorporating external attention signals yields consistent gains, outperforming text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieving an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836. Overall, we provide evidence that incorporating external attention signals yields measurable improvements over text-only baselines, highlighting the importance of real-world dynamics in shaping online virality.

</details>


### [377] [Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02375)
*Marc Dymetman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR training causes diversity collapse in language models because binary rewards and KL-regularization push toward concentrated distributions over few valid outputs under model misspecification, rather than toward the ideal filtered distribution covering all valid outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain theoretically why reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) degrades multi-sample coverage (diversity collapse) despite improving single-sample accuracy, and to provide a mathematical account of this previously observed but poorly understood phenomenon.

Method: The authors provide a theoretical analysis using binary reward structure, policy gradient degeneracy, KL-control limit analysis (as β→0), forward KL asymmetry, explicit formulas linking hyperparameter β to target validity rate μ, and a toy autoregressive experiment illustrating collapse under model misspecification.

Result: Binary rewards create infinite optimal distributions; KL-control selects the filtered model p* in forward KL but this cannot be a direct optimization target; under misspecification, decreasing β drives concentration toward fewer valid outputs rather than the filtered model; alternative divergences targeting p* directly avoid this failure mode.

Conclusion: Diversity collapse in RLVR is structurally inevitable under standard KL-regularized binary reward optimization when the model is misspecified, and solutions require changing the divergence objective to directly target the filtered distribution's support rather than concentrating on high-validity outputs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for improving reasoning in language models, yet models trained with RLVR often suffer from diversity collapse: while single-sample accuracy improves, multi-sample coverage degrades, sometimes falling below the base model. We provide a structural account of this phenomenon grounded in the properties of binary rewards. Binary rewards create a fundamental degeneracy for policy gradient methods: the set of distributions maximizing expected reward is infinite, with no distinguished element. KL-control resolves this degeneracy by selecting, in the limit $β\to 0$, the filtered model $p_*:=a(\cdot\mid\mathcal{Y}_1)$ -- the base model conditioned on validity -- which is the unique fully valid distribution closest to the base model in KL divergence. This selection operates through a nontrivial asymmetry: the tilted distribution $p_{[β]}\propto a(y)\,e^{v(y)/β}$ converges to $p_*$ in forward KL as $β\to 0$, yet $p_*$ cannot serve as a direct optimization target because $\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p_*)$ is infinite for any full-support policy $q$. We develop explicit formulas relating the hyperparameter $β$ to the more interpretable target validity rate $μ$. Under model misspecification -- the typical practical regime -- the pressure to decrease $β$ drives the optimizer toward highly concentrated distributions over a small number of valid outputs, collapsing toward ever fewer as $β$ decreases, rather than toward the filtered model. We illustrate this mechanism on a toy autoregressive experiment and discuss how alternative divergences that target $p_*$ directly -- as pursued empirically by \citet{kruszewski_whatever_2026} -- avoid this failure mode by rewarding coverage of $p_*$'s support rather than concentration on high-validity outputs.

</details>


### [378] [Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02404)
*Michael Helcig,Eldar Kurtic,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces statistically-lossless quantization for LLMs, proposing three levels of losslessness (task-lossless, distribution-lossless, and a theoretical variance law), and achieves high compression rates with significant inference speedups via SLQ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quantization methods involve trade-offs: lossy methods (GPTQ, AWQ) sacrifice fidelity, while lossless methods do not accelerate inference. The paper aims to find a middle ground by exploring statistically-lossless compression that preserves fidelity while also enabling speedups.

Method: The paper defines three notions of losslessness: (1) task-lossless (preserving benchmark accuracy), (2) distribution-lossless (next-token distribution indistinguishable from original, measured by Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR)), and (3) a gamma-squared variance law proving that symmetric quantization inflates noise variance, making asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity. The proposed method, SLQ, is a layer-wise non-uniform quantizer with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search.

Result: SLQ achieves task-lossless compression below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits), distribution-lossless compression at 5-6 bits per parameter on average, and inference speedups of 1.7-3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels.

Conclusion: Statistically-lossless quantization is achievable with appropriate asymmetric techniques, bridging the gap between lossy efficiency and lossless fidelity for LLM deployment.

Abstract: Model quantization has become essential for efficient large language model deployment, yet existing approaches involve clear trade-offs: methods such as GPTQ and AWQ achieve practical compression but are lossy, while lossless techniques preserve fidelity but typically do not accelerate inference. This paper explores the middle ground of statistically-lossless compression through three complementary notions of losslessness for quantized LLMs. First, task-lossless compression preserves zero-shot benchmark accuracy within natural sampling variance and remains achievable at aggressive bitwidths. Second, we formalize the stricter notion of distribution-lossless compression, requiring the quantized model's next-token distribution to be practically indistinguishable from the original, and propose the Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR), the maximum token-agreement probability under optimal coupling, as a directly interpretable fidelity metric (for example, EAR >= 0.99 indicates 99% agreement). Third, we prove a gamma-squared variance law showing that symmetric quantization inflates noise variance by gamma squared relative to asymmetric quantization, making asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity but not for task-level preservation. Using SLQ, a layer-wise non-uniform method with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search, we achieve task-lossless compression at well below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits depending on the model), distribution-lossless compression at 5 to 6 bits per parameter on average, and inference speedups of 1.7 to 3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/SLQ.

</details>


### [379] [Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02405)
*Sofianos Panagiotis Fotias,Vassilis Gaganis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops deep reinforcement learning controllers for CO2 storage that use only real-world deployable well data (not simulator states), achieving near-perfect performance compared to privileged-state policies, and introduces a budget-aware latent model retuning method that outperforms direct model-free retuning under abnormal reservoir conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Closed-loop management of geological CO2 storage needs adaptive control policies that handle uncertain reservoir behavior using only realistically available observations during operation.

Method: Formulates CO2 injection and brine-production as a partially observable sequential decision problem. Compares privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies. Evaluates a latent model-based adaptation pipeline that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under abnormal conditions (injector failure, leakage, connectivity changes).

Result: History-conditioned policies recover nearly all privileged-state performance using only deployable well-level information. Latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under abnormal operating cases with the same simulator budget.

Conclusion: The framework offers a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control.

Abstract: Closed-loop management of geological CO2 storage requires control policies that adapt to uncertain reservoir behavior while relying on observations that are realistically available during operation. This work formulates CO2 injection and brine-production control as a partially observable sequential decision problem and studies deployable deep reinforcement-learning controllers trained with high-fidelity reservoir simulation. We first compare privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies in order to quantify the value of temporal well-response information and training-time privileged simulator states. We then evaluate a latent model-based adaptation pipeline that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under known injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics and reward shift, and compartmentalized reservoir connectivity. The results show that history-conditioned policies recover nearly all of the privileged-state performance while using only deployable well-level information, and that latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same scenario-specific real-simulator budget in the abnormal operating cases. The proposed framework therefore provides a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control.

</details>


### [380] [Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02409)
*Sofianos Panagiotis Fotias,Vassilis Gaganis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) for permutation-invariant Bayesian Optimization, applied to well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) projects, with evaluations on synthetic benchmarks and a real CCS case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Gaussian Process kernels are inefficient for inputs with permutation symmetries (e.g., unordered sets of wells under group control), limiting Bayesian Optimization's effectiveness in CCS well placement problems.

Method: Proposes GP-Perm, a novel GP kernel that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets via a stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, combinable with standard kernels. Also develops DKL-DS, a Deep Kernel Learning baseline using Deep Sets architecture for permutation-invariant embeddings.

Result: Evaluated across 8 use cases (7 synthetic benchmarks + 1 realistic CCS case study using the Johansen formation), demonstrating effectiveness of the proposed permutation-invariant approach.

Conclusion: The proposed GP-Perm kernel and DKL-DS model effectively handle permutation symmetries in Bayesian Optimization for CCS well placement, outperforming standard GP kernels that cannot exploit such invariances.

Abstract: Bayesian Optimization is an iterative method, tailored to optimizing expensive black box objective functions. Surrogate models like Gaussian Processes, which are the gold standard in Bayesian Optimization, can be inefficient for inputs with permutation symmetries, as the most common kernels employed are better suited for vector inputs rather than unordered sets of items. Motivated by this issue, we turn to permutation invariant Bayesian Optimization for well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage projects. The high fidelity black box simulator is instructed to operate wells under group control, giving rise to permutation symmetries within injector and producer groups that cannot be exploited with standard GP kernels. In this work, our main contribution is a novel Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets through a stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, and can be combined with standard kernels for additional vector-valued inputs. As a learned invariant baseline, we also consider a Deep Kernel Learning model (DKL-DS) using the Deep Sets architecture to learn a permutation-invariant embedding. We evaluate the proposed methodology across 8 use cases, comprising seven synthetic benchmarks and one realistic CCS case study (Johansen formation)

</details>


### [381] [Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02435)
*Mehryar Mohri,Jon Schneider,Yutao Zhong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper resolves structural estimation bias in Distributional Alignment Games by (1) generalizing alignment games to Bregman divergences with unbiased polynomial estimators via U-statistics, (2) deriving a minimax optimal polynomial estimator for KL divergence achieving Θ(1/K²) error, and (3) proposing a Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator that reduces variance and accelerates convergence with zero online overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT) algorithms suffer from systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality when estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, destabilizing training.

Method: The authors (1) generalize alignment games to Bregman divergences with unbiased polynomial estimators using U-statistics, (2) derive a minimax optimal polynomial estimator for KL divergence via the Ditzian-Totik theorem, and (3) propose a Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator.

Result: Achieves the fundamental statistical error limit of Θ(1/K²) for KL divergence estimation, and provably accelerates game convergence with zero online computational overhead.

Conclusion: The proposed AQP Estimator offers optimal bias and reduced variance, leading to more efficient and stable training in distributional alignment games.

Abstract: The Distributional Alignment Game framework provides a powerful variational perspective on Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT). However, standard algorithms for these games rely on estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, introducing a systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality that can destabilize training. In this paper, we systematically resolve this structural estimation bias. First, we generalize the alignment game to arbitrary Bregman divergences, showing that for a family of geometries inducing polynomial rewards, we can construct provably exact and unbiased estimators using U-statistics. Second, for the canonical KL divergence game where an exact solution is impossible, we derive a globally robust minimax polynomial estimator that is provably optimal, achieving the fundamental statistical error limit of $Θ(1/K^2)$, which we establish via the Ditzian-Totik theorem. Finally, we synthesize these two approaches to propose a novel Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator, proving that by systematically reducing variance, our method achieves not only optimal bias but also provably accelerated game convergence, leading to more efficient and stable training with zero online computational overhead.

</details>


### [382] [Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02469)
*Yao Shu,Chenxing Wei,Hongbin Lin,Shuang Qiu,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG
