The proposed framework is fully reproducible, scalable, and compatible with existing validation workflows, offering a flexible tool for early detection of unusual regional configurations within the European Statistical System.

</details>


### [408] [SpecKV: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Compression-Aware Gamma Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02888)
*Shikhar Shukla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SpecKV is a lightweight adaptive controller that dynamically selects the speculation length γ per step in speculative decoding using signals (entropy, confidence) from the draft model, achieving 56% improvement over fixed-γ=4 baselines with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding uses a fixed speculation length γ (typically 4), but optimal γ varies across task types and compression levels (FP16, INT8, NF4). Existing systems ignore this variability, leading to suboptimal efficiency.

Method: The authors profile speculative decoding across 4 task categories, 4 γ values, and 3 compression levels, collecting 5,112 step-level records. They train a small MLP on draft model confidence and entropy signals to predict acceptance rates and select the γ that maximizes expected tokens per speculation step.

Result: SpecKV achieves a 56.0% improvement over the fixed-γ=4 baseline with only 0.34 ms overhead per decision (<0.5% of step time). The improvement is statistically significant (p < 0.001, paired bootstrap test).

Conclusion: Adaptive speculation length selection using lightweight signals from the draft model significantly improves speculative decoding efficiency across different compression regimes and task types, with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a small draft model to propose candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies. A critical hyperparameter in this process is the speculation length~$γ$, which determines how many tokens the draft model proposes per step. Nearly all existing systems use a fixed~$γ$ (typically~4), yet empirical evidence suggests that the optimal value varies across task types and, crucially, depends on the compression level applied to the target model. In this paper, we present \textbf{SpecKV}, a lightweight adaptive controller that selects~$γ$ per speculation step using signals extracted from the draft model itself. We profile speculative decoding across 4~task categories, 4~speculation lengths, and 3~compression levels (FP16, INT8, NF4), collecting 5,112 step-level records with per-step acceptance rates, draft entropy, and draft confidence. We demonstrate that the optimal~$γ$ shifts across compression regimes and that draft model confidence and entropy are strong predictors of acceptance rate (correlation~$\approx 0.56$). SpecKV uses a small MLP trained on these signals to maximize expected tokens per speculation step, achieving a 56.0\% improvement over the fixed-$γ$=4 baseline with only 0.34\,ms overhead per decision ($<$0.5\% of step time). The improvement is statistically significant ($p < 0.001$, paired bootstrap test). We release all profiling data, trained models, and notebooks as open-source artifacts.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [409] [LiDAR for Rehabilitation: A Comprehensive Survey of Applications, AI Techniques, and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00879)
*Soumia Siyoucef,Najmeddine Dhieb,Hakim Ghazzai,Eleonora Guanziroli,Franco Molteni,Gianluca Setti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper surveys LiDAR-based rehabilitation technologies (2019-2025), covering 3D body scanning, gait analysis, robotic rehabilitation systems, real-time monitoring, and activity recognition. It is the first dedicated comprehensive review on LiDAR for rehabilitation, highlighting AI processing techniques and open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rehabilitation requires accurate monitoring of patient movements. Traditional sensor systems (camera-based) raise privacy concerns, and wearable sensors are uncomfortable and error-prone. LiDAR offers a promising alternative with advantages in privacy and comfort.

Method: The authors conducted a systematic review of studies from 2019-2025 across multiple areas: 3D body scanning & gait analysis with standalone LiDAR, LiDAR on robotic systems, real-time monitoring & environment scanning for safe navigation, and activity/position recognition. They also analyzed learning-based processing techniques with statistical analysis.

Result: LiDAR demonstrates strong potential in rehabilitation applications, outperforming camera and wearable systems in privacy and comfort. The review identified trends, gaps, and future research opportunities in this domain.

Conclusion: This is the first comprehensive survey on LiDAR for rehabilitation, providing an overview of current methods, AI-based processing techniques, and open challenges for future work.

Abstract: Rehabilitation aims to help patients with limited mobility regain their physical abilities through targeted movements, exercises, stimulation, and other therapeutic methods. Recent advances in technology have introduced sensor-based systems into rehabilitation and clinical practices, enabling real-time monitoring and providing accurate feedback on movement accuracy. Among these sensors, LiDAR has demonstrated strong potential, offering key advantages over conventional techniques such as camera-based systems, which raise privacy concerns, and wearable sensors, which can be uncomfortable and prone to errors. In this work, we review the applications of LiDAR in rehabilitation, post-injury care, and hospital environments, focusing on studies published between 2019 and 2025. Studies across several areas have been explored: 3D body scanning and gait analysis with standalone LiDAR, LiDAR mounted on robotic systems for rehabilitation, real-time monitoring and environment scanning for safe navigation, and activity and position recognition. We also analyze processing techniques, particularly learning-based approaches, and support the discussion with statistical analysis, highlighting trends, gaps, and future research opportunities. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive survey dedicated to LiDAR for rehabilitation applications, providing an overview of current methods, AI-based processing techniques, and open challenges.

</details>


### [410] [ARIS: Agentic and Relationship Intelligence System for Social Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00943)
*Stavya Datta,Fucai Ke,Leimin Tian,Hamid Rezatofighi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ARIS is an agentic AI framework for social robots that combines multimodal reasoning, a graph-based Social World Model, and RAG to improve multi-turn engagement and social-relationship reasoning. Evaluated with Pepper robot (N=23), it significantly outperforms LLM baselines in perceived intelligence, animacy, anthropomorphism, and likeability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current social robotics systems fail at multi-turn engagement, social-relationship reasoning, and contextually grounded dialogue at scale, limiting their effectiveness in real-world interactions.

Method: ARIS integrates three key components: (1) a Social World Model using knowledge graphs to map and update social relationships, (2) a RAG-based conversational pipeline for bounded latency with long dialogue histories, and (3) a modular agentic architecture coordinating speech, vision, and physical actions via structured APIs.

Result: In a user study (N=23) with Pepper robot in dyadic conversation, ARIS achieved significantly higher scores than LLM baseline across perceived intelligence, animacy, anthropomorphism, and likeability.

Conclusion: The ARIS framework effectively addresses key limitations in social robotics through structured social reasoning and scalable dialogue, with open-source release planned to support further research.

Abstract: Foundational models have advanced social robotics, enabling richer perception and communicative interaction with users. However, current systems still struggle with multi-turn engagement, social-relationship reasoning, and contextually grounded dialogue at scale. We present ARIS (Agentic and Relationship Intelligence System), an agentic AI framework that unifies multimodal reasoning, a graph-based Social World Model, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) within a single modular architecture for social robots. We evaluate ARIS with the Pepper robot in a robot-mediated dyadic conversational setting, comparing it against a large language model baseline. A user study (N=23) shows that ARIS yields significantly higher perceived intelligence, animacy, anthropomorphism, and likeability. Our contributions are threefold: (1)~a Social World Model that explicitly maps and updates social relationships between users through a knowledge graph, enabling social reasoning and re-identification across encounters; (2)~an efficient RAG-based conversational pipeline that maintains bounded latency as dialogue histories grow to thousands of exchanges while preserving response relevance; and (3)~system integration and empirical validation of these components within a modular agentic architecture that coordinates speech, vision, and physical action through structured APIs. The implementation of ARIS will be released as open source upon publication.

</details>


### [411] [Ablation Study of Multimodal Perception, Language Grounding, and Control for Human-Robot Interaction in an Object Detection and Grasping Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00963)
*Zi Tian,Guanting Shen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper conducts a controlled ablation study on three key modules (LLM for action extraction, perception for visual grounding, controller for motion execution) in a multimodal human-robot interaction system to isolate their contributions to execution time and success rate, and identify where future engineering gains are most promising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify which components (language model, perception, controller) most strongly influence end-to-end performance, and where the largest engineering improvements can be made in future system revisions.

Method: A two-stage ablation study: first, compare three language models, five perception configurations, and three controllers independently under a common protocol; second, conduct a factorial study over the best candidates to evaluate combinations end-to-end.

Result: The analysis identifies which module choices primarily affect execution time and which primarily affect success rate, revealing the most impactful areas for future engineering gains.

Conclusion: Isolating module contributions through systematic ablation provides actionable insights for prioritizing future improvements in multimodal human-robot interaction pipelines.

Abstract: This manuscript extends our previous multimodal human-robot interaction system by introducing a controlled ablation study of the three modules that most strongly influence end-to-end performance: the large language model used for action extraction, the perception system used for visual grounding, and the controller used for motion execution. The goal is not to redesign the full pipeline, but to isolate the contribution of each component under a common experimental protocol and then evaluate the best combinations end-to-end. We therefore compare three language models, five perception configurations, and three controllers, followed by a second-stage factorial study over the best candidates. The resulting analysis is intended to clarify which choices primarily affect execution time, which primarily affect success rate, and where the largest engineering gains are likely to come from in future revisions of the system.

</details>


### [412] [Value Functions for Temporal Logic: Optimal Policies and Safety Filters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01051)
*Oswin So,William Sharpless,Sylvia Herbert,Chuchu Fan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper addresses optimal policy issues in undiscounted infinite-horizon RL for temporal logic tasks, showing greedy value maximization fails for reach-avoid/Until specifications. They propose non-Markovian history-based policies that achieve optimality for nested TL specifications, and demonstrate Q-function as a safety filter beyond simple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bellman equations for reach/avoid problems are well-studied, but the gap between value optimality and policy optimality in undiscounted infinite-horizon settings creates pathologies where greedy Q-maximization indefinitely defers task completion for reach-avoid/Until specifications.

Method: Building on value function decomposition for temporal logic into graph of constituent value functions; constructing non-Markovian policies based on state history; using Q-function as a safety filter for complex TL specifications.

Result: Non-Markovian policies avoid the pathology of indefinite task deferral; proven optimality for nested Until, Globally, and Globally-Until specifications; Q-function safety filtering extended beyond simple avoid/reach-avoid.

Conclusion: Value optimality does not guarantee policy optimality in undiscounted infinite-horizon TL tasks; history-dependent policies are necessary, and Q-functions can serve as safety filters for complex specifications.

Abstract: While Bellman equations for basic reach, avoid, and reach-avoid problems are well studied, the relationship between value optimality and policy optimality becomes subtle in the undiscounted infinite-horizon setting, particularly for more complicated tasks. Greedily maximizing the Q-function can produce policies that indefinitely defer task completion for reach-avoid problems, or equivalently, Until specifications, even when the value function is optimal. Building upon recent results decomposing the value function for temporal logic (TL) into a graph of constituent value functions, we construct non-Markovian policies based on state history that avoid this pathology and prove their optimality with respect to the quantitative robustness score for nested Until, Globally, and Globally-Until specifications. We further show how the Q function can serve as a safety filter for complex TL specifications, extending prior results beyond simple avoid or reach-avoid tasks.

</details>


### [413] [Online Safety Filter for Deformable Object Manipulation with Horizon Agnostic Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01069)
*Jiaxing Li,Hanjiang Hu,Zhuoyuan Wang,Yorie Nakahira,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A constraint-driven online safety filter for deformable object manipulation that uses a neural operator and boundary control barrier function to enforce task-level safety in real time, improving safe trajectory rates by up to 22% over reward shaping methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based approaches for robotic manipulation of deformable media (fluids, cloth, soft objects) encode safety only indirectly through reward shaping, which offers no guarantee of constraint satisfaction at deployment time.

Method: Combines a horizon-agnostic neural operator (learning boundary I/O mapping of PDE dynamics) with a boundary control barrier function (certifying safety via lightweight quadratic program) to create a real-time online safety filter that minimally modifies any nominal control policy.

Result: On FluidLab fluid manipulation tasks, the filter improves safe trajectory rates by up to 22% over unfiltered base policies and reduces the number of steps needed to reach the safe set.

Conclusion: Constraint-driven safety enforcement is both more reliable and more efficient than reward shaping approaches for deformable object manipulation.

Abstract: Safety critical control of robotic manipulation tasks involving deformable media such as fluids, cloth, and soft objects remains challenging because existing learning based approaches encode safety indirectly through reward shaping, which provides no guarantee of constraint satisfaction at deployment. We present a constraint driven online safety filter for deformable object manipulation that enforces explicit task level safety constraints in real time by minimally modifying any nominal control policy. Our approach combines two key components: a horizon agnostic neural operator that learns the boundary input output mapping of the underlying PDE dynamics and generalizes across variable rollout lengths without retraining, and a boundary control barrier function that certifies safety at the task relevant output level via a lightweight quadratic program. The resulting safety constraint is affine in the boundary input rate, enabling real time online filtering. We evaluate the proposed method on fluid manipulation tasks in FluidLab, where the filter improves safe trajectory rates by up to 22% over unfiltered base policies while also reducing the number of steps required to reach the safe set, demonstrating that constraint driven safety enforcement is both more reliable and more efficient than reward shaping approaches.

</details>


### [414] [Sentinel-VLA: A Metacognitive VLA Model with Active Status Monitoring for Dynamic Reasoning and Error Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01191)
*Wenhao Li,Xiu Su,Yichao Cao,Hongyan Xu,Xiaobo Xia,Shan You,Yi Chen,Chang Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Sentinel-VLA introduces a metacognitive VLA model with an active sentinel module for on-demand reasoning and self-correction, achieving over 30% higher task success than SOTA PI0 using 2.6M auto-generated transitions and a Self-Evolving Continual Learning algorithm with Orthogonal Continual Adapter to prevent catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models suffer from limited reasoning capability, lack of status monitoring, and difficulty in self-correction, motivating a metacognitive approach with active monitoring and on-demand reasoning.

Method: Sentinel-VLA uses a sentinel module to monitor real-time execution status and triggers dynamic reasoning or error recovery only when needed (on-demand). Training data (44 tasks, 2.6M transitions) is auto-generated. SECL algorithm enables self-identifying capability boundaries and automatic data collection, while OC-Adapter constrains parameter updates to orthogonal space to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Real-world experiments show Sentinel-VLA boosts task success rate by over 30% compared to the state-of-the-art model PI0.

Conclusion: The metacognitive, on-demand reasoning mechanism combined with continual learning enables robust, computationally efficient embodied manipulation with strong generalization.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have advanced the field of embodied manipulation by harnessing broad world knowledge and strong generalization. However, current VLA models still face several key challenges, including limited reasoning capability, lack of status monitoring, and difficulty in self-correction. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{Sentinel-VLA}, a metacognitive VLA model equipped with an active ``sentinel'' module to monitor real-time execution status. Only when necessary, such as during initial planning or upon detecting an error, the model triggers a dynamic reasoning or formulate error recovery solutions. This on-demand reasoning mechanism ensures robust decision-making while minimizing computational overhead. Notably, all training data (spanning 44 tasks and over 2.6 million transitions) is automatically generated and annotated through our designed pipeline. We also propose the Self-Evolving Continual Learning (SECL) algorithm, which allows Sentinel-VLA to identify its capability boundaries and automatically collect data for expansion, paired with Orthogonal Continual Adapter (OC-Adapter) to constrain parameter updates to an orthogonal space, thereby preventing catastrophic forgetting. Real-world experiments demonstrate that Sentinel-VLA boosts the task success rate by over 30\% compared to the SOTA model, PI0. We will open-source all the code, weights, and data generation pipeline.

</details>


### [415] [VLA-ATTC: Adaptive Test-Time Compute for VLA Models with Relative Action Critic Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01194)
*Wenhao Li,Xiu Su,Dan Niu,Yichao Cao,Hongyan Xu,Zhe Qu,Lei Fan,Shan You,Chang Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VLA-ATTC enhances VLA models with adaptive test-time compute using an uncertainty-based "cognitive clutch" and Relative Action Critic for pairwise action comparison, reducing failure rates by 50% on LIBERO-LONG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models lack deliberative reasoning, relying on fast instinctive processes that fail in complex/ambiguous scenarios requiring deeper consideration.

Method: 1) Uncertainty-based "cognitive clutch" triggers TTC deliberation when needed; 2) Relative Action Critic (RAC) selects optimal actions via pairwise comparisons instead of absolute value estimation; 3) Efficient sampling amortizes computation; 4) Automated data pipeline curates preference pairs without manual annotation.

Result: On LIBERO-LONG benchmark, VLA-ATTC reduces failure rate of SOTA model PI0.5 by over 50%.

Conclusion: Adaptive test-time compute with relative action criticism significantly improves VLA model robustness and deliberation capability.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities and generalization in embodied manipulation. However, their decision-making relies on a fast, instinctive process that lacks deliberation. This strategy often leads to suboptimal or catastrophic actions when facing complex or ambiguous scenarios that require greater consideration. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{VLA-ATTC}, a framework that endows VLA models with adaptive test-time compute (TTC). VLA-ATTC employs an uncertainty-based ``cognitive clutch'' to dynamically transition from reflexive execution to a TTC deliberation phase when necessary. During TTC phase, a novel \textbf{Relative Action Critic} (RAC) model identifies the optimal action from generated candidates via pairwise comparisons. This relative mechanism replaces unstable absolute value estimation, significantly simplifying the learning objective. Furthermore, we introduce an efficient sampling strategy to amortize computational costs and an automated data pipeline that curates preference pairs without manual annotation. On the LIBERO-LONG benchmark, VLA-ATTC reduces the failure rate of the SOTA model PI0.5 by over 50\%. We will open-source all the code and weights.

</details>


### [416] [TAIL-Safe: Task-Agnostic Safety Monitoring for Imitation Learning Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01195)
*Riad Ahmed,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TAIL-Safe is a safety framework for imitation learning policies that uses a learned Q-value function to identify safe state-action sets and applies gradient-based recovery when actions drift outside safe boundaries, validated on a Franka robot with flow-matching policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning policies like flow-matching and diffusion models are sensitive to initial conditions and suffer from compounding drift, making them unsafe for deployment in real-world scenarios where out-of-distribution situations are common.

Method: TAIL-Safe learns a Lipschitz-continuous Q-value function mapping state-action pairs to a long-term safety score based on three task-agnostic criteria (visibility, recognizability, graspability). The zero-superlevel set of this function defines a control invariant set. When actions fall outside this set, a recovery mechanism using gradient ascent on the Q-function (inspired by Nagumo's theorem) steers the policy back. A digital twin built with Gaussian Splatting enables systematic failure data collection.

Result: Experiments with a Franka Emika robot show that flow-matching policies, which fail under runtime perturbations, achieve consistent task success when guided by TAIL-Safe.

Conclusion: TAIL-Safe provides a principled way to identify safe operating regions for imitation learning policies and effectively prevents failures by recovering from unsafe actions, enabling safer deployment of IL policies in real-world environments.

Abstract: Recent imitation learning (IL) algorithms such as flow-matching and diffusion policies demonstrate remarkable performance in learning complex manipulation tasks. However, these policies often fail even when operating within their training distribution due to extreme sensitivity to initial conditions and irreducible approximation errors that lead to compounding drift. This makes it unsafe to deploy IL policies in the field where out-of-distribution scenarios are prevalent. A prerequisite for safe deployment is enabling the policy to determine whether it can execute a task the way it was learned from demonstrations. This paper presents TAIL-Safe, a principled approach to identify, for a trained IL policy, a safe set from where the policy empirically succeeds in completing the learned task. We propose a Lipschitz-continuous Q-value function that maps state-action pairs to a long-term safety score based on three short-term task-agnostic criteria: visibility, recognizability, and graspability. The zero-superlevel set of this function characterizes an empirical control invariant set over state-action pairs. When the nominal policy proposes an action outside this set, we apply a recovery mechanism inspired by Nagumo's theorem that uses gradient ascent to the Q-function to steer the policy back to safety. To learn this Q-function, we construct a high-fidelity digital twin using Gaussian Splatting that enables systematic collection of failure data without risk to physical hardware. Experiments with a Franka Emika robot demonstrate that flow-matching policies, which fail under run-time perturbations, achieve consistent task success when guided by the proposed TAIL-Safe.

</details>


### [417] [To Do or Not to Do: Ensuring the Safety of Visuomotor Policies Learned from Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01201)
*Riad Ahmed,Moniruzzaman Akash,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces "execution guarantee," a safety measure for imitation learning policies that ensures maximum task success from specific safe regions in state space, demonstrated on a Franka robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety assurance in imitation learning is underexplored despite being critical for field robotics, and there is no universal definition of safety in IL.

Method: The authors propose execution guarantee - a policy-agnostic safety measure leveraging view synthesis to identify safe regions in state space and Nagumo's sub-tangentiality condition (set invariance) to prove and operationalize safety guarantees.

Result: Experiments with a Franka robot (simulation and real world) show that the proposed safety analysis enables various IL policies to achieve maximum task success with guarantee.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that recovery policies (a by-product of the safety analysis) can help mitigate the safety-performance tradeoff in imitation learning.

Abstract: Task success has historically been the primary measure of policy performance in imitation learning (IL) research. This characteristics strictly limits the ubiquitous applications of IL algorithms in field robotics where safety assurance, in addition to task-success, is of paramount importance. It is often desirable for an IL-powered robot in the field not to roll out a policy, and hence score a poor performance, if the safety is not guaranteed. Although this trade-off between safety and performance is well investigated in classical control literature, policy safety is a heavily underexplored domain in IL research. There is no universal definition of safety in IL. To make things worst, many existing theoretical works on safety is notoriously difficult to extend to IL-powered robots in the field. This paper offers important insights on the safety and performance of IL policies. We propose execution guarantee, a policy-agnostic safety measure that guarantees the maximum task success for a visuomotor IL policy, despite minor run-time changes, from within a specific region in the state space. We leverage recent advances in view synthesis to identify such regions in the state space for an IL policy and explore a fundamental result on set invariance - namely, Nagumo's sub-tangentiality condition - to prove and operationalize execution guarantee from inside that region. Experiments with a Franka robot, both in simulation and real world, demonstrate how the proposed safety analysis allows various IL policies to achieve maximum task success with guarantee. We also demonstrate some interesting results on how a recovery policy - a by-product of the proposed safety analysis - can help to increase the policy performance and thereby mitigating the safety-performance tradeoff in IL.

</details>


### [418] [Dynamics Aware Quadrupedal Locomotion via Intrinsic Dynamics Head](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01227)
*Aman Arora,Nalini Ratha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a training framework for quadrupedal locomotion that jointly trains a Control Policy with an Intrinsic Dynamics (ID) Head to learn state-to-torque dynamics. The ID Head enables a dynamics reward for more predictable behavior and allows tuning of learned dynamics via training coefficients. The method achieves better convergence in simulation and significant efficiency gains (torque efficiency 16.8%, action rate 18.6%, mechanical power 12.8%) in real-robot experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadrupedal locomotion requires understanding physical dynamics for agile movement across complex terrains. Existing approaches lack explicit mechanisms for the control policy to reason about underlying dynamics, motivating a framework that integrates dynamics learning directly into policy training.

Method: The framework concurrently trains an Intrinsic Dynamics (ID) Head that learns state-to-torque dynamics alongside the Control Policy in simulation. A dynamics reward based on the ID Head encourages predictable dynamical behavior, and training coefficients control the influence of learned dynamics on the resulting policy.

Result: Simulation experiments show convergence to better optima across standard locomotion rewards. Real-robot experiments demonstrate sim-to-real transfer with improvements in torque efficiency (16.8%), action rate (18.6%), mechanical power (12.8%), and safe torque occupancy (6.4%).

Conclusion: The proposed ID Head framework effectively enables control policies to learn and reason about dynamics, leading to more efficient and smoother quadrupedal locomotion that transfers well from simulation to real robots.

Abstract: Quadrupedal locomotion plays a critical role in enabling agile, versatile movement across complex terrains. Understanding and estimating the underlying physical dynamics are essential for achieving efficient and stable quadrupedal locomotion. We propose a novel training framework for quadrupedal locomotion that enables the Control Policy to understand and reason about physical dynamics. In simulation, we concurrently train an Intrinsic Dynamics (ID) Head that learns state-to-torque dynamics alongside the Control Policy, and we define a dynamics reward enabled by the ID Head that encourages the Policy toward more predictable dynamical behavior. We also provide a mechanism to tune the learned dynamics in the resulting Policy by controlling the training coefficients of the ID Head. Our simulation experiments show that this mechanism drives convergence to better optima across a wide range of standard quadrupedal locomotion rewards, yielding more efficient and smoother policies. Our real-robot experiments demonstrate sim-to-real transfer of these improvements, with significant gains in torque efficiency (16.8%), action rate (18.6%), and mechanical power (12.8%), while improving safe torque occupancy by 6.4%.

</details>


### [419] [A Principled Approach for Creating High-fidelity Synthetic Demonstrations for Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01232)
*Moniruzzaman Akash,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a demonstration synthesis framework that preserves expert motion structure using Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) retargeted within 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes, with an analytic obstacle-aware DMP for collision avoidance, achieving lower deviation and higher task success compared to planner/optimization-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3DGS-based synthesis pipelines use sampling-based planners or trajectory optimization that deviate substantially from expert demonstrations, discarding subtle spatial-temporal structure critical for contact-rich and shape-sensitive manipulation, which harms downstream policy learning.

Method: The framework models expert trajectories using Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) and retargets them to new goals/objects/viewpoints in a reconstructed 3DGS scene, preserving phase-consistent, shape-preserving motion. It introduces an analytic obstacle-aware DMP formulation operating directly on the 3DGS continuous density field for collision avoidance.

Result: Evaluated on a Spot mobile manipulator across three manipulation tasks; produces trajectories with lower deviation and collision rates, and yields higher task success when training diffusion-based visuomotor policies compared to planner- and optimization-based synthesis.

Conclusion: Treating expert trajectories as a strong prior during demonstration synthesis preserves critical motion structure for contact-rich manipulation, and the proposed analytic obstacle-aware DMP within 3DGS enables safe, diverse demonstration generation without sacrificing fidelity.

Abstract: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have enabled visually realistic demonstration generation from a single expert trajectory and a short multi-view scan. However, existing 3DGS-based synthesis pipelines typically generate new motions using sampling-based planners or trajectory optimization, which often deviate substantially from the expert's demonstrated path. While such deviations may be acceptable for tasks insensitive to motion shape, they discard subtle spatial and temporal structure that is critical for contact-rich and shape-sensitive manipulation, causing increased demonstration diversity to harm downstream policy learning. We argue that demonstration synthesis should treat the expert trajectory as a strong prior. Building on this principle, we propose a framework that synthesizes diverse task demonstrations while explicitly preserving expert motion structure. We model the expert trajectory using Dynamic Movement Primitives (DMPs) and retarget it to new goals, object configurations, and viewpoints within a reconstructed 3DGS scene, yielding phase-consistent, shape-preserving motion by construction. To safely realize this expert-preserving diversity in cluttered scenes, we introduce an analytic obstacle-aware DMP formulation that operates directly on the continuous density field induced by the 3DGS representation. This enables collision avoidance while minimally perturbing the nominal expert motion, unifying photorealistic rendering and geometric reasoning without additional scene representations. We evaluate our approach on a Spot mobile manipulator across three manipulation tasks with increasing sensitivity to trajectory fidelity. Compared to planner- and optimization-based synthesis, our method produces trajectories with lower deviation and collision rates and yields higher task success when training diffusion-based visuomotor policies.

</details>


### [420] [Bi-Level Reinforcement Learning Control for an Underactuated Blimp via Center-of-Mass Reconfiguration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01289)
*Xiaorui Wang,Hongwu Wang,Yue Fan,Hao Cheng,Feitian Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a bi-level reinforcement learning framework for goal-directed tracking control of underactuated blimps with center-of-mass (CoM) reconfiguration, using two thrusters and a movable internal slider. The outer policy plans CoM configuration while the inner policy controls thrust, achieving superior tracking accuracy and robustness in both simulation and real-world experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve energy efficiency and payload capacity of blimps by using a compact, underactuated architecture (two thrusters + movable internal slider) instead of conventional overactuated designs, while addressing the control challenges arising from underactuation and nonlinear CoM-vehicle coupling.

Method: A bi-level reinforcement learning framework: (1) outer policy learns task-dependent CoM configuration planning before flight, (2) inner policy generates continuous thrust commands for straight-line tracking, supported by a two-stage learning strategy with convergence analysis.

Result: Extensive simulations and real-world experiments on a 27-goal evaluation set show the proposed method consistently outperforms fixed-CoM baselines and PID-based controllers in tracking accuracy, robustness, and sim-to-real transfer.

Conclusion: The bi-level RL framework effectively handles underactuation and nonlinear coupling in CoM-reconfigurable blimps, offering a promising hardware-efficient solution with strong real-world transferability.

Abstract: This paper investigates goal-directed tracking control of underactuated blimps with center-of-mass (CoM) reconfiguration. Unlike conventional overactuated blimp designs that rely on redundant actuation for simplified control, this paper focuses on a compact architecture consisting of two thrusters and a movable internal slider, aiming to improve energy efficiency and payload capacity. This hardware-efficient configuration introduces significant underactuation and strong nonlinear coupling between CoM dynamics and vehicle motion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a bi-level reinforcement learning framework that explicitly decouples task-level CoM planning from continuous thrust control. The outer policy determines a target-dependent CoM configuration prior to flight, while the inner policy generates thrust commands to track straight-line references. To ensure stable learning, this paper introduces a two-stage learning strategy, supported by a convergence analysis of the resulting bi-level process. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments on a 27-goal evaluation set demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms fixed-CoM baselines and PID-based controllers, achieving higher tracking accuracy, enhanced robustness, and reliable sim-to-real transfer.

</details>


### [421] [Terrain Perception for Agricultural UAVs in Complex Farmland via Rotating mmWave Radar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01340)
*Zhihao Zhan,Le Tao,Shaobin Li,Chenxin Fang,Xingrui Yang,Liang Li,Rui Fan,Yuhang Ming*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a rotating mmWave radar-based terrain perception framework for agricultural UAVs, achieving 94.42% F1 score in ground segmentation, outperforming the closest rival (90.48%), enabling robust terrain-following flight in complex farmland.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate terrain perception for agricultural UAV terrain-following flight is challenging due to occlusions, complex terrain, and disturbances. Existing fixed-FoV radar systems and LiDAR-based methods yield incomplete and unreliable terrain estimation.

Method: A low-cost rotating mmWave radar design (mechanically rotating to enlarge spatial coverage) plus a pose-consistent terrain reconstruction pipeline tailored for sparse, noisy, partially observable radar data.

Result: F1 score of 94.42 for ground segmentation (closest rival 90.48), improved terrain coverage and estimation accuracy, leading to more robust terrain-following flight.

Conclusion: The proposed rotating mmWave radar framework significantly improves terrain perception reliability for agricultural UAVs in complex farmland, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Accurate terrain perception is essential for terrain-following flight of agricultural unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), yet remains challenging in real-world farmland due to occlusions, complex terrain geometry, and environmental disturbances. Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar is a promising sensing modality for this task due to its robustness to adverse conditions; however, existing UAV-mounted radar systems rely on fixed field of view (FoV) and terrain extraction methods designed for dense LiDAR data, leading to incomplete and unreliable terrain estimation. To address these limitations, we present a low-cost rotating mmWave radar-enabled terrain perception framework for agricultural UAVs operating in complex farmland environments. Specifically, a mechanically rotating sensing design is introduced to enlarge spatial coverage and improve terrain observability beyond the limitations of fixed-view radar under dynamic low-altitude flight. Building upon this sensing capability, we further design a pose-consistent terrain reconstruction pipeline tailored for sparse, noisy, and partially observable radar data, enabling reliable ground extraction and continuous terrain surface estimation in challenging agricultural scenarios. The complete system is deployed on a real agricultural UAV platform and comprehensively evaluated through extensive field experiments. Experimental results demonstrate improved terrain coverage and estimation accuracy, achieving an F1 score of 94.42 for ground segmentation, while the closest rival only achieves 90.48. Thus, leading to more robust terrain following flight.

</details>


### [422] [Assistance Without Interruption: A Benchmark and LLM-based Framework for Non-Intrusive Human-Robot Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01368)
*Yuedi Zhang,Shuanghao Bai,Wanqi Zhou,Haoran Zhang,Qi Zhang,Zhirong Luan,Badong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces non-intrusive assistance as a new HRI paradigm where robots proactively support human activities without interruption, validated via NIABench benchmark and a hybrid LLM-scoring model approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HRI paradigms rely on direct commands, explicit negotiation, or history-based interventions. The authors argue for a non-intrusive assistance paradigm where the robot supports ongoing human activities while strictly avoiding interruptions.

Method: A hybrid architecture combining an LLM with a scoring model: semantic retrieval prunes large candidate action sets, and a ranker evaluates human-step and robot-action pairs for timing and cross-step dependency reasoning. A new benchmark NIABench with tailored metrics is also established.

Result: Experiments on both NIABench and real-world scenarios show the method achieves proactive, non-intrusive assistance that reduces human effort while preserving task effectiveness.

Conclusion: Non-intrusive assistance is a viable and beneficial HRI paradigm; the proposed hybrid method effectively balances proactivity with non-intrusiveness.

Abstract: Human-robot interaction (HRI) has long studied how agents and people coordinate to achieve shared goals. In this work, we formalize and benchmark the non-intrusive assistance as an independent paradigm of HRI, where a robot proactively supports a human's ongoing multi-step activities while strictly avoiding interruptions. Unlike conventional HRI tasks that rely on direct commands, explicit negotiation, or proactive interventions based on user habits and history, our task treats the human's plan as the primary process and formulates assistance as a joint decision over when to act and what to do. To systematically evaluate this problem, we establish a simulation benchmark, NIABench, along with new metrics tailored to the non-intrusive assistance task. We further propose a hybrid architecture that integrates an LLM with a scoring model. The scoring model first applies semantic retrieval to prune large candidate action sets, and then a ranker evaluates human-step and robot-action pairs, enabling reasoning over timing and cross-step dependencies. Comprehensive experiments on both NIABench and real-world scenarios demonstrate that our method achieves proactive, non-intrusive assistance that reduces human effort while preserving task effectiveness.

</details>


### [423] [ESARBench: A Benchmark for Agentic UAV Embodied Search and Rescue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01371)
*Daoxuan Zhang,Ping Chen,Jianyi Zhou,Shuo Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces ESAR (Embodied Search and Rescue) and ESARBench, a benchmark for evaluating Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-driven UAV agents in realistic search and rescue scenarios using high-fidelity simulation environments built with Unreal Engine 5 and AirSim.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UAV SAR research relies on traditional vision and path-planning methods, lacking a unified benchmark for embodied MLLM-powered agents. The authors aim to bridge this gap by proposing a standardized task and evaluation framework.

Method: The authors propose ESAR (autonomous aerial agents explore environments, identify rescue clues, reason about victim locations) and build ESARBench with four high-fidelity environments mapped from real GIS data, dynamic variables (weather, time, stochastic clues), 600 tasks modeled from real rescue cases, and comprehensive evaluation metrics.

Result: Evaluation of diverse baselines (heuristics to MLLM-based ObjectNav agents) reveals critical bottlenecks in spatial memory, aerial adaptation, and the trade-off between search efficiency and flight safety in the ESAR task.

Conclusion: ESARBench serves as a valuable resource to advance research in Embodied Search and Rescue, highlighting key challenges for MLLM-driven UAV agents in realistic SAR scenarios.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has empowered Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) with exceptional capabilities in spatial reasoning, semantic understanding, and complex decision-making, making them inherently suited for UAV Search and Rescue (SAR). However, existing UAV SAR research is dominated by traditional vision and path-planning methods and lacks a comprehensive and unified benchmark for embodied agents. To bridge this gap, we first propose the novel task of \textbf{Embodied Search and Rescue (ESAR)}, which requires aerial agents to autonomously explore complex environments, identify rescue clues, and reason about victim locations to execute informed decision-making. Additionally, we present \textbf{ESARBench}, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate MLLM-driven UAV agents in highly realistic SAR scenarios. Leveraging Unreal Engine 5 and AirSim, we construct four high-fidelity, large-scale open environments mapped directly from real-world Geographic Information System (GIS) data to ensure photorealistic landscapes. To rigorously simulate actual rescue operations, our benchmark incorporates dynamic variables including weather conditions, time of day, and stochastic clue placement. Furthermore, we create a dataset of 600 tasks modeled after real-world rescue cases and propose a robust set of evaluation metrics. We evaluate diverse baselines, ranging from traditional heuristics to advanced ground and aerial MLLM-based ObjectNav agents. Experimental results highlight the challenges in ESAR, revealing critical bottlenecks in spatial memory, aerial adaptation, and the trade-off between search efficiency and flight safety. We hope ESARBench serves as a valuable resource to advance research on Embodied Search and Rescue domain. Source code and project page: https://4amgodvzx.github.io/ESAR.github.io.

</details>


### [424] [SixthSense: Task-Agnostic Proprioception-Only Whole-Body Wrench Estimation for Humanoids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01427)
*Xingzhou Chen,Xiayan Xu,Yan Ning,Jiyu Yu,Yizheng Zhang,Siyi Qian,Lingzhu Xiang,Jiahao Chen,Yuquan Wang,Haodong Zhang,Ling Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SixthSense is a task-agnostic approach that estimates whole-body contact timing, location, and wrenches on humanoid robots using only proprioception and IMU data, employing conditional flow matching to achieve unprecedented contact perception performance across diverse behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots lack force-interaction capabilities for practical tasks because existing contact estimation methods require idealistic assumptions and hard-to-obtain measurements that are unavailable in practice.

Method: SixthSense uses conditional flow matching to tokenize proprioceptive histories and estimate spatiotemporally sparse contact-event flows, inferring whole-body contact timing, location, and wrenches from only proprioception and IMU data.

Result: Achieved unprecedented performance in diverse behaviors including standing, walking, and whole-body motion-tracking policies.

Conclusion: SixthSense serves as an effective plug-and-play perception module for practical humanoid applications like collision detection, physical human-robot interaction, and force-feedback teleoperation.

Abstract: Humanoid robots are entering our physical world at scale, yet as oversized toys--good at singing and dancing, but short on force-interaction capabilities for practical tasks. Bridging this gap necessitates prioritizing reliable contact perception as a fundamental requirement. Estimating external wrenches in humanoids is complicated by floating-base dynamics and indeterminate contact locations. Existing analytical frameworks require idealistic assumptions and hard-to-obtain measurements, which are often unavailable in practice. To bridge this gap, we propose SixthSense, a task-agnostic approach that infers whole-body contact timing, location, and wrenches from proprioception and IMU data alone. To capture the multi-modal dynamics between unstructured contact inputs and the uncertain motion outputs, we employ conditional flow matching to tokenize proprioceptive histories and estimate a spatiotemporally sparse contact-event flow. SixthSense serves as a plug-and-play perception module for applications including collision detection, physical human-robot interaction, and force-feedback teleoperation. Experiments across standing, walking, and whole-body motion-tracking policies showcased unprecedented performance in diverse behaviors.

</details>


### [425] [Evidence-Based Landing Site Selection and Vison-Based Landing for UAVs in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01432)
*Sina Sajjadi,Jacopo Panerati,Sina Soleymanpour,Varunkumar Mehta,Farrokh Janabi-Sharifi,Iraj Mantegh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a probabilistic framework for autonomous UAV landing that separates decision-making from visual servoing, using evidence-based belief maps and geometric constraints to select safe landing sites, validated in real and simulated environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous landing in cluttered environments remains safety-critical for UAVs, especially under noisy perception caused by sensor uncertainty and platform disturbances like vibration.

Method: Landing safety is modeled as a latent variable inferred via recursive accumulation of frame-wise visual likelihoods (flatness, slope, obstacles), combined with a hard geometric constraint based on minimum landing radius. Landing site is selected via constrained MAP estimation, then tracked using ORB features and executed via IBVS.

Result: Validated through real-world lab experiments and high-fidelity NVIDIA Isaac Sim simulations, showing consistent, cautious, and stable landing behavior across domains.

Conclusion: The proposed evidence-based probabilistic framework effectively separates perception uncertainty from execution, yielding robust and safe autonomous landing for UAVs in cluttered environments.

Abstract: Autonomous landing in cluttered or unstructured environments remains a safety-critical challenge for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), particularly under noisy perception caused by sensor uncertainty and platform-induced disturbances such as vibration. This paper presents an evidence-based probabilistic framework for autonomous UAV landing that explicitly separates decision-making under uncertainty from execution via visual servoing. Landing safety is modeled as a latent variable and inferred through recursive accumulation of frame-wise visual likelihoods derived from flatness, slope, and obstacle cues, yielding a temporally consistent belief map that is robust to transient perception errors. Physical feasibility is enforced through a hard geometric constraint based on the minimum required landing radius of the UAV, ensuring that undersized but visually appealing regions are rejected. The final landing site is selected using constrained maximum a posteriori estimation. Once selected, the UAV locks onto the target region using ORB feature tracking and performs precise alignment and descent via image-based visual servoing (IBVS). The proposed approach is validated through both real-world laboratory experiments and high-fidelity simulations in Nvidia Isaac Sim, demonstrating consistent, cautious, and stable landing behavior across domains.

</details>


### [426] [High-Speed, Scalable Sensor Readout for Dexterous Robotic Hands via Shift-Register Multiplexing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01434)
*Jaehoon Kim,Lazaros Christoforidis,Michalis Papadakis,Victor Kartsch,Robert K. Katzschmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a scalable analog sensor readout architecture based on SIPO shift-register principles for dexterous robotic hands, validated with 20 sensor channels at 1 kHz sampling rate, demonstrating high accuracy in joint sensing (0.446% APE) and tactile sensing (0.125 N RMSE, 93.4% contact classification).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing readout architectures for dexterous robotic hands impose trade-offs between sensor count, wiring complexity, and sampling bandwidth, limiting the integration of many heterogeneous sensors.

Method: A scalable analog sensor readout architecture using a serial-in parallel-out (SIPO) shift-register principle that supports heterogeneous analog sensors, scalable expansion via three signal lines, and fast configurable sampling.

Result: Validated on a tendon-driven hand with 16 joint + 4 tactile sensor modules (20 channels total) at 1 kHz full-scan rate, stable up to 1.5 kHz. Joint sensing: 0.446% max APE, sub-degree error. Tactile: 0.125 N RMSE for force, 93.4% for contact classification.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture offers a practical path toward fully sensorized robotic hands for dexterous manipulation by overcoming the trade-offs between sensor count, wiring, and bandwidth.

Abstract: Dexterous robotic hands require high-speed multimodal sensing across many degrees of freedom, yet existing readout architectures often impose trade-offs between sensor count, wiring complexity, and sampling bandwidth. This paper presents a scalable analog sensor readout architecture based on a serial-in parallel-out (SIPO) shift-register principle. The proposed architecture supports versatile integration of heterogeneous analog-output sensors, scalable expansion using only three signal lines between sensor modules, and fast, configurable sampling. We validate the approach on a tendon-driven robotic hand integrating 16 joint sensor modules and one four-channel tactile sensor module, enabling acquisition of 20 sensor channels at a full-scan rate of 1 kHz, with stable operation up to 1.5 kHz. Joint sensor characterization showed a maximum slope absolute percentage error (APE) of 0.446% and sub-degree estimation error, indicating that the proposed readout system does not significantly degrade sensing performance. For tactile sensing, LSTM-based models achieved an RMSE of 0.125 N for force estimation and 93.4% accuracy for five-class contact-location classification, and were deployed for real-time inference at 1 kHz. System-level experiments showed that the joint sensors provide more accurate feedback than motor-based estimation during interaction, while the tactile sensor enables responsive force estimation in contact. The proposed architecture offers a practical path toward fully sensorized robotic hands for dexterous manipulation.

</details>


### [427] [Decompose and Recompose: Reasoning New Skills from Existing Abilities for Cross-Task Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01448)
*Xitie Zhang,Aming Wu,Yahong Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Decompose and Recompose, a skill reasoning framework using atomic skill-action pairs as intermediate representations to achieve zero-shot cross-task generalization in robotic manipulation by decomposing seen demonstrations into interpretable skill-action alignments and recomposing them for unseen tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-task generalization in open-world robotic manipulation is challenging; existing in-context learning methods only provide low-level continuous action sequences as context, failing to capture composable skill knowledge and degenerating into superficial trajectory imitation.

Method: Uses atomic skill-action pairs as intermediate representations; constructs a task-adaptive dynamic demonstration library via visual-semantic retrieval combined with skill sequences from a planning agent, plus a coverage-aware static library to fill missing skill patterns.

Result: Validated on AGNOSTOS benchmark and real-world environments, demonstrating zero-shot cross-task generalization capability.

Conclusion: The proposed skill reasoning framework with skill-action pairs as intermediate representations effectively enables compositional reasoning for skill composition and execution ordering in unseen tasks.

Abstract: Cross-task generalization is a core challenge in open-world robotic manipulation, and the key lies in extracting transferable manipulation knowledge from seen tasks. Recent in-context learning approaches leverage seen task demonstrations to generate actions for unseen tasks without parameter updates. However, existing methods provide only low-level continuous action sequences as context, failing to capture composable skill knowledge and causing models to degenerate into superficial trajectory imitation. We propose Decompose and Recompose, a skill reasoning framework using atomic skill-action pairs as intermediate representations. Our approach decomposes seen demonstrations into interpretable skill--action alignments, enabling the model to recompose these skills for unseen tasks through compositional reasoning. Specifically, we construct a task-adaptive dynamic demonstration library via visual-semantic retrieval combined with skill sequences from a planning agent, complemented by a coverage-aware static library to fill missing skill patterns. Together, these yield skill-comprehensive demonstrations that explicitly elicit compositional reasoning for skill composition and execution ordering. Experiments on the AGNOSTOS benchmark and real-world environments validate our method's zero-shot cross-task generalization capability.

</details>


### [428] [LLM-Foraging: Large Language Models for Decentralized Swarm Robot Foraging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01461)
*Peihan Li,Joanna Gutierrez,Fabian Hernandez,Qi Lu,Lifeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LLM-Foraging augments CPFA with LLM tactical decisions at three key points, achieving training-free transfer across different team sizes, arena sizes, and resource distributions, outperforming GA-tuned baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Swarm foraging algorithms like CPFA rely on offline parameter optimization (GA/RL) that produces policies tightly coupled to specific configurations (team size, arena size, resource distribution). Performance degrades when deployment conditions change, and retraining is computationally expensive.

Method: LLM-Foraging augments the CPFA state machine with an LLM tactical decision-maker at three structured points: post-deposit, central-zone arrival, and search starvation. Each robot runs its own LLM client using locally observable state, while the existing CPFA motion/sensing stack executes actions. The LLM serves as a general decision policy, eliminating the need for training at deployment.

Result: Evaluated in Gazebo with TurtleBot3 robots across 36 configurations (4-10 robots, 6x6 to 10x10m arenas, three resource distributions). LLM-Foraging collects more resources than GA-tuned CPFA baseline across all configurations and is more consistent.

Conclusion: Using LLMs as tactical decision-makers in swarm foraging enables training-free transfer across varying deployment conditions, outperforming traditional GA-tuned approaches that lack cross-configuration generalization.

Abstract: Swarm foraging algorithms, such as the central-place foraging algorithm (CPFA), typically rely on offline parameter optimization using genetic algorithms (GA) or reinforcement learning, yielding policies tightly coupled to a specific combination of team size, arena size, and resource distribution. When deployment conditions change, performance degrades, and retraining is computationally expensive. We propose LLM-Foraging, a decentralized swarm controller that augments the CPFA state machine with a large language model (LLM) tactical decision-maker at three structured decision points, namely post-deposit, central-zone arrival, and search starvation. Each robot runs its own LLM client and queries it using only locally observable state, while the existing CPFA motion and sensing stack executes the selected action. Because the LLM serves as a general decision policy rather than parameters fitted to a single configuration, the controller is training-free at deployment and transfers across configurations without re-optimization. We evaluate LLM-Foraging in Gazebo with TurtleBot3 robots across 36 configurations spanning team sizes of 4 to 10 robots, arena sizes from 6x6 to 10x10 meters, and three resource distributions (clustered, powerlaw, random). LLM-Foraging collects more resources than the GA-tuned CPFA baseline across the evaluated configurations and is more consistent, a property that the GA's single-configuration tuning does not transfer.

</details>


### [429] [Action Agent: Agentic Video Generation Meets Flow-Constrained Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01477)
*Jeffrin Sam,Nguyen Khang,Yara Mahmoud,Miguel Altamirano Cabrera,Dzmitry Tsetserukou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Action Agent is a two-stage framework combining LLM-orchestrated video generation (Stage I) with Flow-Constrained Diffusion Transformer for velocity control (Stage II), achieving 73.2% simulated and 64.7% real-world navigation success on a Unitree G1 humanoid at 40-47 Hz.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify agentic navigation video generation with flow-constrained diffusion control for multi-embodiment robot navigation, addressing the gap between trajectory imagination and execution in a scalable, embodiment-aware manner.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) LLM orchestrates video diffusion model selection, prompt refinement, and cross-task memory; (2) FlowDiT integrates DINOv2, optical flow, CLIP embeddings with action-space denoising diffusion to generate velocity commands. Pretrained on RECON, fine-tuned on 203 Unitree G1 episodes.

Result: Stage I: 35% → 86% video generation success. Stage II: 73.2% simulation success, 64.7% real-world task completion on Unitree G1 at 40-47 Hz. Validated across three embodiments (humanoid, drone, wheeled robot).

Conclusion: Decoupling trajectory imagination from execution yields a scalable, embodiment-aware paradigm for language-guided navigation across diverse robot platforms.

Abstract: We present Action Agent, a two-stage framework that unifies agentic navigation video generation with flow-constrained diffusion control for multi-embodiment robot navigation. In Stage I, a large language model (LLM) acts as an orchestration module that selects video diffusion models, refines prompts through iterative validation, and accumulates cross-task memory to synthesize physically plausible first-person navigation videos from language and image inputs. This increases video generation success from 35% (single-shot) to 86% across 50 navigation tasks. In Stage II, we introduce FlowDiT, a Flow-Constrained Diffusion Transformer that converts optimized goal videos and language instructions into continuous velocity commands using action-space denoising diffusion. FlowDiT integrates DINOv2 visual features, learned optical flow for ego-motion representation, and CLIP language embeddings for semantic stopping. We pretrain on the RECON outdoor navigation dataset and fine-tune on 203 Unitree G1 humanoid episodes collected in Isaac Sim to calibrate velocity dynamics. A single 43M-parameter checkpoint achieves 73.2% navigation success in simulation and 64.7% task completion on a real Unitree G1 in unseen indoor environments under open-loop execution, while operating at 40--47 Hz. We evaluate Action Agent across three embodiments: a Unitree G1 humanoid (real hardware), a drone, and a wheeled mobile robot (Isaac Sim), demonstrating that decoupling trajectory imagination from execution yields a scalable and embodiment-aware paradigm for language-guided navigation.

</details>


### [430] [Cut-In Gap Acceptance Toward Autonomous vs. Human-Driven Vehicles: Evidence from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01485)
*Abdulaziz Alhuraish,Yuhang Wang,Hao Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Human drivers accept significantly shorter gaps (7.58m vs 9.57m) and execute faster cut-ins when merging in front of autonomous vehicles compared to human-driven vehicles, based on analysis of over 25,000 real-world highway scenarios from the Waymo Open Motion Dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether human drivers behave more aggressively (accept shorter gaps) when cutting in front of autonomous vehicles versus human-driven vehicles, exploiting the AV's conservative rule-based behavior.

Method: Using the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (25,906 highway scenarios at 10Hz), an eight-criterion lane-change detector extracted 706 HDV-to-AV and 3,172 HDV-to-HDV cut-in events. Statistical tests (p-values, Cohen's d, chi-squared) and speed-matched resampling were applied to compare gap acceptance and cut-in speed between conditions.

Result: Median accepted gap was 1.99m shorter for AV targets (7.58m vs 9.57m, p=5.76e-8, d=-0.224). Cut-in speeds were 37% higher toward AVs (51.7 vs 37.7 km/h, d=0.502). 68% of AV-targeted cut-ins were below 10m vs 51.8% for HDVs (χ²=60.5, p<1e-13). Effects persisted after speed-matching.

Conclusion: There is a systematic, safety-relevant asymmetry in human gap-acceptance behavior toward AVs that warrants AV-specific calibration of motion-planning safety envelopes and traffic simulation models.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are widely known to follow conservative, rule-based motion policies that surrounding drivers can learn to anticipate. A direct consequence is that human drivers may accept shorter longitudinal gaps when cutting in front of an AV than when targeting another human-driven vehicle (HDV). We test this hypothesis using the Waymo Open Motion Dataset (WOMD), which provides 25,906 real-world highway scenarios at 10 hertz. An eight-criterion lane-change detector extracts 706 HDV-to-AV and 3,172 HDV-to-HDV cut-in events from the same traffic environment. The median accepted gap in front of the Waymo AV is 7.58 meters versus 9.57 meters for HDV targets, a 1.99 meter reduction that is statistically significant (p equals 5.76 times 10 to the negative eighth power, d equals negative 0.224) and persists under speed-matched resampling. Cut-in speeds toward the AV are 37 percent higher (51.7 versus 37.7 kilometers per hour, d equals 0.502), and 68.0 percent of AV-targeted cut-ins occur below the 10 meter gap boundary versus 51.8 percent of HDV-targeted events (chi-squared equals 60.5, p is less than 10 to the negative thirteenth power). These results reveal a systematic and safety-relevant asymmetry in human gap-acceptance behavior that warrants AV-specific calibration of both motion-planning safety envelopes and traffic simulation models.

</details>


### [431] [Distributed Algorithm with Emergent Area Partitioning and Base Station's Situation Awareness for Multi-Robot Patrolling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01501)
*Kazuho Kobayashi,Shohei Kobayashi,Seiya Ueno,Takehiro Higuchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces the LR-PT algorithm for multi-robot patrolling, which uses local information for target selection, improves situation awareness at base stations, and autonomously partitions areas to outperform existing methods in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance multi-robot patrol efficiency and operator situation awareness at base stations, enabling better prediction, decision-making, and emergency interventions during surveillance missions.

Method: The LR-PT (Local Reactive and Partition) algorithm allows robots to independently select patrol targets using locally available information, integrating patrol needs and reporting urgency into a unified utility function. It autonomously emerges area partitions to avoid local optima and ensure comprehensive coverage.

Result: LR-PT outperformed existing methods in simulations, achieving frequent patrols of all locations, enhanced situation awareness, and robustness against communication constraints and robot failures.

Conclusion: LR-PT effectively leverages swarm robotics principles to address real-world multi-robot patrolling challenges, demonstrating superior performance through local decision-making and emergent area partitioning.

Abstract: Patrolling with multiple robots offers efficient surveillance to detect and manage undesired situations. This necessitates improved patrol efficiency and operator situation awareness at base stations. Enhanced situation awareness enables operators to predict robots' behaviors, support recognition and decision-making, and execute emergency interventions. This study presents the Local Reactive and Partition (LR-PT) algorithm, a novel multi-robot patrolling approach. In simulations, LR-PT outperformed existing methods by ensuring frequent patrols of all locations of interest and enhancing the situation awareness of the base station. Robots independently select patrol targets based on locally available information, integrating patrol needs and the urgency of reporting mission progress to the base station into a unified utility function. This locality also contributes to robustness against communication constraints and robot failures, as demonstrated in this research. The algorithm further autonomously emerged the area partition, which can avoid falling into local optima and realize the comprehensive patrol over the whole mission area. The simulation results demonstrated the superior performance of LR-PT for multi-robot patrolling, utilizing the advantages of swarm robotics and addressing real-world operational challenges.

</details>


### [432] [Dynamics Distillation for Efficient and Transferable Control Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01516)
*Xunjiang Gu,Kashyap Chitta,Mahsa Golchoubian,Vladimir Suplin,Igor Gilitschenski*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Sim2Sim2Sim bridges high-fidelity and scalable simulators via distilled learned dynamics, enabling efficient policy training that transfers back to realistic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous driving simulators offer either physical realism or computational scalability, but not both simultaneously.

Method: Distills simulator dynamics into a highly parallelizable learned dynamics model, trains control policies purely in this distilled environment, then deploys them back to the source high-fidelity simulator.

Result: Demonstrates more efficient policy optimization and reliable transfer under challenging dynamics; shows predictive accuracy alone is insufficient for evaluating learned dynamics as RL training environments.

Conclusion: Learned dynamics models should be assessed not just by accuracy but by the quality of policies they enable.

Abstract: Robust control policy learning for autonomous driving requires training environments to be both physically realistic and computationally scalable, properties that existing simulators provide only in isolation. We introduce Sim2Sim2Sim, a framework that bridges high-fidelity vehicle simulation and scalable reinforcement learning by distilling simulator dynamics into a highly parallelizable learned dynamics model. By training control policies purely within this distilled environment and deploying them back into the high-fidelity source simulator, we demonstrate more efficient policy optimization and reliable transfer under challenging dynamics. We further show that predictive accuracy alone does not fully characterize a learned dynamics model's suitability as a reinforcement learning training environment, which should also be assessed by the quality of the policies it enables.

</details>


### [433] [VOFA: Visual Object Goal Pushing with Force-Adaptive Control for Humanoids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01518)
*Zichao Hu,Zifan Xu,Dongsik Chang,He Yin,Linh Tran,Roberto Martín-Martín,Peter Stone,Jingyu Qiao,Joydeep Biswas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VOFA is a visual goal-conditioned humanoid loco-manipulation system for pushing heavy objects to arbitrary goal positions using onboard perception, achieving over 90% simulation and 80% real-world success on the Booster T1 humanoid robot, including objects up to 17kg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humanoid robots need to push large objects to goal configurations in real-time with only onboard egocentric perception, dealing with unknown object mass, friction, noisy sensing, and actuation errors — challenges not addressed by prior works that rely on privileged information or lack robustness.

Method: A two-level hierarchical architecture: (1) a high-level visuomotor policy that processes noisy onboard observations and outputs goal-conditioned commands, and (2) a low-level force-adaptive whole-body controller that provides robustness to variations in object physical properties.

Result: Over 90% success in simulation and over 80% success in real-world trials on the Booster T1 humanoid robot, successfully pushing objects weighing up to 17kg (exceeding half the robot's body weight).

Conclusion: VOFA demonstrates that a hierarchical visuomotor + whole-body control approach enables robust, goal-conditioned heavy object pushing using only onboard egocentric perception, without requiring knowledge of object physical properties.

Abstract: The ability to push large objects in a goal-directed manner using onboard egocentric perception is an essential skill for humanoid robots to perform complex tasks such as material handling in warehouses. To robustly manipulate heavy objects to arbitrary goal configurations, the robot must cope with unknown object mass and ground friction, noisy onboard perception, and actuation errors; all in a real-time feedback loop. Existing solutions either rely on privileged object-state information without onboard perception or lack robustness to variations in goal configurations and object physical properties. In this work, we present VOFA, a visual goal-conditioned humanoid loco-manipulation system capable of pushing objects with unknown physical properties to arbitrary goal positions. VOFA consists of a two-level hierarchical architecture with a high-level visuomotor policy and a low-level force-adaptive whole-body controller. The high-level policy processes noisy onboard observations and generates goal-conditioned commands to operate in closed loop across diverse object-goal configurations, while the low-level whole-body controller provides robustness to variations in object physical properties. VOFA is extensively evaluated in both simulation and real-world experiments on the Booster T1 humanoid robot. Our results demonstrate strong performance, achieving over 90% success in simulation and over 80% success in real-world trials. Moreover, VOFA successfully pushes objects weighing up to 17kg, exceeding half of the Booster T1's body weight.

</details>


### [434] [Good in Bad (GiB): Sifting Through End-user Demonstrations for Learning a Better Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01529)
*Noushad Sojib,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GiB is an algorithm that automatically identifies and removes erroneous subtasks from imperfect human demonstrations while keeping good ones, enabling more robust robot policy learning from mixed-quality data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning typically assumes high-quality demonstrations, but real-world non-expert users often produce imperfect data. Discarding entire flawed demonstrations wastes valuable data, while learning from errors leads to unsafe policies.

Method: GiB first trains a self-supervised model to learn latent features and assigns binary weights to label demonstrations as good or bad. It then models the latent feature distribution of high-quality segments and uses Mahalanobis distance to detect and evaluate poor-quality subtasks.

Result: Validated on Franka robot in both simulated and real-world multi-step tasks, demonstrating improved policy performance when learning from mixed-quality human demonstrations.

Conclusion: GiB effectively filters erroneous subtasks from imperfect demonstrations, allowing robust policy learning without discarding valuable data, and works with any downstream policy learning algorithm.

Abstract: Imitation learning offers a promising framework for enabling robots to acquire diverse skills from human users. However, most imitation learning algorithms assume access to high-quality demonstrations an unrealistic expectation when collecting data from non-expert users, whose demonstrations often contain inadvertent errors. Naively learning from such demonstrations can result in unsafe policy behavior, while discarding entire demonstrations due to occasional mistakes wastes valuable data, especially in low-data settings. In this work, we introduce GiB (Good-in-Bad), an algorithm that automatically identifies and discards erroneous subtasks within demonstrations while preserving high-quality subtasks. The filtered data can then be used by any policy learning algorithm to train more robust policies. GiB first trains a self-supervised model to learn latent features and assigns binary weights to label each demonstration as good or bad. It then models the latent feature distribution of high-quality segments and uses the Mahalanobis distance to detect and evaluate poor-quality subtasks.
