We validate GiB on the Franka robot in both simulated and real-world multi-step tasks, demonstrating improved policy performance when learning from mixed-quality human demonstrations.

</details>


### [435] [An Efficient Metric for Data Quality Measurement in Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01544)
*Noushad Sojib,Momotaz Begum*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a fast, automated metric based on power spectral density (PSD) to rank and filter low-quality end-user demonstrations for fine-tuning imitation learning policies, avoiding expensive policy rollouts and achieving better task success rates in real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning pre-trained IL policies with end-user demonstrations in deployment environments is promising for handling OOD scenarios, but end-user demos often contain poor-quality artifacts (oscillations, abrupt adjustments). Existing automated data curation methods require policy rollouts, which are computationally expensive and impractical for real-world deployment.

Method: The authors propose a PSD-based metric that computes the power spectral density of demonstration trajectories. Lower PSD values indicate smoother, higher-quality demonstrations; higher values indicate erratic trajectories. The metric requires no policy learning, environment interaction, or expert labeling.

Result: Evaluated on two benchmark IL datasets and a user study with older adults at a retirement facility. PSD-curated data yields higher task success rates and smoother execution trajectories compared to uncurated baselines and two competitive data-ranking methods.

Conclusion: The PSD metric is a fast, efficient, and scalable solution for in-the-field demonstration data curation, effectively filtering low-quality demonstrations without costly rollouts or expert annotation.

Abstract: Imitation learning (IL) has seen remarkable progress, yet field deployment of IL-powered robots remains hindered by the challenge of out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. Fine-tuning pre-trained policies with end-user demonstrations collected in deployment environments is a promising strategy to address this challenge. However, end-user demonstrations are frequently of poor quality, characterized by excessive corrective motions, oscillations, and abrupt adjustments that degrade both learned and fine-tuned policy performance. Existing automated approaches for curating demonstration data require policy rollouts in the environment, making them computationally expensive and impractical for real-world deployment. In this paper, we propose a fast, efficient, and fully automated demonstration ranking metric based on the power spectral density (PSD) of demonstration trajectories. The PSD metric requires no policy learning, environment interaction, or expert labeling, making it well-suited for scalable, in-the-field data curation. Lower PSD values correspond to smoother, higher-quality demonstrations, while higher PSD values indicate erratic, artifact-laden trajectories. We evaluate the proposed metric on two benchmark imitation learning datasets comprising expert and lay-user demonstrations, and through a user study with older adults at a retirement facility, where collected demonstrations are used to fine-tune $\pi0.5$ \cite{intelligence2025pi_} for a daily living task. Results demonstrate that PSD-curated data yields policies with higher task success rates and smoother execution trajectories compared to uncurated baselines and two competitive data-ranking methods.

</details>


### [436] [Hydra-DP3: Frequency-Aware Right-Sizing of 3D Diffusion Policies for Visuomotor Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01581)
*Jinhao Zhang,Zhexuan Zhou,Huizhe Li,Yichen Lai,Wenlong Xia,Haoming Song,Youmin Gong,Jie Mei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HDP3 is a lightweight 3D diffusion policy that uses a Diffusion Mixer decoder with two-step DDIM inference, achieving SOTA performance with <1% parameters of prior methods by exploiting the low-frequency dominance of smooth robot action trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion-based visuomotor policies inherit complex image-generation-style decoders and multi-step sampling, which are overkill for robot action trajectories that are inherently smooth and low-frequency dominated.

Method: Proposes Hydra-DP3 (HDP3) with a lightweight Diffusion Mixer decoder supporting two-step DDIM inference, justified by frequency-domain analysis showing action denoising error saturates after very few reverse steps.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, MetaWorld, and real-world tasks with fewer than 1% of the parameters and substantially lower inference latency than prior 3D diffusion policies.

Conclusion: Robot action trajectories are highly smooth with low-frequency energy concentration, enabling much simpler denoising models than image generation; two-step denoising is sufficient for effective action generation.

Abstract: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies perform well in robotic manipulation, yet current methods still inherit image-generation-style decoders and multi-step sampling. We revisit this design from a frequency-domain perspective. Robot action trajectories are highly smooth, with most energy concentrated in a few low-frequency discrete cosine transform modes. Under this structure, we show that the error of the optimal denoiser is bounded by the low-frequency subspace dimension and residual high-frequency energy, implying that denoising error saturates after very few reverse steps. This further suggests that action denoising requires a much simpler denoising model than image generation. Motivated by this insight, we propose Hydra-DP3(HDP3), a pocket-scale 3D diffusion policy with a lightweight Diffusion Mixer decoder that supports two-step DDIM inference. Our synthetic experiments validate the theory and support the sufficiency of two-step denoising. Futhermore, across RoboTwin2.0, Adroit, MetaWorld, and real-world tasks, HDP3 achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer than 1% of the parameters of prior 3D diffusion-based policies and substantially lower inference latency.

</details>


### [437] [Lateral String Stability for Vehicle Platoons: Formulation, Definition, and Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01731)
*Sixu Li,Swaroop Darbha,Yang Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a framework for lateral string stability in vehicle platooning, focusing on path-relative tracking errors. It introduces an arc-length (Eulerian) viewpoint and proposes two control strategies: feedback-feedforward (onboard sensing only) and learn-from-predecessor (with V2V communication). Results show onboard sensing alone cannot guarantee error attenuation, while V2V communication enables it, with specific structural controller requirements identified.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lateral string stability, which governs propagation of path-tracking errors that can lead to unsafe deviations, remains underexplored despite extensive work on longitudinal string stability. Its importance grows as autonomous vehicles depend on onboard sensing and map-free navigation, where sensor occlusions and tight formations amplify safety risks.

Method: The paper develops an arc-length (Eulerian) viewpoint framework that clarifies how tracking errors propagate from one vehicle to the next. It proposes L2 lateral string stability definition and analyzes two control strategies: feedback-feedforward (onboard sensing only) and learn-from-predecessor (with V2V communication), evaluated against tracking error vector and lateral (cross-track) error measures.

Result: Onboard sensing alone cannot guarantee attenuation of path-tracking errors (fundamental safety limitation). V2V communication enables true error attenuation. The analysis identifies structural controller requirements, showing that nonzero feedback on specific measurements is essential for guaranteeing stability.

Conclusion: The framework reveals a fundamental limitation of onboard-only sensing for lateral stability and demonstrates that V2V communication is necessary for safe lateral string stability in vehicle platooning.

Abstract: Platooning of connected and automated vehicles provides significant benefits in terms of energy efficiency, traffic throughput, and, most critically, safety. These safety benefits depend on string stability, which dictates how disturbances propagate along a vehicle string. Although longitudinal string stability has been extensively examined, lateral string stability, which governs the propagation of path-tracking errors that can lead to unsafe deviations from the desired path, remains underexplored. Its importance is growing as autonomous vehicles increasingly depend on onboard sensing and map-free navigation, where sensor occlusions and tight formations amplify safety risks. This paper presents a framework for lateral string stability that focuses directly on safety-critical, path-relative tracking errors and enables consistent comparison across vehicles that follow the same planned path. The key element of the framework is an arc-length (Eulerian) viewpoint, a departure from traditional analyses, that clarifies how tracking errors at a given point on the path propagate from one vehicle to the next. Building on this foundation, we propose the definition of L2 lateral string stability along with two control strategies: a feedback-feedforward strategy that relies solely on onboard sensing, and a novel learn-from-predecessor strategy that makes use of vehicle-to-vehicle communication. Both strategies are analyzed for lateral string stability with respect to two error measures: tracking error vector and lateral (cross-track) error. Our results show that onboard sensing alone cannot guarantee attenuation of path-tracking errors, imposing a fundamental safety limitation, while V2V communication enables true error attenuation. The analysis further identifies structural controller requirements, showing that nonzero feedback on specific measurements is essential for guaranteeing stability.

</details>


### [438] [Anticipation-VLA: Solving Long-Horizon Embodied Tasks via Anticipation-based Subgoal Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01772)
*Zhilong Zhang,Wenyu Luo,Haonan Wang,Yifei Sheng,Yidi Wang,Hanyuan Guo,Haoxiang Ren,Xinghao Du,Yuhan Che,Tongtong Cao,Lei Yuan,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Anticipation-VLA introduces an adaptive, recursive subgoal generation mechanism that improves VLA model robustness in long-horizon robotic tasks by dynamically adjusting future goals as task states evolve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models struggle with long-horizon tasks due to compounding errors, and prior methods using fixed-granularity subtask decomposition cannot adapt to varying execution complexity.

Method: The authors propose Anticipation-VLA, a hierarchical model combining an anticipation model (adaptive and recursive future subgoal generation via finetuned UMM) with a goal-conditioned VLA policy for low-level action execution.

Result: Experiments in simulated and real-world robotic tasks show Anticipation-VLA outperforms baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of adaptive, recursive subgoal generation.

Conclusion: Adaptive, recursive subgoal generation is key to robust policy execution in long-horizon tasks, and the hierarchical VLA approach is validated both in simulation and real-world settings.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for embodied intelligence, enabling robots to perform tasks based on natural language instructions and current visual input. However, existing VLA models struggle with long-horizon tasks due to compounding errors. Prior methods decompose tasks into subtasks of fixed granularity, which cannot adapt to the varying complexity of execution states, limiting their robustness in long-horizon tasks. To overcome this, we introduce Anticipation Model, which adaptively and recursively generates future subgoals. This model continuously adapts as the task unfolds, adjusting future subgoals in response to evolving dynamics, facilitating more reliable planning paths. Building on this concept, we propose Anticipation-VLA, a hierarchical VLA model that leverages the anticipation model to generate actionable subgoals that guide VLA policy execution. We implement Anticipation-VLA with finetuning a Unified Multimodal Model (UMM) for high-level subgoal generation and a goal-conditioned VLA policy for low-level action execution. Experiments in both simulated and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of Anticipation-VLA, highlighting the importance of adaptive and recursive subgoal generation for robust policy execution.

</details>


### [439] [On the Characterization and Limits of 4D Radar for Aided Inertial Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01773)
*Morten Nissov,Kostas Alexis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper develops a reliable FMCW radar-aided inertial navigation estimator by analyzing fundamental measurement relations, deriving noise models from first principles, and designing a factor graph-based estimator with first-order noise approximation. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate superior robustness and accuracy, even beyond standard operating range.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FMCW radar is promising for aided inertial navigation in challenging environments where LiDAR and vision fail, but its adoption is hindered by complex, noisy measurements that make reliable estimation difficult.

Method: The authors derive noise models from first principles applied to FMCW radar signal processing, then design a factor graph-based estimator using first-order approximation for measurement noise propagation.

Result: Simulation validates noise source significance, first-order approximation validity, and state-dependent covariance. Extensive real-world experiments show superior robustness and accuracy across diverse environments and flight profiles, including beyond standard operating range.

Conclusion: The proposed FMCW radar estimator achieves reliable and accurate navigation performance in challenging conditions, with insights confirmed across both simulation and real-world experiments.

Abstract: Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar is a promising sensor for aided inertial navigation, due to its robustness in environments that challenge traditional alternatives, such as LiDAR and vision. However, its widespread adoption is hindered by complex, noisy measurements, which make reliable estimation difficult. This manuscript addresses these challenges by analyzing the fundamental measurement relations of FMCW radar sensing and developing a reliable estimator. Noise models are derived by applying first principles to the underlying signal processing of a typical radar sensor. These models guide the design of a factor graph-based estimator, utilizing a first-order approximation for the measurement noise propagation. The approach is first examined through simulation, evaluating the significance of different noise sources, the validity of the first-order approximation, and the state-dependent nature of the covariance expressions. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior robustness and accuracy of the proposed method across diverse field environments and flight profiles, including beyond the radar's standard operating range. Furthermore, the experiments confirm the insights from the simulation regarding the behavior and performance of different estimator configurations relative to their operating conditions. The evaluation data and estimator implementation are made available at https://github.com/ntnu-arl/rig.

</details>


### [440] [Optimizing Trajectory-Trees in Belief Space: An Application from Model Predictive Control to Task and Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01860)
*Camille Phiquepal,Marc Toussaint*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes using trajectory-trees (branched plans) instead of sequential trajectories for partially observable robotic planning, showing benefits in both MPC (via PO-MPC with D-AuLa optimization) and TAMP (via PO-LGP), with experiments in autonomous driving and small belief-state problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional sequential trajectories fail to capture multiple possible contingencies in partially observable environments, where the optimal action depends on future observations.

Method: (1) PO-MPC: Optimizes tree-structured trajectories with a single branching in belief space, solved via Distributed Augmented Lagrangian (D-AuLa) for parallelization. (2) PO-LGP: Extends Logic-Geometric Programming to partially observable problems using decision trees at task level and trajectory-trees at motion level.

Result: D-AuLa achieves real-time MPC performance through parallelization; PO-LGP works for small belief-state problems and scales via explorative macro-action policies.

Conclusion: Planning with trajectory-trees improves control costs and handles partial observability more effectively than sequential trajectories, with practical real-time feasibility.

Abstract: This paper explores the benefits of computing arborescent trajectories (trajectory-trees) instead of commonly used sequential trajectories for partially observable robotic planning problems. In such environments, a robot infers knowledge from observations, and the optimal course of action depends on these observations. \revise{Trajectory-trees, optimized in belief space, naturally capture this dependency by branching where the belief state is expected to evolve into multiple distinct scenarios, such as upon receiving an observation. Unlike sequential trajectories, which model a single forward evolution of the system, trajectory-trees capture multiple possible contingencies.}
  First, we focus on Model Predictive Control (MPC) and demonstrate the benefits of planning tree-like trajectories. We formulate the control problem as the optimization of a tree with a single branching (PO-MPC). This improves performance by reducing control costs through more informed planning. To satisfy the real-time constraints of MPC, we develop an optimization algorithm called Distributed Augmented Lagrangian (D-AuLa), which leverages the decomposability of the PO-MPC formulation to parallelize and accelerate the optimization. We apply the method to both linear and non-linear MPC problems using autonomous driving examples.
  Second, we address Task And Motion Planning (TAMP), and introduce a planner (PO-LGP) reasoning on decision trees at task level, and trajectory-trees at motion-planning level. This approach builds upon the Logic-Geometric-Programming Framework (LGP) and extends it to partially observable problems. The experiments show the method's applicability to problems with a small belief state size, and scales to larger problems by optimizing explorative policies, which are used as macro-actions in an overarching task plan.

</details>


### [441] [Phone2Act: A Low-Cost, Hardware-Agnostic Teleoperation System for Scalable VLA Data Collection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01948)
*Om Mandhane,Bipin Yadav,Sangeetha Prasanna Ram,Gopalakrishnan Narayanan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Phone2Act transforms commodity smartphones into 6-DoF robot controllers via ARCore, using modular ROS 2 architecture to support diverse robot platforms without code changes. It collects synchronized multi-camera + state data directly in LeRobot format for immediate VLA model training, validated by 90% success rate on real-world pick-and-place tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing teleoperation frameworks are prohibitively expensive due to specialized hardware and tight coupling to specific robot platforms, creating a barrier for many research groups to collect diverse, high-quality manipulation data for VLA model training.

Method: Phone2Act uses Google ARCore on commodity smartphones for 6-DoF tracking, a modular ROS 2 architecture with interchangeable bridge nodes to decouple control from hardware, and a Universal Recorder that synchronizes multi-camera RGB streams with robot state, exporting directly in LeRobot format.

Result: Fine-tuned GR00T-N1.5 on 130 collected episodes achieved 90% success rate on a real-world multi-stage pick-and-place task deployed on a physical Dobot CR5.

Conclusion: Phone2Act demonstrates that low-cost, hardware-agnostic teleoperation using commodity smartphones is feasible and effective for VLA data collection and model fine-tuning, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for manipulation research.

Abstract: Collecting diverse, high-quality manipulation data for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model training remains prohibitively expensive for many research groups, as existing teleoperation frameworks rely on specialized hardware or are tightly coupled to specific robot platforms. We present Phone2Act, a low-cost, hardware-agnostic teleoperation framework that transforms a commodity smartphone into a 6-DoF robot controller via Google ARCore. Built on a modular ROS 2 architecture, Phone2Act decouples control logic from hardware specifics through interchangeable bridge nodes, supporting platforms from industrial cobots to low-cost bimanual arms without code modification. A Universal Recorder synchronizes multi-camera RGB streams with robot state feedback and exports demonstrations natively in the LeRobot dataset format, eliminating post-processing and enabling immediate VLA fine-tuning. We validate the framework by fine-tuning GR00T-N1.5 on 130 collected episodes, achieving a 90% success rate on a real-world multi-stage pick-and-place task deployed on a physical Dobot CR5.

</details>


### [442] [Sonar-GPS Fusion for Seabed Mapping in Turbid Shallow Waters with an Autonomous Surface Vehicle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01949)
*Yisheng Zhang,Michael Xu,Alan Williams,Matthew Gray,Nare Karapetyan,Miao Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A drift-resilient seabed mapping framework integrating FMT-based local alignment with EKF-based global trajectory optimization, achieving sub-meter accuracy for oyster farm mapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional optical methods fail in turbid shallow waters like shellfish farms; existing sonar systems suffer from low-resolution positioning and accumulated drift over long trajectories.

Method: Integrates local FLS frame alignment using Fourier-Mellin transform (FMT) with global trajectory optimization via EKF fusing GPS, IMU, and compass data, plus variance-based image blending.

Result: 9.5% RMSE reduction vs FMT-only baseline; sub-meter reconstruction accuracy with high-resolution textures for oyster inventory estimation.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively reduces drift and enables accurate, high-resolution seabed mapping for aquaculture applications in challenging coastal environments.

Abstract: Accurate seabed mapping is essential for habitat monitoring and infrastructure inspection. In turbid, shallow coastal waters, such as shellfish aquaculture farms, the effectiveness of traditional optical methods is limited. Autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) equipped with forward-looking sonar (FLS) offer a promising alternative. However, existing sonar-based systems face challenges in achieving fine resolution mapping over long trajectories due to low-resolution positioning measurements and accumulated drift over long trajectories. In this paper, we present a drift-resilient seabed mapping framework that integrates local FLS frame alignment using the Fourier-Mellin transform (FMT) with global trajectory optimization based on an extended Kalman filter (EKF) that fuses global positioning system (GPS), inertial measurement unit (IMU), and compass data. A variance-based image blending strategy is used to further reduce visual artifacts in overlapping regions. Field trials on a structured oyster farm site show that our framework helps reduce drift in RMSE by 9.5% relative to the FMT-only baseline. This framework also enables sub-meter reconstruction accuracy and preservation of high-resolution textures needed for oyster inventory estimation within the mapped areas.

</details>


### [443] [Optimized and kinematically feasible multi-agent motion planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01996)
*Anja Hellander,Kristoffer Bergman,Daniel Axehill*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-step method for MAMP that first finds a feasible solution using CBS or PBS, then improves it via optimal control, with application to tractor-trailer systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To find optimized and kinematically feasible solutions for multi-agent motion planning problems in autonomous systems.

Method: Two-step approach: (1) initial feasible solution via CBS or PBS, (2) solution improvement using multi-phase optimal control (OCP) warm-started with the initial solution. Also introduces optimized motion primitive generation under duration constraints.

Result: For tractor-trailer systems: lattice-based planner outperforms SIPP-IP; CBS outperforms PBS with higher success rate and lower runtime, though both yield similar quality after improvement.

Conclusion: The proposed two-step framework effectively finds optimized, kinematically feasible MAMP solutions, with CBS being preferable for tractor-trailer systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent motion planning (MAMP) is an important problem for autonomous systems with multiple agents. In this work we propose a two-step method for finding optimized and kinematically feasible solutions to MAMP problems. The first step finds an initial feasible solution using state-of-the-art methods such as conflict-based search (CBS) or priority-based search (PBS), and the second step is an improvement step which improves the solution by solving a multi-phase optimal control problem (OCP) where the initial solution is used to warm-start the solver. We also propose a method for generating motion primitives in an optimized way under the constraint that the primitive durations are all multiples of the same sample time.
  We evaluate our proposed framework on a MAMP problem for tractor-trailer systems. We extend the safe interval path planning with interval projections (SIPP-IP) algorithm so it can handle more general cost functions and larger agents, but our results show that for the tractor-trailer system a simple lattice-based planner performs better due to less conservative collision checks. Our experiments also indicate that CBS performs better than PBS for this system as it achieves a higher success rate in environments with obstacles and had a lower average runtime, although both planners achieve solutions of similar quality after the improvement step.

</details>


### [444] [Neural Backward Reach-Avoid Tubes with MPC Supervision for High-Dimensional Systems: An Application to Safe Spacecraft Docking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02021)
*Santiago Thorup,Luca Castelletto,Zeyuan Feng,Somil Bansal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based Backward Reach-Avoid Tube (BRAT) framework for autonomous spacecraft docking that combines Hamilton-Jacobi reachability with MPC-supervised neural value function training, achieving better success rates and computational efficiency than existing methods in 6D and 13D settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous spacecraft docking requires collision avoidance and target reachability under coupled 6DOF dynamics. Traditional HJ reachability provides formal guarantees but is limited to low-dimensional systems, while learning-based approaches struggle with tightly coupled reach-avoid settings in docking problems.

Method: A neural approximation of the HJ value function trained with PDE-based losses and curriculum-driven MPC supervision. In deployment, two controllers are used: a value gradient-driven controller and a value-function-augmented terminal MPC for horizon reachability enforcement.

Result: Evaluated on 6D planar docking (against grid-based ground truth) and scaled to full 13D system, the method outperforms existing approaches in both success rate and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The tight integration of HJ structure with MPC supervision enables scalable reach-avoid analysis for high-dimensional docking, bridging formal guarantees and real-time performance.

Abstract: Autonomous spacecraft docking requires control policies that simultaneously ensure collision avoidance and target reachability under coupled, high-dimensional translational-rotational dynamics. Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability provides formal reach-avoid guarantees, but classical solvers are limited to low-dimensional systems. Learning-based approaches have begun to scale HJ analysis, yet they struggle in reach-avoid settings, especially where goal and failure sets are tightly coupled, as in docking. We propose a learning-based Backward Reach-Avoid Tube (BRAT) framework that addresses this challenge by tightly integrating HJ structure with MPC-based supervision. In the offline phase, we train a neural approximation of the HJ value function using PDE-based losses augmented with curriculum-driven MPC supervision, which provides informative value targets and stabilizes training in regions where purely PDE-based methods fail. In the online phase, the learned value function is deployed through two real-time controllers: (i) a value gradient-driven controller, and (ii) a value-function-augmented terminal MPC that explicitly enforces reachability at the horizon. We evaluate the proposed method on a 6D planar docking problem against grid-based ground truth and then scale to the full 13D system. Across both settings, our approach outperforms existing methods in success rate and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [445] [VILAS: A VLA-Integrated Low-cost Architecture with Soft Grasping for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02037)
*Zijian An,Hadi Khezam,Bill Cai,Ran Yang,Shijie Geng,Yiming Feng,Yue,Zheng,Lifeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VILAS is a low-cost robotic manipulation platform integrating a collaborative arm, gripper, and cameras with a ZMQ-based architecture for VLA policy learning. It features a kirigami-based soft gripper for fragile object handling and successfully deploys three VLA models (pi_0, pi_0.5, GR00T N1.6) on grape grasping tasks, showing effective policy learning on affordable hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable end-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) policy learning and deployment on low-cost, modular robotic hardware, making advanced manipulation capabilities more accessible.

Method: VILAS integrates a Fairino FR5 arm, Jodell RG52-50 gripper, dual cameras, and a ZMQ-based architecture for teleoperation, data collection, and policy deployment. A kirigami-based soft compliant gripper extension enables safe manipulation of fragile objects without force sensing. Three VLA models (pi_0, pi_0.5, GR00T N1.6) were fine-tuned from pretrained checkpoints on a demonstration dataset.

Result: Grape grasping experiments validated that all three VLA models successfully learned and deployed manipulation policies on the low-cost modular hardware, providing practical insights into real-world VLA model deployment.

Conclusion: Capable manipulation policies can be successfully trained and deployed on low-cost modular hardware, and the study offers practical insights into real-world characteristics of current VLA models.

Abstract: We present VILAS, a fully low-cost, modular robotic manipulation platform designed to support end-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) policy learning and deployment on accessible hardware. The system integrates a Fairino FR5 collaborative arm, a Jodell RG52-50 electric gripper, and a dual-camera perception module, unified through a ZMQ-based communication architecture that seamlessly coordinates teleoperation, data collection, and policy deployment within a single framework. To enable safe manipulation of fragile objects without relying on explicit force sensing, we design a kirigami-based soft compliant gripper extension that induces predictable deformation under compressive loading, providing gentle and repeatable contact with delicate targets. We deploy and evaluate three state-of-the-art VLA models on the VILAS platform: pi_0, pi_0.5, and GR00T N1.6. All models are fine-tuned from publicly released pretrained checkpoints using an identical demonstration dataset collected via our teleoperation pipeline. Experiments on a grape grasping task validate the effectiveness of the proposed system, confirming that capable manipulation policies can be successfully trained and deployed on low-cost modular hardware. Our results further provide practical insights into the deployment characteristics of current VLA models in real-world settings.

</details>


### [446] [AoI-Aware Multi-Robot Sensing and Transport on Connected Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02107)
*John Tadrous*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper studies AoI minimization in robot networks where mobile robots both sense (with parallel, stochastic delays) and convey data to a base station, deriving lower bounds and optimal resource allocation via convex optimization and shortest-path-tree conveyor architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze and minimize Age of Information (AoI) in mobile robot monitoring networks, where AoI accounts for both stochastic parallel sensing delays and hop-based propagation delays, which has not been jointly modeled before.

Method: Derives per-node and network-wide AoI lower bound decomposing into sensing and propagation terms; minimizes sensing component via discretely convex resource allocation solved by greedy water-filling; constructs shortest-path-tree conveyor architecture with Euler-walk deployment.

Result: The proposed architecture attains the AoI lower bound in a full-conveyor regime; numerical simulations validate the impact of sensing allocation and conveyor deployment on AoI performance.

Conclusion: AoI in multi-robot monitoring can be optimally balanced by separable convex resource allocation for sensing, and the shortest-path-tree conveyor architecture achieves the theoretical lower bound.

Abstract: A team of mobile robots monitors spatially distributed processes and delivers measurements to a base, where AoI is measured from sensing start, capturing both stochastic parallel sensing delays and hop-based propagation. At each non-base node, multiple robots may collaborate, yielding node-dependent geometric group sensing times, while other robots act as mobile conveyors that transport samples along unit-time edges. The paper first derives a per-node and network-wide AoI lower bound that decomposes into a sensing term, determined by mean group sensing times, and a propagation term, given by shortest-path distances. It then shows that minimizing the sensing component yields a separable discretely convex resource allocation problem, solved optimally by a greedy water-filling algorithm. A shortest-path-tree conveyor architecture with an Euler-walk deployment is constructed and proven to attain the lower bound in a full-conveyor regime. Numerical simulations illustrate the impact of sensing allocation and conveyor deployment on AoI performance.

</details>


### [447] [Robotic Desk Organization: A Multi-Primitive Approach to Manipulating Heterogeneous Objects via Environmental Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02135)
*Yi Dong. Yangjun Liu,Jinjun Duan,Yang Li,Zhendong Dai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A task-oriented framework for desktop organization that enables service robots to manipulate both rigid and deformable planar objects using environment-assisted primitives (contact-based grasping, edge-based push-grasping, levering-based grasping) and a dedicated perception pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Desktop organization is challenging for service robots due to heterogeneous objects (rigid and deformable) and diverse manipulation objectives like collection and stacking.

Method: (1) Perception pipeline augmenting existing datasets with uncommon items, enabling geometry-based pose/keypoint estimation and environmental constraint detection (e.g., table edges); (2) Environment-assisted manipulation primitives: contact-based grasping for small objects, edge-based push-grasping for planar rigid objects, levering-based grasping for planar deformable objects; (3) Task planner integrating these primitives for multi-object organization.

Result: Sufficient real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: The research provides practical manipulation primitives for rigid and deformable planar objects, highlighting the importance of environmental and interobject constraints in complex multi-object manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Desktop organization remains challenging for service robots because of heterogeneous objects and diverse manipulation objectives, such as collection and stacking. In this article, a task-oriented framework is presented for organizing planar rigid and deformable objects on desks. A perception pipeline was developed that augments existing datasets with uncommon desktop items and makes geometry-based pose and keypoint estimation possible, along with the detection of environmental constraints, such as table edges. To handle diverse manipulation requirements, environment-assisted primitives are used, including contact-based grasping for small objects, edge-based push-grasping for planar rigid objects, and levering-based grasping for planar deformable objects. These primitives leverage environmental and interobject constraints to improve robustness. A task planner was designed to integrate these primitives into multiobject organization. Sufficient real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework. This research provides practical manipulation primitives for planar rigid and deformable objects, highlighting the role of environmental and interobject constraints in complex multiobject manipulation tasks. Code and video are available online.

</details>


### [448] [Sampling-Based Control via Entropy-Regularized Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02147)
*Vincent Pacelli,Akash Ratheesh,Evangelos A. Theodorou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OT-MPC is a sampling-based MPC algorithm using optimal transport to replace mode-averaging with targeted refinement, achieving better real-time control in complex robotics tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sampling-based MPC methods (MPPI, CEM) suffer from mode-averaging and pathological behaviors in complex cost landscapes because their information-theoretic objectives ignore the geometry of the control problem.

Method: OT-MPC uses entropy-regularized optimal transport to compute an optimal coupling between candidate control sequences and low-cost proposals, then refines candidates toward promising samples via closed-form, gradient-free Sinkhorn updates.

Result: Improved success rates over existing methods on navigation, manipulation, and locomotion tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating optimal transport geometry into sampling-based MPC resolves mode-averaging issues and enhances real-time control performance across diverse robotic tasks.

Abstract: Sampling-based model predictive control methods like MPPI and CEM are essential for real-time control of nonlinear robotic systems, particularly where discontinuous dynamics preclude gradient-based optimization. However, these methods derive from information-theoretic objectives that are agnostic to the geometry of the control problem, leading to pathological behaviors such as mode-averaging when the cost landscape is complex. We present OT-MPC, a sampling-based algorithm that overcomes these limitations through an entropy-regularized optimal transport formulation. By computing an optimal coupling between candidate control sequences and low-cost proposals, OT-MPC refines candidates toward nearby promising samples while coordinating updates across the ensemble to maintain coverage of the solution space. We derive closed-form, gradient-free updates via the Sinkhorn algorithm, enabling real-time performance. Experiments on navigation, manipulation, and locomotion tasks demonstrate improved success rates over existing methods.

</details>


### [449] [Do We Really Need Immediate Resets? Rethinking Collision Handling for Efficient Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02192)
*Shanze Wang,Xinming Zhang,Siwei Cheng,Xianghui Wang,Hailong Huang,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Multi-Collision reset Budget (MCB) framework that allows robots to retry after collisions during training instead of immediately resetting the environment, which improves exploration and navigation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DRL navigation training treats every collision as an immediate task failure with global environment reset, which prevents agents from exploring challenging obstacle configurations and slows early learning.

Method: MCB decouples local collision termination from global environment resets, granting the agent a multi-collision budget to retry difficult configurations within the same episode during training.

Result: Experiments on simulated and real-world robots show MCB accelerates early-stage exploration, improves success rate and navigation efficiency, with small collision budgets yielding the largest gains.

Conclusion: Challenging the standard single-collision reset convention, MCB demonstrates that allowing limited retries during training benefits learning without compromising deployment performance.

Abstract: Should a single collision necessarily terminate an entire navigation episode? In most deep reinforcement learning (DRL) frameworks for robot navigation, this remains the standard practice: every collision immediately triggers a global environment reset and is penalized as a complete task failure. While a collision during deployment naturally indicates task failure, applying the same treatment during training prevents the agent from exploring challenging obstacle configurations, which slows learning progress in the early training phase. In this work, we challenge this convention and propose a Multi-Collision reset Budget (MCB) framework that decouples local collision termination from global environment resets, allowing the agent to retry difficult configurations within the same episode. Experiments on multiple simulated and real-world robotic platforms show that the framework accelerates early-stage exploration and improves both success rate and navigation efficiency over conventional single-collision reset baselines, with a small collision budget producing the largest gains.

</details>


### [450] [Change-Robust Online Spatial-Semantic Topological Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02227)
*Jiaming Wang,Jizhuo Chen,Diwen Liu,Atharva Ghotavadekar,Jiaxuan Da,Linh Kästner,Harold Soh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CROSS replaces metric SLAM with an online topological graph of keyframes and uses sequential hypothesis testing in SE(3) with Gaussian-mixture pose beliefs for change-robust spatial-semantic reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SLAM-based spatial-semantic pipelines degrade under appearance shifts and scene dynamics due to brittle data association and relocalization.

Method: An online, pose-aware topological graph of RGB-D keyframes instead of a globally consistent metric map, with sequential hypothesis testing in continuous SE(3) and bounded Gaussian-mixture belief over poses.

Result: Improved robustness over SLAM-based and topological baselines in object-goal navigation under lighting shifts and furniture rearrangement, while maintaining safety under perceptual aliasing.

Conclusion: CROSS representation offers a more robust alternative to metric SLAM for spatial-semantic reasoning in changing environments.

Abstract: Autonomous robots require change-robust spatial-semantic reasoning: using spatial and semantic knowledge to decide where to go, how to get there, and where the robot is despite environmental change. Existing approaches typically attach semantics to SLAM-built metric maps, but these pipelines are brittle under appearance shifts and scene dynamics, where data association and relocalization degrade. We propose a Change-Robust Online Spatial-Semantic (CROSS) representation that replaces a globally consistent metric substrate with an online, pose-aware topological graph of RGB-D keyframes. The system explicitly reasons over perceptual ambiguity using sequential hypothesis testing in continuous SE(3). Our estimator maintains a bounded Gaussian-mixture belief over poses, enabling principled handling of loop closures and kidnapped-robot events. Experiments under severe appearance change, including real-robot object-goal navigation with lighting shifts and furniture rearrangement, demonstrate improved robustness over SLAM-based and topological baselines while remaining safe under perceptual aliasing.

</details>


### [451] [Exact Higher-Order Derivatives for SE(3) via Analytical/AD Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02252)
*Frank O. Kuehnel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a hybrid analytical/automatic-differentiation (AD) method for rapidly prototyping SE(3) estimation objectives, enabling exact Hessians and higher-order derivatives from a single gradient implementation, achieving ~5x speedup over finite-differencing while requiring only ~70 lines of extra code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fast prototyping of new SE(3) estimation objectives is awkward because existing Lie-group frameworks (GTSAM, Sophus, SymForce, etc.) are optimized for first-order workloads, leaving a gap for compactly obtaining exact Hessians, observed-information matrices, and higher-order derivative tensors needed for Newton steps and covariance estimates.

Method: A hybrid analytical/AD recipe where the practitioner writes the NLL gradient once (generic over scalar type), places the analytical/AD seam at the point-action interface y = Tx, uses closed-form Lie-group Jacobians up to this interface, and applies AD beyond it. The same source is instantiated with ordinary floats for gradients, vector-seeded dual numbers for exact Hessians in one forward-mode pass, and nested dual numbers for higher-order tensors. Also includes a stabilized SO(3)/SE(3) scalar basis to avoid NaNs at origin under seeded AD.

Result: On a 6-DoF, 5-landmark SE(3) NLL benchmark, the seeded-Hessian path is ~5x faster than finite-differencing the AD gradient, matches a nested-AD oracle to machine precision, and adds only ~70 lines of analytical-Jacobian code over an AD-only baseline.

Conclusion: The result is a practical, predictable path from rapidly written SE(3) objectives to exact higher-order derivatives with no finite-difference tuning required.

Abstract: Fast prototyping of new SE(3) estimation objectives remains awkward in practice. Modern Lie-group frameworks -- GTSAM, manif, Sophus, SymForce, Ceres -- target first-order workloads through different code-generation and automatic-differentiation strategies, each optimized for a particular seam between hand-derived geometry and generic differentiation. The remaining gap is a compact, AD-safe path from these first-order primitives to exact Hessians, observed-information matrices, and higher-order derivative tensors: the quantities needed for exact Newton steps, observed-information covariance estimates, and covariance correction.
  This paper presents a hybrid analytical/AD recipe for SE(3) negative log-likelihoods. The practitioner writes the NLL gradient once, generic over a scalar type, and places the analytical/AD seam at the point-action interface y = Tx. Closed-form Lie-group Jacobians are used up to this interface; AD is applied only beyond it. The same source is then instantiated with ordinary floating-point scalars for gradients, vector-seeded dual numbers for exact Hessians in a single forward-mode pass, and nested dual numbers for higher-order derivative tensors. On a representative 6-DoF, 5-landmark SE(3) NLL, the advocated seeded-Hessian path is approximately 5x faster than finite-differencing the AD gradient on this benchmark while matching a nested-AD oracle to machine precision. The implementation adds roughly 70 lines of analytical-Jacobian code over an AD-only baseline. We also identify and fix a removable singularity in the standard SO(3)/SE(3) scalar basis that would otherwise produce NaNs at the origin under seeded AD, and we audit which Lie-group derivative tensors require this stabilized basis. The result is a practical path from rapidly written SE(3) objectives to exact higher-order derivatives, with predictable runtime and no finite-difference tuning.

</details>


### [452] [SAGA: A Robust Self-Attention and Goal-Aware Anchor-based Planner for Safe UAV Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02301)
*Junhao Wei,Yanxiao Li,Dexing Yao,Yifu Zhao,Haochen Li,Qibin He,Baili Lu,Xiaofan Zou,Dingcheng Yang,Sio-Kei Im,Yapeng Wang,Xu Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SAGA is a self-attention and goal-aware anchor-based planner for agile UAV navigation that formulates local planning as a one-stage regression-and-ranking problem over motion anchors, achieving 100% success rate across various speed settings and outperforming existing planners like YOPO, Ego-planner, and Fast-planner in cluttered environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agile UAV navigation in cluttered environments requires a planner that is both computationally efficient and structurally expressive enough to reason over multiple feasible motions, which existing methods struggle to achieve.

Method: SAGA formulates local planning as a one-stage joint regression-and-ranking problem over a fixed lattice of motion anchors, transforms anchor-aligned features into geometry-aware tokens with cross-anchor global reasoning via self-attention, introduces polar positional encoding (PPE) from anchor yaw/pitch, and uses a goal-aware modulation module to inject velocity, acceleration, and target information.

Result: SAGA achieves 100% success rate at 2.0, 3.0, and 4.0 m/s in cluttered pillar-map environments, while YOPO drops from 90.91% to 62.50%, Ego-planner from 71.43% to 52.63%, and Fast-planner from 52.63% to 38.46%. At 4.0 m/s, SAGA improves average safety from 1.9843m to 2.3888m and reduces flight time from 40.46s to 27.49s over YOPO.

Conclusion: Explicit polar positional encoding is critical for stable cross-anchor reasoning and safe passage selection in cluttered scenes, and SAGA's self-attention and goal-aware design enables robust, safe, and efficient UAV navigation at high speeds.

Abstract: Agile unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) navigation in cluttered environments demands a planning architecture that is both computationally efficient and structurally expressive enough to reason over multiple feasible motions. This paper presents SAGA, a robust self-attention and goal-aware anchor-based planner for safe UAV autonomous navigation. SAGA formulates local planning as a one-stage joint regression-and-ranking problem over a fixed lattice of motion anchors. Given a depth image and a body-frame motion state, the planner predicts refined terminal states and planning scores for all anchors in a single forward pass, after which the best candidate is decoded into a dynamically feasible trajectory. The key idea of SAGA is to transform anchor-aligned features into geometry-aware tokens and perform cross-anchor global reasoning with self-attention. To preserve directional structure in the token space, we further introduce a polar positional encoding derived from anchor yaw and pitch. In addition, a goal-aware modulation module injects velocity, acceleration, and target information into the token representation before final score prediction. Experiments in cluttered pillar-map environments under maximum speed settings of 2.0, 3.0, and 4.0~m/s show that SAGA consistently achieves a 100\% success rate, while YOPO drops from 90.91\% to 62.50\%, Ego-planner from 71.43\% to 52.63\%, and Fast-planner from 52.63\% to 38.46\%. Under the 4.0~m/s maximum speed setting, SAGA also improves average safety from 1.9843~m to 2.3888~m and minimum safety from 0.4390~m to 0.7576~m over YOPO, while reducing total flight time from 40.4631~s to 27.4901~s. The comparison with SAGA w/o PPE further shows that explicit polar positional encoding is critical for stable cross-anchor reasoning and safe passage selection in cluttered scenes.

</details>


### [453] [Natural Gradient Bayesian Filtering: Geometry-Aware Filter for Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02306)
*Chang Liu,Wenhan Cao,Zeju Sun,Tianyi Zhang,Jiayu Yuan,Yi Zeng,Ting Yuan,Yao Lyu,Wei Wu,Stephen Shing-Toung Yau,Shengbo Eben Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This tutorial presents a geometry-aware Gaussian filtering approach called the Natural Gradient Gaussian Approximation (NANO) filter, which uses natural gradient descent on the statistical manifold of Gaussian distributions to perform prediction and measurement updates, and shows that a single natural-gradient step recovers the classical Kalman update for linear-Gaussian cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian filtering is essential for state estimation in complex systems like aerospace, but exact solutions only exist for linear Gaussian models. Nonlinear systems require approximations, and existing Gaussian filters (e.g., EKF, UKF) are widely used but lack geometric awareness of the underlying statistical manifold.

Method: The paper reinterprets Gaussian filtering from an information-geometric perspective, treating prediction and measurement updates as inference procedures over state distributions. The NANO filter applies natural gradient descent on the statistical manifold of Gaussian distributions to iteratively refine the posterior mean and covariance while preserving positive definiteness of the covariance matrix.

Result: The NANO filter is shown to have fundamental connections to classical Kalman filtering: a single natural-gradient step exactly recovers the Kalman measurement update in the linear-Gaussian case. The approach is validated through case studies in satellite attitude estimation, SLAM, and state estimation for quadruped and humanoid robots.

Conclusion: The information-geometric perspective offers a principled framework for Gaussian filtering that respects the intrinsic geometry of the Gaussian family, with theoretical connections to classical methods and practical applicability to nonlinear estimation problems in robotics and aerospace.

Abstract: Bayesian filtering is a cornerstone of state estimation in complex systems such as aerospace systems, yet exact solutions are available only for linear Gaussian models. In practice,nonlinear systems are handled through tractable approximations,with Gaussian filters such as the extended and unscented Kalman filters being among the most widely used methods. This tutorial revisits Gaussian filtering from an information-geometric perspective, viewing the prediction and measurement update steps as inference procedures over state distributions. Within this framework, we introduce a geometry-aware Gaussian filtering approach that leverages natural gradient descent on the statistical manifold of Gaussian distributions. The resulting Natural Gradient Gaussian Approximation (NANO) filter iteratively refines the posterior mean and covariance while respecting the intrinsic geometry of the Gaussian family and preserving the positive definiteness of the covariance matrix. We further highlight fundamental connections to the classical Kalman filtering, showing that a single natural-gradient step exactly recovers the Kalman measurement update in the linear-Gaussian case. The practical implications of the proposed framework are illustrated through case studies in representative nonlinear estimation problems,including satellite attitude estimation, simultaneous localization and mapping, and state estimation for robotic systems including quadruped and humanoid robots.

</details>


### [454] [ShapeGrasp: Simultaneous Visuo-Haptic Shape Completion and Grasping for Improved Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02347)
*Lukas Rustler,Matej Hoffmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ShapeGrasp is a robotic grasp-and-complete pipeline that iteratively refines 3D object shape using tactile and proprioceptive feedback from grasp attempts, achieving 84-91% grasp success rates with improved shape reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans grasp unfamiliar objects by integrating initial visual estimates with tactile and proprioceptive feedback during interaction; the authors aim to implement this biologically-inspired approach in robotics to improve grasping of unknown objects.

Method: ShapeGrasp uses an iterative pipeline: (1) infers complete 3D shape from a single RGB-D view via implicit surface visuo-haptic shape completion, (2) generates candidate grasps via rigid-body simulation, (3) executes the best feasible grasp, (4) fuses tactile contacts and gripper occupancy data to update shape, and (5) re-estimates pose and regrasps upon failure.

Result: Achieved 84% grasp success with a three-finger gripper and 91% with a two-finger gripper in real-world experiments, outperforming baselines, while also improving 3D shape reconstruction quality across all evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: ShapeGrasp is the first approach to update shape representations following real-world grasps, demonstrating that iterative visuo-haptic feedback significantly improves both grasping performance and shape reconstruction quality on unfamiliar objects.

Abstract: Humans grasp unfamiliar objects by combining an initial visual estimate with tactile and proprioceptive feedback during interaction. We present ShapeGrasp, a robotic implementation of this approach. The proposed method is an iterative grasp-and-complete pipeline that couples implicit surface visuo-haptic shape completion (creation of full 3D shape from partial information) with physics-based grasp planning. From a single RGB-D view, ShapeGrasp infers a complete shape (point cloud or triangular mesh), generates candidate grasps via rigid-body simulation, and executes the best feasible grasp. Each grasp attempt yields additional geometric constraints -- tactile surface contacts and space occupied by the gripper body -- which are fused to update the object shape. Failures trigger pose re-estimation and regrasping using the refined shape. We evaluate ShapeGrasp in the real world using two different robots and grippers. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first approach that updates shape representations following a real-world grasp. We achieved superior results over baselines for both grippers (grasp success rate of 84% with a three-finger gripper and 91% with a two-finger gripper), while improving the 3D shape reconstruction quality in all evaluation metrics used.

</details>


### [455] [Feedback Motion Planning for Stochastic Nonlinear Systems with Signal Temporal Logic Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02361)
*Liqian Ma,Zishun Liu,Glen Chou,Yongxin Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework for feedback motion planning under signal temporal logic (STL) specifications for stochastic nonlinear systems, using predicate erosion and probabilistic reachable tubes to ensure high-probability satisfaction, demonstrated on robots including a real quadruped.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe and verifiable motion planning for stochastic nonlinear systems under temporal logic constraints, addressing the intractability of chance-constrained STL problems.

Method: Predicate erosion strategy to convert stochastic STL problem into deterministic one with tightened constraints; probabilistic reachable tubes (PRT) derived via contraction theory and feedback control to bound stochastic deviation from nominal trajectories.

Result: Validated on simulated robotic systems and real-world quadrupedal robot; less conservative and higher satisfaction probability compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables high-probability STL satisfaction for stochastic nonlinear systems with practical effectiveness on real robots.

Abstract: We study feedback motion planning for continuous-time stochastic nonlinear systems under signal temporal logic (STL) specifications. We propose a framework that synthesizes control policies for chance-constrained STL trajectory optimization problems, with the goal of ensuring that the closed-loop stochastic system satisfies a given STL formula with high probability (e.g., 99.99\%). Our approach is based on a predicate erosion strategy that transforms the intractable stochastic problem into a deterministic STL trajectory optimization problem with tightened STL formula constraints. The amount of erosion is determined by a probabilistic reachable tube (PRT) that bounds the deviation between the stochastic trajectory and an associated nominal trajectory. To compute such bounds, we leverage contraction theory and feedback design, and develop several tracking controllers. This yields a complete feedback motion planning pipeline which can be implemented by numerical optimizations. We demonstrate the efficacy and versatility of the proposed framework through simulations on several robotic systems and through experiments on a real-world quadrupedal robot, and show that it is less conservative and achieves higher specification satisfaction probability than representative baselines.

</details>


### [456] [Robust Adaptive Predictive Control for Hook-Based Aerial Transportation Between Moving Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02370)
*Péter Antal,Andrea Carron,Melanie Zeilinger,Roland Tóth,Tamás Péni*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robust adaptive MPC using a MuJoCo digital twin model and EKF-based parameter estimation enables autonomous pick-and-place between moving platforms with a hook-equipped aerial manipulator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable autonomous pick-and-place operations between moving platforms using a hook-equipped aerial manipulator, which requires handling complex dynamics, aerodynamic uncertainties, and uncertain payloads.

Method: Digital twin modeling in MuJoCo for predictive dynamics, robust adaptive MPC with zero-order robust optimization (zoRO) for uncertainty propagation, and extended Kalman filter (EKF) for online parameter estimation.

Result: Effectiveness validated in complex simulated scenarios and real-world flight experiments.

Conclusion: The proposed approach achieves robust constraint satisfaction, high performance, and computational efficiency for aerial pick-and-place tasks.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel model predictive control (MPC) approach for autonomous pick-and-place between moving platforms with a hook-equipped aerial manipulator. First, for accurate and rapid modeling of the complex dynamics, a digital twin model of the quadcopter equipped with a hook-based gripper, implemented in MuJoCo, is constructed and used as the predictive model for the MPC. To handle uncertainties of the predictive model (e.g. due to aerodynamics and uncertain payloads), a robust adaptive MPC approach is proposed. By systematic integration of zero-order robust optimization (zoRO) based uncertainty propagation and an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for parameter estimation, the MPC algorithm ensures robust constraint satisfaction, high performance, and computational efficiency. The effectiveness of the proposed method is evaluated in complex simulated scenarios and in real-world flight experiments.

</details>


### [457] [Shared Autonomy Assisted by Impedance-Driven Anisotropic Guidance Field](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02410)
*Sihan Chen,Hang Xu,Yupu Lu,Chen Wang,Benfang Duan,Ruixing Jia,Jia Pan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes Impedance-Driven Anisotropic Guidance Field Enhanced Shared Autonomy (IAGF-SA), a novel paradigm that enables embodied, physically-grounded communication of robot intent through adaptive impedance control, improving human-robot collaboration without needing additional interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current shared autonomy research focuses on robots inferring human intent but overlooks whether humans can understand robot intent in return, leading to reduced collaboration effectiveness. Existing solutions using additional interfaces are unintuitive and limited.

Method: IAGF-SA extends shared autonomy with an embodied communication channel using impedance control. It adaptively modulates the robot's dynamic response to human input, creating an anisotropic guidance field that intuitively and continuously communicates robot intent while physically guiding human actions.

Result: User studies across three scenarios and two teleoperation interfaces show IAGF-SA improves task performance, human-robot agreement, and subjective experience.

Conclusion: IAGF-SA effectively enhances human-robot communication and collaboration by establishing mutual understanding through physically-grounded, intuitive intent communication without requiring additional interfaces.

Abstract: Shared autonomy (SA) enables robots to infer human intent and assist in its achievement. While most research focuses on improving intent inference, it overlooks whether humans can understand the robot's intent in return. Without such mutual understanding, collaboration becomes less effective, degrading user experience and task performance. To address this gap, previous studies have explicitly conveyed the robot intent through additional interfaces, which remain unintuitive and limited in expressiveness. Inspired by impedance control, we propose Impedance-Driven Anisotropic Guidance Field Enhanced Shared Autonomy (IAGF-SA), a novel paradigm that extends SA with an embodied, physically-grounded communication channel. This channel adaptively modulates the robot's dynamic response to human input, enabling intuitive, continuous, physically-grounded robot intent communication while naturally guiding human actions. User studies across three scenarios and two teleoperation interfaces indicate that IAGF-SA improves task performance, human-robot agreement, and subjective experience, thus demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing human-robot communication and collaboration.

</details>


### [458] [Higher-Order Flexible Configurations of Planar Parallel Manipulators Constructed by Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02434)
*Yudi Zhao,Georg Nawratil*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper studies singular configurations of planar 3-RPR parallel manipulators by analyzing solution pairs from the direct kinematic problem, using input parametrization and relative orientation to increase flexion order of averaged configurations, with visualization and potential extension to spherical/spatial manipulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate singular configurations arising from averaging technique applied to solution pairs of the direct kinematic problem for planar 3-RPR parallel manipulators.

Method: Parametrizing input pairs and determining their relative orientation without computing polynomial zeros, to increase the flexion order of averaged configurations.

Result: Visualized results for concrete examples demonstrating the methodology's effectiveness.

Conclusion: The methodology extends to spherical and spatial analogues of planar 3-RPR parallel manipulators.

Abstract: This paper investigates singular configurations of planar 3-RPR parallel manipulators, which result from applying the averaging technique to solution pairs of their direct kinematic problem. Without computing the zeros of the corresponding degree 6 polynomial we parametrize the input pairs and determine their relative orientation in a way that the flexion order of the averaged configurations increases. Moreover, the obtained results are visualized for concrete examples. The presented methodology can also be used for studying the spherical and spatial analogues of planar 3-RPR parallel manipulators.

</details>


### [459] [Visibility-Aware Mobile Grasping in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02487)
*Tianrun Hu,Anxing Xiao,David Hsu,Hanbo Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified mobile grasping system with whole-body planning and active perception for safe manipulation in unknown dynamic environments under limited FOV, achieving 68.8% and 58.0% success rates in static and dynamic settings, outperforming baselines by ~20%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile grasping in dynamic, unknown environments with limited field-of-view faces a fundamental trade-off between perception (seeing) and action (moving), while prior work assumes known/static settings or decouples these objectives, risking collisions from unobserved dynamic obstacles.

Method: The system includes (1) an iterative low-level whole-body planner with velocity-aware active perception for safe navigation, and (2) a hierarchical high-level planner using behavior trees to adaptively generate subgoals for exploration and handling runtime failures.

Result: Achieves 68.8% and 58.0% success rates in unknown static and dynamic environments respectively, with 22.8% and 18.0% improvements over the baseline approach, plus improved collision safety.

Conclusion: The unified approach effectively balances perception and action for safe mobile grasping under limited FOV, validated across 400 simulated scenarios and real-world deployment on a Fetch robot.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of mobile grasping in dynamic, unknown environments where a robot must operate under a limited field-of-view. The fundamental challenge is the inherent trade-off between ``seeing'' around to reduce environmental uncertainty and ``moving'' the body to achieve task progress in a high-dimensional configuration space, subject to visibility constraints. Previous approaches often assume known or static environments and decouple these objectives, failing to guarantee safety when unobserved dynamic obstacles intersect the robot's path during manipulation. In this paper, we propose a unified mobile grasping system comprising two core components: (1) an iterative low-level whole-body planner coupled with velocity-aware active perception to navigate dynamic environments safely; and (2) a hierarchical high-level planner based on behavior trees that adaptively generates subgoals to guide the robot through exploration and runtime failures. We provide experimental results across 400 randomized simulation scenarios and real-world deployment on a Fetch mobile manipulator. Results show that our system achieves a success rate of 68.8\% and 58.0\% in unknown static and dynamic environments, respectively, significantly boosting success rates by 22.8\% and 18.0\% over the \nam approach in both unknown static and dynamic environments, with improved collision safety.

</details>


### [460] [Adaptive Gait Generation for Multi-Terrain Exoskeletons via Constrained Kernelized Movement Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02513)
*Edoardo Trombin,Miroljub Mihailovic,Matheus Henrique Ferreira Moura,Luca Tonin,Emanuele Menegatti,Stefano Tortora*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Kernelized Movement Primitives (KMP)-based adaptive gait generation framework for lower limb exoskeletons that learns from human demonstrations and adapts to various indoor terrains (flat ground, slopes, stairs, obstacles) using RGB-D camera environmental information, validated in both simulation and real-world experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current lower limb exoskeletons lack effective adaptive gait planning for real-world environments and are mostly limited to flat, even terrain. There is an open challenge in generating environment-aware, physiologically consistent gait trajectories in real-time.

Method: A KMP-based framework that learns probabilistic gait representations from limited human demonstrations in joint and task spaces, then adapts trajectories using environmental information from an onboard RGB-D camera by treating the adaptive gait generation as a linearly constrained optimization problem with via-points.

Result: The method was validated in simulations across multiple scenarios (flat-ground walking, slopes, stairs, obstacle crossing) and demonstrated effectiveness and robustness on a commercial LLE in real-world experiments.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility of an environment-aware gait planning system for intelligent lower limb exoskeletons to assist people with disabilities in daily life.

Abstract: Lower limb exoskeletons (LLEs) present the potential to make motor-impaired individuals walk again. Their application in real-world environments is still limited by the lack of effective adaptive gait planning. Indeed, current exoskeletons are meant to walk only on a flat and even terrain. Generating environment-aware, physiologically consistent gait trajectories in real-time is an open challenge. To overcome this, we propose a novel Kernelized Movement Primitives (KMP)-based framework for adaptive gait generation (AGG) across multiple indoor terrains. The proposed approach learns a probabilistic representation of human gait in both the joint and task spaces from a limited number of human demonstrations, representing natural gait characteristics and ensuring kinematic feasibility. In addition, the learned trajectories are adapted using environmental information extracted from an onboard RGB-D camera by treating the AGG as a linearly constrained optimization problem with via-points. The proposed method has been thoroughly validated first in simulations for gait generation in different scenarios, such as flat-ground walking, slopes, stairs, and obstacles crossing. Finally, the effectiveness and robustness of the method have been demonstrated with experiments on a commercial LLE in real-world scenarios. The results obtained demonstrate the feasibility of an environment-aware gait planning system for a new generation of intelligent lower limb exoskeletons for assisting people with disabilities in their every-day life.

</details>


### [461] [A Semantic Autonomy Framework for VLM-Integrated Indoor Mobile Robots: Hybrid Deterministic Reasoning and Cross-Robot Adaptive Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02525)
*Bogdan Felician Abaza,Andrei-Alexandru Staicu,Cristian Vasile Doicin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents the Semantic Autonomy Stack, a six-layer framework enabling indoor mobile robots to interpret natural language instructions. It features a hybrid deterministic-VLM reasoning system where 88% of instructions are resolved in under 0.1ms without invoking a language model, and a five-category semantic memory system enabling cross-robot knowledge transfer with 103,000-fold latency reduction. Validated on Raspberry Pi 5 robots with 100% accuracy across 82 scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous navigation frameworks (e.g., ROS 2 Navigation 2) can navigate to metric coordinates but cannot interpret natural language instructions expressing human intent. Vision-Language Models offer semantic reasoning but suffer from high inference latency (2-9 seconds per decision) and lack cross-session memory, limiting practical robot deployment.

Method: A six-layer Semantic Autonomy Stack with: (1) a seven-step parametric resolver handling 88% of instructions deterministically in under 0.1ms without VLM/GPU/camera; (2) a five-category semantic memory framework with explicit scope taxonomy (global environment, per-operator preferences, per-robot capabilities) enabling cross-session learning and cross-robot transfer via shared compiled digests; (3) hybrid deterministic-VLM reasoning where only genuinely ambiguous instructions escalate to VLM inference.

Result: 88% of instructions resolved in under 0.1ms without VLM; 103,000-fold latency reduction on transferred knowledge; 100% semantic transfer accuracy (33/33, 95% CI [0.894, 1.000]); 100% semantic resolution accuracy; concurrent multi-robot operation feasible on Raspberry Pi 5 with no GPU and zero training data.

Conclusion: The Semantic Autonomy Stack effectively bridges the gap between metric navigation frameworks and natural language understanding by combining deterministic resolution for routine instructions with VLM reasoning for ambiguous cases, while enabling persistent cross-robot memory. This enables practical, cost-effective semantically autonomous indoor navigation on low-cost edge hardware.

Abstract: Autonomous indoor mobile robots can navigate reliably to metric coordinates using established frameworks such as ROS 2 Navigation 2, yet they lack the ability to interpret natural language instructions that express intent rather than positions. Vision-Language Models offer the semantic reasoning required to bridge this gap, but their inference latency (2-9 seconds per decision on consumer hardware) and session-by-session amnesia limit practical deployment. This paper presents the Semantic Autonomy Stack, a six-layer reference framework for semantically autonomous indoor navigation, and validates a complete instance featuring hybrid deterministic-VLM reasoning and cross-robot adaptive memory on physical robots with off-the-shelf edge hardware. A seven-step parametric resolver handles 88% of instructions in under 0.1 milliseconds without invoking a language model, camera, or GPU; only genuinely ambiguous instructions escalate to VLM reasoning. A five-category semantic memory framework with explicit scope taxonomy (global environment knowledge, per-operator preferences, per-robot capabilities) enables cross-session learning and cross-robot knowledge transfer: preferences learned through VLM interactions on one robot are promoted to deterministic resolution and transferred to a second robot via a shared compiled digest, achieving a measured latency reduction of 103,000-fold. Experimental validation on two custom-built differential-drive robots across 82 scenario-level decisions and three sessions demonstrates 100% semantic transfer accuracy (33/33, 95% CI [0.894, 1.000]), 100% semantic resolution accuracy, and concurrent multi-robot operation feasibility - all on Raspberry Pi 5 platforms with no onboard GPU, requiring zero training data.

</details>


### [462] [Beyond Specialization: Robust Reinforcement Learning Navigation via Procedural Map Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02528)
*Christian Jestel,Nicolas Bach,Marvin Wiedemann,Jan Finke,Peter Detzner*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper investigates how different procedural map generators affect DRL navigation policy generalization, finding that training on a combined generator set achieves 91.5% success, A* subgoal inputs are the dominant robustness factor (98.9%), and learned speed adaptation outperforms classical controllers at higher speeds.


<details>
