  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DRL navigation policies overfit to training environments because manual design limits diversity. The authors aim to systematically compare how different procedural map generators affect policy generalization, which has not been done before.

Method: Four procedural generators (sparse, maze, graph, Wave Function Collapse) with guaranteed navigability are integrated into MuRoSim, a 2D LiDAR navigation simulator. Five DRL policies are cross-evaluated on 1000 seeded maps per generator across three training seeds. A* path-planner subgoal inputs and GRU recurrence are tested as architectural improvements.

Result: Cross-generator transfer is strongly asymmetric (sparse-trained policy drops to 3.3% on mazes). Combined training achieves 91.5±1.1% success. A* subgoal inputs raise success to 98.9±0.4%, outperforming GRU recurrence. DRL policies outperform classical Carrot+A* controller at high speeds (24.9% vs DRL at 2.0 m/s). Real-world RoboMaster experiments confirm sim-to-real transfer.

Conclusion: Training on diverse procedural generators significantly improves generalization. A* subgoal conditioning is the most critical architectural choice for robustness. Learned speed adaptation gives DRL a decisive advantage over classical controllers at higher speeds. Recurrence helps mitigate failure modes in complex mazes during real-world deployment.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) navigation policies often overfit to the structure of their training environments, as environmental diversity is typically constrained by the manual effort required to design diverse scenarios. While procedural map generation offers scalable diversity, no prior work systematically compares how different generator types affect policy generalization. We integrate four generators (sparse, maze, graph, and Wave Function Collapse) with guaranteed navigability into MuRoSim, a 2D simulator focusing on training efficiency for LiDAR-based navigation. We cross-evaluate five navigation policies on 1000 seeded maps per generator across three training seeds. Results show a strongly asymmetric cross-generator transfer: a specialist trained on sparse layouts falls to 3.3% success on mazes, whereas a policy trained on the combined generator set achieves 91.5 +/- 1.1% mean success. We further demonstrate that A* path-planner subgoal inputs are the dominant factor for robustness, raising success from the 90.2 +/- 1.4% feedforward baseline to 98.9 +/- 0.4% and outperforming GRU recurrence, which only improves the reactive baseline. The DRL policies outperform a classical Carrot+A* controller, which matches their success only at low speeds (1.0 m/s) but collapses to 24.9% at 2.0 m/s. This highlights learned speed adaptation as the decisive advantage of the learned approach. Real-world experiments on a RoboMaster confirm sim-to-real transfer in a cluttered arena, while a maze-like layout exposes remaining failure modes that recurrence helps mitigate.

</details>


### [463] [Sim-to-Real Transfer and Robustness Evaluation of Reinforcement Learning Control with Integrated Perception on an ASV for Floating Waste Capture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02529)
*Luis F. W. Batista,Stéphanie Aravecchia,Cédric Pradalier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A field-validated system combining camera-based polarimetric perception and lightweight DRL controller for floating-waste detection and capture on autonomous surface vessels, with a sim-to-real testing methodology enabling reproducible field trials across 14 disturbance regimes, achieving centimeter-level accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous surface vessels for waste removal face challenges from varying hydrodynamics, external disturbances, and difficult water-surface perception, requiring robust sim-to-real transfer methods.

Method: Combines camera-based polarimetric perception with a lightweight DRL controller trained entirely in simulation; uses a two-stage simulation protocol with a perception abstraction module mimicking real camera behavior; tested across 14 disturbance regimes in matched simulation and field experiments.

Result: Centimeter-level terminal accuracy; robust control performance under evaluated perturbations; main degradation source is insufficient actuation-model fidelity; demonstrated in search-and-capture over areas up to 450 m² using real camera detections.

Conclusion: Practical lessons include improved actuation-model fidelity, targeted domain randomization, and careful management of latency and timestamps across modules; remaining challenges highlighted.

Abstract: Autonomous surface vessels for floating-waste removal operate under varying hydrodynamics, external disturbances, and challenging water-surface perception. We present a field-validated system that combines camera-based polarimetric perception with a lightweight DRL-based controller for floating-waste detection and capture. Camera detections are converted into water-surface target points and tracked by a controller trained entirely in simulation and deployed directly on a retrofitted ASV platform. Our main contribution is a sim-to-real testing methodology that combines a two-stage simulation protocol with a perception abstraction module designed to mimic real camera behavior, enabling reproducible field trials and explicit evaluation of the sim-to-real gap. We apply this framework in matched simulation and field experiments across 14 disturbance regimes to expose failure modes and evaluate robustness. The results show centimeter-level terminal accuracy and indicate robust control performance under the evaluated perturbation regimes. The main source of degradation is insufficient actuation-model fidelity. We also demonstrate the system in a search-and-capture application using real camera detections in real-world conditions over areas of up to $450~m^2$. The study distills practical lessons for reliable transfer, including improved actuation-model fidelity, targeted domain randomization, and careful management of latency and timestamps across modules, while highlighting remaining challenges.

</details>


### [464] [Orchestrating Spatial Semantics via a Zone-Graph Paradigm for Intricate Indoor Scene Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02537)
*Meisheng Zhang,Shizhao Sun,Yang Zhao,Ziyuan Liu,Zhijun Gao,Jiang Bian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ZoneMaestro is a unified framework for autonomous 3D indoor scene synthesis that uses zone-graph orchestration instead of object-centric synthesis, achieving robust scene generation in complex, non-convex rooms through a novel zone-based logic, a new dataset (Zone-Scene-10K), and an Alternating Alignment Strategy with Z-GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods fail in non-convex rooms with tightly coupled spatial constraints because data-driven generators lack topological priors for long-horizon planning, and iterative agents fragment semantics and become geometrically brittle.

Method: ZoneMaestro introduces Zone-Graph Orchestration with zone-based logic, a large-scale Zone-Scene-10K dataset with Zone-Graph annotations, and an Alternating Alignment Strategy that cycles between reasoning internalization and Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (Z-GRPO) without external physics engines.

Result: ZoneMaestro resolves the density-safety dichotomy and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both structural coherence and intent adherence on the SCALE benchmark.

Conclusion: Zone-Graph Orchestration is an effective paradigm shift for 3D indoor scene synthesis in irregular, densely constrained environments, validated through both the proposed dataset and stress-test benchmark.

Abstract: Autonomous 3D indoor scene synthesis breaks down in non-convex rooms with tightly coupled spatial constraints. Data-driven generators lack topological priors for long-horizon planning, while iterative agents fragment semantics and become geometrically brittle. We present ZoneMaestro, a unified framework that shifts the paradigm from object-centric synthesis to Zone-Graph Orchestration. By internalizing a novel zone-based logic, ZoneMaestro translates high-level semantic intent into functional zones and topological constraints, enabling robust adaptation to diverse architectural forms. To support this, we construct Zone-Scene-10K, a large-scale dataset enriched with explicit Zone-Graph annotations. We further introduce an Alternating Alignment Strategy that cycles between reasoning internalization and Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (Z-GRPO), effectively reconciling the tension between semantic richness and geometric validity without relying on external physics engines. To rigorously evaluate spatial intelligence beyond convex primitives, we formally define the task of Intricate Spatial Orchestration and release SCALE, a stress-test benchmark for irregular indoor scenarios with complex, dense spatial relations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ZoneMaestro resolves the density-safety dichotomy, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both structural coherence and intent adherence.

</details>


### [465] [CoRAL: Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based Control for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02600)
*Berk Çiçek,Mert K. Er,Özgür S. Öğüz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoRAL is a modular framework that uses LLMs as cost designers (not direct controllers) and VLMs for semantic priors, combined with online system identification and retrieval-based memory, to enable zero-shot contact-rich manipulation, achieving >50% success rate improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs/VLMs lack explicit physical grounding and adaptive control for contact-rich manipulation; existing black-box policies cannot handle the ambiguity of physical parameters from visual data or dynamic contact requirements.

Method: CoRAL decouples high-level reasoning (LLM as cost designer for MPPI) from low-level control. It uses a neuro-symbolic adaptation loop: VLM provides semantic priors (mass/friction) → online system identification refines them → LLM iteratively modulates cost-function structure based on interaction feedback. Includes retrieval-based memory for reusing successful strategies.

Result: Validated on simulation and real hardware (e.g., flipping objects against walls). Outperforms VLA and foundation-model baselines by >50% average success rate in unseen contact-rich scenarios, effectively handling sim-to-real gaps.

Conclusion: The hierarchical decoupling of semantic reasoning from reactive execution bridges the gap between slow LLM inference and dynamic contact requirements, enabling robust zero-shot generalization in contact-rich manipulation.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in high-level reasoning and semantic understanding, applying them directly to contact-rich manipulation remains a challenge due to their lack of explicit physical grounding and inability to perform adaptive control. To bridge this gap, we propose CoRAL (Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based control), a modular framework that enables zero-shot planning by decoupling high-level reasoning from low-level control. Unlike black-box policies, CoRAL uses LLMs not as direct controllers, but as cost designers that synthesize context-aware objective functions for a sampling-based motion planner (MPPI). To address the ambiguity of physical parameters in visual data, we introduce a neuro-symbolic adaptation loop: a VLM provides semantic priors for environmental dynamics, such as mass and friction estimates, which are then explicitly refined in real time via online system identification, while the LLM iteratively modulates the cost-function structure to correct strategic errors based on interaction feedback. Furthermore, a retrieval-based memory unit allows the system to reuse successful strategies across recurrent tasks. This hierarchical architecture ensures real-time control stability by decoupling high-level semantic reasoning from reactive execution, effectively bridging the gap between slow LLM inference and dynamic contact requirements. We validate CoRAL on both simulation and real-world hardware across challenging and novel tasks, such as flipping objects against walls by leveraging extrinsic contacts. Experiments demonstrate that CoRAL outperforms state-of-the-art VLA and foundation-model-based planner baselines by boosting success rates over 50% on average in unseen contact-rich scenarios, effectively handling sim-to-real gaps through its adaptive physical understanding.

</details>


### [466] [AnchorD: Metric Grounding of Monocular Depth Using Factor Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02667)
*Simon Dorer,Martin Büchner,Nick Heppert,Abhinav Valada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A training-free depth grounding framework (AnchorDepth) that aligns monocular depth estimation priors with raw sensor depth via factor graph optimization, improving metric accuracy on challenging non-Lambertian surfaces without retraining. Also introduces a benchmark dataset with dense ground truth for such surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Depth sensors struggle on transparent, specular, and non-Lambertian surfaces. Monocular depth estimation provides structural priors but lacks metric accuracy. Existing methods require retraining or rely on limited CAD-based annotations.

Method: Training-free factor graph optimization framework that performs patch-wise affine alignment, anchoring monocular depth foundation model predictions to raw sensor depth while preserving geometric structure and discontinuities.

Result: Consistent depth performance improvements across diverse sensors and domains without any (re-)training. A benchmark dataset with dense scene-wide ground truth (via matte reflection spray + multi-camera fusion) is introduced.

Conclusion: The proposed grounding framework effectively bridges monocular depth priors and raw sensor depth, providing metric-accurate dense depth estimation for robotics without requiring retraining.

Abstract: Dense and accurate depth estimation is essential for robotic manipulation, grasping, and navigation, yet currently available depth sensors are prone to errors on transparent, specular, and general non-Lambertian surfaces. To mitigate these errors, large-scale monocular depth estimation approaches provide strong structural priors, but their predictions can be potentially skewed or mis-scaled in metric units, limiting their direct use in robotics. Thus, in this work, we propose a training-free depth grounding framework that anchors monocular depth estimation priors from a depth foundation model in raw sensor depth through factor graph optimization. Our method performs a patch-wise affine alignment, locally grounding monocular predictions in metric real-world depth while preserving fine-grained geometric structure and discontinuities. To facilitate evaluation in challenging real-world conditions, we introduce a benchmark dataset with dense scene-wide ground truth depth in the presence of non-Lambertian objects. Ground truth is obtained via matte reflection spray and multi-camera fusion, overcoming the reliance on object-only CAD-based annotations used in prior datasets. Extensive evaluations across diverse sensors and domains demonstrate consistent improvements in depth performance without any (re-)training. We make our implementation publicly available at https://anchord.cs.uni-freiburg.de.

</details>


### [467] [Learning Equivariant Neural-Augmented Object Dynamics From Few Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02699)
*Sergio Orozco,Tushar Kusnur,Brandon May,George Konidaris,Laura Herlant*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PIEGraph combines a physically informed spring-mass analytical model with an equivariant graph neural network to learn object dynamics for both rigid and deformable objects using limited real-world interaction data, outperforming baselines in robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning data-efficient object dynamics models for robotic manipulation remains challenging, especially for deformable objects. Existing approaches require large amounts of interaction data and fail to maintain physical feasibility over long horizons.

Method: PIEGraph combines two components: (1) a physically informed particle-based analytical model (spring-mass system) to enforce physically feasible motion, and (2) an equivariant graph neural network with a novel action representation exploiting symmetries in particle interactions.

Result: The method enables accurate dynamics prediction and reliable downstream robotic manipulation planning, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in simulation and on real robot hardware for reorientation and repositioning tasks with ropes, cloth, stuffed animals, and rigid objects.

Conclusion: Combining analytical physics models with equivariant graph neural networks and symmetry-aware action representations enables data-efficient learning of physically feasible dynamics for both rigid and deformable objects in robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Learning data-efficient object dynamics models for robotic manipulation remains challenging, especially for deformable objects. A popular approach is to model objects as sets of 3D particles and learn their motion using graph neural networks. In practice, this is not enough to maintain physical feasibility over long horizons and may require large amounts of interaction data to learn. We introduce PIEGraph, a novel approach to combining analytical physics and data-driven models to capture object dynamics for both rigid and deformable bodies using limited real-world interaction data. PIEGraph consists of two components: (1) a \textbf{P}hysically \textbf{I}nformed particle-based analytical model (implemented as a spring--mass system) to enforce physically feasible motion, and (2) an \textbf{E}quivariant \textbf{Graph} Neural Network with a novel action representation that exploits symmetries in particle interactions to guide the analytical model. We evaluate PIEGraph in simulation and on robot hardware for reorientation and repositioning tasks with ropes, cloth, stuffed animals and rigid objects. We show that our method enables accurate dynamics prediction and reliable downstream robotic manipulation planning, which outperforms state of the art baselines.

</details>


### [468] [Temporally Consistent Object 6D Pose Estimation for Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02708)
*Kateryna Zorina,Vojtech Priban,Mederic Fourmy,Josef Sivic,Vladimir Petrik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper improves single-view RGB object pose estimation for robot control by using a factor graph approach that enforces temporal consistency, incorporates motion models, estimates pose uncertainty, and performs online optimization, demonstrating significant improvements on benchmarks and stable performance in real robot tracking tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Off-the-shelf single-view object pose estimators lack temporal consistency and robustness needed for stable vision-based robot feedback control.

Method: A factor graph approach that (i) incorporates object motion models, (ii) explicitly estimates object pose measurement uncertainty, and (iii) integrates both components in an online optimization-based estimator with outlier rejection and smoothing.

Result: Significant improvement on standardized pose estimation benchmarks and experimentally validated stability in a feedback-based robot control task where the object is tracked by a camera on a torque-controlled manipulator.

Conclusion: Factor graph-based temporal consistency enforcement with motion models and uncertainty estimation can make single-view RGB pose estimators suitable for stable robot control.

Abstract: Single-view RGB object pose estimators have reached a level of precision and efficiency that makes them good candidates for vision-based robot control. However, off-the-shelf methods lack temporal consistency and robustness that are mandatory for a stable feedback control. In this work, we develop a factor graph approach to enforce temporal consistency of the object pose estimates. In particular, the proposed approach: (i) incorporates object motion models, (ii) explicitly estimates the object pose measurement uncertainty, and (iii) integrates the above two components in an online optimization-based estimator. We demonstrate that with appropriate outlier rejection and smoothing using the proposed factor graph approach, we can significantly improve the results on standardized pose estimation benchmarks. We experimentally validate the stability of the proposed approach for a feedback-based robot control task in which the object is tracked by the camera attached to a torque controlled manipulator.

</details>


### [469] [Tensegrity crutches with compliance from a pre-stressed self-tensile module improve ground reaction force profiles, speed, effort, comfort, and perceived stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02710)
*Jingxian Gu,Joanna Spyra,Andrew Walski,Lyla Elsaesser,Samuel Bierner,Dobromir Dotov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Researchers developed a biologically inspired tensegrity crutch that outperformed both rigid and spring-loaded designs by reducing peak loading rates and improving user-reported effort, comfort, pain, and usability, likely due to its nonlinear stiffness, ground-following, and force feedback properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Six million US crutch users face limitations from rigid designs (poor sensory feedback, secondary joint injuries) and spring-loaded designs (compromised stability). The authors aimed to create a crutch with better mechanical properties using bio-inspired tensegrity.

Method: Designed a pre-stressed self-tensile two-cell tensegrity crutch module. Compared it with rigid and spring-loaded crutches via axial loading tests, overground straight walking and turning trials. 18 healthy adults (knee blocker simulating unilateral injury) rated effort, comfort, pain, stability, and usability.

Result: Tensegrity and spring-loaded crutches both reduced peak loading rates vs. rigid. Tensegrity improved effort, comfort, pain, and usability. Spring-loaded reduced perceived stability and walking speed.

Conclusion: Bio-inspired tensegrity crutches offer overall improvement over existing designs, with nonlinear stiffness, ground-following, and force feedback as key beneficial mechanical properties.

Abstract: Purpose: Six million people use crutches as mobile aids in the US. Rigid designs with no axial mobility limit sensory feedback and lead to secondary injury on the upper joints. Spring-loaded designs offer compliance but may compromise stability. We designed a biologically inspired tensegrity crutch with a compliant module aiming to achieve favorable mechanical properties. The terminal module was a pre-stressed self-tensile two-cell tensegrity structure. We compared the tensegrity crutch to commercial rigid and spring-loaded crutches in mechanical tests using axial loading, in overground straight and turning walking, and in participant experience. Methods: In human trials, healthy young adults (N=18) with no recent lower-body injury performed straight walking and turning trials at a comfortable self-selected pace. A knee blocker simulated unilateral injury of the dominant leg. After using each type of crutch, participants reported their perceived levels of effort, comfort, pain, stability, and usability. Results: Compared to the rigid design, both spring-loaded and tensegrity conditions reduced peak loading rates. The tensegrity design improved effort, comfort, pain, and usability. Spring-loaded crutches reduced perceived stability and walking speed. Conclusion: The biologically inspired tensegrity crutches were an overall improvement to existing designs. Simulations and mechanical testing suggest that nonlinear stiffness, ground-following, and force feedback are among the beneficial mechanical properties that underlie this improvement.

</details>


### [470] [Parking Assistance for Trailer-Truck Transport Vehicles Using Sensor Fusion and Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02716)
*George Alenchery,Thomas Jeske,Tova Quinones,Lentz Fortune,Tristan Lindo-Slones,Amber Jones,Jordan Fletcher*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework for autonomous truck parking that integrates perception, motion planning, control systems, and infrastructure awareness, using sensor fusion, Hybrid A* path planning, NMPC, and data-driven systems. A proof-of-concept A* simulation with tractor-trailer kinematics was implemented, though jackknife prevention remains unresolved.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-speed maneuvers like parking remain highly challenging for autonomous systems, especially for articulated trailer-truck vehicles, motivating the need for a coordinated system-level framework.

Method: The framework integrates perception (sensor fusion), motion planning (Hybrid A*), control (Nonlinear Model Predictive Control), and infrastructure awareness. A proof-of-concept A* path planning simulation was adapted to include a tractor-trailer kinematic model.

Result: A command-line simulation demonstrating articulated vehicle path planning was achieved, but jackknife prevention was identified as an area requiring further development.

Conclusion: System-level coordination is critical for reliable and scalable autonomous truck parking, though key challenges like jackknife prevention still need to be addressed.

Abstract: Autonomous driving technology has rapidly evolved over the past decade, offering significant improvements in transportation efficiency, safety, and cost reduction. While much of the progress has focused on highway driving and obstacle avoidance, low-speed maneuvers such as parking remain among the most difficult challenges for autonomous systems. This challenge is especially pronounced in trailer-truck transport vehicles due to their articulated motion and environmental constraints. This paper presents a proposed framework for autonomous truck parking that integrates perception, motion planning, control systems, and infrastructure awareness. By combining sensor fusion, Hybrid A* path planning, nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC), and data-driven parking systems, this work highlights the importance of system-level coordination for reliable and scalable autonomous parking solutions. As a proof-of-concept implementation, we adapted an open-source A* path planning simulation to incorporate a tractor-trailer kinematic model, demonstrating articulated vehicle path planning within a command-line simulation environment, with jackknife prevention identified as an area requiring further development.

</details>


### [471] [Latent Bridge: Feature Delta Prediction for Efficient Dual-System Vision-Language-Action Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02739)
*Yudong Liu,Yuan Li,Zijia Tang,Yuxi Zheng,Yueqian Lin,Qinsi Wang,Yi Li,Shuangjun Liu,Shuai Zhang,Taotao Jing,Dashan Gao,Ning Bi,Jingwei Sun,Yiran Chen,Hai Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Latent Bridge is a lightweight model that predicts VLM output deltas between timesteps, reducing VLM backbone calls by 50-75% while retaining 95-100% performance in robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual-system VLA models are bottlenecked by the VLM backbone that executes at every control step, producing temporally redundant features, leading to computational inefficiency.

Method: Latent Bridge predicts VLM output deltas between timesteps, allowing the action head to operate on predicted outputs while calling the expensive VLM backbone periodically. It is instantiated on two VLA architectures (GR00T-N1.6 with feature-space bridge and π0.5 with KV-cache bridge) using a task-agnostic DAgger training pipeline.

Result: Across LIBERO suites, RoboCasa kitchen tasks, and ALOHA sim transfer-cube task, Latent Bridge achieves 95-100% performance retention while reducing VLM calls by 50-75%, yielding 1.65-1.73x net per-episode speedup.

Conclusion: The approach generalizes across different VLA designs and significantly improves inference efficiency without sacrificing task performance.

Abstract: Dual-system Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models achieve state-of-the-art robotic manipulation but are bottlenecked by the VLM backbone, which must
  execute at every control step while producing temporally redundant features. We propose Latent Bridge, a lightweight model that predicts VLM output
  deltas between timesteps, enabling the action head to operate on predicted outputs while the expensive VLM backbone is called only periodically. We
  instantiate Latent Bridge on two architecturally distinct VLAs: GR00T-N1.6 (feature-space bridge) and π0.5 (KV-cache bridge), demonstrating that the
  approach generalizes across VLA designs. Our task-agnostic DAgger training pipeline transfers across benchmarks without modification. Across four
  LIBERO suites, 24 RoboCasa kitchen tasks, and the ALOHA sim transfer-cube task, Latent Bridge achieves 95-100% performance retention while reducing
  VLM calls by 50-75%, yielding 1.65-1.73x net per-episode speedup.

</details>


### [472] [DynoSLAM: Dynamic SLAM with Generative Graph Neural Networks for Real-World Social Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02759)
*Danil Tokhchukov,Veronika Morozova,Gonzalo Ferrer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DynoSLAM integrates socially-aware GNNs into SLAM factor graph optimization via stochastic World Model and Monte Carlo rollouts to handle pedestrian-dense environments, outperforming deterministic models by capturing multimodal epistemic uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional SLAM algorithms fail in dynamic environments with moving pedestrians due to their static environment assumption and reliance on rigid heuristics or deterministic priors.

Method: A tightly-coupled Dynamic GraphSLAM framework that uses a stochastic World Model with GNN-based Monte Carlo rollouts to capture pedestrian motion uncertainty, embedded into the factor graph via dynamic Mahalanobis distance factors.

Result: The stochastic formulation maintains highly accurate retrospective tracking and prevents optimization failures caused by the deterministic "argmax problem" in simulated experiments.

Conclusion: Extracting empirical mean and covariance of future pedestrian states provides a probabilistic safety envelope for downstream planners, enabling anticipatory and collision-free navigation in crowded environments.

Abstract: Traditional Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) algorithms rely heavily on the static environment assumption, which severely limits their applicability in real-world spaces populated by moving entities, such as pedestrians. In this work, we propose DynoSLAM, a tightly-coupled Dynamic GraphSLAM architecture that integrates socially-aware Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) directly into the factor graph optimization. Unlike conventional approaches that use rigid constant-velocity heuristics or deterministic single-agent neural priors, our framework formulates pedestrian motion forecasting as a stochastic World Model. By utilizing Monte Carlo rollouts from a trained GNN, we capture the multimodal epistemic uncertainty of human interactions and embed it into the SLAM graph via a dynamic Mahalanobis distance factor. We demonstrate through extensive simulated experiments that this stochastic formulation not only maintains highly accurate retrospective tracking but also prevents the optimization failures caused by the deterministic "argmax problem". Ultimately, extracting the empirical mean and covariance matrices of future pedestrian states provides a mathematically rigorous, probabilistic safety envelope for downstream local planners, enabling anticipatory and collision-free robot navigation in densely crowded environments.

</details>


### [473] [LiDAR Teach, Radar Repeat: Robust Cross-Modal Navigation in Degenerate and Varying Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02809)
*Renxiang Xiao,Yichen Chen,Yuanfan Zhang,Qianyi Shao,Yushuai Chen,Yuxuan Han,Yunjiang Lou,Liang Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LTR² is a cross-modal LiDAR-Teach-and-Radar-Repeat navigation system that uses LiDAR for teaching under normal conditions and 4D millimeter-wave radar for robust repeating under adverse conditions, featuring a novel Cross-Modal Registration network and adaptive fine-tuning for long-term autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Teach-and-Repeat navigation systems lack a systematic solution to handle various environmental variations such as weather degradation, ephemeral dynamics, and structural changes, limiting long-term autonomous operation.

Method: LTR² uses LiDAR during teaching and 4D radar during repeating, with a Cross-Modal Registration (CMR) network that exploits Doppler-based motion priors and physical laws of LiDAR intensity and radar power density, plus an adaptive fine-tuning strategy for long-term adaptation.

Result: The CMR network achieves state-of-the-art cross-modal registration on open datasets, and LTR² demonstrates centimeter-level accuracy over 40+ km across 6 months on three robot platforms, including nighttime smoke conditions, outperforming existing approaches.

Conclusion: LTR² provides a robust, accurate, and long-term viable solution for autonomous navigation under diverse environmental disturbances through systematic cross-modal Teach-and-Repeat.

Abstract: Long-term autonomy requires robust navigation in environments subject to dynamic and static changes, as well as adverse weather conditions. Teach-and-Repeat (T\&R) navigation offers a reliable and cost-effective solution by avoiding the need for consistent global mapping; however, existing T\&R systems lack a systematic solution to tackle various environmental variations such as weather degradation, ephemeral dynamics, and structural changes. This work proposes LTR$^2$, the first cross-modal, cross-platform LiDAR-Teach-and-Radar-Repeat system that systematically addresses these challenges. LTR$^2$ leverages LiDAR during the teaching phase to capture precise structural information under normal conditions and utilizes 4D millimeter-wave radar during the repeating phase for robust operation under environmental degradations. To align sparse and noisy forward-looking 4D radar with dense and accurate omnidirectional 3D LiDAR data, we introduce a Cross-Modal Registration (CMR) network that jointly exploits Doppler-based motion priors and the physical laws governing LiDAR intensity and radar power density. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive fine-tuning strategy that incrementally updates the CMR network based on localization errors, enabling long-term adaptability to static environmental changes without ground-truth labels. We demonstrate that the proposed CMR network achieves state-of-the-art cross-modal registration performance on the open-access dataset. Then we validate LTR$^2$ across three robot platforms over a large-scale, long-term deployment (40+ km over 6 months), including challenging conditions such as nighttime smoke. Experimental results and ablation studies demonstrate centimeter-level accuracy and strong robustness against diverse environmental disturbances, significantly outperforming existing approaches.

</details>


### [474] [Semantic Risk-Aware Heuristic Planning for Robotic Navigation in Dynamic Environments: An LLM-Inspired Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02862)
*Hamza Ahmed Durrani,Rafay Suleman Durrani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Semantic Risk-Aware Heuristic (SRAH) planner that integrates LLM-inspired cost functions into A* search with replanning. In 200 randomized grid-world trials, SRAH achieves 62.0% success rate vs BFS (56.5%) and Greedy (4.0%), showing that lightweight LLM-inspired heuristics improve navigation safety and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate LLM reasoning principles into classical robot path planning to improve safety and robustness by penalizing geometrically cluttered or high-risk zones.

Method: SRAH encodes LLM-inspired cost functions into an A* search framework, augmented with closed-loop replanning upon dynamic obstacle detection.

Result: SRAH achieves 62.0% task success rate, outperforming BFS (56.5%) by 9.7% relative improvement and Greedy (4.0%) by a large margin over 200 randomized trials in a 15x15 grid-world.

Conclusion: Lightweight LLM-inspired heuristics provide measurable safety and robustness gains for autonomous robot navigation, as demonstrated by consistent improvements across varying obstacle densities.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning principles into classical robot path planning represents a rapidly emerging research direction. In this paper, we propose a Semantic Risk-Aware Heuristic (SRAH) planner that encodes LLM-inspired cost functions penalising geometrically cluttered or high-risk zones into an A$^*$ search framework, augmented with closed-loop replanning upon dynamic obstacle detection. We evaluate SRAH against two established baselines Breadth-First Search (BFS) with replanning and a Greedy heuristic without replanning across 200 randomised trials in a $15{\times}15$ grid-world with 20\% static obstacle density and stochastic dynamic obstacles. SRAH achieves a task success rate of 62.0\%, outperforming BFS (56.5\%) by 9.7\% relative improvement and Greedy (4.0\%) by a large margin. We further analyse the trade-off between planning overhead, path efficiency, and failure-recovery count, and demonstrate via an obstacle-density ablation that semantic cost shaping consistently improves navigation across environments of varying difficulty. Our results suggest that even lightweight, LLM-inspired heuristics provide measurable safety and robustness gains for autonomous robot navigation.

</details>


### [475] [MolmoAct2: Action Reasoning Models for Real-world Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02881)
*Haoquan Fang,Jiafei Duan,Donovan Clay,Sam Wang,Shuo Liu,Weikai Huang,Xiang Fan,Wei-Chuan Tsai,Shirui Chen,Yi Ru Wang,Shanli Xing,Jaemin Cho,Jae Sung Park,Ainaz Eftekhar,Peter Sushko,Karen Farley,Angad Wadhwa,Cole Harrison,Winson Han,Ying-Chun Lee,Eli VanderBilt,Rose Hendrix,Suveen Ellawela,Lucas Ngoo,Joyce Chai,Zhongzheng Ren,Ali Farhadi,Dieter Fox,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MolmoAct2 is an open, fully reproducible VLA (Vision-Language-Action) model for robot control, featuring: (1) MolmoER backbone for embodied reasoning, (2) massive datasets including the largest open bimanual dataset, (3) OpenFAST action tokenizer, (4) flow-matching continuous-action architecture, and (5) MolmoThink adaptive-depth reasoning. It outperforms strong baselines across 7 simulation/real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models fall short on real-world deployment criteria: frontier models are closed, open-weight alternatives are tied to expensive hardware, reasoning-augmented policies suffer prohibitive latency, and fine-tuned success rates remain below dependable thresholds. The authors aim to provide a fully open, practical, and performant generalist robot controller.

Method: Five key contributions: (1) MolmoER - VLM backbone specialized for spatial/embodied reasoning, trained on 3.3M samples with "specialize-then-rehearse" recipe; (2) Three new datasets: MolmoAct2-BimanualYAM (720 hrs, largest open bimanual dataset), quality-filtered Franka (DROID) and SO100/101 subsets; (3) OpenFAST - open-weight, open-data action tokenizer trained on millions of trajectories across 5 embodiments; (4) Architecture redesign grafting flow-matching continuous-action expert onto discrete-token VLM via per-layer KV-cache conditioning; (5) MolmoThink - adaptive-depth reasoning that re-predicts depth tokens only for changing scene regions.

Result: MolmoAct2 outperforms strong baselines including Pi-05 across 7 simulation and real-world benchmarks. MolmoER surpasses GPT-5 and Gemini Robotics ER-1.5 across 13 embodied-reasoning benchmarks. This is the most extensive empirical study of any open VLA to date.

Conclusion: The authors release model weights, training code, and complete training data, establishing a fully open ecosystem for VLA research. MolmoAct2 demonstrates that open models can achieve state-of-the-art performance on both action and embodied reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models aim to provide a single generalist controller for robots, but today's systems fall short on the criteria that matter for real-world deployment. Frontier models are closed, open-weight alternatives are tied to expensive hardware, reasoning-augmented policies pay prohibitive latency for their grounding, and fine-tuned success rates remain below the threshold for dependable use. We present MolmoAct2, a fully open action reasoning model built for practical deployment, advancing its predecessor along five axes. We introduce MolmoER, a VLM backbone specialized for spatial and embodied reasoning, trained on a 3.3M-sample corpus with a specialize-then-rehearse recipe. We release three new datasets spanning low-to-medium cost platforms, including MolmoAct2-BimanualYAM, 720 hours of teleoperated bimanual trajectories that constitute the largest open bimanual dataset to date, together with quality-filtered Franka (DROID) and SO100/101 subsets. We provide OpenFAST, an open-weight, open-data action tokenizer trained on millions of trajectories across five embodiments. We redesign the architecture to graft a flow-matching continuous-action expert onto a discrete-token VLM via per-layer KV-cache conditioning. Finally, we propose MolmoThink, an adaptive-depth reasoning variant that re-predicts depth tokens only for scene regions that change between timesteps, retaining geometric grounding at a fraction of prior latency. In the most extensive empirical study of any open VLA to date, spanning 7 simulation and real-world benchmarks, MolmoAct2 outperforms strong baselines including Pi-05, while MolmoER surpasses GPT-5 and Gemini Robotics ER-1.5 across 13 embodied-reasoning benchmarks. We release model weights, training code, and complete training data. Project page: https://allenai.org/blog/molmoact2

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [476] [A Coupled Fourth Order Telegraph Diffusion Framework Using Grayscale Indicators for Image Despeckling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00881)
*Manish Kumar,Rajendra K. Ray*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A fourth-order coupled hyperbolic-parabolic PDE model for despeckling that reduces noise while preserving edges and fine details, with mathematical proof of existence and superior performance over existing methods on SAR, ultrasound, and standard images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional second-order PDE-based despeckling methods introduce staircase artifacts and blur fine details when removing speckle noise from coherent imaging systems like SAR and medical ultrasound.

Method: A nonlinear fourth-order coupled hyperbolic-parabolic PDE model with two evolution equations: one for fourth-order diffusion (speckle reduction, smooth transitions) and another for refining an edge indicator (texture/structure preservation). The diffusion coefficient is adaptively constructed using image intensity and grayscale-based indicator. Existence of weak solution proved via Schauder fixed-point theorem. Implemented with finite-difference scheme and Gauss-Seidel iteration.

Result: Outperforms HPCPDE and TDFM on standard grayscale images, real SAR/ultrasound data, and speckle-corrupted color images in terms of PSNR, MSSIM, and Speckle Index.

Conclusion: The proposed fourth-order coupled PDE model achieves superior despeckling performance over conventional PDE techniques by avoiding staircase artifacts, preserving fine details, and maintaining structure-aware denoising.

Abstract: Speckle noise severely limits the quality of images acquired from coherent imaging systems such as Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) and medical ultrasound. Traditional second-order PDE-based despeckling approaches, although popular, often introduce staircase artifacts and blur fine details. To overcome these limitations, we present a nonlinear, fourth-order coupled hyperbolic-parabolic PDE model that effectively reduces noise while preserving the structure. The framework consists of two evolution equations: one governing fourth-order diffusion for effective speckle reduction and smooth intensity transitions, and another refining an edge indicator to protect textures and structural features. The diffusion coefficient is adaptively constructed using both the image intensity variable u and a grayscale-based indicator function, ensuring structure-aware denoising while avoiding blocky artifacts and preserving fine details. We also prove the existence of a weak solution to the proposed model by applying Schauder fixed-point theorem. A finite-difference scheme with Gauss Seidel iteration is employed for efficient implementation. We compare the proposed model with the existing coupled second-order PDE model (HPCPDE) and the fourth-order telegraph diffusion model (TDFM). The results show that our model consistently outperforms these approaches. Experiments on standard grayscale images, real SAR and ultrasound data, as well as speckle-corrupted color images, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance over conventional PDE-based techniques in terms of PSNR, MSSIM, and Speckle Index.

</details>


### [477] [A Proof-of-Concept Study of Multitask Learning for Cranial Synthetic CT Generation Across Heterogeneous MRI Field Strengths](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00923)
*Zhuoyao Xin,Yiren Zhang,Christopher Wu,Dong Liu,Chunming Gu,Elena Greco,Erik H. Middlebrooks,Jun Hua,Jia Guo*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a modular, structurally coupled deep learning framework for synthesizing CT images from MRI data that works robustly across different MRI field strengths and acquisition protocols, demonstrating improved performance and generalization on multi-site datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneity across MRI field strengths and acquisition protocols limits the generalizability of existing CT synthesis methods, hindering clinical applications like attenuation correction, radiotherapy planning, and image-guided interventions.

Method: The authors formulate cranial CT synthesis as a modular, structurally coupled problem and design a deep learning framework that adapts to variations in field strength and imaging protocols while preserving anatomical consistency.

Result: Experiments on multi-site datasets show improved performance and generalization compared to conventional approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed method enables reliable CT synthesis across heterogeneous MRI settings, supporting broader clinical translation.

Abstract: Accurate synthesis of computed tomography (CT) images from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is clinically valuable for cranial applications such as attenuation correction, radiotherapy planning, and image-guided interventions. However, heterogeneity across MRI field strengths and acquisition protocols limits the generalizability of existing methods. In this study, we formulate cranial CT synthesis as a modular, structurally coupled problem and propose a deep learning framework to improve robustness across heterogeneous MRI conditions. The model is designed to adapt to variations in field strength and imaging protocols while preserving anatomical consistency. Experiments on multi-site datasets demonstrate improved performance and generalization compared with conventional approaches. The proposed method enables reliable CT synthesis across heterogeneous MRI settings, supporting broader clinical translation.

</details>


### [478] [Reconstruction Interval Z-Phase Dependence of AI Detection Sensitivity in CT Lung Nodule Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00971)
*Dan Soliman*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: AI lung nodule detection sensitivity degrades at thicker CT reconstruction intervals, and when the reconstruction interval exceeds nodule diameter, detection probability varies significantly (up to 17.6 percentage points) depending on the nodule's position (z-phase) within the reconstruction cycle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize how the z-phase (nodule position within the reconstruction cycle) affects deep learning-based lung nodule detection sensitivity, a factor not previously studied.

Method: Retrospective analysis of LIDC-IDRI dataset; re-analyzed detection results from a 154-case perturbation study. Z-phase defined as fractional position of nodule center within reconstruction cycle, folded to [0,0.5]. Sensitivity stratified by z-phase bin, reconstruction interval (1/3/5mm), and d/D ratio (reconstruction interval / nodule diameter).

Result: At 5mm interval, sensitivity was 71.6% vs 84.8% at 1mm baseline. Within 5mm condition, sensitivity varied 17.6pp across z-phase bins. For d/D >= 1.0, sensitivity was 61.4% with systematic z-phase effect; for d/D < 0.5, sensitivity was 92.4% with no significant z-phase effect.

Conclusion: AI detection sensitivity depends on reconstruction interval-to-nodule diameter ratio. When ratio approaches or exceeds 1.0, z-phase becomes dominant source of per-study detection variance — an invisible stochastic effect not captured by protocol-level quality metrics or AI confidence scores.

Abstract: Background: Sensitivity of AI-assisted lung nodule detection systems is known to vary with CT acquisition parameters including radiation dose, reconstruction kernel, and slice thickness. However, the dependence of detection probability on nodule position within the reconstruction cycle -- the z-phase -- has not, to the author's knowledge, been characterized for deep learning-based detection systems. Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed using the LIDC-IDRI dataset. Detection results from a previously validated 154-case perturbation study were re-analyzed. For each consensus nodule (>=4-reader agreement), z-phase was defined as the fractional position of the nodule center within the reconstruction cycle, folded to [0, 0.5]. Detection sensitivity was stratified by z-phase bin, reconstruction interval (1mm, 3mm, 5mm), and by the ratio of reconstruction interval to nodule diameter (d/D). Results: At 5mm reconstruction interval, sensitivity was 71.6% vs 84.8% at 1mm baseline. Within the 5mm condition, sensitivity varied by 17.6 percentage points across z-phase bins. Stratified by d/D ratio, sensitivity was 92.4% for d/D < 0.5, 78.0% for 0.5 <= d/D < 1.0, and 61.4% for d/D >= 1.0, with a systematic z-phase effect present only in the d/D >= 1.0 stratum. Conclusions: AI detection sensitivity depends on the ratio of reconstruction interval to nodule diameter. When this ratio approaches or exceeds 1.0 -- as occurs for 3-6mm nodules at 5mm reconstruction -- z-phase becomes the dominant source of per-study detection variance. This stochastic effect is invisible to protocol-level quality metrics and not reflected in AI confidence scores.

</details>


### [479] [Evolution of NVENC Efficiency: A Longitudinal Analysis of HQ and UHQ Tuning Efficiency, Latency and Energy Trade-offs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01187)
*Kasidis Arunruangsirilert,Jiro Katto*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper analyzes NVIDIA's NVENC hardware encoding from Pascal to Blackwell generations, evaluating the new "Ultra High Quality" (UHQ) mode. While Blackwell achieves significant BD-Rate gains (up to 22.79% in UHQ), it incurs over 400% latency increase and 40% power consumption rise, making UHQ unsuitable for real-time communications but viable for VoD transcoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid growth of uplink-intensive applications demands video coding solutions that achieve both high Rate-Distortion (RD) efficiency and ultra-low latency, motivating the evaluation of NVIDIA's latest hardware encoding capabilities.

Method: Longitudinal performance analysis of NVENC across GPU architectures (Pascal to Blackwell), comparing standard low-latency configurations against the new UHQ tuning mode using BD-Rate metrics, latency measurements, and power consumption analysis.

Result: Blackwell architecture achieves 5.94% BD-Rate gain in standard modes and up to 22.79% in UHQ modes, but UHQ introduces over 400% end-to-end latency increase and up to 40% GPU power consumption rise due to its hybrid CUDA-CUDA core pipeline and aggressive B-frame structures.

Conclusion: UHQ bridges the quality gap with software encoders but its prohibitive serialization delay makes it unsuitable for interactive real-time communications; it is better positioned as a specialized solution for Video-on-Demand (VoD) transcoding.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of uplink-intensive applications necessitates video coding solutions that balance high Rate-Distortion (RD) efficiency with ultra-low latency. This paper presents a longitudinal performance analysis of NVIDIA hardware encoding (NVENC), spanning from Pascal to the emerging Blackwell generation. We specifically evaluate the operational viability of the new "Ultra High Quality" (UHQ) tuning mode against standard low-latency configurations. Our results demonstrate that while the Blackwell architecture breaks historical efficiency plateaus, achieving a 5.94% BD-Rate gain in standard modes and up to 22.79% in UHQ modes, these gains incur severe system-level penalties. We reveal that UHQ operates as a hybrid pipeline, offloading complexity to CUDA cores and enforcing aggressive temporal structures (up to 7 B-frames) that increase end-to-end latency by over 400% and GPU board power consumption by up to 40%. Consequently, while UHQ successfully bridges the quality gap with software encoders, its prohibitive serialization delay renders it unsuitable for interactive real-time communications, positioning it instead as a specialized solution for Video-on-Demand (VoD) transcoding.

</details>


### [480] [A Target-Free Harmonization Method for MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01282)
*Minjun Kim,Dong Ju Mun,Hwihun Jeong,Hangyeol Park,Haechang Lee,Se Young Chun,Jongho Lee*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: TgtFreeHarmony is a privacy-preserving MRI harmonization framework that works without needing target domain data, using Bayesian optimization and disentanglement to estimate target style for improved downstream task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MRI harmonization methods require access to both source and target domain data, leading to data sharing between institutions and raising patient privacy concerns, limiting practical clinical deployment.

Method: TgtFreeHarmony estimates target domain style by searching the manifold of MRI domain style via a disentanglement-based generator, using Bayesian optimization guided by a downstream task model's performance trained on target domain data.

Result: Evaluated on brain tissue segmentation across multiple institutes, the method effectively harmonizes source images to target images and improves downstream task performance.

Conclusion: TgtFreeHarmony enables harmonization without any target-domain data access, establishing a privacy-preserving direction that can be realistically deployed in clinical environments.

Abstract: In MRI, variations in scan parameters, sequence, or hardware can lead to discrepancies in image appearance, even for the same subject. These inconsistencies, known as domain shifts, can hinder image analysis and degrade the performance of deep learning models trained on data from specific target domains. MRI image harmonization aims to address these issues by aligning source domain images to the target domain images while preserving biological information such as anatomical structures. However, most existing harmonization approaches require access to both source and target domain data in training or test time. This dependence induces data sharing between institutions, raising concerns about patient privacy and substantially limiting the harmonization approaches that can be practically deployed in clinical settings. To overcome these limitations, we introduce TgtFreeHarmony, the harmonization framework tailored for target-free scenarios, eliminating the need for target domain data and any data sharing, enabling privacy-preserving harmonization directly within the source institution. Our approach estimates the target domain style by searching the manifold of MRI domain style constructed via a disentanglement-based generator using Bayesian optimization guided by the performance of a downstream task model, which is trained on target domain data. We evaluated our method on the brain tissue segmentation task across multiple institutes and demonstrated that it effectively harmonizes source images into target images, leading to improved downstream task performance. By enabling harmonization without any access to target-domain data, TgtFreeHarmony establishes a new direction of harmonization preserving data privacy that can be realistically deployed within clinical environments.

</details>


### [481] [Cardiac Mesh Flow: One-Step Generation of 3D+t Cardiac Four-Chamber Meshes via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01884)
*Qiang Ma,Qingjie Meng,Mengyun Qiao,Paul M. Matthews,Declan P. O'Regan,Wenjia Bai*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Cardiac Mesh Flow is a generative flow model for 3D+t cardiac mesh generation that uses flow matching to achieve high-fidelity, diverse, and anatomically consistent four-chamber heart meshes with temporal coherence and controllable generation based on chamber volumes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models for cardiac shape synthesis either lack anatomical correspondence across time/subjects or suffer from VAE trade-offs between reconstruction fidelity and generative diversity, motivating a new approach.

Method: Cardiac Mesh Flow uses flow matching to perform one-step generation of multi-scale free-form deformation fields, which warp a template mesh to produce cardiac four-chamber meshes across a cardiac cycle, with controllable generation conditioned on chamber volumes.

Result: Cardiac Mesh Flow achieves high fidelity and diversity on both unconditional and conditional generation, outperforming state-of-the-art 3D+t cardiac mesh generation methods.

Conclusion: The proposed flow matching-based approach effectively addresses the fidelity-diversity trade-off and anatomical correspondence issues in cardiac shape modelling, enabling precise control over synthetic heart generation.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal (3D+t) generative modelling of cardiac shape and motion is crucial for understanding heart structure and function at population scale. Existing generative models for cardiac shape synthesis either adopt volumetric shape representations that lack anatomical correspondence across different time points and subjects, or rely on VAE-based frameworks that suffer from a trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and generative diversity. In this work, we propose Cardiac Mesh Flow, a novel generative flow model for 3D+t cardiac four-chamber mesh generation with anatomical correspondence, temporal coherence, and periodic consistency. Leveraging the flow matching technique, Cardiac Mesh Flow performs efficient one-step generation of multi-scale free-form deformation fields, which warp a template mesh to generate cardiac four-chamber meshes across a cardiac cycle. Furthermore, Cardiac Mesh Flow enables controllable generation conditioned on cardiac chamber volumes, allowing precise control of the synthetic heart. Experimental results demonstrate that Cardiac Mesh Flow achieves high fidelity and diversity on both unconditional and conditional generation, compared to state-of-the-art 3D+t cardiac mesh generation methods.

</details>


### [482] [Deep Speckle Holography Redefines Label-free Nanoparticle Phenotyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01982)
*Yanmin Zhu,Yuxing Li,Jingyan Chen,Derek Yuen-Wa Ho,Chutian Wang,Yuzhe Zhang,Xue-Qi Wang,Bo Lu,James Kar-Hei Fang,Francis Chi Chung Ling,Loza F. Tadesse,Edmund Y. Lam*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Deep speckle holography is a physics-informed generative framework that enables simultaneous, label-free measurement of nanoparticle size, morphology, composition, and species-resolved abundance from a single optical measurement in complex unprocessed fluids, covering a 10-order-of-magnitude dynamic range in 0.9 seconds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional nanoparticle metrology cannot simultaneously resolve particle size, morphology, composition, and species-specific abundance from a single label-free measurement in mixed, unprocessed fluids—a long-standing limitation the authors aim to overcome.

Method: The method uses complex forward speckle-holographic fields as an information-rich optical space, combined with a physics-informed generative framework (deep speckle holography) to extract multidimensional particle signatures from a single non-contact measurement.

Result: The method works across purified suspensions, mixed populations, environmental waters, human urine, and other native fluids, delivering multidimensional readouts in 0.9 seconds over a 10-order-of-magnitude dynamic range, without purification, labeling, or destructive preprocessing.

Conclusion: Deep speckle holography enables direct label-free nanoparticle phenotyping in real-world fluids, moving from isolated-particle characterization to multidimensional inference in complex mixtures, with applications in real-time tracking of nanoparticle transformations and non-invasive nanomedicine quality control.

Abstract: Nanoparticle metrology has long been constrained by the assumption that, in mixed and unprocessed fluids, particle size, morphology, composition, and species-specific abundance cannot be resolved simultaneously from a single label-free measurement. Here, we revisit this long-standing limitation by showing that complex forward speckle-holographic fields define an information-rich optical space for multidimensional particle signatures. We report deep speckle holography, a physics-informed generative framework that profiles particle identity, size, morphology, and species-resolved abundance from a single non-contact optical measurement. Across purified suspensions, mixed particle populations, environmental waters, human urine, and other unprocessed native fluids, the method enables direct nanoparticle inference without purification, labeling, or destructive preprocessing, delivering concurrent multidimensional readouts in 0.9 s over a dynamic range spanning 10 orders of magnitude. Deep speckle holography establishes a route toward direct label-free nanoparticle phenotyping in real-world fluids, moving nanoscale measurement beyond isolated-particle characterization toward multidimensional inference in complex mixtures, and expanding the scope of questions nanoscale measurement can address, from real-time tracking of nanoparticle transformations in living and environmental systems to non-invasive quality control of nanomedicine formulations, and beyond.

</details>


### [483] [DriftDecode: One-Step Wireless Image Decoding via Drifting-Inspired Detail Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02325)
*Jingwen Fu,Ming Xiao,Mikael Skoglund*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: DriftDecode is a one-step decoder for wireless image transmission that uses an SNR-conditioned U-Net with a drift-inspired texture loss, achieving 30ms latency (4.8× speedup over iterative methods) and up to 1.13dB PSNR gain, supporting recovery-oriented one-step decoding as an alternative to iterative generative decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based and flow-based wireless image decoders rely on iterative inference, causing substantial latency. Since received signals already preserve coarse source image structure, wireless decoding is better viewed as a recovery (detail restoration) task rather than generation from scratch.

Method: DriftDecode couples a one-step U-Net decoder with an SNR-conditioned design and a drift-inspired instance-level texture loss. The loss reformulates the drifting-field mechanism from generative drifting models in perceptual feature space, guiding local features toward spatially aligned ground-truth while suppressing mismatched textures.

Result: On DIV2K and MNIST under AWGN and Rayleigh fading channels, DriftDecode achieves 30ms decoding latency (4.8× speedup over 10-step flow-matching), consistently outperforms MSE-only training, and yields up to 1.13dB PSNR gain on MNIST under Rayleigh fading.

Conclusion: Recovery-oriented one-step decoding is an effective alternative to iterative generative decoding for low-latency wireless image transmission, offering a favorable quality-latency tradeoff.

Abstract: Generative receivers for wireless image transmission can improve reconstruction quality, but diffusion-based and flow-based decoding relies on iterative inference and therefore incurs substantial latency. In wireless image transmission, however, the received signal already preserves the coarse structure of the source image. Wireless decoding is therefore better viewed as a recovery task than as image generation from scratch, and the main challenge lies in restoring channel-impaired details. Motivated by this recovery-oriented perspective, this paper proposes DriftDecode, a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)-conditioned one-step decoder for wireless image reconstruction. DriftDecode couples a one-step U-Net decoder with a drift-inspired instance-level texture loss. The loss reformulates the drifting-field mechanism from generative drifting models in perceptual feature space, guiding each reconstructed local feature toward its spatially aligned ground-truth counterpart while suppressing mismatched textures. Experiments on DIV2K and MNIST under additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) and Rayleigh fading channels show a favorable quality-latency tradeoff. DriftDecode achieves 30~ms decoding latency, providing a 4.8$\times$ speedup over a 10-step flow-matching decoder, while consistently outperforming MSE-only training and yielding up to 1.13~dB PSNR gain on MNIST under Rayleigh fading. These results support recovery-oriented one-step decoding as an effective alternative to iterative generative decoding for low-latency wireless image transmission.

</details>


### [484] [Development and Validation of an Integrated LiDAR-Camera System for Real-Time Monitoring of Underground Longwall Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02516)
*Pasindu Ranasinghe,Bikram Banerjee,Simit Raval*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A flameproof LiDAR-camera fusion system is designed for real-time 3D monitoring in underground longwall mining, achieving low-latency (10 Hz) colorized point cloud transmission under 25 Mb/s bandwidth, with thermal management reducing surface temperature from 106°C to 70°C.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time spatial monitoring in underground longwall operations is challenging due to methane risks, poor visibility, heat, confinement, and limited bandwidth. Existing camera systems lack depth, and standalone LiDAR provides only monochromatic or periodic 3D data.

Method: A solid-state LiDAR and industrial RGB camera are integrated within a certified flameproof enclosure. The system includes LiDAR-camera fusion, low-light image enhancement, enclosure-aware calibration, and passive thermal management via heat sinking and conduction.

Result: Peak surface temperature reduced from 106°C to 70°C; internal temperature stabilized at 57°C; >97% LiDAR points within camera FOV; processed colorized point clouds transmitted at up to 10 Hz with bandwidth <25 Mb/s.

Conclusion: The proposed enclosure-aware LiDAR-camera system is viable for real-time 3D monitoring in hazardous underground mining, balancing safety, thermal constraints, geometric accuracy, and bandwidth efficiency.

Abstract: Real-time spatial monitoring in underground longwall operations is challenging due to methane-related safety risks, poor visibility, elevated thermal loads, spatial confinement, and bandwidth-limited communications. Currently available camera-based monitoring provides visual context but lacks direct depth information, while standalone underground LiDAR scanners are limited to monochromatic or periodic 3D mapping. This paper presents the design, integration, and experimental validation of a LiDAR-camera monitoring system built around a certified flameproof enclosure that prevents flame propagation into the surrounding atmosphere. The system combines a solid-state LiDAR, an industrial RGB camera, and an onboard processor within a compact hardware assembly, supporting LiDAR-camera fusion, low-light image enhancement, and real-time processing. Laboratory experiments evaluated LiDAR and camera performance through the protective polycarbonate dome and quantified optical and geometric distortions introduced by the enclosure. Thermal testing showed that iterative component placement, heat sinking, and passive conduction reduced peak surface temperature from 106 °C to 70 °C, with internal temperature stabilising at 57 °C. Furthermore, a representative longwall simulation was created to evaluate the complete sensing, fusion, and transmission workflow under controlled geometric and low-light conditions. In the final configuration, more than 97% of LiDAR points fell within the camera field of view, supporting reliable colourisation. Enclosure-aware calibration and correction maintained geometric accuracy, while processed colourised point clouds were transmitted at up to 10 Hz with sustained bandwidth below 25 Mb/s.

</details>


### [485] [Biological Spatial Priors Regularize Foundation Model Representations for Cross-Site MSI Generalization in Colorectal Cancer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02660)
*Dasari Naga Raju*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A study showing that injecting biologically motivated spatial priors (peripheral distance encoding based on Crohn's-like lymphocytic reactions) into foundation model + TransMIL pipelines improves MSI prediction from H&E slides and enhances cross-site generalization, achieving AUC 0.959 on TCGA-COAD and perfect MSS specificity on TCGA-READ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MSI status prediction from H&E WSIs using deep learning models suffers from poor cross-site generalization due to site-specific texture variations in foundation model representations. The authors aim to use known MSI histology (Crohn's-like lymphocytic reaction at tumor invasive margin) as spatial priors to guide models toward more site-invariant features.

Method: Two biologically motivated spatial priors are introduced: (1) peripheral distance encoding reflecting lymphocytic reaction at tumor margin, and (2) local immune neighborhood encoding reflecting lymphocyte-to-tumor ratio in tile neighborhoods. These priors are injected into a TransMIL aggregator before self-attention, combined with UNI2-h or Virchow2 foundation model features. Evaluated on TCGA-COAD (train, 137 slides) and TCGA-READ (external test, 50 slides) with six foundation model + MIL aggregator baselines.

Result: Peripheral distance encoding achieved MSI AUC 0.959 on COAD (vs 0.957 best baseline) and MSS specificity 1.000 on READ (vs 0.939 baseline). Local immune neighborhood encoding gave comparable internal AUC but lower cross-site specificity, indicating margin proximity is a more site-invariant signal.

Conclusion: Biologically grounded spatial priors act as regularizers that reduce reliance on site-specific imaging patterns, improving generalization of MSI prediction across institutions without retraining.

Abstract: Predicting microsatellite instability (MSI) status from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) whole slide images (WSIs) offers a practical alternative to molecular testing, but models trained at one institution tend to generalize poorly to slides acquired at a different site. Foundation model representations, despite their generality, still encode site-specific texture alongside the conserved biological morphology underlying MSI. We investigate whether tile-level spatial priors derived from known MSI histology can guide these representations toward more site-invariant features. We introduce a biologically motivated spatial prior based on peripheral distance encoding, reflecting the Crohn's-like peripheral lymphocytic reaction at the tumor invasive margin, and evaluate a secondary local immune neighborhood encoding reflecting the lymphocyte-to-tumor ratio in each tile's immediate spatial neighborhood. Both priors are injected into a TransMIL aggregator before self-attention, allowing the transformer to integrate spatial biological context with UNI2-h or Virchow2 features across all attention layers. We evaluate six foundation model and MIL aggregator combinations as a reference, then assess the effect of each spatial prior. Training on TCGA-COAD (137 slides) and evaluating externally on TCGA-READ (50 slides) without retraining, peripheral distance encoding achieves MSI AUC 0.959 +/- 0.012 on COAD and MSS specificity 1.000 on READ, compared to 0.957 and 0.939 for the strongest reference configuration. Local immune neighborhood encoding achieves comparable internal AUC but lower cross-site specificity, suggesting margin proximity encodes a more site-invariant biological signal than local immune density. Results suggest biologically grounded spatial priors act as regularizers that reduce reliance on site-specific imaging patterns.

</details>


### [486] [Cool-chic 5.0: Faster Encoding and Inter-Feature Entropy Modeling for Overfitted Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02726)
*Théo Ladune,Pierrick Philippe,Pierre Jaffuer,Théophile Blard,Sylvain Kervadec,Félix Henry,Gordon Clare*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Cool-chic 5.0 is a next-generation overfitted image codec that achieves better compression than H.266/VVC (-11% rate reduction) and rivals MLIC++ while using 10× fewer encoding iterations and 250× lower decoding complexity, with open-source release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve overfitted codec compression performance while drastically reducing the high encoding complexity that typically limits their practical use.

Method: Updates decoder architecture and optimizes the optimization process for Cool-chic, an overfitted codec that learns a content-specific decoder during encoding.

Result: Outperforms all overfitted codecs with 10× fewer encoding iterations; achieves -11% bitrate reduction vs. H.266/VVC; competitive with MLIC++ while having 250× lower decoding complexity.

Conclusion: Cool-chic 5.0 sets a new state-of-the-art among overfitted codecs, balancing strong compression with practical encoding and decoding costs, and is publicly available.

Abstract: Overfitted codecs compress an image by learning a decoder tailored to the content during the encoding. As such, they trade increased encoding complexity for strong compression performance and low decoding complexity. This work introduces Cool-chic 5.0, the latest version in the Cool-chic series of overfitted codecs, featuring an updated decoder architecture and an improved optimization process. Cool-chic 5.0 outperforms all overfitted codecs with 10 times less encoding iterations. It offers -11% rate reduction compared to the state-of-the-art conventional codec H.266/VVC. It is also competitive with modern autoencoders such as MLIC++ while featuring a decoding complexity 250 times lower. This work is made open-source at https://github.com/Orange-OpenSource/Cool-Chic.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [487] [Continuous Algebraic Diversity: Unifying Spectral, Wavelet, and Time-Frequency Analysis via Lie Group Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00848)
*Mitchell A. Thornton*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a computable criterion for selecting among Fourier, wavelet, time-frequency, and spherical harmonic analysis by extending algebraic diversity (AD) to Lie groups. It provides theorems on signal-noise separation, unification of classical methods under different groups, and a polynomial-time algorithm for blind group matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no existing criterion that can automatically select among Fourier, wavelet, time-frequency analysis, or spherical harmonics for a given signal processing task. The paper aims to provide a principled, computable selection method.

Method: The AD framework is extended from finite groups to Lie groups acting on L²(ℝ). The group-averaged estimator generalizes sums to Haar measure integrals. A continuous replacement theorem uses the Duflo-Moore operator to explain noise floors (e.g., in wavelets from affine group non-unimodularity). A commutativity residual δ, measured via Hilbert-Schmidt norms, serves as the selection criterion. A double-commutator generalized eigenvalue problem solves blind group matching in polynomial time.

Result: Classical spectral analysis ↔ translation group; wavelet analysis ↔ affine group; time-frequency analysis ↔ Heisenberg-Weyl group; spherical harmonics ↔ SO(3). The discretization theorem shows discrete AD results approximate the continuous theory as Z_M → (ℝ,+) when M→∞.

Conclusion: The extended AD framework provides the first known computable criterion for selecting among these fundamental signal analysis methods, unifying them under group theory while enabling automatic, principled selection.

Abstract: We provide a computable criterion for selecting among Fourier, wavelet, and time-frequency analysis by extending the algebraic diversity (AD) framework to Lie groups acting on $L^2(\mathbb{R})$. To our knowledge, there is no other criterion that provides this selection capability. The group-averaged estimator generalizes from a finite sum over group elements to an integral with respect to Haar measure. A Continuous Replacement Theorem establishes signal-noise separation under equivariance and ergodicity conditions, with a noise operator $\mathcal{N}_G = C_ρ^{-2}$ determined by the Duflo-Moore operator that explains the frequency-dependent noise floor in wavelet analysis as a consequence of the affine group's non-unimodularity. A Unification Theorem shows that classical spectral analysis corresponds to the translation group, wavelet analysis to the affine group, time-frequency analysis to the Heisenberg-Weyl group, and spherical harmonics to SO(3). The commutativity residual $δ$, extended to Hilbert-Schmidt operator norms, provides a principled selection criterion among these groups. A double-commutator generalized eigenvalue problem solves the blind group matching problem in polynomial time. A Discretization Recovery Theorem establishes that all discrete AD results are sampling approximations to the continuous theory, with $\mathbb{Z}_M \to (\mathbb{R},+)$ as $M \to \infty$.

</details>
