### [488] [Deep Learning for Multi-Antenna Modulation Recognition of Radio Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00849)
*Tao Chen,Shilian Zheng,Jiepeng Chen,Zhangbin Pei,Qi Xuan,Xiaoniu Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes MAMR-IQ, a deep learning method for multi-antenna modulation recognition that concatenates raw IQ signals from multiple antennas into a CNN, outperforming existing methods. Also introduces a data augmentation technique for few-shot scenarios using antenna IQ sequence exchange.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning has advanced automatic modulation recognition in single-antenna systems, but its application in multi-antenna modulation recognition (MAMR) remains limited. The goal is to fully exploit diversity gain in multi-antenna systems.

Method: Concatenates raw in-phase and quadrature (IQ) signals from multiple antennas and feeds them into a convolutional neural network (CNN). For few-shot scenarios, proposes data augmentation by exchanging IQ sequences between any two antennas.

Result: MAMR-IQ outperforms existing deep learning-based MAMR methods (direct voting and weight average) in both recognition accuracy and computational complexity. The proposed data augmentation further improves recognition accuracy.

Conclusion: MAMR-IQ is an effective and efficient deep learning approach for multi-antenna modulation recognition, and the antenna-exchange augmentation method helps mitigate limited training data issues.

Abstract: Multi-antenna receiving systems have become a prevalent technical solution in communication systems. Meanwhile, deep learning has achieved significant progress in automatic modulation recognition tasks in single-antenna systems. However, the application of deep learning in multi-antenna modulation recognition (MAMR) tasks is still limited. In this paper, we propose an MAMR method namely MAMR-IQ to fully explore the diversity gain of a multi-antenna receiving system, which concatenates the raw received in-phase and quadrature (IQ) signals of multiple antennas and feeds them into a convolutional neural network. Simulation results show that the proposed MAMR-IQ method outperforms two existing deep learning-based MAMR methods which are based on direct voting (DV) and weight average (WA) in terms of both recognition accuracy and computational complexity. To address the problem of limited training data in few-shot scenarios, we further propose a data augmentation method that involves exchanging IQ sequences received by any two antennas to generate augmented samples. Simulation results show that with the proposed augmentation method, the recognition accuracy can be further improved.

</details>


### [489] [1BT: One-Block Transformer for EEG-Based Cognitive Workload Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00856)
*Stefanos Gkikas,Christian Arzate Cruz,Thomas Kassiotis,Giorgos Giannakakis,Raul Fernandez Rojas,Randy Gomez*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces 1BT, a One-Block Transformer for EEG-based cognitive workload assessment. It uses a minimal latent bottleneck with cross-attention and lightweight self-attention. Tested on 11 participants across three tasks, the model achieves high classification performance with under 0.5M parameters and 0.02 GFLOPs, enabling real-time monitoring on resource-constrained devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate and continuous cognitive workload estimation is essential for adaptive human-machine systems, but existing architectures struggle to balance representational capacity with computational efficiency for practical deployment.

Method: 1BT (One-Block Transformer) aggregates multi-channel EEG temporal sequences via a minimal latent bottleneck using a single cross-attention module followed by lightweight self-attention. A controlled study with 11 participants performing abstract reasoning, numerical problem-solving, and an interactive video game under two workload levels was conducted.

Result: The final model achieves high workload classification performance with under 0.5 million parameters and 0.02 GFLOPs. Systematic architectural analysis identified the most compact configuration preserving high performance.

Conclusion: The 1BT model demonstrates an efficient design direction for real-time cognitive workload monitoring in resource-constrained settings.

Abstract: Accurate and continuous estimation of cognitive workload is fundamental to creating adaptive human-machine systems. However, designing architectures that balance representational capacity with computational efficiency has been challenging for practical deployment. This paper introduces 1BT, a One-Block Transformer for compact and efficient EEG-based cognitive workload assessment. The model aggregates multi-channel temporal sequences via a minimal latent bottleneck, using a single cross-attention module followed by lightweight self-attention. A controlled study involving 11 participants performing three cognitively diverse tasks (abstract reasoning, numerical problem-solving, and an interactive video game) was conducted with continuous EEG recordings across two workload levels. Systematic architectural analysis identifies the most compact configuration that preserves high performance, while substantially lowering computational cost. The final model achieves high workload classification performance with under 0.5 million parameters and 0.02 GFLOPs, paving the way for a design direction for real-time cognitive workload monitoring in resource-constrained settings.

</details>


### [490] [Foundation Model Guided Dual-Branch Co-Adaptation for Source-Free EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00857)
*Peiliang Gong,Han Zhang,Zhen Jiang,Chenyu Liu,Ziyu Jia,Xinliang Zhou,Daoqiang Zhang,Xiaoli Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: FUSED integrates EEG Foundation Models with source-free domain adaptation via dual-branch co-adaptation, achieving state-of-the-art cross-subject EEG decoding across multiple paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SFDA methods rely on limited internal knowledge of source-pretrained models, causing poor cross-domain generalization and unreliable pseudo-labels. Meanwhile, EEG Foundation Models' potential in SFDA remains unexplored.

Method: A dual-branch co-adaptation framework (Foundation Model + Specialist Model) with Consensus Filtering, Two-Stage Pseudo-Label Refinement, and a calibrate-then-distill pipeline using mutual information maximization and knowledge distillation.

Result: Consistent state-of-the-art performance across motor imagery, emotion recognition, and SSVEP EEG paradigms.

Conclusion: Foundation-guided synergy via FUSED effectively enables robust, privacy-preserving cross-subject EEG decoding, and is the first work to leverage EEG FMs within SFDA.

Abstract: Source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) provides a practical solution to cross-subject EEG decoding by adapting source-pretrained models to unlabeled target domains without accessing source data. However, existing SFDA methods rely solely on the limited internal knowledge of source-pretrained models, leading to inferior cross-domain generalization and unreliable pseudo-labels. Although EEG Foundation Models (FMs) pretrained on large-scale data exhibit strong generalizability, their potential in SFDA remains largely unexplored. To this end, we propose FUSED, a Foundation-guided Source-free EEG Decoding framework that integrates a large-scale FM with a compact Specialist Model (SM) via dual-branch co-adaptation. Specifically, we introduce a Co-adaptation mechanism equipping both branches with linear and prototype views, enabling cross-branch pseudo-label generation. Additionally, we design a Consensus Filtering Mechanism that exploits the FM's inherent stability to identify high-quality samples, along with a Two-Stage Pseudo-Label Refinement scheme to suppress error accumulation through cross-branch arbitration. Finally, we calibrate the FM's decision boundaries via mutual information maximization with the SM, followed by knowledge distillation from FM to SM, forming a principled calibrate-then-distill pipeline. To our knowledge, FUSED is the first work to leverage EEG FMs within the SFDA framework for cross-subject EEG decoding. Extensive experiments across three EEG paradigms, including motor imagery, emotion recognition, and SSVEP, demonstrate consistent state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of foundation-guided synergy for robust and privacy-preserving EEG decoding.

</details>


### [491] [A Hybrid Windkessel-Neural Approach for Improved Noninvasive Blood Pressure Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00858)
*Vaibhav Gollapalli,Aniruth Ananthanarayanan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid approach combining Windkessel models (physics-based) with machine learning to improve cuffless blood pressure estimation from wearable devices, addressing the interpretability and physiological validity issues of pure ML models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cuffless BP estimation uses ML models with high numerical accuracy but lacks interpretability, physiological validity, and clinical applicability. Cuff-based methods are unsuitable for continuous monitoring due to inconvenience, invasiveness, calibration needs, and size.

Method: The authors combine Windkessel (a physical model) with ML by reformulating Windkessel into a system of ODEs that can be integrated into neural networks, embedding physical constraints into the data-driven approach.

Result: The proposed technique is demonstrated using the MIMIC-II database from UCI Machine Learning Repository.

Conclusion: Incorporating physical constraints (Windkessel) into ML models improves consistency with physics, interpretability, and robustness for cuffless BP estimation.

Abstract: Owing to the recent advancements in wearable devices for health care, the importance of BP estimation without cuffs increases. Cuff technologies are inappropriate for continuous BP measurement due to their inconvenient usage, invasive character, necessity of calibration, large size, and inability to perform long-term monitoring. Normally, the algorithm used for cuffless BP prediction employs machine learning models that operate according to the data-driven approach. However, although they show high numerical accuracy, ML models do not provide any interpretability, resulting in poor physiological validity and clinical applicability. We propose a combination of Windkessel and ML models that incorporates the physical aspects into the latter one. It is performed by reformulating Windkessel into a form that will allow employing ML models. The result is a system of ODEs which can be used in the neural network. Thus, the inclusion of physical constraints improves the data-driven approach by making models consistent with physics, understandable, and robust. For illustration, we apply the described technique using a publicly available MIMIC-II database that we obtain from the UCI Machine Learning Repository.

</details>


### [492] [How Well Can We Decode Vowels from Auditory EEG -- A Rigorous Cross-Subject Benchmark with Honest Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00865)
*Xiaoyang Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a reproducible cross-subject benchmark for decoding five vowels (a, e, i, o, u) from auditory EEG under strict leave-one-subject-out evaluation. The best model (XGBoost) achieves 24.5% accuracy (chance 20%). Vowel information is real but weak, mainly carried by early transient auditory responses. Code and scripts are released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior EEG-based phoneme decoding studies suffer from within-subject evaluation, small sample sizes, and weak leakage control. The authors aim to establish a rigorous, reproducible cross-subject benchmark to properly assess the true decoding performance.

Method: 14 pipelines from classical ML, deep learning, and Riemannian methods are compared on OpenNeuro ds006104 (16 subjects, 61 channels, 256 Hz) using strict leave-one-subject-out evaluation with training-only normalization and anti-leakage checks. Additional analyses include ablation studies, pairwise vowel decoding, within-subject CV, ERP analysis, temporal generalization, and electrode importance.

Result: Best full-feature model (XGBoost): 24.5% accuracy (chance 20%). Differential entropy features with LightGBM achieve 25.5%. After multiple comparison correction, strong pairwise model advantages are limited. Classical methods are competitive with deep models in this low signal-to-noise regime.

Conclusion: Vowel information in auditory EEG is real but weak, primarily encoded in early transient auditory responses. Cross-subject decoding remains challenging. The study emphasizes the need for rigorous evaluation standards and provides reproducible benchmarks for future work.

Abstract: EEG based phoneme decoding is promising for brain computer interfaces, but many prior studies rely on within subject evaluation, small cohorts, or weak leakage control. We present a reproducible cross subject benchmark for five class vowel decoding (a, e, i, o, u) from auditory EEG using OpenNeuro ds006104 (16 subjects, 61 channels, 256 Hz). Under strict leave one subject out evaluation with training only normalization and explicit anti leakage checks, we compare 14 pipelines from classical machine learning, deep learning, and Riemannian methods. The best full feature model (XGBoost) reaches 24.5 percent accuracy (chance 20 percent), while differential entropy features with LightGBM reach 25.5 percent in feature specific analysis. After multiple comparison correction, strong pairwise model advantages are limited. Classical methods are competitive with deep models in this low signal regime. Additional analyses (ablation, pairwise vowels, within subject CV, ERP, temporal generalization, and electrode importance) indicate that vowel information is real but weak and mainly carried by early transient auditory responses. We release code and evaluation scripts for full reproducibility.

</details>


### [493] [Robust Cross-Domain WiFi Fall Detection via Physics-Driven Attention-Enhanced Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00869)
*Yingzhe Wang,Cunhua Pan,Ruijing Liu,Shaokai Li,Hong Ren,Kezhi Wang,Jiangzhou Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a robust, domain-generalizable WiFi-based fall detection framework using an Attention-Enhanced CNN-Transformer hybrid architecture with a physics-driven Dynamic Variance Gate and Physics-Aware Data Augmentation, achieving 97.6% accuracy in NLoS and 98.8% in unseen environments, validated on edge computing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning approaches for WiFi CSI-based fall detection suffer severe performance degradation in unseen environments due to static background overfitting and NLoS signal attenuation, motivating the need for a robust, domain-generalizable solution.

Method: Proposes a three-component framework: (1) Dynamic Variance Gate (DVG) that calculates local temporal variance as a soft-attention mask to eliminate static DC components and amplify motion; (2) Physics-Aware Data Augmentation for learning invariant morphological signatures; (3) CBAM-integrated CNN-Transformer hybrid for spatiotemporal feature refinement and sequence modeling.

Result: 97.6% accuracy in NLoS scenarios and 98.8% in completely unseen environments without target-domain fine-tuning, validated across four distinct indoor environments with real-world edge deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates robust cross-domain generalization for WiFi-based fall detection, enabling practical, privacy-preserving, low-latency whole-home elderly safety monitoring on commercial edge hardware.

Abstract: Device-free fall detection utilizing WiFi Channel State Information (CSI) has emerged as a promising, privacy-preserving solution for elderly health monitoring in the Internet of Things (IoT) era. However, existing deep learning approaches suffer from severe performance degradation when deployed in unseen environments due to static background overfitting and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) signal attenuation. To address these critical bottlenecks, we propose a robust, domain-generalizable framework featuring a novel Attention-Enhanced CNN-Transformer hybrid architecture. First, we design a physics-driven \textbf{Dynamic Variance Gate (DVG)} to dynamically calculate local temporal variance, acting as a soft-attention mask that eliminates static environmental DC components while amplifying dynamic human motion. Second, we introduce a Physics-Aware Data Augmentation strategy to force the network to learn invariant morphological signatures rather than environment-specific noise. Furthermore, a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) is integrated to refine spatiotemporal features prior to Transformer-based sequence modeling. Extensive cross-domain evaluations across four distinct indoor environments demonstrate that our method achieves 97.6\% accuracy in NLoS scenarios and 98.8\% in completely unseen environments without target-domain fine-tuning. Finally, we deploy the proposed framework on an edge computing system equipped with commercial WiFi NICs. Real-world live inference field tests confirm the system's robustness against unseen environmental layouts and its capability for continuous, low-latency whole-home safety monitoring.

</details>


### [494] [An Algorithm for On-Sensor Agnostic Detection of Changes in Human Activity for Ultra-Low-Power Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00870)
*Sara Rimoldi,Arianna De Vecchi,Hazem Hesham Yousef Shalby,Federica Villa*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A lightweight change-detection gate using dynamic template matching reduces computational load of HAR on wearables by over 67% by invoking the full network only when activity changes, achieving ~98% sensitivity and ~75% specificity without training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable HAR systems waste energy performing continuous classification even during long periods of unchanged activity.

Method: A lightweight, non-parametric change-detection gate based on dynamic template matching (~16k FLOPs/step) that requires no training and no predefined activity classes, with a brief device-specific calibration phase.

Result: Reduces computational load by over 67%; achieves 98% sensitivity/75% specificity on UCA-EHAR, and 97% sensitivity/76% specificity on WISDM.

Conclusion: The proposed gate enables efficient, practical HAR on wearables by reliably detecting activity transitions while minimizing unnecessary full-network invocations.

Abstract: Wearable devices running Human Activity Recognition(HAR) on Inertial Measurement Units~(IMUs) waste energy by performing continuous classification for each window, even during long periods of unchanged activity. We address this with a lightweight change-detection gate: a non-parametric algorithm based on dynamic template matching that runs continuously at only approximately 16kFLOPs per step, requires no offline training, and does not need prior definition of target activity classes. The gate invokes the full HAR network only when it detects an activity change, reducing the computational load by over 67% in realistic monitoring settings. The algorithm is evaluated on smart glasses, smartwatch, and smartphone data, requiring only a brief device-specific calibration phase. The gate achieves 98% sensitivity on UCA-EHAR, ensuring no genuine activity transition is missed, while 75% specificity keeps unnecessary HAR invocations low. Results on WISDM are 97% sensitivity and 76% specificity, demonstrating robustness and flexibility to various settings.

</details>


### [495] [NAKUL-Med: Spectral-Graph State Space Models with Dynamics Kernels for Medical Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00871)
*Badri N. Patro,Vijay S. Agneeswaran*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: NAKUL extends state space models for multi-channel physiological signals via dynamic kernel generation, spectral context modeling, and graph-guided spatial attention, achieving 91.7% accuracy on motor imagery BCI with 28% fewer parameters and 2x faster inference than EEG-Conformer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SSMs struggle with multi-channel physiological signals due to three limitations: fixed kernels cannot capture multi-scale temporal dynamics (e.g., motor preparation vs. execution transients), Markovian state updates restrict global context for periodic oscillations, and channel-independent processing ignores spatial electrode topology.

Method: Three contributions: (1) Dynamic Kernel Generation - parallel SSM branches with varying kernel sizes (3,5,7,11) weighted by a meta-network for adaptive temporal scaling; (2) Spectral Context Modeling - FFT-based operations with learnable Gaussian frequency band filters for global periodic patterns; (3) Graph-Guided Spatial Attention - fixed electrode topology provides spatial biases to multi-head attention.

Result: 91.7±0.6% on BCI Competition IV-2a motor imagery, matching EEG-Conformer (92.1±0.7%) with 28% fewer parameters (2.5M vs 3.5M) and 2.0× faster inference (4.3ms vs 8.7ms). Generalizes to EEG emotion (83.6%), multimodal EEG-fMRI (91.4%), and ultrasound (92.8%). Ablations: dynamic kernels contribute +2.6%.

Conclusion: NAKUL effectively addresses SSM limitations for medical signals, matching SOTA with better efficiency, and demonstrates interpretable scale selection patterns correlated with known neural dynamics, plus architectural versatility across modalities.

Abstract: State space models (SSMs) achieve linear-time complexity but struggle with multi-channel physiological signals due to three limitations: fixed kernels cannot capture multi-scale temporal dynamics (motor preparation over hundreds of milliseconds vs. execution transients in tens of milliseconds), Markovian state updates restrict global context for periodic oscillations, and channel-independent processing ignores spatial electrode topology. We introduce NAKUL, extending SSMs for medical signal analysis through three contributions: (1) Dynamic Kernel Generation-parallel SSM branches with varying kernel sizes (3, 5, 7, 11 timesteps) are weighted by a meta-network that analyzes input statistics, enabling adaptive temporal scale selection; (2) Spectral Context Modeling-FFT-based operations with learnable Gaussian frequency band filters capture global periodic patterns in $O(N \log N)$ complexity; (3) Graph-Guided Spatial Attention-fixed electrode topology provides spatial biases to multi-head attention for principled cross-channel interaction. On BCI Competition IV-2a motor imagery (our primary benchmark), NAKUL achieves 91.7$\pm$0.6\% accuracy, matching EEG-Conformer (92.1$\pm$0.7\%) while using 28\% fewer parameters (2.5M vs 3.5M) and 2.0$\times$ faster inference (4.3ms vs 8.7ms). The model generalizes to EEG emotion recognition (83.6\%), multimodal EEG-fMRI (91.4\%), and medical imaging (92.8\% on ultrasound), demonstrating architectural versatility. Ablations show dynamic kernels contribute +2.6\% and exhibit interpretable scale selection patterns correlated with known neural dynamics.

</details>


### [496] [Multi-View Hierarchical Representation Learning of Fetal Hemodynamics for Maternal Hypertension Detection at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00872)
*Alireza Rafiei,Anahí Venzor Strader,Esteban Castro Aragón,Victoriana Rosibely Sut Serech,Enma Carolina Coyote Ixen,Reza Sameni,Peter Rohloff,Gari D. Clifford,Nasim Katebi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: AutoHyPE, a hierarchical attention network analyzing fetal Doppler ultrasound data from 3,255 pregnant women, detects maternal hypertension with 0.80 AUROC, demonstrating that fetal cardiac activity encodes maternal hemodynamic status for scalable, low-cost prenatal monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hypertensive disorders during pregnancy remain a major cause of maternal/fetal morbidity worldwide, but current diagnosis relies on intermittent cuff-based blood pressure measurements that are biased and cannot capture continuous physiological dynamics.

Method: Proposed AutoHyPE - a hierarchical attention network with prototype-based contrastive learning and multi-view strategy, trained on a large-scale dataset of 3,255 women (8,170 visits) with fetal Doppler ultrasound recordings paired with maternal blood pressure measurements.

Result: AutoHyPE achieved 0.80 AUROC for maternal hypertension detection, outperforming baselines with balanced class performance and no degradation in edge deployment scenarios.

Conclusion: Fetal cardiac mechanical activity contains hemodynamic features indicative of maternal hypertension status, supporting a paradigm shift toward continuous, objective monitoring of maternal health using low-cost ultrasound technology as a complementary approach to traditional blood pressure measurements.

Abstract: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy remain a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity worldwide, yet diagnosis relies on intermittent cuff-based blood pressure measurements that are prone to bias and fail to capture continuous physiological dynamics. Growing evidence suggests that fetal cardiovascular activity is associated with maternal-placental hemodynamics and may encode markers of maternal hypertension. To analyze this, we collected a large-scale dataset of fetal one-dimensional Doppler ultrasound recordings paired with maternal blood pressure from 3,255 pregnant women across 8,170 antenatal visits in rural Guatemala. We developed AutoHyPE, a hierarchical attention network that models short- and long-term signal structure, incorporating a novel prototype-based contrastive learning and multi-view strategy to enhance representation robustness under long-tailed class distribution and biological variability. AutoHyPE achieved an AUROC of 0.80 for maternal hypertension detection, outperforming baseline approaches while maintaining balanced performance across classes, with no performance degradation in an edge deployment scenario. Our findings demonstrated that fetal cardiac mechanical activity contains hemodynamic features indicative of maternal hypertension status. This supports a promising paradigm shift toward continuous, objective monitoring of maternal health using existing, low-cost ultrasound technology and introduces a complementary approach to traditional methods based on blood pressure measurements, advancing scalable prenatal care.

</details>


### [497] [SPAT: A Semantic Port-Aware Adaptive-Rate Transmission Protocol for Semantic Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00897)
*Yunhao Wang,Shuai Ma,Bin Shen,Shouhan Shi,Youlong Wu,Guangming Shi,Xiang Cheng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: SPAT is a semantic communication protocol that embeds port info into semantic representations, uses differentiated uplink/downlink processing, and adaptively adjusts transmission rates based on channel conditions and feature importance, outperforming TCP/UDP/SITP in reconstruction quality and latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing transport mechanisms rely on explicit port headers and bit-level validation, making them vulnerable to header corruption and packet loss, which is problematic for semantic communication in 6G networks.

Method: SPAT jointly embeds source/destination port information into semantic representations to reduce header dependency; uses port identification for uplink and destination-aware conditional gating for downlink; and incorporates an adaptive-rate controller that dynamically adjusts transmitted semantic channels based on channel conditions and feature importance.

Result: On AFHQ and ImageNet-10 datasets with real-world measurements, SPAT consistently outperforms TCP, UDP, and SITP in reconstruction quality across different SNRs while maintaining low-latency transmission.

Conclusion: SPAT effectively addresses header corruption issues in semantic communication by embedding port information into semantic representations and adaptively controlling transmission rates, achieving superior robustness and efficiency compared to traditional protocols.

Abstract: With the evolution of 6G, semantic communication has emerged as a promising paradigm by prioritizing the delivery of task-relevant meaning over strict bit-level correctness. However, existing transport mechanisms still rely on explicit port headers and bit-level validation, making them vulnerable to header corruption and the resulting packet loss. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Semantic Port-Aware Adaptive-Rate Transmission Protocol (SPAT) for semantic communication. The proposed framework jointly embeds source and destination port information into semantic representations, thereby reducing dependence on explicit port headers while enabling robust port-aware transmission. Furthermore, a differentiated semantic processing mechanism is developed for uplink and downlink scenarios, where port identification is introduced for uplink service recognition and destination-aware conditional gating is designed for downlink selective decoding. In addition, an adaptive-rate controller is incorporated to dynamically adjust the number of transmitted semantic channels according to channel conditions and feature importance, thereby improving both robustness and transmission efficiency. Experimental results on the AFHQ and ImageNet-10 datasets, together with real-world experimental measurements, demonstrate that SPAT consistently outperforms TCP, UDP, and SITP in reconstruction quality across different SNRs while maintaining low-latency transmission.

</details>


### [498] [Transfer Learning for Tonal Noise Prediction in VRF Units Using Thermodynamic and Vibration Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00895)
*ZhiWei Su,Ding Wang,Yuan Guo,Yang Qiao,HongJun Cao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes Domain-invariant Partial Least Squares (Di-PLS), an unsupervised transfer learning method, to accurately predict second-order harmonic noise from twin-rotary compressors in VRF outdoor units under varying conditions, achieving superior performance (within 3 dB error) using acceleration signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The 2f harmonic noise from twin-rotary compressors strongly fluctuates with environmental thermal load and valve opening, making accurate prediction difficult with conventional mechanism-based models.

Method: Domain-invariant Partial Least Squares (Di-PLS) — an unsupervised transfer learning approach that extracts cross-condition common features and minimizes distribution discrepancy between source and target domains.

Result: Di-PLS significantly outperforms traditional PLS; acceleration-based Di-PLS achieves best performance with prediction errors within 3 dB for all test cases.

Conclusion: Structural vibration has a stronger and more direct causal link to acoustic radiation than thermodynamic states, despite thermodynamics driving dynamic changes.

Abstract: The second-order harmonic (2f) component generated by twin-rotary compressor is a dominant low-frequency noise source of variable refrigerant flow (VRF) outdoor units, yet its amplitude fluctuates strongly with environmental thermal load and valve opening, making it difficult to assess accurately using conventional mechanism-based models. This paper proposes an unsupervised transfer learning method based on Domain-invariant Partial Least Squares (Di-PLS) to accurately predict 2f noise levels under new conditions using different signals. Prediction models utilizing thermodynamic signals and acceleration signals are constructed respectively, and the generalization performance of the proposed Di-PLS is systematically compared with traditional Partial Least Squares (PLS). Results demonstrate that Di-PLS significantly outperforms PLS by extracting cross-condition common features and minimizing the distribution discrepancy between the source and target domains. Specifically, the acceleration-based Di-PLS model achieves the best performance, maintaining prediction errors within 3 dB for all test cases. This superiority over thermodynamic-based models highlights a physical insight: while thermodynamic states drive dynamic changes, structural vibration possesses a stronger and more direct causal link to acoustic radiation.

</details>


### [499] [A Deep Learning Model for Battery State Prediction towards Intelligent Energy Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00898)
*Athanasios Koukosiasa,Vasileios Tzanidakis,Sotiris Athanasiou,Kostas Kolomvatsos*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Deep Learning framework for forecasting battery health indicators (remaining capacity and lifetime) to enable predictive maintenance and efficient energy management in applications like electric vehicles and energy storage systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate forecasting of battery health indicators is critical for ensuring reliability, safety, and operational efficiency of electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems.

Method: A dedicated computational framework integrating advanced neural network architectures with large-scale training datasets for modeling battery degradation dynamics and operational trends.

Result: The DL-based predictive modeling approach shows potential for significantly contributing to sustainable and intelligent energy management systems.

Conclusion: Deep Learning predictive modeling can serve as a decision support mechanism for optimal battery management, enabling predictive maintenance and efficient energy resource allocation.

Abstract: Accurate forecasting of battery health indicators, including remaining capacity and lifetime, is of paramount importance for ensuring the reliability, safety, and operational efficiency of applications such as electric vehicles and large scale energy storage infrastructures. The result of the forecasting can be adopted to build an advanced monitoring mechanism for continuous checking batteries' health status to assist in the efficient real-time management of numerous applications. This research investigates the development and implementation of a Deep Learning (DL) model for the prediction of the future state and performance of industrial electrochemical energy storage systems. To address this challenge, we propose a dedicated computational framework that integrates advanced neural network architectures with large-scale training datasets, enabling precise modeling of batteries degradation dynamics and operational trends. The proposed approach provides a decision support mechanism for the optimal management of batteries facilitating both predictive maintenance and the efficient allocation of energy resources. Our findings highlight the potential of DL-based predictive modeling to significantly contribute to the advancement of sustainable and intelligent energy management systems.

</details>


### [500] [Floor Plan-Agnostic Detection of Gait Speed Drifts Using Ambient Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00900)
*Marina Vicini,Martin Rudorfer,Zhuangzhuang Dai,Ahmad Beltagui,Luis J. Manso*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a floor plan-agnostic method for detecting gait speed drifts in older adults using sparse ambient sensors, eliminating the need for home floor plans. It uses sensor-to-sensor transitions and non-parametric statistical tests, showing performance comparable to or better than methods requiring floor plans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gait speed is a critical health indicator for older adults, but existing ambient sensor-based methods require home floor plans, which is often impractical for real-world deployment.

Method: The method identifies informative sensor-to-sensor transitions, analyzes duration fluctuations, applies non-parametric statistical tests to compare recent periods to baseline, and aggregates daily results for robust drift detection.

Result: Evaluated on simulated datasets across four home layouts, the method achieves performance comparable to or exceeding state-of-the-art baselines that require floor plan information.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates a scalable, cost-effective approach for gait drift detection without floor plans, laying groundwork for validation in real-world environments.

Abstract: Gait speed is a vital health indicator for older adults, as changes in gait speed can reflect physiological and functional decline. Ambient sensors offer a promising, privacy-preserving solution for continuous in-home monitoring of gait speed; although it is often limited by methods requiring a home floor plan, which is frequently unfeasible. This paper proposes a novel, floor plan-agnostic method to detect gait speed drifts using only sparse ambient sensors. Our approach identifies informative sensor-to-sensor transitions and analyses fluctuations in their duration. For each sequence a non-parametric statistical test detects changes between a recent period and an initial baseline; and daily test results are aggregated to provide a robust drift detection response. We evaluate our method on a simulated dataset across four different home layouts, showing performance comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, a state-of-the-art baseline that requires floor plan information. This work demonstrates a feasible approach for scalable, cost effective gait drift detection monitoring, providing a foundation for future validation in complex real-world environments.

</details>


### [501] [Toward Real-Time Circadian Phase Estimation with Low Latency from Wearable Sensing Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00910)
*Mengzhu Xu,Nemanja Cabrilo,Merel van Gilst,Jean-Paul Linnartz*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a low-latency framework for real-time estimation of human circadian phase using short historical windows of wearable data (e.g., light exposure and physical activity), achieving a mean CMAE of 1.19 h with tree-based models plateauing at ~8 hours of history.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time, low-latency circadian phase estimation on edge wearable devices without requiring full circadian cycle recordings or retrospective analysis.

Method: A low-latency framework using cosinor-fitted core body temperature as reference, evaluated with different sensor modalities, window lengths, and model classes (tree-based vs. sequence-based) on free-living data from 14 participants.

Result: Estimation accuracy saturates at ~8 hours of history; tree-based models plateau after 480 min, while sequence-based models benefit from longer contexts; using only light and activity, mean CMAE = 1.19 h.

Conclusion: Real-time circadian phase estimation is feasible with short wearable data windows, providing practical guidance for deployable monitoring on edge devices.

Abstract: Accurate estimation of the human circadian phase plays an important role in personalized health monitoring, but most existing wearable-based approaches operate retrospectively and require full circadian cycle recordings, leading to high estimation latency and substantial data and computational burden for real-time deployment on edge devices.
  In this study, we investigated whether circadian phase can be estimated in real time using only short historical windows of wearable data. We propose a low latency framework that estimates instantaneous circadian phase from past observations, with a cosinor-fitted core body temperature rhythm serving as the reference.
  Data from a free-living field study involving 14 participants were used to systematically evaluate the effects of sensor modality selection, historical window length, and model class under participant-based cross-validation. The results showed that estimation accuracy improves with increasing window length but saturates at approximately 8 hours of history. Tree-based models reached a performance plateau beyond 480 minutes, whereas sequence-based models continued to benefit from longer temporal contexts. When relying solely on light exposure and physical activity, the proposed approach achieved a mean circular mean absolute error (CMAE) of 1.19 h.
  These findings provide practical guidance for efficient and deployable real-time circadian phase monitoring using wearables.

</details>


### [502] [Equation-Free Digital Twins for Nonlinear Structural Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00950)
*Mohammad Mahdi Abaei,Ahmad BahooToroody,Arttu Polojärvi,Heikki Remes,Ulf Tyge Tygesen,Mikko Suominen,Michael Beer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a rank-optimized digital twin framework using Koopman operator theory and Hankel-matrix embeddings for real-time structural state reconstruction under non-stationary, nonlinear, and stochastic conditions. Validated on an NREL 5MW floating offshore wind turbine, it achieves high-fidelity virtual sensing (R² > 0.95 at 1 Hz, > 0.99 at higher rates) without requiring prior knowledge of mass/stiffness matrices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monitoring high-dimensional engineering structures in extreme environments is limited by non-stationary excitation, nonlinear kinematics, and stochastic forcing. Traditional model-based and black-box data-driven methods struggle in real time, especially under sensor failure or partial observability.

Method: A rank-optimized digital twin framework based on Koopman operator theory, Hankel-matrix embeddings, and dynamic mode decomposition. It lifts operational data into a linear invariant subspace, enabling autonomous, input-blind reconstruction without a priori mass or stiffness matrices.

Result: On an NREL 5MW spar-buoy floating offshore wind turbine: separates structural resonances from deterministic 3P rotor harmonics under colored noise (where standard subspace identification fails). Achieves R² > 0.95 at 1 Hz and > 0.99 at higher sampling rates. Estimates physical Lyapunov time ~1.0 s, defining the predictability horizon.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a computationally efficient and resilient digital twin approach for real-time identification and virtual sensing of complex structural dynamics in extreme environments.

Abstract: Monitoring high-dimensional engineering structures in extreme environments is limited by non-stationary excitation, nonlinear structural kinematics, and stochastic forcing. Traditional model-based and black-box data-driven methods often struggle to resolve these dynamics in real time, particularly under sensor failure or partial observability. This paper introduces a rank-optimized digital twin framework based on Koopman operator theory, Hankel-matrix embeddings, and dynamic mode decomposition. By lifting operational data into a linear invariant subspace, the method enables autonomous, input-blind reconstruction of structural states without requiring a priori mass or stiffness matrices.
  The framework is validated on an NREL 5MW spar-buoy floating offshore wind turbine, representing a challenging coupled aero-hydro-servo-elastic system. Results show that the rank-optimized Koopman-Hankel manifold separates structural resonances from deterministic 3P rotor harmonics under colored noise, where standard subspace identification can be unreliable. A rolling-horizon virtual sensing strategy achieves high-fidelity reconstruction at critical structural hotspots, with coefficient of determination greater than 0.95 at 1 Hz data assimilation and accuracy exceeding 0.99 at higher sampling rates. By estimating a physical Lyapunov time of approximately 1.0 s, the study defines the predictability horizon associated with the system information barrier. The proposed framework provides a computationally efficient and resilient digital twin approach for real-time identification and virtual sensing of complex structural dynamics.

</details>


### [503] [Adaptive 3D-RoPE: Physics-Aligned Rotary Positional Encoding for Wireless Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00968)
*Chenyu Zhang,Xinchen Lyu,Chenshan Ren,Shuhan Liu,Qimei Cui*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes Adaptive 3D-RoPE, a physics-aligned rotary positional encoding for wireless foundation models that dynamically adapts to channel conditions, achieving up to 10.7 dB NMSE reduction under 8x scale extrapolation and improving zero-shot generalization across unseen scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CSI models inherit static or one-dimensional positional priors from NLP and vision, which misalign with wireless channel physics by lacking explicit relative decay, collapsing the 3D spatio-temporal-frequency structure, and remaining scenario-rigid.

Method: Proposes Adaptive 3D-RoPE with a learnable, axis-decoupled 3D frequency bank for disentangling multi-dimensional phase dependencies, plus a lightweight channel-conditioned controller that dynamically modulates the prior via compact global CSI descriptors, enabling sample-adaptive positional encoding.

Result: Achieves up to 10.7 dB NMSE reduction under 8x antenna scale extrapolation, 1.07 dB zero-shot improvement across unseen mobility scenarios, and 0.90 dB in low-frequency-to-millimeter-wave tasks over 100 datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed physics-aligned positional encoding transforms static positional encoding into a dynamic, coherence-aware inductive bias that resolves heterogeneous channel physics and establishes a structural cornerstone for wireless foundation models.

Abstract: Positional encoding plays a pivotal role in determin?ing the extrapolation and generalization performance of wireless foundation models for channel state information (CSI) modeling, latent characterization, and task-specific prediction. However, existing CSI models inherit static or one-dimensional positional priors from natural language and vision architectures, which fundamentally misalign with the intrinsic physics of wireless channels by lacking explicit relative decay, collapsing the 3D spatio-temporal-frequency structure, and remaining scenario?rigid. This paper proposes Adaptive 3D-RoPE, a physics-aligned rotary positional encoding that establishes the structural corner?stone for wireless foundation models. The framework integrates a learnable, axis-decoupled 3D frequency bank to explicitly disentangle multi-dimensional phase dependencies, coupled with a lightweight channel-conditioned controller that dynamically modulates the prior via compact global CSI descriptors. This sample-adaptive mechanism transforms positional encoding from a static transformer component into a dynamic, coherence-aware inductive bias to resolve heterogeneous channel physics. Extensive experiments across 100 datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed scheme in both scale extrapolation and zero-shot generalization. Compared to the state-of-the-art, our method achieves up to a 10.7 dB reduction in normalized mean square error (NMSE) under 8 times antenna scale extrapolation. Given the same CSI input scales, our method can also improve zero-shot NMSE by 1.07 dB across unseen mobility scenarios and 0.90 dB in low-frequency-to-millimeter-wave tasks.

</details>


### [504] [Integrating acoustic tapping with a UAV platform for tile condition classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01068)
*Piedad J. Miranda,Ronan Reza,Leonel Lagos,Mackenson Telusma,Christine A. Langton,Fernando Moreu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper studies how UAV-induced vibrations affect acoustic tap-testing for building tile inspection, and proposes an energy-based signal correction method combined with PCA to restore classification accuracy to over 98% under dynamic perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensuring structural integrity of building tiles is important for public safety and urban infrastructure durability, but UAV-based acoustic inspection faces accuracy degradation due to motion-induced vibrations.

Method: A controlled experimental framework using a Stewart platform to simulate UAV flight oscillations, with energy-based signal correction and PCA for dimensionality reduction and classification of acoustic data.

Result: Classification accuracy degrades significantly under increasing perturbations but can be restored above 98% using the proposed energy-based filtering approach.

Conclusion: The proposed energy-based correction method effectively preserves classification performance under UAV-induced motion disturbances, enabling reliable facade inspection.

Abstract: Ensuring the structural integrity of building tiles is important for public safety and the durability of urban infrastructure. This study proposes a controlled experimental framework to quantify the effect of Unmanned Aerial vehicle (UAV) induced dynamic perturbations on acoustic tap-testing reliability for facade inspection. This work explicitly analyzes vibration-induced degradation and introduces an energy-based signal correction method to preserve classification performance under motion disturbances. In addition, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to process and classify wirelessly acquired acoustic data, reducing dimensionality while preserving key defect related features. A Stewart platform is used to reproduce controlled oscillatory conditions derived from UAV flight characterization, enabling systematic evaluation across multiple vibration amplitudes. Results show that classification accuracy degrades significantly under increasing perturbations, but can be restored above 98% using the proposed energy-based filtering approach.

</details>


### [505] [Propagation Mechanism-Aware Near-Field Spatially Non-Stationary Channel Estimation and Environment Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01162)
*Yuan Liu,Xuesong Cai,Dipankar Saha,M. R. Bhavani Shankar,Björn Ottersten*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a GC-SAGE algorithm for near-field spatially non-stationary channel estimation and environment mapping in ELAA-ISAC systems, validated by ray-based simulation and field measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extremely large aperture arrays (ELAAs) enable high-throughput ISAC but face challenges from near-field spherical wavefront effects and spatial non-stationarity (SNS). Existing methods do not jointly account for multi-bounce, blockage-induced partial visibility, and hybrid reflection-scattering propagation in a unified manner.

Method: A unified parametric sensing channel model is proposed that captures SNS through spatially varying visibility and amplitude of multipath components. Geometric constraints (GCs) based on environmental interaction points are embedded as absolute propagation delays. A GC-space-alternating generalized expectation-maximization (GC-SAGE) algorithm is developed for joint near-field channel parameter estimation and scatterer/reflector localization.

Result: The proposed approach is validated using both ray-based simulation and field measurement data, demonstrating effective detection of channel SNS and accurate environment mapping.

Conclusion: The GC-SAGE algorithm successfully addresses near-field SNS challenges in ELAA-ISAC by jointly estimating channel parameters and mapping environmental scatterers/reflectors, with validation from both simulation and real-world measurements.

Abstract: Extremely large aperture arrays (ELAAs) benefit the dual functions of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems by enabling high-throughput data streams and high angular resolution with near-field spatial diversity. However, near-field spherical wavefront effects and spatial non-stationarity (SNS) bring challenges to both communication and sensing. This paper studies near-field spatially non-stationary channel estimation and environment mapping by jointly accounting for multi-bounce, blockage-induced partial visibility, and hybrid reflection-scattering propagation. We propose a unified parametric sensing channel model that represents the SNS phenomenon (due to partial array blockage, diffraction, and specular reflection) through spatially varying visibility and amplitude of each multipath across the array. To regularize the spatially varying delays caused by propagation mechanisms, we incorporate geometric constraints (GCs) based on environmental interaction points, embedding them into the model as absolute propagation delays. We then develop a GC-space-alternating generalized expectation-maximization (GC-SAGE) algorithm to estimate near-field channel parameters and locate environment scatterers/reflectors. Moreover, the GC-SAGE calculates per antenna path amplitudes based on the delays determined by the coordinates of scatterers/reflectors and transceivers, thereby effectively detecting channel SNS. Both ray-based simulation and field measurement are used to validate the proposed approach.

</details>


### [506] [AULAs: A Novel Family of Augmented ULAs for Enhanced Localization of Non-Circular Sources with Reduced Mutual Coupling Effects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01228)
*Abdul Hayee Shaikh,Xiaoguang Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces augmented ULAs (AULAs) and their variants for improved DOA estimation of non-circular signals by jointly optimizing both difference and sum co-arrays, achieving larger virtual aperture and more degrees of freedom.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sparse arrays optimize only for the difference co-array, underutilizing the sum co-array from non-circular signals; state-of-the-art designs suffer from a three-way performance trade-off.

Method: AULAs combine one sparse ULA, two dense ULAs, and two separate elements to splice holes/lags in both co-arrays. Variants include SAULAs (minimizing redundancy), TSAULAs (reducing mutual coupling), and Co-TSAULAs (combining benefits). All share a unified design framework with closed-form expressions.

Result: The proposed designs achieve larger virtual aperture and increased degrees of freedom for non-circular signal localization. Simulation results validate effectiveness.

Conclusion: The AULA family provides flexible, configurable sparse array designs that jointly optimize sum and difference co-arrays, overcoming prior trade-offs and supporting convenient aperture extension.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce a family of novel sparse array designs called the augmented ULAs (AULAs) for the localization of non-circular signals (NCS). Accurate direction of arrival (DOA) estimation and the ability to resolve multiple targets are critical in modern wireless communication systems. Most existing sparse arrays are optimized solely for the difference co-array, making them less efficient at utilizing the sum co-array resulting from the non-zero pseudo-covariance of NCS. Meanwhile, state-of-the-art designs for joint optimization of the sum and difference co-arrays remain constrained by a three-way performance trade-off. The proposed AULAs configure single sparse and two dense ULAs alongside two separate elements to achieve a perfect splicing of holes and lags in the difference and sum co-array. This results in a larger virtual aperture and increased DOFs for NCS. Building on this structure, other variants of AULAs are developed, each exhibiting distinct characteristics. The shifted AULAs (SAULAs) judiciously displace the AULAs structure to minimize co-array redundancy and further enhance the DOFs. A transformed SAULAs (TSAULAs) design is proposed, which mitigates mutual coupling effects by converting the dense ULAs of SAULAs into sparse ULAs. By reconfiguring the elements of TSAULAs, the complementary TSAULAs (Co-TSAULAs) design inherits the desirable properties of SAULAs and TSAULAs.All these structures belong to a unified design framework, within which one configuration can be adapted into another during the design phase to meet different performance requirements. Meanwhile, they provide in-built physical locations for convenient extension to a larger aperture. Closed-form expressions for precise element placements, DOFs, and weight functions are derived. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

</details>


### [507] [Antenna Coding Design for Pixel Antenna Empowered Rate-Splitting Multiple Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01267)
*Haobo Huang,Yijie Mao,Hongyu Li,Shanpu Shen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper combines pixel antennas with rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) to improve spectral efficiency in MU-MISO systems, proposing a joint precoding and antenna coding design optimized via alternating optimization and an efficient codebook-based selection algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance spectral efficiency in multi-user systems by integrating controllable analog-domain pixel antennas with digital-domain RSMA interference management under imperfect CSI.

Method: Proposes a pixel antenna empowered RSMA framework with joint precoding and antenna coding design, solved via alternating optimization combining WMMSE and SEBO, plus an efficient online codebook-based antenna coder selection.

Result: The proposed approach significantly improves spectral efficiency over RSMA with fixed antennas and SDMA with the same pixel antennas. RSMA also achieves similar performance with simpler antenna configurations or smaller codebooks compared to SDMA.

Conclusion: Pixel antenna empowered RSMA is a promising technique for enhancing spectral efficiency, with advantages in antenna complexity and codebook size over SDMA.

Abstract: This work explores the integration of pixel antennas and rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) to enhance spectral efficiency in multi-user multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) systems. Pixel antennas offer controllable antenna characteristics via antenna coding from the analog domain, whereas RSMA provides efficient interference management from the digital domain. We propose a novel pixel antenna empowered RSMA transmission framework where each user employs a pixel antenna. Under imperfect channel state information at the transmitter, we formulate a joint precoding and antenna coding design problem to maximize the ergodic sum-rate. An alternating optimization algorithm based on the weighted minimum mean square error (WMMSE) approach and the successive exhaustive Boolean optimization (SEBO) is first developed to solve the problem. We then propose an efficient online antenna coder selection algorithm relying on an offline-designed codebook to reduce computational complexity. Numerical results show that the proposed pixel antenna empowered RSMA significantly improves spectral efficiency compared to both RSMA with fixed antennas and space-division multiple access (SDMA) employing the same pixel antenna configuration. Moreover, compared to SDMA, RSMA maintains the same performance with a simpler pixel antenna configuration or a smaller codebook size.

</details>


### [508] [Spectral- and Energy-efficient Multi-BS Multi-RIS Pinching-antenna Systems: A GNN-based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01307)
*Changpeng He,Yang Lu,Wei Chen,Bo Ai,Arumugam Nallanathan,Zhiguo Ding*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a three-stage graph neural network (GNN) to jointly optimize PA placement, RIS phase shifts, transmit beamforming, and BS-UE association in multi-BS multi-RIS pinching-antenna systems for sum rate and energy efficiency maximization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To maximize sum rate and energy efficiency in coordinated downlink transmission within a complex multi-BS multi-RIS pinching-antenna system, which involves highly coupled mixed-variable optimization.

Method: A three-stage GNN integrating heterogeneous and homogeneous graph representations, trained end-to-end in an unsupervised manner.

Result: The proposed GNN outperforms representative baselines, generalizes well to unseen numbers of UEs, RISs, and BSs, maintains millisecond-level inference time, and shows performance gains increase with more PAs.

Conclusion: The proposed three-stage GNN design is effective for optimizing the multi-BS multi-RIS PA system, and PAs enhance both SR and EE.

Abstract: This paper investigates coordinated downlink transmission in a multi-base station (multi-BS) multi-reconfigurable intelligent surface (multi-RIS)-assisted pinching-antenna (PA) system, where each user equipment (UE) is associated with a single BS and each BS is equipped with movable PAs deployed on parallel waveguides. We formulate sum rate (SR) and energy efficiency (EE) maximization problems by jointly optimizing PA placement, RIS phase shifts, transmit beamforming, and BS-UE association under constraints of inter-PA spacing, power budget, and unit-modulus phase shift. To address the resulting highly coupled mixed-variable problem, we propose a three-stage graph neural network (GNN) that integrates heterogeneous and homogeneous graph representations and is trained end-to-end in an unsupervised manner. Extensive numerical results demonstrate that the proposed three-stage GNN consistently outperforms representative system and learning baselines, generalizes well to unseen numbers of UEs, RISs, and BSs, and maintains millisecond-level inference time. Besides, the results validate the effectiveness of the proposed design from both system and architectural perspectives. Moreover, PAs are shown to enhance SR and EE, and the performance gain is enlarged with increasing number of PAs.

</details>


### [509] [Analysis and Compensation of Tx and Rx IQ Imbalances in AFDM System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01328)
*Hongjun Liu,Liaoyuan Zeng,Junhao Tian,Qingyu Li,Fuchen Xu,Chengxiang Liu,Guanghui Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper investigates the impact of joint transmitter and receiver in-phase/quadrature imbalance (IQI) on AFDM systems, showing AFDM suffers more severe IQI than OFDM/OTFS. It derives analytical BER expressions and proposes a cascade compensation scheme that effectively mitigates the error floor caused by IQI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AFDM is a promising new multicarrier waveform with competitive BER performance in doubly selective channels, but its susceptibility to hardware impairments like IQI has not been studied. Understanding and mitigating IQI effects is crucial for practical AFDM deployment.

Method: The authors derive analytical expressions for pairwise and average bit error probability as functions of IQI parameters, and propose a cascade compensation scheme that first compensates Rx IQI (converting improper Gaussian noise to AWGN) then eliminates Tx IQI through judicious design.

Result: AFDM suffers more severe IQI than OFDM and OTFS due to its chirp-assisted modulation. Joint Tx and Rx IQI introduce an error floor in BER performance. The proposed cascade compensation effectively mitigates these impairments.

Conclusion: IQI significantly limits AFDM's achievable SNR and data rates, but the proposed compensation scheme can effectively eliminate both Tx and Rx IQI effects, making AFDM more practical for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) is a recently proposed multicarrier waveform whose bit error rate (BER) performance in doubly selective channels is comparable to that of orthogonal time-frequency space (OTFS) and superior to that of orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM). In this paper, the impacts of joint transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx) in-phase and quadrature imbalance (IQI) on AFDM signals are investigated, where we show that AFDM suffers more severe IQI than OFDM and OTFS due to the inherent feature of complicated chirp-assisted modulation. We further derive analytical expressions for the pairwise and average bit error probability as a function of the IQI parameters. These indicate that such distortions significantly limit the achievable operating signal-to-noise ratio at the receiver side and data rates. To this end, we propose a cascade compensation scheme to mitigate these effects. Specifically, we first compensate for Rx IQI to convert the improper Gaussian noise into additive white Gaussian noise, and then apply a judicious design to eliminate the Tx IQI. Both analytical and simulation results reveal that joint Tx and Rx IQI introduce an error floor in the BER performance of AFDM systems, whereas the proposed approach effectively compensates such impairments.

</details>


### [510] [MU-SHOT-Fi: Self-Supervised Multi-User Wi-Fi Sensing with Source-free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01369)
*Ahmed Y. Radwan,Hina Tabassum*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: MU-SHOT-Fi is a source-free unsupervised domain adaptation framework for single- and multi-user WiFi CSI-based human activity recognition, using permutation-invariant set prediction, Hungarian matching, occupancy-weighted information maximization, binary rotation prediction, and contrastive predictive coding to handle domain shifts across environments, frequencies, and orientations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models for WiFi-based HAR generalize poorly across environments, especially in multi-user settings where overlapping activities cause CSI entanglement and domain shifts. Privacy constraints limit access to labeled source data, motivating the need for source-free unsupervised domain adaptation.

Method: MU-SHOT-Fi uses permutation-invariant set prediction with Hungarian matching during source training, followed by frozen-classifier backbone adaptation in the target domain. Key techniques: (1) occupancy-weighted information maximization to prevent model collapse, (2) binary rotation prediction as spatial self-supervision, and (3) for single-user (SU-SHOT-Fi), contrastive predictive coding for temporal consistency.

Result: Extensive experiments on WiMANS and Widar 3.0 datasets show MU-SHOT-Fi effectively recovers multi-user exact-activity classification under large domain shifts (cross-environment, cross-frequency, cross-orientation), while maintaining accurate occupancy estimation and preventing collapse toward dominant classes.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses source-free domain adaptation in multi-user WiFi sensing, demonstrating robust performance under various domain shifts without requiring access to labeled source data.

Abstract: Deep learning has been widely adopted for WiFi CSI-based human activity recognition (HAR) due to its ability to learn spatio-temporal features in a privacy-preserving and cost-effective manner. However, DL-based models generalize poorly across environments, a challenge amplified in multi-user settings where overlapping activities cause CSI entanglement and domain shifts. Practical deployments often limit access to labeled source data due to privacy constraints, motivating source-free adaptation using only unlabeled target-domain CSI and a pre-trained source model. In this paper, we propose MU-SHOT-Fi, a source-free unsupervised domain adaptation framework for single- and multi-user Wi-Fi sensing. MU-SHOT-Fi employs permutation-invariant set prediction with Hungarian matching during source training, followed by frozen-classifier backbone adaptation in the target domain. To enable stable adaptation without labels, we introduce occupancy-weighted information maximization that prevents model collapse by focusing diversity regularization on likely-occupied slots while excluding the dominant class from marginal entropy. Additionally, we employ binary rotation prediction as spatial self-supervision that exploits CSI frequency-time structure to learn domain-invariant features. For single-user scenarios, we introduce SU-SHOT-Fi by replacing occupancy weighting with standard information maximization and incorporating contrastive predictive coding to exploit temporal consistency. Extensive experiments on the WiMANS and Widar 3.0 datasets across cross-environment, cross-frequency, cross-orientation, and combined domain shifts demonstrate that MU-SHOT-Fi effectively recovers multi-user exact-activity classification performance under large domain shifts while maintaining accurate occupancy estimation and preventing collapse toward dominant classes.

</details>


### [511] [Doppler Tomography Using Rydberg Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01499)
*Peter Vouras,Bariscan Yonel,Alexandra Artusio-Glimpse*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper explores using Rydberg atom-based quantum sensors for tomographic imaging, introducing a method to create nulls in images via local oscillator modulation and a deblurring algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To apply Rydberg sensor technology, which leverages electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) in alkali atoms excited to Rydberg states, to tomographic imaging applications through modeled system dynamics.

Method: The authors model the system dynamics of Rydberg sensors for tomographic imaging. They propose modulating the radiated local oscillator (LO) used for phase recovery to place nulls in the image, and develop a deblurring algorithm.

Result: A novel null-placement method via LO modulation and an image deblurring algorithm for Rydberg sensor-based tomographic imaging.

Conclusion: Rydberg quantum sensors can be effectively applied to tomographic imaging, with new techniques for image manipulation (null placement) and enhancement (deblurring).

Abstract: Novel sensors that leverage the quantum properties of atoms for measuring propagating electromagnetic fields are becoming increasingly practical for a variety of applications. These sensors rely on the phenomenon of electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT), which is induced in a confined vapor of alkali atoms when the atoms are excited to a high-energy quantum state, known as a Rydberg state, with multiple resonant optical fields. In this state, the atoms are highly sensitive to electromagnetic radiation and yield a measurement output proportional to the magnitude of an impinging electric field when resonant with a Rydberg-Rydberg transition. In this paper, we consider the use of Rydberg sensors for a tomographic imaging application through a set of modeled system dynamics. Our contribution includes a novel method for placing nulls in the image by modulating the radiated local oscillator (LO) that is used to recover phase information from the received signal. We also present an algorithm for deblurring the image.

</details>


### [512] [Benchmarking Wireless Representations: High-Dimensional vs. Compressed Embeddings for Efficiency and Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02009)
*Murilo Batista,Shirin Salehi,Saeed Mashdour,Paul Zheng,Rodrigo C. de Lamare,Anke Schmeink*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes trade-offs between high-dimensional learned channel embeddings and compressed autoencoder-based representations, benchmarking them across tasks including LoS/NLoS classification, beam selection, and a newly introduced power allocation task, finding that compressed representations offer better noise robustness and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the cost-benefit trade-offs of high-dimensional channel embeddings in practical wireless systems, particularly regarding data efficiency, noise robustness, and computational complexity.

Method: Benchmarking multiple wireless representations: high-dimensional learned embeddings from a foundation model, compact autoencoder-based representations, and raw data baselines across diverse downstream tasks including power allocation as a new task.

Result: High-dimensional embeddings perform well in few-shot regimes but incur high latency and parameter overhead; compressed autoencoder representations show better noise robustness, stable performance, and lower computational/transmission costs.

Conclusion: There are clear trade-offs between representation dimensionality and performance; compressed latent representations are more practical for real-world deployment due to efficiency and robustness advantages.

Abstract: Building on recent advances in representation learning for wireless channels, this work investigates the cost-benefit trade-offs of high-dimensional channel embeddings in practical systems. We benchmark multiple wireless representations: high-dimensional learned embeddings from a wireless foundation model, compact autoencoder-based representations with significantly lower dimensionality, and raw data baselines, evaluating their performance across diverse downstream tasks. We then systematically analyze data efficiency, noise robustness, and computational complexity, explicitly characterizing the resource overhead associated with high-dimensional embeddings. Beyond standard tasks such as line-of-sight/non-line-of-sight (LoS/NLoS) classification and beam selection, we introduce power allocation as a new downstream task. Our results reveal clear trade-offs: while high-dimensional embeddings can perform well in few-shot regimes for certain tasks, they incur substantial latency and parameter overhead. In contrast, compressed latent representations learned by autoencoders demonstrate improved noise robustness and more stable performance across tasks, while significantly reducing computational and transmission costs.

</details>


### [513] [A Time-Synchronized Video Reference System for Data Analysis of Body-Attached Sensor Nodes in Outdoor Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01540)
*Lukas Schulthess,Fabian Pleisch,Matheo Käch,Björn P. Bruhin,Michele Magno,Luca Benini,Christoph Leitner*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A lightweight Timecode Generator (TCG) using GNSS-derived time to produce Linear Timecode (LTC) for camera audio channels, enabling low-power, unobtrusive synchronization of wearable multi-sensor systems without continuous handshaking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing wireless synchronization methods for wearable body-attached multi-sensor systems either lack objective visual validation (field scenarios) or are impractical (laboratory systems). Commercial video timecode solutions require continuous device-to-device handshaking, increasing power consumption and hindering small, unobtrusive hardware designs.

Method: The proposed TCG converts GNSS-derived time directly into an LTC signal injected into a camera's audio channel. It supports 24, 25, and 30 fps frame rates. The system eliminates continuous handshaking and can be activated immediately before recording, reducing power consumption.

Result: Accurate time alignment maintained for several minutes without GNSS updates. At 30 fps, alignment lasts 543 s before potential frame-level shift. Average power consumption is 35.37 mW, achieving up to 75 h operating time with two AAA batteries.

Conclusion: The TCG provides a low-power, field-deployable solution for synchronizing wearable sensor data with video reference, enabling smaller batteries and unobtrusive hardware designs for outdoor and unconstrained movement analysis.

Abstract: Wearable body-attached multi-sensor systems enable detailed analysis of human motion and physiological signals in sports, rehabilitation, and movement research. While wireless synchronization techniques can reliably align sensor data streams, interpreting and validating complex or unconstrained activities often requires an additional, objective visual reference. Existing laboratory-grade reference systems provide high accuracy but are impractical for outdoor or field deployments. In contrast, commercial video timecode solutions typically rely on local device-to-device synchronization, which increases the power required to maintain synchronization. This is not desirable in many application scenarios. This paper presents a lightweight Timecode Generator (TCG) that converts Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-derived time directly into a Linear Timecode (LTC) signal that is injected into the recording via a camera audio channel. The approach eliminates continuous handshaking, allowing the system to be activated immediately before the action of interest, thus reducing power consumption and enabling smaller batteries and unobtrusive hardware designs of body-attached sensor nodes. The TCG supports common video frame rates of 24, 25, and 30 frames per second (fps). Experimental evaluation confirms that accurate time alignment is maintained for several minutes without GNSS updates. At 30 fps, the alignment duration is 543 s before a potential frame-level shift occurs. With an average power consumption of 35.37 mW, the system achieves an operating time of up to 75 h when powered by two standard AAA alkaline batteries.

</details>


### [514] [The AECM Algorithm for Deterministic Maximum Likelihood Direction Finding in the Presence of Gaussian Mixture Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02309)
*Mingyan Gong,Bin Lyu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an AECM algorithm for DOA estimation in Gaussian mixture noise, which updates DOA estimates sequentially and achieves faster stable convergence compared to the SAGE algorithm, with similar per-iteration complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The existing SAGE algorithm for DOA estimation in Gaussian mixture noise cannot properly converge under unequal signal powers, motivating the need for a more robust and convergent algorithm.

Method: The authors apply the Alternating Expectation-Conditional Maximization (AECM) algorithm, an extension of SAGE, which uses multiple less informative complete data versions and golden section search to update DOA estimates sequentially (one by one).

Result: The AECM algorithm has almost the same per-iteration computational complexity as SAGE, but yields faster stable convergence and is computationally more efficient, especially under unequal signal powers.

Conclusion: The AECM algorithm is a superior alternative to SAGE for DOA estimation in Gaussian mixture noise, offering better convergence properties and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Gaussian mixture noise can model non-Gaussian noise and also be used when outliers are present. For deterministic maximum likelihood direction finding in Gaussian mixture noise, the Space-Alternating Generalized Expectation-maximization (SAGE) algorithm, an extension of the expectation-maximization algorithm, was applied and designed by Kozick and Sadler twenty odd years ago, which simultaneously updates direction of arrival (DOA) estimates at each iteration and cannot properly converge under unequal signal powers. In this article, the Alternating Expectation-Conditional Maximization (AECM) algorithm, an extension of the SAGE algorithm, is applied and designed, which utilizes multiple less informative versions of the complete data and the golden section search method to update DOA estimates at each iteration sequentially (one by one). Theoretical analysis shows that the AECM algorithm has almost the same computational complexity of each iteration as the SAGE algorithm. However, numerical results show that the AECM algorithm yields faster stable convergence and is computationally more efficient.

</details>


### [515] [Physics-Guided Deep Learning For High Resolution X-ray Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01543)
