*Shao Xian Lee,Aashwin Ananda Mishra,Ariel Arnott,Meriame Berboucha,Nina Boiadjieva,Gourab Chatterjee,Eric Cunningham,Nick Czapla,Gilliss Dyer,Jonathan Ehni,Robert Ettelbrick,Anna Grassi,Mickael Grech,Philip Hart,Dimitri Khaghani,Hae Ja Lee,Peregrine McGehee,Bob Nagler,Paul Neumayer,Caterina Riconda,Marc Welch,Andrea Zabala,Eric Galtier,Quynh L. Nguyen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a Physics-Guided Deep Learning (U-Net) approach to remove structured, non-stationary artifacts in X-ray transmission reconstructions for HED/IFE experiments, outperforming traditional flat-field normalization, Fourier filtering, and Dynamic Flat-Field Normalization, achieving significant SSIM improvement (0.345→0.906, 0.0679→0.945) while using deep ensembles for uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imperfections in X-ray imaging systems introduce structured, non-stationary features that degrade single-shot HED/IFE experimental data. Standard flat-field normalization fails to remove these artifacts when shot-to-shot drift occurs, biasing measurements of electron density, velocity, and feature sizes.

Method: Modeled artifacts as a separable feature layer, trained a U-Net architecture to estimate and infer artifacts directly from experimental data, and used deep ensembles for epistemic uncertainty estimates.

Result: Achieved SSIM improvement from 0.345 to 0.906 and from 0.0679 to 0.945 in synthetic injection tests, outperforming Fourier filtering and Dynamic Flat-Field Normalization, with better filament profile preservation.

Conclusion: Physics-Guided Deep Learning effectively suppresses structured artifacts while preserving signal fidelity, with deep ensembles providing OOD robustness for reliable single-shot X-ray diagnostics.

Abstract: Imperfections in X-ray imaging systems can limit their performance, especially in High Energy Density (HED) or Inertial Fusion Energy (IFE)-relevant experiments that are typically single shot, by introducing structured, non-stationary features that overlap with the signal of interest. When the X-ray transmission is reconstructed by typical flat-field normalization, even small shot-to-shot drift of structured features imprints residual patterns onto transmission maps, degrading signal visibility and biasing measurements such as electron density, velocity and feature sizes. We investigate this limitation by modeling the artifacts as a separable feature layer and training a U-Net architecture to estimate and infer them directly from the experimental data. We compare our method against Fourier filtering and more advanced procedures like Dynamic Flat-Field Normalization (DFFN) to evaluate artifact suppression capability and signal preservation in the reconstructed transmission maps. In multiple synthetic injection tests, our Physics-Guided Deep Learning approach is able to obtain an improvement in mean Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) from 0.345 to 0.906 and from 0.0679 to 0.945, while better preserving filament profiles and reducing degradation of the filament signal during artifact suppression. Additionally, we utilize deep ensembles to obtain predictive epistemic uncertainty estimates for the U-Net based reconstruction, to ensure Out Of Distribution (OOD) robustness for this procedure.

</details>


### [516] [A Miniaturized In-Mouth pH Sensing System for Real-Time Intraoral Telemetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01545)
*Lukas Schulthess,Philipp Schilk,Julian Moosmann,Andrea Gubler,Christian Vogt,Florian J. Wegehaupt,Michele Magno*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents the first battery-powered, low-power wireless and wearable pH telemetry system for real-time intraoral pH monitoring using glass electrodes, replacing bulky analog amplification units with a miniaturized frontend, neck-worn BLE node, and dedicated software.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current gold standard for intraoral pH measurement uses glass electrodes but requires large analog amplification/recording units, limiting study design and participant comfort during lengthy measurements (up to an hour).

Method: A miniaturized pH telemetry frontend (8.6x3.3mm, 0.2g) integrated with a custom dental prosthesis digitizes pH signals directly in the mouth, transmits data via BLE to a neck-worn node (37.6g), and uses dedicated software for calibration, drift compensation, and reporting.

Result: The system achieves wireless real-time pH monitoring with 8.89 mW power consumption at 10 Hz data transmission rate, significantly reducing hardware footprint compared to traditional setups.

Conclusion: The proposed wearable system enables more practical, comfortable, and flexible intraoral pH monitoring for dental caries and erosion research, overcoming key limitations of existing measurement approaches.

Abstract: Dental caries is one of the most common chronic diseases worldwide, caused by acid production from bacterial metabolism of fermentable carbohydrates and affecting people of all ages. To evaluate the cariogenic and erosive properties of widely consumed food products, such as energy drinks, intraoral pH changes are measured during consumption. The gold standard for such measurements is miniaturized silicon-lithium-barium glass membrane electrodes. These electrodes allow dental plaque to form on their surface, thereby enabling in situ monitoring of pH changes in a biologically relevant environment. Due to their high impedance and susceptibility to external interference, they can currently only be measured using a large analog amplification and recording unit, which is highly limiting for study design and participant comfort, as individual measurements can take upwards of an hour. This work presents the first battery-powered, low power wireless and wearable pH telemetry evaluation system designed for real time intraoral pH monitoring with glass electrodes. The system comprises a miniaturized pH telemetry frontend, a neck-worn Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) node, and software tools for data acquisition, visualization, and reporting. The front end integrates with a custom dental prosthesis, directly digitizing the pH signal in the mouth and minimizing noise. The data is transmitted over BLE to a host computer, and analyzed using dedicated software that supports calibration, drift compensation, region marking, and PDF report generation. The system integrates an 8.6 by 3.3 mm, 0.2 g pH front-end and a 37.6 g neck-worn BLE node which consume 8.89 mW to transmit data at 10 Hz to a host computer during a measurement.

</details>


### [517] [Channel-Aware Waveform Selection Criteria Across Different Waveform Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01587)
*Hamza Haif,Abdelali Arous,Huseyin Arslan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a generalized channel model capturing complex propagation dynamics (birth-death, Doppler spreading, time-varying delays) and reveals that AFDM/OTFS only outperform under sparse ideal conditions, while OFDM/DFT-s-OFDM achieve superior reliability under realistic channel conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional sparse and quasi-stationary channel models used for 6G waveform evaluation obscure the true propagation complexity in dense urban, high mobility, and heterogeneous deployments, necessitating a more realistic modeling approach.

Method: Proposes a generalized scalable channel model with cluster birth-death dynamics, Doppler spectral spreading, time-varying delays, and piecewise local stationarity; derives effective input-output relationships for main 6G waveforms; develops a channel-aware waveform prioritization framework based on delay-Doppler resolvability, stationarity conditions, effective SINR, and UE cell distribution.

Result: AFDM and OTFS retain spectral efficiency advantage and path combining gains only under sparse, resolvable, stationary conditions; OFDM and DFT-s-OFDM can be tuned for superior reliability and more stable performance under the proposed realistic channel model.

Conclusion: Waveform selection for 6G must account for realistic channel complexity; simpler waveforms like OFDM and DFT-s-OFDM may be more robust in practical deployments than advanced waveforms like AFDM/OTFS.

Abstract: Waveform evaluation for sixth generation (6G) networks has largely relied on sparse and quasi-stationary channel models that enabled mathematical tractability, diversity gains, and Doppler robustness. However, such models obscure the propagation complexity of dense urban environments, high mobility scenarios, and heterogeneous network deployments. This paper sheds light on a generalized and scalable channel model that incorporates cluster birth-death dynamics, Doppler spectral spreading, time-varying delays, and piecewise local stationarity. Based on this model, the effective input-output relationships of the main 6G waveforms are derived, exposing waveform dependent interference structures that remain hidden under conventional sparse assumptions. Building on these effective channels, a channel-aware waveform prioritization framework is developed based on delay-Doppler resolvability, stationarity conditions, effective signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR), and user equipment (UE) cell distribution. Simulation results under the proposed channel model using 3GPP CDL parameters confirm that affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) and orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) retain their spectral efficiency advantage and path combining gains only under sparse, resolvable, stationarity conditions, whereas orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) and discrete Fourier transform spread (DFT-s)-OFDM can be both tuned to achieve superior reliability and more stable performance under the proposed channel model.

</details>


### [518] [Sparsity and Resolvability: Re-evaluating Channel Representations For Next Generation Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01588)
*Hamza Haif,Abdelali Arous,Huseyin Arslan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a signal processing framework for 6G wireless networks that uses receiver-observable indicators (power capture, coefficient correlation, effective resolution, leakage) to characterize channel sparsity, resolvability, and selectivity under high mobility, clustered scattering, and hardware impairments. It introduces an interchanged domain frame concept that adapts representation and component separation based on propagation regime, effective SNR, and application objectives, with case studies using the Extended Vehicular A channel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical assumptions on channel sparsity, resolvability, and stationarity fail under 6G conditions (high mobility, clustered scattering, hardware impairments). Performance assessments based on apparent sparsity or nominal delay/Doppler separation are misleading due to finite observation, sampling granularity, windowing, and fractional delay/Doppler spreading.

Method: Develops a signal processing framework linking sparsity, resolvability, and selectivity through receiver-observable indicators (power capture fraction, coefficient correlation, effective delay/Doppler resolution, processing-induced leakage). Proposes an interchanged domain frame concept that adapts representation and component separation based on propagation regime, effective SNR, and application objectives.

Result: Using the Extended Vehicular A channel profile, the framework shows that different representations lead to different equalization and detection behavior, with implications for communication, sensing, and physical layer security.

Conclusion: The proposed framework and interchanged domain frame concept enable more accurate performance assessment and adaptive representation selection for 6G wireless systems under challenging propagation conditions.

Abstract: As wireless networks transition toward 6G, high mobility, clustered scattering, and hardware impairments increasingly challenge classical assumptions on channel sparsity, resolvability, and stationarity. In these regimes, performance assessments based on apparent sparsity or nominal delay and Doppler separation can be misleading, since finite observation, sampling granularity, windowing, and fractional delay or Doppler spreading introduce coupling and leakage that reshape the effective channel seen by the receiver. This article provides a signal processing centric framework that links sparsity, resolvability, and selectivity through receiver observable indicators, including the fraction of power captured by dominant coefficients, the level of coefficient correlation, the effective delay and Doppler resolving capability over the observation window, and processing induced leakage. Building on these observations, we propose an interchanged domain frame concept principle, where the representation and the degree of component separation are adapted according to the propagation regime, the effective SNR under impairments, and the application objective. Using the Extended Vehicular A channel profile as a running case study, we show how different representations lead to different equalization and detection behavior, with implications for communication, sensing, and physical layer security.

</details>


### [519] [Why Model Selection Fails in Time Series Forecasting: An Empirical Study of Instability Across Data Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01608)
*Tahir Cetin Akinci,Alfredo A. Martinez-Morales*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper investigates why rule-based model selection fails in time series forecasting, finding that simple descriptor-driven heuristics achieve low accuracy and cannot generalize across different data regimes and forecasting horizons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time series forecasting models show inconsistent performance across datasets, and it's unclear whether model selection can be reliably guided by simple data characteristics.

Method: The authors introduce a descriptor-based framework characterizing time series by trend strength, seasonality, noise level, and temporal dependence, then formulate a rule-based selection mechanism mapping data regimes to candidate models, evaluated on multiple real-world datasets.

Result: Rule-based model selection achieves low accuracy with correct identification in only a small fraction of cases, with significant discrepancies between recommended and optimal models, especially in noisy and mixed regimes.

Conclusion: Simple heuristic rules fail to generalize, and model selection in time series forecasting is inherently context-dependent, requiring more adaptive, data-driven strategies.

Abstract: Time series forecasting models often exhibit inconsistent performance across datasets with varying statistical and structural properties. Despite the wide range of available forecasting techniques, it remains unclear whether model selection can be reliably guided by simple data characteristics. This paper investigates why rule-based model selection fails in time series forecasting by analyzing the relationship between data-regime descriptors and model performance. A descriptor-based framework is introduced to characterize time series using measurable properties, including trend strength, seasonality, noise level, and temporal dependence. Based on these descriptors, a rule-based selection mechanism is formulated to map data regimes to candidate forecasting models. The approach is evaluated on multiple real-world datasets across different domains and forecasting horizons. The results show that rule-based model selection achieves low accuracy, with correct model identification occurring in only a small fraction of cases. Significant discrepancies are observed between recommended and empirically optimal models, particularly in noisy and mixed regimes. Further analysis reveals that model performance is highly sensitive to both dataset characteristics and forecasting horizon, resulting in substantial ranking instability across scenarios. These findings explain why simple heuristic rules fail to generalize and demonstrate that forecasting performance cannot be reliably predicted using static, descriptor-based approaches. This study provides empirical evidence that model selection in time series forecasting is inherently context-dependent and highlights the need for more adaptive, data-driven strategies.

</details>


### [520] [A Wideband Narrow Beam 1x6 Linear Antenna Array for Automotive Radar and 5G Millimetre-Wave Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01631)
*Muhammad Asfar Saeed,Augustine O. Nwajana*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a 1x6 linear microstrip patch antenna array for automotive radar and 5G mm-wave applications, achieving high directivity, narrow beamwidth, and wide bandwidth through optimized inter-element spacing and impedance matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a compact, high-performance beamforming antenna system for high-resolution automotive radar (e.g., V2V, lane change detection, blind spot monitoring at 28 GHz) and 5G mm-wave communications (e.g., point-to-point wireless backhaul).

Method: A 1x6 linear microstrip patch antenna array is designed with six rectangular radiating patches. The primary patch is excited via a microstrip feedline, and the remaining patches are interconnected through narrow 0.1 mm microstrip lines for power distribution. Optimal inter-element spacing enables constructive/destructive interference for narrow beam formation.

Result: Reflection coefficient better than 10 dB across the target frequency range; narrow beamwidth with high directivity; favorable current distribution and radiation patterns.

Conclusion: The proposed design provides a promising compact solution for high-performance beamforming antenna systems in intelligent transportation and next-generation 5G wireless networks.

Abstract: This paper presents the design and performance analysis of a 1x6 linear microstrip patch antenna array tailored for automotive radar and 5G millimetre-wave (mm-wave) applications. The proposed antenna array comprises six rectangular radiating patches with the primary patch excited using a microstrip feedline, while the remaining patches are interconnected through narrow microstrip lines with a width of 0.1 mm, enabling effective power distribution along the array. Optimal inter-element spacing facilitates constructive and destructive interference, enabling the formation of a narrow beam with enhanced directivity and a wide operational bandwidth. The high-gain radiation characteristics are achieved through the combined effects of the six-element linear configuration and precise impedance matching. Key performance metrics including reflection coefficient, current distribution, and radiation patterns have been analysed. Results demonstrate a reflection coefficient better than 10 dB across the target frequency range and a narrow beamwidth with high directivity, making the array suitable for high-resolution automotive radar and 5G mm-wave communications. Potential applications include vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) radar sensing, lane change detection, blind spot monitoring at 28 GHz, and high-capacity point-to-point wireless backhaul links. The design offers a promising solution for compact, high-performance beamforming antenna systems in intelligent transportation and next-generation wireless networks.

</details>


### [521] [Coherence-Minimized Sensing Matrix Design for MRI Reconstruction via Dual-Space Projection Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01753)
*Siyuan Feng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes PAQ, a dual-space projection framework that reshapes the equivalent dictionary to reduce mutual coherence in CS-MRI under Cartesian undersampling, combining a random rotator (Q) and an orthogonalization projector (P) to improve compressed sensing MRI reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CS-MRI suffers from severe structural coherence between Fourier measurements and spatial sparsity bases (e.g., DCT) under highly accelerated Cartesian undersampling, which violates the low-coherence requirement for classical sparse recovery and causes stagnation.

Method: A dual-space projection framework (PAQ) with: (1) a diagonal-dominant random rotator (Q) in feature space to disrupt structural alignment, and (2) an active orthogonalization projector (P) in measurement space to whiten residual correlations. The approach reshapes the equivalent dictionary rather than designing heuristic sampling masks.

Result: Theoretical guarantee that the mechanism bounds mutual coherence with exponentially decaying tail. Empirical results on clinical MRI datasets under 20% Cartesian sampling show that PAQ preconditioners plugged into ISTA significantly suppress aliasing artifacts and yield consistent PSNR improvements.

Conclusion: The dual-space mechanism effectively mitigates the coherence bottleneck in CS-MRI, offering a principled alternative to heuristic mask design for improving reconstruction quality under high acceleration.

Abstract: Compressed sensing magnetic resonance imaging (CS-MRI) heavily relies on the low mutual coherence between the measurement matrix and the sparsity basis. However, under highly accelerated Cartesian undersampling, the severe structural coherence between Fourier measurements and spatial bases, discrete cosine transform (DCT) for example, fundamentally violates this requirement, causing classical sparse recovery algorithms to stagnate. To mitigate this fundamental bottleneck, we propose a synergistic dual-space projection framework, denoted as $\mathbf{PAQ}$. Instead of merely designing heuristic sampling masks, our method directly reshapes the equivalent dictionary. Specifically, we introduce a diagonal-dominant random rotator $\mathbf{Q}$ in the feature space to probabilistically disrupt structural alignment, and an active orthogonalization projector $\mathbf{P}$ in the measurement space to deterministically whiten the residual correlations. We theoretically demonstrate that this dual-space mechanism bounds the mutual coherence with an exponentially decaying tail via sub-exponential distribution properties. Experimental validations on clinical MRI datasets under 20\% Cartesian sampling demonstrate that plugging the $\mathbf{PAQ}$ preconditioners into the standard ISTA solver significantly suppresses aliasing artifacts and yields consistent peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) improvements.

</details>


### [522] [Data driven approach for Outdoor Channel Prediction in 5G and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01777)
*A. Sathi Babu,V. Udaya Sankar,Vishnu Ram OV*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a data-driven channel estimation method using machine learning (Linear Regression, SVR, Decision Tree) with ray-tracing data at 7GHz for 5G+ networks, achieving best MAE of 7.52×10⁻⁵ and RMSE of 9.29×10⁻⁵ with Linear Regression, deployable as a Digital Twin.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional pilot-based channel estimation methods increase computational and communication complexity. A data-driven approach is needed to reduce overhead and improve efficiency in 5G and beyond wireless networks.

Method: Ray tracing is used to generate channel data at 7GHz. Three ML models—Linear Regression, Support Vector Regression, and Decision Tree Regression—are trained using transmitter location and user location as features, with channel coefficient as the target.

Result: Linear Regression outperforms the other models, achieving MAE of 7.5155×10⁻⁵ and RMSE of 9.2861×10⁻⁵.

Conclusion: A data-driven approach with Linear Regression is effective for channel estimation in 5G+ networks, and the framework can be deployed as a Digital Twin, reducing complexity compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: An evolution of Wireless Communications towards 5G and beyond provides improved user experience in terms of quality of services. Understanding and estimating Channel information plays crucial role in providing better user experience. Traditional methods of channel estimation involves periodically sending pilots (known signals), estimating channel and send back estimated channel information to the BS which increases computational complexity and communication complexity. Hence, we focus on data driven approach for channel estimation. This work can be deployed as Digital twin in 5G and beyond wireless networks. In this work, we explore a channel estimation mechanism at 7GHz frequency band for a given user location. This work involves data generation using Ray tracing mechanism and Machine learning model training that contains feature variables such as transmitter location, user location and target variable as channel coefficient . We explored Linear Regression, Support Vector Regression and Decision Tree Regression. We found via simulations that Linear Regression performs (with MAE of $\mathbf{7.5155\times10^{-5}}$ and RMSE of $\mathbf{9.2861\times10^{-5}}$) better than Support Vector Regression and Decision Tree Regression.

</details>


### [523] [Discover Fast Power Allocation Solution for Multi-Target Tracking via AlphaEvolve Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01794)
*Zhenkang Hou,Wenqiang Pu,Junkun Yan,Rui Zhou,Hongwei Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes AlphaEvolve, an LLM-guided evolutionary search method that autonomously discovers a closed-form power allocation solution for multi-target tracking, achieving near-optimal performance (1.51% loss) with 1000x speedup over iterative solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar resource allocation is computationally expensive with iterative optimization; there is a need for real-time scheduling, robust generalization, and low data dependency.

Method: AlphaEvolve uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to evolve a compact, interpretable scoring function from physically-inspired features, then applies a constraint-satisfying transformation to yield feasible power allocations.

Result: Near-optimal tracking accuracy (1.51% relative performance loss), reliable generalization across diverse scenarios and target counts, and over 1000x speedup compared to conventional iterative solvers.

Conclusion: LLM-guided symbolic search shows strong potential for revolutionizing radar resource management and broader engineering optimization problems.

Abstract: Efficient radar resource allocation is a fundamental yet computationally challenging problem, as optimal solutions typically require iterative optimization with high complexity. Motivated by the need for real-time scheduling, robust generalization, and low data dependency, this paper proposes a novel paradigm that leverages large language model (LLM)-guided evolutionary search (AlphaEvolve) to autonomously discover a closed-form power allocation solution for multi-target tracking. The approach encodes high-dimensional radar states into physically inspired features, then evolves a compact and interpretable scoring function, which is transformed to feasible power allocations via a deterministic constraint-satisfying transformation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the discovered closed-form solution achieves near-optimal tracking accuracy (average relative performance loss of only $1.51\%$), reliable generalization across diverse scenarios and target counts, and over three orders of magnitude speedup compared to conventional iterative solvers. These results highlight the potential of LLM-guided symbolic search to revolutionize not only radar resource management but also broader classes of engineering optimization problems.

</details>


### [524] [Waveform Index Modulation for Backscatter Communications in RIS-Based MIMO Radars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01838)
*Mehri Nikzad,Luca Venturino,Emanuele Grossi,Xiaodong Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a symbiotic RIS system that simultaneously assists radar transmission and conveys information to a reader via backscattering, using subarray partitioning, orthogonal phase codes for MIMO radar, and index modulation for low-complexity communication without channel state information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate communication and radar functionalities in a symbiotic RIS system, enabling information transmission via backscattering without degrading radar performance.

Method: The RIS is partitioned into subarrays that redirect radar signals and superimpose slow-time modulation via orthogonal phase codes. Information is encoded by selecting an unordered subset of orthogonal codewords (index modulation), preserving the transmit beampattern.

Result: Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed backscatter communication approach.

Conclusion: The proposed index modulation scheme enables low-complexity detection at the reader without requiring channel state information, while maintaining MIMO radar operations.

Abstract: This paper studies a symbiotic system in which a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) assists a radar transmitter while conveying information to a reader via backscattering. The RIS is partitioned into subarrays that redirect the radar signal toward the angular sector under inspection and superimpose a slow-time modulation using orthogonal phase codes, thereby implementing MIMO radar functionalities. Communication is achieved by encoding information in the selection of an unordered subset of orthogonal codewords, without altering the RIS transmit beampattern. At the reader, the proposed index modulation scheme enables low-complexity detection without requiring channel state information. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed backscatter communication approach.

</details>


### [525] [Beyond Data-Physics Consistency: A Cross-Correlated Physics-Informed Neural Network for Robust Inverse Scattering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01851)
*Shilong Sun*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CC-PINN introduces cross-correlated residuals, Fourier feature MLP with weight normalization, and FFT acceleration to overcome traditional PINN limitations in electromagnetic inverse scattering, achieving robust high-contrast target reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional PINN methods for ISP rely on independent data-equation and state-equation residuals, causing local minima and low computational efficiency for high-contrast targets or multi-frequency data. A new paradigm beyond the data-physics consistency framework is needed.

Method: (1) Fourier feature MLP with weight normalization; (2) Cross-correlated residual term directly coupling dielectric parameters/internal total field to external observation field; (3) Zero-padding-based 2D-FFT acceleration for Green's function integration.

Result: CC-PINN reconstructs high-contrast dielectric targets with high fidelity on both synthetic and measured data. Convergence robustness far exceeds classical PINN algorithms under both multi-frequency and frequency-hopping strategies.

Conclusion: The cross-correlated physics-informed framework effectively breaks the decoupling issue in traditional PINNs for ISP, significantly enhancing robustness and fidelity for challenging inverse scattering problems.

Abstract: The electromagnetic inverse scattering problem (ISP), due to its inherent strong nonlinearity and severe ill-posedness, has long been a core challenge in microwave imaging. In recent years, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have provided a novel paradigm for solving ISPs by embedding Maxwell's equations into the deep learning optimization process. However, conventional PINN methods rely solely on independent data-equation and state-equation residuals to construct the consistency loss, which easily causes them to fall into local minima and suffer from low computational efficiency when facing high-contrast targets or multi-frequency observation data. To transcend the traditional data-physics consistency framework, this paper proposes a novel cross-correlated physics-informed neural network (CC-PINN). The core innovations of this work include: (1) constructing a Fourier feature MLP network architecture based on weight normalization, which possesses excellent capability for solving inverse scattering problems; (2) introducing a cross-correlated residual term that directly couples the reconstructed dielectric parameters and the predicted internal total field to the external observation field, breaking the decoupling between the contrast source and the permittivity optimization in traditional PINNs and significantly enhancing the robustness of PINNs for ISP; (3) introducing a zero-padding-based 2D-FFT acceleration algorithm, which reduces the computational complexity of the forward Green's function integration. Experimental results on synthetic and measured data demonstrate that CC-PINN can reconstruct high-contrast dielectric targets with high fidelity, and its convergence robustness far exceeds that of PINN algorithms using classical cost functions, regardless of whether simultaneous multi-frequency processing or frequency-hopping strategies are employed.

</details>


### [526] [Sensing-Constrained Diversity-Multiplexing Tradeoff in MIMO ISAC: A Geometric Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01889)
*Yinuo Du,Ziping Lu,Xiao Shen,Hanying Zhao,Yuan Shen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper investigates the diversity-multiplexing tradeoff (DMT) in MIMO integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, quantifying the fundamental communication performance sacrifice when using sensing-optimal waveforms under Rayleigh fading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental tradeoff between sensing and communication in MIMO ISAC systems, specifically answering how much MIMO communication gain is sacrificed when integrating optimal sensing capabilities—a pivotal unanswered question in ISAC system design.

Method: The authors analyze a mono-static MIMO ISAC system under Rayleigh fading with sensing-optimal waveform constraints, leveraging geometric properties of generalized Stiefel manifolds and large-deviation analysis to characterize the asymptotic outage probability.

Result: A mathematical formulation of the asymptotic outage probability for the sensing-constrained ISAC channel, yielding an elegant converse bound on the sensing-constrained DMT.

Conclusion: The work provides a fundamental answer to the communication performance sacrifice required for integrating optimal sensing in MIMO ISAC systems, quantified through the derived DMT bounds.

Abstract: Diversity and multiplexing are the two fundamental gains of multiple-input and multiple-output (MIMO) communications, enabling systems to simultaneously achieve increased reliability and higher data rates. The intricate interplay between these two metrics is captured by the celebrated diversity-multiplexing tradeoff (DMT). With the rapid evolution of wireless technologies, low-latency integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has emerged as a key enabler for 6G applications, including extended reality (XR) and massive digital twins. Consequently, understanding the DMT within MIMO ISAC systems becomes critical. In this paper, we investigate the communication DMT in a mono-static MIMO ISAC system under Rayleigh fading, specifically when the transmitter is constrained to emit sensing-optimal waveforms. By unveiling the geometric properties of generalized Stiefel manifolds and employing large-deviation analysis, we characterize the asymptotic outage probability of this typical ISAC channel. This formulation yields an elegant converse bound on the sensing-constrained DMT. Ultimately, our work provides an answer to a pivotal unanswered question in ISAC system design: How much MIMO gain is fundamentally sacrificed in communication to integrate optimal sensing capabilities?

</details>


### [527] [Long Sync Word Frame Synchronization for Future Wireless Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01890)
*Dimitris Nikolaidis*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a practical frame synchronization scheme using digital correlation with long sync words in a standard modem chain, implemented with GNU Radio and VIVADO, achieving high accuracy under severe signal distortions in Rayleigh NLOS channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frame synchronization is critical in wireless channels where signals are heavily distorted, and existing methods may not handle extreme distortion levels practically with minimal extra resources.

Method: The scheme combines a modem chain (built in GNU Radio) and a frame synchronization architecture (built in VIVADO). Digital correlation using XNOR gates and summations with long sync words is employed. Data is transmitted/received over Rayleigh NLOS channels, and frame synchronization is tested via a Verilog testbench with GNU Radio output.

Result: The method achieves high synchronization accuracy even under distortion levels much higher than those reported by other methods.

Conclusion: Digital correlation with long sync words in a standard modem chain is a practical, resource-efficient solution for robust frame synchronization under severe signal distortions.

Abstract: Frame synchronization is the act of accurately detecting frames in an incoming transmission and extracting their payload. It is especially important in environments such as wireless channels where signals are significantly distorted. Digital correlation is the simplest form of frame synchronization where XNOR gates and summations are used to perform correlation and detect binary sync words attached to each frame. In this paper we demonstrate how digital correlation with long syncwords in the context of a standard modem (modulator/demodulator) chain solves the problem of frame synchronization under significant signal distortions in a practically implementable manner with minimal extra resources. The scheme consists of two parts, the modem chain and the frame synchronization architecture. The modem chain is built inside GNU radio software tool and the architecture inside VIVADO. Data transmission and reception in the Rayleigh NLOS channel is performed by the modem and frame synchronization by the architecture with data produced by GNU radio via a verilog testbench. Results indicate high synchronization accuracy with distortion levels much higher than what is reported by other methods.

</details>


### [528] [Beamforming Design for Pinching Antenna Systems Enabled Cognitive Radio Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01943)
*Haochen Li,Ruikang Zhong,Zhaoming Hu,Zheng Zhang,Yaoyue Hu,Chao Dong*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a pinching antenna system (PASS) assisted cognitive radio (CR) system, formulates a sum rate maximization problem under constraints, and solves it via WMMSE-based alternating optimization with closed-form updates, achieving performance gains over benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance spectral efficiency in cognitive radio systems by leveraging pinching antenna technology for flexible deployment, addressing interference constraints and power budget limitations.

Method: A WMMSE-based alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is developed: auxiliary variables updated in closed form, digital beamforming subproblem transformed to convex, and PA deployment refined via element-wise optimization.

Result: Numerical results validate effectiveness and show consistent performance gains compared to conventional benchmark schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed PASS-assisted CR design with AO-based optimization outperforms conventional schemes, demonstrating the potential of pinching antennas for cognitive radio systems.

Abstract: A pinching antenna system (PASS) assisted cognitive radio (CR) system is proposed. A secondary system sum rate maximization problem is formulated by jointly considering the base station (BS) power budget, the pinching antenna (PA) deployment constraints, and the interference tolerance requirements of primary users. To address the resulting non-convex problem, a tractable reformulation based on the weighted minimum mean-square error (WMMSE) approach is adopted, followed by the development of an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm. Within this framework, the auxiliary variables are updated in closed form, enabling an efficient transformation of the digital beamforming subproblem to a convex form, while the PA deployment is refined through a tailored element-wise optimization strategy. Numerical results validate the effectiveness of the proposed design and show consistent performance gains compared with conventional benchmark schemes.

</details>


### [529] [Molecular ISAC via Markov State-Space Modeling: Joint Distance Sensing and Data Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01975)
*Ruifeng Zheng,Pengjie Zhou,Martín Schottlender,Veronika Volkova,Juan A. Cabrera,Frank H. P. Fitzek,Pit Hofmann*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a molecular ISAC framework using microfluidic molecular communication for joint distance sensing and data detection, designing a pilot-assisted receiver with DFE that achieves accurate sensing and improved BER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit molecular observations for both physical-parameter sensing and data detection simultaneously, demonstrating mutual benefit between sensing and communication in molecular environments.

Method: A distance-parameterized Markov state-space model is established to capture distance-dependent channel impulse responses and inter-symbol interference. A pilot-assisted low-complexity receiver is designed combining distance initialization, decision-feedback equalization (DFE), and iterative joint refinement.

Result: Accurate distance sensing and improved bit error ratio (BER) are achieved, showing mutual benefit between sensing and communication in microfluidic MC.

Conclusion: Microfluidic molecular communication is a representative platform for molecular ISAC, where sensing and communication mutually enhance each other.

Abstract: This paper develops a molecular integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) framework that exploits the same molecular observations for physical-parameter sensing and data detection. As a representative instantiation, we consider a microfluidic molecular communication (MC) channel and study transmitter--receiver (TX--RX) distance sensing, where the distance affects the propagation delay, transient response, and inter-symbol interference structure. A distance-parameterized Markov state--space model is established to obtain distance-dependent channel impulse responses and a block observation model for on-off keying signaling. Based on this model, we design a pilot-assisted low-complexity receiver that combines distance initialization, decision-feedback equalization (DFE), and iterative joint refinement. Numerical results show accurate distance sensing and improved bit error ratio (BER), demonstrating the mutual benefit between sensing and communication and highlighting microfluidic MC as a representative platform for molecular ISAC.

</details>


### [530] [Modeling and Mitigation of 7.125-7.40 GHz Terrestrial Network RFI on the Passive Earth Exploration Satellite Service in 6.725-7.125 GHz Band](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02079)
*Md Toufiqur Rahman,Hariharan Venkat,Chung-Tse Michael Wu,Ivan Seskar,Narayan B. Mandayam*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes RF interference from 7.125-7.4 GHz 6G terrestrial networks to passive EESS sensors in the adjacent 6.725-7.125 GHz band, showing that by 2040, guard bands of 25 MHz will be insufficient and must be widened to 35 MHz or user rates reduced to 300 Mbps to meet ITU thresholds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The 7.125-7.4 GHz band is attractive for next-generation TN deployments due to large bandwidths and favorable propagation, and recent U.S. presidential actions have cleared this band for 6G. However, successful deployment requires guaranteeing coexistence with incumbents in adjacent bands, particularly passive EESS sensors.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive RFI analysis using TN base stations equipped with filtennas (filtering antennas) and transmit precoders for RFI mitigation. They model increasing BS deployments across the contiguous U.S. from 2030 to 2040 and characterize guard band sizes needed to protect EESS sensors.

Result: A 100 Mbps increase in TN user rates causes roughly 2.45 dB RFI increase at EESS sensors. With a 25 MHz guard band, no significant RFI occurs in 2030 for rates up to 500 Mbps, but by 2040 the same systems cause RFI ~4 dB above ITU thresholds. Mitigation requires either widening guard bands to 35 MHz or reducing user rates to 300 Mbps.

Conclusion: Without proper guard band adjustments or rate constraints, 6G TN deployments in the 7.125-7.4 GHz band will cause harmful interference to adjacent-band passive EESS sensors by 2040, necessitating proactive coexistence engineering.

Abstract: The 7.125-7.4 GHz band is attractive for next generation Terrestrial Network (TN) deployments owing to the large bandwidths available and favorable propagation characteristics. Furthermore, recent U.S. Presidential actions have cleared the usage of this band for 6G by stipulating relocation of federal incumbents that share this band. However, this deployment can only be successful if we can also guarantee coexistence of these networks with existing incumbents operating in adjacent bands. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the Radio Frequency Interference (RFI) caused by the proposed TNs in the 7.125-7.4 GHz band at passive Earth Exploration Satellite Service (EESS) sensors that operate in the adjacent 6.725-7.125 GHz band. Using TN base stations (BSs) equipped with filtennas (filtering antennas) as well as transmit precoders for RFI mitigation, we carry out an RFI analysis that accounts for increasing BS deployments in the contiguous U.S. over a 10 year period from 2030 to 2040. We also characterize the size of the guard bands needed to protect the EESS sensors for different BS deployment densities. With appropriate filtenna and precoder design, our results reveal that a 100 Mbps increase in the rate requirements of the TN users results in an RFI increase of roughly 2.45 dB at the EESS sensors. For a 25 MHz Guard Band, simulations show that in 2030, there is no significant RFI for user rates upto 500 Mbps. However, the same systems in 2040 would cause RFI that is around 4 dB above the ITU mandated threshold for passive EESS sensors. This would need to be countered by (a) increasing Guard Band width to 35 MHz, or (b) by reducing the user data rate requirements to 300 Mbps.

</details>


### [531] [Context-Aware Wireless Token Communication via Joint Token Masking and Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02123)
*Junyong Shin,Joohyuk Park,Yongjeong Oh,Jihong Park,Jinho Choi,Yo-Seb Jeon*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a context-aware token communication framework using a shared masked language model (MLM) at both transmitter and receiver. The receiver uses Bayesian inference combining channel likelihoods with MLM priors for robust token detection, while the transmitter selectively masks tokens that can be inferred by the Rx, concentrating power on more informative tokens. This achieves up to 1.77X and 1.63X performance gains over conventional schemes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional token-based communication systems treat tokens independently and allocate transmission resources uniformly, wasting limited wireless resources under channel impairments by ignoring token dependencies.

Method: The framework uses a shared MLM between Tx and Rx. The Rx performs context-aware token detection via Bayesian formulation (channel likelihoods + MLM priors). The Tx employs context-aware token masking to omit reliably inferable tokens and focus power on more informative ones.

Result: Up to 1.77X performance gain on Europarl corpus and 1.63X on WikiText-103 compared to conventional and existing token communication schemes.

Conclusion: The unified Tx-Rx framework significantly improves reconstruction performance over noisy channels by leveraging contextual dependencies among tokens.

Abstract: The increasing use of token-based representations in language-driven applications has motivated wireless token communication, where tokens are treated as fundamental units for transmission. However, conventional communication systems overlook dependencies among tokens and allocate transmission resources uniformly, leading to inefficient use of limited wireless resources under channel impairments. In this paper, we propose a context-aware token communication framework that leverages a masked language model (MLM) as a shared contextual model between the transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx). At the Rx, we develop a context-aware token detection method that integrates channel likelihoods with MLM-based contextual priors under a Bayesian formulation, enabling robust token inference over noisy channels. At the Tx, we propose a context-aware token masking strategy that selectively omits tokens that can be reliably inferred at the Rx, allowing the available power budget to be concentrated on more informative tokens. These components are jointly designed through a shared MLM, establishing a unified Tx-Rx framework for efficient token transmission and detection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves reconstruction performance compared to conventional and existing token communication schemes, achieving up to 1.77X and 1.63X performance gains on the Europarl corpus and WikiText-103 datasets, respectively.

</details>


### [532] [Waveform Design for 6G ISAC Systems Under Full-Duplex Residual Self-Interference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02157)
*Ning Wei,Aimin Tang,Yin Xu,Wenze Qu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel time-division ISAC waveform with a dual-power phase-coded pulse to overcome imperfect full-duplex self-interference, achieving reliable long-range sensing and eliminating detection blind range in 6G ISAC systems for low-altitude wireless networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imperfect full-duplex radios cause severe residual self-interference (RSI) that degrades continuous communication waveforms (e.g., OFDM) for radar sensing, limiting the long-range sensing capabilities required for emerging low-altitude wireless networks (LAWN).

Method: The authors propose: (1) a time-division ISAC waveform with a dual-power phase-coded pulse (high-power + low-power sequence); (2) a complementary and inverse-phase sequence group for autocorrelation and cross-correlation sidelobe suppression; (3) a parameterized mismatched filter optimized for detection; (4) a hierarchical one-dimensional CFAR-CA detector leveraging perfect range-domain autocorrelation.

Result: Simulations show significant improvement in maximum detection range and multi-target detection capability over existing OFDM and LFM pulse baselines, with effective coverage of the blind range for small-RCS targets.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-power pulse waveform effectively exploits imperfect full-duplex operations to enable reliable long-range sensing while eliminating half-duplex detection blind range, advancing 6G ISAC for LAWN applications.

Abstract: In this paper, the waveform design for 6G integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems is investigated, with a particular focus on the practical limitations imposed by imperfect full-duplex radios. Under such imperfections, continuous communication waveforms, such as OFDM, suffer from severe full-duplex residual self-interference (RSI) for radar sensing, which significantly restricts the long-range sensing capabilities required by emerging low-altitude wireless networks (LAWN). To address this challenge, we propose a novel time-division ISAC waveform that integrates a specially developed dual-power phase-coded pulse for sensing into the communication frame under full-duplex RSI. Specifically, the dual-power sensing pulse consists of a high-power sequence followed by a low-power sequence, effectively exploiting imperfect full-duplex operations to achieve reliable long-range sensing while eliminating the detection blind range inherent to conventional half-duplex pulse radars. Furthermore, a complementary and inverse-phase sequence group is designed to ensure perfect autocorrelation and robust cross-correlation sidelobe suppression, so as to enhance multi-target detection capability. As for sensing signal processing, a parameterized mismatched filter is developed and optimized to maximize the detection performance, tailored to the proposed pulse structure. In addition, we design a hierarchical one-dimensional CFAR-CA detector that can exploit the perfect range-domain autocorrelation characteristics of the proposed waveform to further improve the detection performance. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed design significantly improves the maximum detection range and multi-target detection capability compared to existing OFDM and LFM pulse baselines, while effectively covering the blind range for targets with small RCS.

</details>


### [533] [When Eavesdroppers Reason: Agentic Eavesdropping Attacks on Semantic Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02204)
*Shunpu Tang,Qianqian Yang,Zhiguo Shi,Jiming Chen,Xuemin,Shen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes an LLM-orchestrated agentic eavesdropper for semantic communication systems, consisting of three agents (optimization, perception, refinement) that can infer private information from intercepted signals without requiring wiretap channel state information, achieving over 75% eavesdropping success rate at SNR ≥ 5 dB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic communication (SemCom) systems face serious privacy concerns due to their end-to-end joint source-channel coding (JSCC) architecture. Current eavesdropping attacks rely on fixed-configuration solvers and often require instantaneous wiretap channel state information (CSI), which may lead future secure SemCom designs to overlook potentially severe privacy risks.

Method: The proposed eavesdropper is an LLM-orchestrated closed-loop workflow with three agents: 1) Optimization agent - adaptively performs joint semantic-and-channel inversion to recover private information from intercepted signals without requiring wiretap CSI. 2) Perception agent - evaluates the optimization agent's effectiveness and assesses whether recovered private semantics are reasonable, providing feedback. 3) Refinement agent - analyzes recovered content and uses a generative prior to refine promising candidates into more realistic and complete private reconstructions while preserving consistency with the intercepted signal.

Result: Simulations over a MIMO Rayleigh fading channel show the proposed eavesdropper achieves more than 75% eavesdropping success rate at SNR ≥ 5 dB even without wiretap CSI, highlighting a severe privacy threat for future secure SemCom systems.

Conclusion: The proposed LLM-orchestrated agentic eavesdropper poses a significant privacy threat to semantic communication systems, achieving high eavesdropping success without requiring wiretap channel state information. This highlights the need for future secure SemCom designs to address such severe privacy risks.

Abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has emerged as a promising paradigm for next-generation networks. However, its typical end-to-end joint source--channel coding (JSCC) architecture also raises serious privacy concerns. To guide future secure SemCom design, it is important to understand how serious such leakage can be. Nevertheless, existing eavesdropping attacks mainly rely on fixed-configuration solvers and often require instantaneous wiretap channel state information (CSI) to achieve effective privacy inference. This may lead future secure SemCom designs to overlook potentially severe risks. To address this, we propose a large language model (LLM)-orchestrated agentic eavesdropper. Specifically, the proposed eavesdropper forms a closed-loop workflow with three functional agents. The optimization agent adaptively performs joint semantic-and-channel inversion to recover private information from the intercepted signal without requiring wiretap CSI. The perception agent evaluates the effectiveness of the optimization agent and assesses whether the recovered private semantics are reasonable, providing feedback to the optimization agent. The refinement agent further analyzes the recovered content and uses a generative prior to refine promising candidates into more realistic and complete private reconstructions while preserving consistency with the intercepted signal. Simulation results over a MIMO Rayleigh fading channel show that the proposed eavesdropper achieves more than $75\%$ eavesdropping success rate at $\mathrm{SNR}\geq 5$~dB even without wiretap CSI, highlighting a severe privacy threat that future secure SemCom systems must address.

</details>


### [534] [Optimal Pilot Pattern Design for LMMSE Channel Estimation in OFDM Systems with Finite Block Size over Doubly Dispersive Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02213)
*Xuyao Yu,Zijun Gong,Zhilu Lai*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes two heuristic algorithms (convex relaxation with randomized rounding and greedy selection) for designing LMMSE-optimal pilot patterns in OFDM systems over doubly dispersive channels, formulated as A-optimal sensor selection, outperforming conventional rectangular and diamond lattice patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Driven by emerging high-mobility applications in 5G-Advanced and 6G systems, and recent developments in OTFS modulation, there is renewed interest in pilot pattern design over doubly dispersive channels.

Method: The problem is formulated as A-optimal sensor selection. Two heuristic algorithms are proposed, both combining an initialization stage with local swap refinement: (1) convex relaxation with randomized rounding, and (2) greedy selection.

Result: Simulations on practical resource block dimensions show the proposed designs consistently outperform conventional rectangular and diamond lattice patterns.

Conclusion: The proposed heuristic algorithms provide effective LMMSE-optimal pilot patterns for OFDM systems over doubly dispersive channels with finite time-frequency grids.

Abstract: Pilot pattern design over doubly dispersive channels has regained significant research interest, driven by emerging high-mobility applications in 5G-Advanced and 6G systems, as well as recent developments in Orthogonal Time Frequency Space (OTFS) modulation. This paper addresses the design of LMMSE-optimal pilot patterns for OFDM systems over doubly dispersive channels with finite time-frequency grids. We formulate the problem as A-optimal sensor selection and propose two heuristic algorithms, both combining an initialization stage with local swap refinement. The first employs convex relaxation with randomized rounding, while the second uses greedy selection. Simulations on practical resource block dimensions demonstrate that the proposed designs consistently outperform conventional rectangular and diamond lattice patterns.

</details>


### [535] [A Review on Quantum Satellite Communications: Challenges and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02272)
*Omar Alnaseri,Yassine Himeur,Ahmed Al Asadi,Mohammed A. Ala'anzy,Rida Gadhafi,Shadi Atalla,Wathiq Mansoor*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This review paper identifies and prioritizes major technical challenges (atmospheric loss, beam pointing, payload constraints, etc.) hindering large-scale deployment of quantum satellite communication (QSC), surveys recent advances in protocols, hybrid architectures, turbulence mitigation, and AI-assisted optimization, and discusses future directions including quantum Internet, daylight operation, satellite repeaters, and space-based quantum computing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically identify and prioritize the major technical bottlenecks that still hinder large-scale deployment of quantum satellite communication systems, providing a roadmap for researchers and engineers working toward practical and resilient QSC systems.

Method: A review approach that centers on open technical challenges (atmospheric loss, beam pointing/tracking, payload constraints, synchronization, scalability, integration with terrestrial infrastructure), provides concise background on core concepts and enabling technologies (including Micius mission milestones), and surveys recent advances in protocols, hybrid space-terrestrial architectures, turbulence mitigation, and AI-assisted optimization.

Result: Identification of key technical bottlenecks for QSC deployment and a structured survey of recent protocol advances, hybrid architectures, turbulence mitigation techniques, and AI-based optimization approaches.

Conclusion: Future QSC development should focus on quantum Internet integration, daylight operation capabilities, satellite-supported quantum repeaters, and space-based quantum computing to achieve practical and resilient systems.

Abstract: Quantum satellite communication (QSC) is emerging as a strategic technology for secure global networking and long-distance quantum connectivity. This review prioritizes the major challenges that still hinder large-scale deployment, including atmospheric loss, beam pointing and tracking, payload constraints, synchronization, scalability, and integration with terrestrial infrastructure. To contextualize these issues, we provide only a concise overview of the core concepts and enabling technologies behind QSC, together with representative milestones such as the Micius mission. Building on this background, the paper surveys recent advances in protocols, hybrid space--terrestrial architectures, turbulence mitigation, and AI-assisted optimization. It then examines future directions, including quantum Internet integration, daylight operation, satellite-supported repeaters, and space-based quantum computing. By centering the discussion on open technical bottlenecks and emerging research trajectories, this review aims to support researchers and engineers working toward practical and resilient QSC systems.

</details>


### [536] [TeRFS: Temporal-Evolving Radio Field Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02359)
*Pengyang Zhang,Wenlihan Lu,Shijian Gao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: TeRFS is a new radio field synthesis method using anisotropic spherical Gaussian basis and temporal envelopes to track rapid multipath changes, achieving 11.5% lower MSE and 6.9x training speedup over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RF field synthesis approaches use static assumptions and cannot track rapid multipath reorganization in dynamic scenes.

Method: TeRFS uses anisotropic spherical Gaussian (ASG) directional basis bound to analytical temporal envelopes, creating a birth-and-death mechanism for precise multipath lifecycle tracking.

Result: 11.5% reduction in MSE, 6.9x training speedup, and robust tracking with median absolute error of 1.52 dB even in extreme structural mutation environments.

Conclusion: TeRFS establishes utility for high-mobility wireless applications by enabling precise temporal tracking of dynamic multipath reorganizations.

Abstract: While radio-frequency (RF) field synthesis is fundamental to wireless networking, current approaches remain constrained by static assumptions, leaving them unable to track the rapid multipath reorganization of dynamic scenes. Modeling these transitions requires addressing two coupled challenges: explicit temporal representation and the capture of discrete path lifecycles. To bridge this gap, Temporal-Evolving Radio Field Synthesis (TeRFS) is introduced. TeRFS utilizes an anisotropic spherical Gaussian (ASG) directional basis to represent sparse, sharp angular structures, bound to analytical temporal envelopes that regulate path lifecycles. This formulation induces a mathematical birth-and-death mechanism, enabling individual multipath trajectories to emerge and vanish with temporal precision, a capability beyond the reach of standard smooth interpolation. Evaluations demonstrate that TeRFS outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines, achieving an 11.5% reduction in mean squared error (MSE) alongside a 6.9 times training speedup. Even in environments characterized by extreme structural mutation, TeRFS maintains robust tracking of dynamic reorganizations, limiting median absolute error to 1.52 dB and establishing its utility for high-mobility wireless applications.

</details>


### [537] [A Scalable 256-Antenna Distributed MIMO Testbed with Real-Time Fully Digital Beamforming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02388)
*Dumitra Iancu,Vilgot Snygg,Sijia Cheng,Lina Tinnerberg,Mikael Henriksson,Emil Bergman,Anders J Johansson,Baktash Behmanesh,Ove Edfors,Liang Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents LuLIS, a scalable D-MIMO testbed at Lund University using 16 RFSoC boards to support up to 256 coherent RF chains, enabling real-time distributed MIMO processing with flexible deployment options.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance D-MIMO technology from theory to practice, practical testbeds are essential for understanding real-world possibilities and limitations of distributed massive MIMO systems.

Method: The testbed uses 16 AMD Zynq UltraScale RFSoC ZCU216 evaluation boards as distributed processing nodes, each supporting 16 antennas, with FPGA-accelerated real-time MIMO processing and a scalable architecture that avoids latency overhead.

Result: Initial uplink results shown for 4 UEs communicating with 64, 128, and 256 base-station antennas, demonstrating the system's operational capability.

Conclusion: The LuLIS testbed provides a flexible, scalable platform for D-MIMO research, supporting both distributed and co-located deployments without hardware redesign, facilitating practical advancement of massive MIMO technologies.

Abstract: Distributed massive MIMO (D-MIMO) is a promising technology for future generation wireless systems as it takes advantage of both an increased array aperture and a decentralized processing architecture and topology. In order to truly understand the possibilities and limitations of these approaches in real scenarios, practical realization of testbeds is an essential step in the technology advancement. This work presents the Lund University Large Intelligent Surface testbed -- LuLIS, that can operate up to 256 coherent radio frequency (RF) chains using 16 AMD Zynq UltraScale RFSoC ZCU216 evaluation boards acting as distributed processing nodes. Real-time processing is facilitated by acceleration and distribution of MIMO processing algorithms on the FPGA fabric of the boards. The system is easily scalable, as increasing the number of antennas is done in multiples of 16 by adding more RFSoCs, which also implies addition of another processing node. The design allows up-scaling without hardware redesign, introduction of large latencies or data transfer overhead. The testbed is flexible in terms of deployment, with options of fully distributing the nodes (as in D-MIMO) or co-locating them (as in more traditional Massive MIMO). A detailed description of the implementation of the testbed is presented and initial results are shown for an uplink (UL) transmission from four single-antenna user equipments (UEs) to 64, 128 and 256 base-station antennas.

</details>


### [538] [2D Waveguide-Fed Metasurfaces: Physically Consistent Modeling, Validation, and Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02400)
*Panagiotis Gavriilidis,George C. Alexandropoulos*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a comprehensive electromagnetics-compliant framework for 2D waveguide-fed metasurface antennas using coupled dipole formulation, incorporating electric and magnetic dipole responses, multiple feeds, near/far-field characterization, passivity-constrained radiation-reaction corrections, and a novel input impedance model, validated against full-wave simulations and enabling differentiable beamforming optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Programmable metasurface-based antenna arrays are promising for XL-MIMO in 6G, but accurate modeling capturing mutual coupling and guided-wave excitation remains challenging, requiring physically consistent representations of metamaterial elements.

Method: A coupled dipole formulation framework for 2D waveguide-fed metasurface antennas, incorporating both electric and magnetic dipole responses, multiple excitation feeds, radiation-reaction corrections via passivity constraints, and a novel input impedance model for accepted power computation.

Result: Validated against full-wave electromagnetic simulations; enables differentiable beamforming design optimization; demonstrated effectiveness in achieving directive beamforming and sector-wide coverage.

Conclusion: The proposed modeling framework provides a physically consistent, accurate, and differentiable representation of metasurface antennas, facilitating advanced beamforming optimization for future XL-MIMO systems.

Abstract: Antenna array architectures based on programmable metasurfaces are emerging as a promising solution for scalable implementations of the eXtremely Large Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (XL-MIMO) systems paradigm, envisioned for 6-th Generation (6G), and beyond, wireless networks. However, their accurate modeling, quantifying the role of key structural features, such as strong mutual coupling and guided-wave excitation, remains challenging, amplifying the need for physically consistent representations of the constituent metamaterial elements. In this paper, capitalizing on the coupled dipole formulation, we develop a comprehensive electromagnetics-compliant framework for 2-Dimensional (2D) waveguide-fed metasurface antennas. The proposed model extends relevant existing modeling approaches by incorporating both electric and magnetic dipoles' responses, accounting for multiple excitation feeds, and enabling accurate characterization in both the near- and far-field regimes. Radiation-reaction corrections based on passivity constraints are derived and shown to ensure the passivity of the overall dipole system. In addition, we present a novel input impedance model for the considered architecture enabling explicit computation of the accepted power, and facilitating efficient beamforming design under realistic power constraints. All modeling components developed in this paper are validated against full-wave electromagnetic simulations. Furthermore, the analytical structure of the proposed model enables the formulation of a differentiable beamforming design optimization problem over both the considered metasurface geometry and its feed excitations. The presented numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed modeling framework in achieving both directive beamforming and sector-wide coverage.

</details>


### [539] [Over-the-Air Computation via Segmented Waveguide-Enabled Pinching-Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02408)
*Songnan Gu,Hao Jiang,Chongjun Ouyang,Dian Fan,Yuanwei Liu,Arumugam Nallanathan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a SWAN-assisted AirComp framework with three transmission architectures (SS, phase-shifter-free SA, and phase-shifter-enabled SA) and demonstrates through numerical results that these approaches achieve lower computation mean-squared error than the conventional PASS system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the performance of over-the-air computation (AirComp) by leveraging segmented waveguide-enabled pinching-antenna systems, addressing limitations of conventional pinching-antenna systems (PASS).

Method: Proposes SWAN framework with three architectures (SS, phase-shifter-free SA, phase-shifter-enabled SA) and develops low-complexity algorithms for optimizing pinching-antenna placement and per-segment phase shifts.

Result: Both SS and SA architectures achieve lower computation mean-squared error than conventional PASS, with segment-wise phase control further improving SA performance.

Conclusion: The proposed SWAN-assisted AirComp framework outperforms conventional PASS, and phase-shifter-enabled SA provides the best performance among the three architectures.

Abstract: A segmented waveguide-enabled pinching-antenna system (SWAN)-assisted over-the-air computation (AirComp) framework is proposed. Three transmission architectures, namely segment selection (SS), phase-shifter-free segment aggregation (SA), and phase-shifter-enabled SA, are developed for uplink signal aggregation. For each architecture, low-complexity algorithms are developed to optimize the pinching-antenna placement and the per-segment phase shifts. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approaches and the superiority of SWAN over the conventional pinching-antenna system (PASS). It is shown that both SS and SA achieve lower computation mean-squared error than the conventional PASS, while segment-wise phase control further improves the performance of SA.

</details>


### [540] [On Near-Far-Field Boundaries in Wireless Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02424)
*Alexander Stutz-Tirri,Ahmad Dkhan,Hadi Sarieddeen,Christoph Studer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Maxwell's equations-based framework to analyze the near-field to far-field transition region, finding that existing single-letter distance thresholds are insufficient to predict this transition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the core question of how to determine whether a wireless system operates in the near-field or far-field region, and to evaluate the effectiveness of existing distance threshold metrics.

Method: A framework grounded in Maxwell's equations following IEEE definitions, combined with numerical experiments using both analytical models and full-wave electromagnetic simulations for single- and multi-antenna systems.

Result: All considered single-letter distance thresholds are insufficient to predict the transition region between NF and FF.

Conclusion: Existing near-far-field boundary metrics are inadequate, and there are important caveats with frequently used NF and FF concepts that need attention.

Abstract: Near-field (NF) multi-antenna wireless communication and sensing have attracted growing research interest in recent years. A core question in this area is how to determine whether a wireless system is operating in the NF or far-field (FF) region. In this work, we propose a framework grounded in Maxwell's equations to analyze the transition region between the NF and FF, following the IEEE definition that specifies where the NF ends and the FF begins. Using this framework, we (i) compare a variety of traditional and recently introduced single-letter distance thresholds, often referred to as near-far-field boundaries, and (ii) conduct numerical experiments with both single- and multi-antenna wireless systems and with analytical models as well as full-wave electromagnetic simulations. Our results indicate that all of the considered single-letter distance thresholds are insufficient to predict the transition region between the NF and FF regions. Moreover, we highlight several important caveats associated with frequently (and recently) used NF and FF concepts.

</details>


### [541] [The interplay of signal-to-noise ratio and variance misspecification in Gaussian mixtures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02448)
*Vladimir Serov,Amnon Balanov,Tamir Bendory*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper studies how using a wrong variance assumption in Gaussian mixture models affects mean estimation and clustering, revealing phase transitions between under-smoothing (bias grows as SNR decreases) and over-smoothing (clusters collapse) regimes, with hard-assignment clustering emerging as a limiting case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the consequences of variance misspecification in Gaussian mixture models, which is common in practice but often overlooked, and to characterize how the mismatch ratio ρ = τ/σ interacts with signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) to affect estimation quality.

Method: The authors analyze a family of mismatched likelihood functions where data has true variance σ² but estimation uses variance τ². They derive phase diagrams as functions of ρ and SNR, comparing soft-assignment (EM-like) and hard-assignment (k-means-like) procedures, with theoretical analysis of squared error and label recovery.

Result: Under-smoothing (ρ&lt;1) causes squared error scaling as SNR⁻¹. Over-smoothing (ρ&gt;1) causes cluster collapse when ρ² exceeds 1 + λ·SNR. Hard-assignment (τ→0) inherits these biases. Low SNR scenarios exhibit large bias even with mild misspecification, while high SNR is largely robust.

Conclusion: Variance misspecification and hard-assignment can induce substantial bias in low-SNR settings, but have negligible effects in high SNR—caution is warranted in low-SNR applications like genomics or neuroimaging.

Abstract: We study estimation and clustering in Gaussian mixture models under variance misspecification. Observations are generated with true variance $σ^2$, while the component means are estimated using a likelihood with variance $τ^2$, yielding a family of mismatched likelihood functions parameterized by the ratio $ρ=τ/σ$. We show that the interplay between $ρ$ and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) induces a sharp phase diagram. Under correct specification ($ρ=1$), maximum likelihood recovers the true means, independently of the SNR. However, once the model is misspecified, two different regimes emerge. Under under-smoothing ($ρ<1$), the estimated Gaussian means are displaced from the truth, and in low SNR this discrepancy grows as the SNR decreases: for every fixed $ρ<1$, the squared error scales as $\mathrm{SNR}^{-1}$. Under over-smoothing ($ρ>1$), the fitted likelihood blurs the cluster separation, causing distinct component means to collapse towards the overall mixture center once $ρ^2$ exceeds a threshold of the form $1 + λ\,\mathrm{SNR}$, where $λ$ depends on the geometry of the true means. We further show that the hard assignment objective arises as the limit $τ\to 0$ of the same mismatched likelihood family, and derive corresponding low- and high-SNR results for hard-assignment mean estimation and latent-label recovery. Furthermore, in low SNR, Bayes-optimal clustering is close to random guessing, and the hard-assignment target remains far from the true means. These results show that in low-SNR applications, even mild variance misspecification or hard-assignment procedures can induce substantial bias, whereas in high SNR these effects are largely absent.

</details>


### [542] [Reliable Narrowband Interference Detection via Backward Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02486)
*Xin Su,Meiyi Zhu,Osvaldo Simeone,Marco Di Renzo,Carlo Fischione*

Main category: eess.SP
