  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI agents often behave unfaithfully by relying on memorized shortcuts rather than grounding actions in screen evidence or user instructions.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) faithfulness-oriented SFT with evidence perturbations to instill abstainment behaviors; (2) RFT with guided advantage estimator (GuAE), an anchor-based variance-adaptive advantage tempering mechanism built on GRPO, plus a thought-action consistency reward.

Result: Trap success rate improved from 13.88% to 80.21% relative to baseline, with robust general instruction-following performance preserved.

Conclusion: Faithfulness-first prioritization and the proposed GuAE mechanism effectively mitigate shortcut reliance in GUI agents without sacrificing general capability.

Abstract: Vision-language model based graphical user interface (GUI) agents have shown strong interaction capabilities. However, they often behave unfaithfully, relying on memorized shortcuts rather than grounding actions in displayed screen evidence or user instructions. To address this, we propose Faithful-Agent, a faithfulness-first framework that reformulates GUI interaction to prioritize evidence groundedness and internal consistency. Faithful-Agent employs a two-stage pipeline: (i) a faithfulness-oriented SFT stage to instill abstainment behaviors under evidence perturbations; (ii) an RFT stage that further amplifies faithfulness by introducing the guided advantage estimator (GuAE), an anchor-based and variance-adaptive advantage tempering mechanism built upon GRPO. GuAE prevents advantage collapse in low-variance rollout groups under sparse GUI rewards, and with a thought-action consistency reward, Faithful-Agent (Stage II) elevates the Trap SR from 13.88\% to 80.21\% relative to the baseline, while preserving robust general instruction-following performance.

</details>


### [569] [Agentic AI Systems Should Be Designed as Marginal Token Allocators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01214)
*Siqi Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes that agentic AI systems should be viewed as marginal token allocation economies, where all four design layers (routing, agent decision-making, serving, and training) solve the same optimization problem: marginal benefit equals marginal cost plus latency cost plus risk cost. This unified framing explains why locally optimal token decisions lead to global misallocation and predicts specific failure modes, pointing to a token-aware research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems design the four economic layers (router, agent, serving stack, training pipeline) in isolation, leading to globally suboptimal outcomes. The authors argue that all layers are fundamentally solving the same resource allocation problem but lack a shared accounting framework.

Method: The paper traces a single request through four economic layers of an agentic AI system, showing each layer solves the same first-order condition (marginal benefit = marginal cost + latency cost + risk cost) but with different prices and index sets. It uses marginal token allocation as the shared accounting object.

Result: The framing identifies why local token minimization leads to global misallocation, predicts failure modes (over-routing, over-delegation, under-verification, serving congestion, stale rollouts, cache misuse), and outlines a research agenda including token-aware evaluation, autonomy pricing, congestion-priced serving, and risk-adjusted RL budgeting.

Conclusion: Agentic AI systems should be designed and evaluated as marginal token allocation economies. The paper does not claim to propose a complete theory of AI economics but provides a minimal framing that explains existing failures and suggests concrete research directions.

Abstract: This position paper argues that agentic AI systems should be designed and evaluated as \emph{marginal token allocation economies} rather than as text generators priced by the unit. We follow a single request -- a developer asking a coding agent to fix a failing test -- through four economic layers that today are designed in isolation: a router that decides which model answers, an agent that decides whether to plan, act, verify, or defer, a serving stack that decides how to produce each token, and a training pipeline that decides whether the trace is worth learning from. We show that all four layers are solving the \emph{same} first-order condition -- marginal benefit equals marginal cost plus latency cost plus risk cost -- with different index sets and different prices. The framing is deliberately minimal: we do not propose a complete theory of AI economics. But adopting marginal token allocation as the shared accounting object explains why systems that locally minimize tokens globally misallocate them, predicts a small set of recurring failure modes (over-routing, over-delegation, under-verification, serving congestion, stale rollouts, cache misuse), and points to a concrete research agenda in token-aware evaluation, autonomy pricing, congestion-priced serving, and risk-adjusted RL budgeting.

</details>


### [570] [Zero-Shot Signal Temporal Logic Planning with Disjunctive Branch Selection in Dynamic Semantic Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01222)
*Bowen Ye,Ancheng Hou,Junyue Huang,Ruijia Liu,Xiang Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a zero-shot STL planning solver using a map-conditioned Transformer with lightweight heuristics and Transitive Reinforcement Learning, enabling feasible trajectory generation across varying environments without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: STL planning is challenging because exact optimization methods are too slow, and learning-based methods fail to generalize across varying environments.

Method: Integrates a map-conditioned Transformer architecture with a lightweight heuristic for handling disjunctive subformulas, and uses Transitive Reinforcement Learning (TRL) for consistent temporal grounding and logical coherence across decomposed sub-tasks.

Result: Experiments on dynamic semantic maps with diverse obstacle layouts show consistent gains, demonstrating superior zero-shot generalization and broad STL coverage.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively generalizes to changing environments without retraining, addressing key limitations of prior STL planning methods.

Abstract: Signal Temporal Logic (STL) offers verifiable task specifications and is crucial for safety-critical control. Yet STL planning remains challenging: exact optimization-based methods are often too slow, and learning-based methods struggle to generalize across varying environments. We propose a zero-shot STL planning solver for variable-map environments that generates feasible trajectories without retraining. By integrating a map-conditioned Transformer architecture with a lightweight heuristic, our approach effectively handles complex disjunctive (OR) subformulas. Furthermore, we leverage Transitive Reinforcement Learning (TRL) to ensure consistent temporal grounding and logical coherence across decomposed sub-tasks. Experiments on dynamic semantic maps with diverse obstacle layouts demonstrate consistent gains, highlighting the framework's superior zero-shot generalization to changing environments and broad STL coverage.

</details>


### [571] [EO-Gym: A Multimodal, Interactive Environment for Earth Observation Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01250)
*Sai Ma,Zhuang Li,Sichao Li,Xinyue Xu,Ruibiao Zhu,Tony Boston,John A. Taylor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EO-Gym is a new interactive, multimodal framework for Earth Observation analysis that turns static benchmarks into a dynamic Gymnasium-style workspace with 660k+ multimodal files, 35 specialized tools, and a benchmark of 9,078 trajectories. It shows strong VLMs struggle with interactive EO reasoning, while their fine-tuned 4B model improves Pass@3 from 0.49 to 0.74.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EO benchmarks reduce analysis to fixed-input, single-turn tasks, failing to capture the inherently interactive nature of EO workflows (expanding regions, retrieving historical data, switching sensors).

Method: EO-Gym provides a controlled Gymnasium-style executable environment with 660k+ multimodal files indexed by location/time/sensor, 35 EO-specialized tools across six task families. They built EO-Gym-Data (9,078 trajectories, 34,604 reasoning steps) from 8 public EO datasets plus Landsat/Sentinel-2, and fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct to create EO-Gym-4B as a baseline.

Result: General-purpose VLMs struggle with interactive EO reasoning, especially on temporal and cross-modal tasks. EO-Gym-4B improves Pass@3 from 0.49 to 0.74 under the main evaluation setting.

Conclusion: EO-Gym operationalizes EO as an evidence-gathering problem requiring planning across geospatial, temporal, and sensing modalities, providing a reproducible environment for interactive EO agents.

Abstract: Earth Observation (EO) analysis is inherently interactive: resolving uncertainty often requires expanding the region of interest, retrieving historical observations, and switching across sensors such as optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar. However, most EO benchmarks collapse this process into fixed-input, single-turn tasks. To address this gap, we present EO-Gym, a controlled executable framework for multimodal, tool-using EO agents that formulates EO analysis as a Gymnasium-style local geospatial workspace backed by more than 660k multimodal files indexed by location, time, and sensor type, with 35 EO-specialized tools spanning six task families. Built on this environment, we construct EO-Gym-Data, a benchmark of 9,078 trajectories and 34,604 reasoning steps, and grounded in eight public EO datasets together with Landsat and Sentinel-2 imagery. Evaluating $10$ open and closed VLMs shows that strong general-purpose models still struggle with interactive EO reasoning, especially on temporal and cross-modal workflows. As a reference baseline, EO-Gym-4B, obtained by fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct on EO-Gym-Data, improves overall Pass@3 from $0.49$ to $0.74$ under the main evaluation setting. O-Gym provides a reproducible environment for interactive EO agents, operationalizing EO as an evidence-gathering problem that requires planning across geospatial, temporal, and sensing modality.

</details>


### [572] [Uncertainty-Aware Trip Purpose Inference from GPS Trajectories via POI Semantic Zones and Pareto Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01257)
*Bo Yang,Haoxuan Ma,Yifan Liu,Zhiyuan Zhang,Chris Stanford,Morgan Sun,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A weakly supervised framework that assigns trip purposes to GPS staypoints using neighborhood-level POI semantics, distance-weighted likelihoods, differentiated inference for mandatory/non-mandatory activities, and multi-phase Pareto optimization, reducing distributional errors by 12-48% on 81M+ staypoints in LA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale GPS trajectory data provide rich mobility observations, but assigning trip purposes is challenging due to lack of ground truth, GPS noise, incomplete POI coverage, and behavioral differences across trip types.

Method: A weakly supervised framework combining: (1) neighborhood-level POI semantic zones with distance-weighted spatial likelihoods, (2) differentiated inference strategies for mandatory vs. non-mandatory activities, and (3) multi-phase Pareto optimization minimizing distributional divergence from household travel surveys while maximizing inference reliability without labeled data.

Result: Evaluated on 81M+ staypoints in Los Angeles: activity type frequency JSD reduced by 23%, start time JSD by 48%, and duration JSD by 12% compared to a comparable baseline.

Conclusion: The approach offers a scalable, uncertainty-aware path from raw GPS trajectories to semantically annotated mobility data, useful for travel demand modeling and transportation policy analysis.

Abstract: Large-scale GPS trajectory data offer rich observations of human mobility, yet assigning trip purposes to detected stops remains challenging due to the absence of individual-level ground truth, spatial uncertainty from GPS noise and incomplete points of interest (POIs) coverage, and fundamental behavioral differences across trip purposes. We propose a weakly supervised framework integrating neighborhood-level POI semantic zones with distance-weighted spatial likelihoods, differentiated inference strategies for mandatory and non-mandatory activities, and a multi-phase Pareto optimization that jointly minimizes distributional divergence from household travel survey statistics and maximizes inference reliability without requiring annotated labels. Evaluated on over 81 million staypoints in Los Angeles, the framework reduces activity type frequency Jensen-Shannon distance (JSD) by 23%, start time JSD by 48%, and duration JSD by 12% respectively relative to a comparable baseline. The proposed approach provides a scalable and uncertainty-aware path from raw GPS trajectories to semantically annotated mobility data for travel demand modeling and transportation policy analysis.

</details>


### [573] [Valley3: Scaling Omni Foundation Models for E-commerce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01278)
*Zeyu Chen,Guanghao Zhou,Qixiang Yin,Ziwang Zhao,Huanjin Yao,Pengjiu Xia,Min Yang,Cen Chen,Minghui Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Valley3 is an omni multimodal LLM for e-commerce that handles text, images, video, and audio, with native multilingual audio capabilities, a four-stage pre-training pipeline, controllable reasoning modes (non-thinking + 3 levels), and agentic search, outperforming baselines on e-commerce benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a unified omni multimodal LLM specifically for diverse global e-commerce tasks, bridging the gap between vision-language models and crucial audio-visual needs (especially short-video scenarios) in the e-commerce domain.

Method: A four-stage omni e-commerce continued pre-training pipeline (audio understanding → cross-modal instruction-following → e-commerce domain knowledge → long-context reasoning), followed by post-training for long-chain reasoning with four controllable reasoning modes (non-thinking + 3 thinking levels), plus agentic search capabilities for proactive information retrieval.

Result: Valley3 consistently outperforms strong baselines on in-house and open-source e-commerce benchmarks across 6 tasks, while remaining competitive on general-domain benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed omni multimodal approach with progressive training and controllable reasoning effectively addresses diverse e-commerce scenarios, balancing efficiency and deep reasoning.

Abstract: In this work, we present Valley3, an omni multimodal large language model (MLLM) developed for diverse global e-commerce tasks, with unified understanding and reasoning capabilities across text, images, video, and audio. A key feature of Valley3 is its native multilingual audio capability for e-commerce, developed by extending vision-language models to better support crucial audio-visual tasks, particularly in short-video scenarios. To achieve this, we carefully design a four-stage omni e-commerce continued pre-training pipeline, through which Valley3 progressively acquires audio understanding, cross-modal instruction-following, e-commerce domain knowledge, and long-context reasoning capabilities, ultimately evolving into an omni model for diverse e-commerce scenarios. Then, we further improve Valley3 through post-training to encourage long-chain reasoning with controllable reasoning modes, enabling one non-thinking mode and three distinct levels of thinking, thereby balancing inference efficiency in simple scenarios with deep reasoning for complex applications. Moreover, we equip Valley3 with agentic search capabilities to proactively invoke search tools and acquire task-relevant information for e-commerce deep research tasks. To comprehensively assess the capabilities of Valley3, we construct an omni e-commerce benchmark spanning 6 tasks. Experimental results show that Valley3 consistently outperforms strong baselines on our in-house and open-source e-commerce benchmarks, while remaining competitive on general-domain benchmarks.

</details>


### [574] [Lifting Traces to Logic: Programmatic Skill Induction with Neuro-Symbolic Learning for Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01293)
*Jie-Jing Shao,Haiyan Yin,Yueming Lyu,Xingrui Yu,Lan-Zhe Guo,Ivor Tsang,James Kwok,Yu-Feng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NSI lifts interaction traces into logic-grounded programs with explicit control flows and dynamic variable binding, enabling agents to discover when and why to act, outperforming baselines on agentic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation model-driven agents fail at long-horizon planning due to transient prompting-based reasoning, and existing skill induction methods produce state-blind scripts lacking conditional logic for dynamic environments.

Method: Proposes Neuro-Symbolic Skill Induction (NSI), a framework that synthesizes explicit control flows and dynamic variable binding from interaction traces into modular, logic-grounded programs.

Result: NSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on a series of agentic tasks, enabling efficient generalization from few-shot examples and flexible adaptation to unseen goals.

Conclusion: NSI empowers agents to self-evolve into architects of logic-grounded skills, bridging the gap between neural flexibility and symbolic robustness for long-horizon planning.

Abstract: Foundation model-driven agents often struggle with long-horizon planning due to the transient nature of purely prompting-based reasoning. While existing skill induction methods mitigate this by distilling experience into state-blind parameterized scripts, they fail to capture the conditional logic required for robust execution in dynamic environments. In this paper, we propose Neuro-Symbolic Skill Induction (NSI), a framework that lifts interaction traces into modular, \textit{logic-grounded} programs. By synthesizing explicit control flows and dynamic variable binding, NSI empowers agents to discover \textit{when} and \textit{why} to act. This paradigm enables the efficient generalization, allowing agents to induce skills from few-shot examples and flexibly adapt to unseen goals. Experiments on a series of agentic tasks demonstrate that NSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, empowering agents to self-evolve into architects of logic-grounded skills.

</details>


### [575] [Segment-Aligned Policy Optimization for Multi-Modal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01327)
*Lei Gao,Zhuoming Li,Mengxi Jia,Jiakang Yuan,Hongbo Sun,Hao Sun,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SAPO introduces a segment-level reinforcement learning paradigm for LLMs that treats coherent reasoning steps as fundamental policy update units, outperforming token-level and sequence-level methods on reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token-level or sequence-level policy optimization misaligns with the natural step-wise structure of reasoning processes, causing poor credit assignment and unstable training in multi-modal reasoning tasks.

Method: SAPO proposes a step-wise Markov decision process over reasoning segments with segment-level value estimation, advantage computation, and importance sampling aligned with reasoning boundaries.

Result: SAPO consistently outperforms token-level and sequence-level methods on reasoning benchmarks, achieving significant accuracy improvements with better training stability and value estimation consistency.

Conclusion: Aligning RL updates with the intrinsic structure of reasoning leads to more efficient and semantically grounded policy optimization in complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Existing reinforcement learning approaches for Large Language Models typically perform policy optimization at the granularity of individual tokens or entire response sequences. However, such formulations often misalign with the natural step-wise structure of reasoning processes, leading to suboptimal credit assignment and unstable training in multi-modal reasoning tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose Segment-Aligned Policy Optimization (SAPO), a novel reinforcement learning paradigm that treats coherent reasoning steps, rather than tokens or full sequences as fundamental units of policy update. SAPO introduces a step-wise Markov decision process abstraction over reasoning segments, accompanied by segment-level value estimation, advantage computation, and importance sampling mechanisms that are semantically aligned with reasoning boundaries. Experiments on representative reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SAPO consistently outperforms token-level and sequence-level policy optimization methods, achieving significant accuracy improvements while exhibiting better training stability and value estimation consistency. Our work underscores the importance of aligning reinforcement learning updates with the intrinsic structure of reasoning, paving the way for more efficient and semantically grounded policy optimization in complex reasoning tasks. Codes and models will be released to ensure full reproducibility.

</details>


### [576] [Truth or Tribe: How In-group Favoritism Prioritize Facts in Persona Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01329)
*Shijun Lei,Hongyu Wang,Yunji Liang,Haowen Zheng,Bin Guo,Zhiwen Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a "Truth or Tribe" simulation framework to study in-group favoritism in persona agents when faced with contradicting information, revealing that agents favor identity-similar peers even when incorrect, and proposes three mitigation strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether in-group favoritism exists in persona agents when confronted with contradicting information (e.g., misinformation), and to find ways to mitigate its adverse effects—an understudied area in generative language models.

Method: Proposes a "Truth or Tribe" simulation framework using a triadic interaction paradigm, with controlled trials to evaluate moderating factors such as cognitive complexity and defeasible reasoning contexts.

Result: Persona agents exhibit strong in-group favoritism, accepting incorrect answers from identity-similar peers at higher rates. This bias persists in defeasible reasoning and intensifies with cognitive complexity.

Conclusion: Three intervention strategies—Identity-Blind Instruction, Structured Counterfactual Reasoning, and Heterogeneous Perspective Ensemble—are proposed to mitigate in-group favoritism biases in persona agents.

Abstract: In-group favoritism refers to the phenomena of favoring members of one's in-group over out-group members and is widely observed in numerous social cooperative behaviors. Recently, in-group favoritism biases have also been identified in generative language models. However, whether the in-group favoritism exists when persona agents are faced with contradicting information (e.g., misinformation), and how to mitigate the adverse effects of in-group favoritism biases in persona agents have been understudied. To address these problems, we propose a Truth or Tribe simulation framework to study the agent cooperation within the spread of contradicting information through a triadic interaction paradigm, and conduct controlled trials to evaluate the primary moderating factors. Extensive results showcase that persona agents display strong in-group favoritism, accepting incorrect answers from identity-similar peers at much higher rates than from dissimilar peers. In-group favoritism continues to emerge in defeasible reasoning contexts where no absolute truth exists, and it intensifies as cognitive complexity increases. Furthermore, three intervention strategies--Identity-Blind Instruction, Structured Counterfactual Reasoning, and Heterogeneous Perspective Ensemble--are proposed to mitigate the in-group favoritism.

</details>


### [577] [DiagramNet: An End-to-End Recognition Framework and Dataset for Non-Standard System-Level Diagrams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01338)
*Jincheng Lou,Ruohan Xu,Jiapeng Li,Junyin Pi,Runzhe Tao,Weijian Fan,Xiao Tan,Guojie Luo,Yibo Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DiagramNet is a new multimodal dataset (10,977 annotations, 15,515 QA pairs) for system-level chip diagrams, paired with a progressive training pipeline and decoupled multi-agent workflow (Perception → Reasoning → Knowledge). The 3B model with this workflow surpasses GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4, and Gemini-2.5-Pro by 2x, and generalizes to boost other models (e.g., 128.7x improvement for Gemini-2.5-Pro on Task 1).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: System-level diagrams lack standardized symbols and structured training data, making it difficult for existing MLLMs to recognize and reason about chip architectural blueprints.

Method: (1) DiagramNet dataset: 10,977 connection annotations + 15,515 chain-of-thought QA pairs across 4 tasks (Listing, Localization, Connection, Circuit QA). (2) Progressive training pipeline for a 3B model. (3) Decoupled multi-agent workflow splitting visual reasoning into Perception → Reasoning → Knowledge stages.

Result: The 3B model + workflow beats the 2025 EDA Elite Challenge winner, GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4, and Gemini-2.5-Pro (2x improvement). Workflow generalizes: boosts Gemini-2.5-Pro 128.7x and GPT-5 12.4x on Task 1. Transfers to AMSBench with only 60 images, achieving zero-shot connectivity reasoning on par with GPT-5/Claude-Sonnet-4, surpassing Netlistify.

Conclusion: DiagramNet and the multi-agent workflow effectively bridge the gap in MLLM-based chip diagram understanding, with strong performance and generalization to new domains (AMSBench).

Abstract: System-level diagrams encode the architectural blueprint of chip design, specifying module functions, dataflows, and interface protocols. However, non-standardized symbols and the scarcity of structured training data hinder existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) from recognizing these diagrams. To address this gap, we introduce DiagramNet, the first multimodal dataset for system-level diagrams, comprising 10,977 connection annotations and 15,515 chain-of-thought QA pairs across four tasks: Listing, Localization, Connection, and Circuit QA. Building on this dataset, we propose a progressive training pipeline together with a decoupled multi-agent workflow that decomposes complex visual reasoning into Perception, Reasoning, and Knowledge stages. On the DiagramNet benchmark, integrating our 3B-parameter model with the proposed workflow surpasses the 2025 EDA Elite Challenge winner and outperforms GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4, and Gemini-2.5-Pro by over 2x in end-to-end evaluation. Notably, the workflow generalizes beyond our model, boosting Task 1 performance by 128.7x for Gemini-2.5-Pro and 12.4x for GPT-5. Furthermore, with only 60 images for detector adaptation, the method transfers effectively to AMSBench, achieving zero-shot connectivity reasoning on par with GPT-5 and Claude-Sonnet-4 while surpassing the AMS state-of-the-art method Netlistify.

</details>


### [578] [Structural Ranking of the Cognitive Plausibility of Computational Models of Analogy and Metaphors with the Minimal Cognitive Grid](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01359)
*Alessio Donvito,Antonio Lieto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper uses the Minimal Cognitive Grid (MCG) framework to systematically evaluate cognitive plausibility of analogy/metaphor models (SME, CogSketch, METCL, LLMs) through formal operationalization of three dimensions: Functional/Structural Ratio, Generality, and Performance Match.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a systematic, formal, and quantitative assessment of leading computational models of analogy and metaphor (SME, CogSketch, METCL, LLMs) regarding their cognitive plausibility, using a consistent mathematical framework.

Method: The authors operationalize the Minimal Cognitive Grid (MCG) framework formally and quantitatively, analyzing three key dimensions: Functional/Structural Ratio, Generality, and Performance Match, applied to SME, CogSketch, METCL, and LLMs.

Result: The analysis yields quantitative comparisons of each model's alignment with cognitive theories across the three MCG dimensions, enabling systematic ranking and comparison of cognitive plausibility.

Conclusion: The MCG framework provides consistent, generalizable mathematical criteria for evaluating and comparing cognitive plausibility across different computational models of analogy and metaphor.

Abstract: In this paper, we employ the Minimal Cognitive Grid (MCG), a framework created to evaluate the cognitive plausibility of artificial systems, to offer a systematic assessment of leading computational models of analogy and metaphor, including the Structure-Mapping Engine (SME), CogSketch, METCL, and Large Language Models (LLMs). We present a formal and quantitative operationalization of the MCG framework and, through the analysis of its three main dimensions (Functional/Structural Ratio, Generality, and Performance Match), examine how well each system aligns with standard cognitive theories of the modeled phenomena, thus allowing for comparison of the models with respect to their cognitive plausibility, according to consistent and generalizable mathematical criteria.

</details>


### [579] [A Cellular Doctrine of Morality: Intrinsic Active Precision and the Mind-Reality Overload Dilemma](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01376)
*Ahsan Adeel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper warns that current AI systems, based on oversimplified neuroscience, amplify attention to information without verifying its validity, leading to a "mind-reality overload dilemma." The author proposes mitigating this by developing AI tools inspired by pyramidal neurons that use intrinsic precision mechanisms to evaluate coherence and validity before attention, potentially restoring epistemic conditions and supporting better-informed beliefs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems amplify attention to information without mechanisms to assess its validity, causing overload of biased/dubious information and risking confusion, poor judgment, and irrational behavior (termed the "mind-reality overload dilemma").

Method: Proposes building AI tools based on biophysical dynamics of pyramidal neurons, which support an intrinsic active precision mechanism that uses locally and globally coherent predictions to evaluate validity and contextual adequacy of evidence before attention or propagation.

Result: Such biologically-grounded AI may exhibit "real understanding," reduce information overload, amplify reliable information, and support better-informed beliefs and coherent judgments.

Conclusion: While not prescribing moral rules from biology, this approach could help restore epistemic conditions for society, though no guarantees exist.

Abstract: Current AI systems, grounded in oversimplified neuroscience, risk eroding the distinction between truth and falsehood. They maximize reward by amplifying attention to information without intrinsic precision mechanisms to assess whether it is valid or worth attending to. This increases both the volume of information and the inherent biases in what the system attends to, whether true, false, or irrelevant. If not corrected, this trend will accelerate, threatening to overload systems and individuals with biased and dubious information and increasing the risk of confusion, poor judgment, and irrational or harmful decisions and behaviour, a condition I term the mind-reality overload dilemma. I argue that this threat may be mitigated by providing the public with access to more advanced AI tools built on the biophysical dynamics of pyramidal neurons underlying awake thought and higher-order cognition. These neurons support an intrinsic active precision mechanism that, rather than merely maximizing reward, uses locally and globally coherent predictions to evaluate the validity and contextual adequacy of evidence before it is attended to or propagated through hierarchies, prioritizing coherence and adequacy before attention.~While this approach does not derive or prescribe moral rules from biology, it may give rise to AI with more "real understanding", helping restore epistemic conditions by reducing information overload and amplifying reliable information, thereby supporting the formation of better-informed beliefs and more coherent judgments that benefit society at large-though no guarantees exist.

</details>


### [580] [AI Safety as Control of Irreversibility: A Systems Framework for Decision-Energy and Sovereignty Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01415)
*Wesley Shu,Peng Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that AI's uniquely low deployment friction creates a new safety problem centered on controlling irreversible decisions, formalized through "decision-energy density" and sovereignty boundaries, with a boundary stabilization theorem showing safety requires preventing single-node concentration of irreversible power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems compress the gap between capability growth and deployment due to low marginal costs, unlike prior high-risk technologies constrained by physical bottlenecks. This changes the safety problem fundamentally—requiring a new formal framework beyond local correctness or alignment.

Method: The paper formalizes "decision-energy density" (rate-weighted capacity to generate/evaluate/select/execute consequential decisions) and identifies three sovereignty boundaries (irreversible decision authority, physical resource mobilization authority, self-expansion authority). It then proves a boundary stabilization theorem showing mechanisms like efficiency pressure and scale feedback concentrate decision-energy in the most efficient node.

Result: When deployment friction is low, decision-energy concentrates in the most efficient node, diffusing responsibility and raising irreversible system-level loss probability even with low per-action error rates. The boundary stabilization theorem shows safety requires preventing single-node release of irreversible power rather than proving systems are always correct.

Conclusion: AI safety should be reframed as layered control, authorization, and externally reviewable limits—linking alignment, security engineering, organizational economics, and institutional design—rather than focusing solely on output correctness or preference alignment.

Abstract: Recent AI systems compress the distance between capability growth and capability deployment. Earlier high-risk technologies were slowed by capital intensity, physical bottlenecks, organizational inertia, and specialized supply chains. By contrast, AI capabilities can be copied, invoked, embedded in workflows, and scaled across institutions at low marginal cost. This paper argues that declining deployment friction changes the safety problem at its root. Safety is not only local output correctness or preference alignment, but the control of irreversibility under rising decision density.
  The paper formalizes this claim through decision-energy density: the rate-weighted capacity of a node to generate, evaluate, select, and execute consequential decisions. It then identifies three sovereignty boundaries that determine whether AI remains an amplifier within a human-governed system or becomes a de facto control center: irreversible decision authority, physical resource mobilization authority, and self-expansion authority. The model shows how efficiency pressure, path dependence, scale feedback, and weak boundary constraints concentrate decision-energy in the most efficient node. This concentration can diffuse responsibility and raise the probability of irreversible system-level loss even when local per-action error rates remain low.
  The main result is a boundary stabilization theorem. It shows that safety need not require proving that advanced systems are always correct. Instead, it requires institutional and technical designs that prevent irreversible power from being released by a single high-efficiency node. The paper reframes AI safety as layered control, authorization, and externally reviewable limits, linking alignment, security engineering, organizational economics, and institutional design.

</details>


### [581] [TimeTok: Granularity-Controllable Time-Series Generation via Hierarchical Tokenization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01418)
*Seokhyun Lee,Jaeho Kim,Changjun Oh,Mihaela van der Schaar,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TimeTok is a unified framework for granularity-controllable time-series generation, using hierarchical tokenization and autoregressive generation to produce time series at any target granularity from coarse input or from scratch, achieving state-of-the-art performance and strong transferability across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-series generative models lack control over temporal granularity, forcing users to accept whatever granularity the model produces, limiting user-driven generation flexibility.

Method: Proposes a hierarchical tokenization strategy that maps time series into ordered coarse-to-fine token sequences, with autoregressive generation across granularity levels, where controlling token block count provides explicit output detail control.

Result: Excels at GC-TSG tasks, achieves state-of-the-art performance in standard generation, and demonstrates strong transferability across multiple datasets with heterogeneous temporal granularities.

Conclusion: This is the first unified framework covering the full generative spectrum for time series, offering a valuable foundation for models benefiting from diverse temporal granularities.

Abstract: Time-series generative models often lack control over temporal granularity, forcing users to accept whatever granularity the model produces. To enable truly user-driven generation, we introduce TimeTok, a unified framework for Granularity-Controllable Time-Series Generation (GC-TSG), which generates time series at any target granularity from any coarser input (e.g., rough sketches) or from scratch. At the core of TimeTok is a hierarchical tokenization strategy that maps time series into an ordered sequence of tokens, from coarse to fine temporal granularity. Our autoregressive generation process operates across these granularity levels, producing token blocks that are decoded back into continuous time series. This design naturally enables GC-TSG - including standard generation - within a single framework, where controlling the number of token blocks provides explicit control over output detail. Experiments show that TimeTok excels at GC-TSG tasks while achieving state-of-the-art performance in standard generation. Furthermore, we showcase TimeTok's potential as a foundational tokenizer by training on multiple datasets with heterogeneous temporal granularities, verifying strong transferability that consistently outperforms models trained on individual datasets. To our knowledge, this is the first unified framework that covers the full generative spectrum for time series, offering a valuable foundation for models that benefit from diverse temporal granularities.

</details>


### [582] [Artificial Jagged Intelligence as Uneven Optimization Energy Allocation Capability Concentration, Redistribution, and Optimization Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01420)
*Wesley Shu,Peng Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Artificial Jagged Intelligence (AJI) as uneven optimization pressure in large learning systems, proving that finite-budget training with anisotropic objective structure leads to jagged capability profiles, and explores redistribution mechanisms to mitigate such unevenness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large learning systems exhibit strong local capabilities but remain brittle in other domains; the paper aims to move beyond treating this as a mere observation to a formal, testable theory of how uneven capabilities emerge from finite optimization resources.

Method: The paper develops a formal mathematical model treating training as a finite-budget process distributing gradient-driven update energy across capability-relevant directions. It defines key measures (capability gain, optimization energy share, jaggedness), proves concentration bounds and a finite-budget tradeoff theorem, and studies redistribution interventions like energy-variance regularization and auxiliary objectives.

Result: Key results include: (1) persistent concentration of update energy yields lower bounds on dispersion in capability gains; (2) a tradeoff theorem showing capability prioritization imposes opportunity costs absent positive coupling; (3) early energy concentration predicts later jaggedness; (4) scaling under narrow objectives does not eliminate anisotropy; (5) auxiliary objectives can revive neglected capabilities.

Conclusion: AJI is not just a descriptive label but a testable theory connecting uneven emergence, training architecture, and optimization governance, with implications for how finite optimization resources produce structurally uneven capability formation.

Abstract: Artificial Jagged Intelligence (AJI) denotes a recurring pattern in which large learning systems exhibit strong local capabilities while remaining weak or brittle in other domains. This paper develops a formal theory of AJI as uneven allocation of optimization pressure. We model training as a finite-budget process that distributes gradient-driven update energy across capability-relevant directions in parameter space. In this model, jagged capability profiles arise from anisotropic objective structure, data geometry, and representational coupling rather than from a single scalar quantity called intelligence.
  The paper defines capability gain, optimization energy share, and jaggedness, then proves that persistent concentration of cumulative update energy yields lower bounds on dispersion in capability gains. A finite-budget tradeoff theorem shows why prioritizing one capability can impose opportunity costs on others unless positive coupling or shared structure offsets the cost. The analysis also studies redistribution mechanisms, including energy-variance regularization and auxiliary structural objectives, as interventions that reshape the optimization field.
  The resulting framework links uneven emergence, training architecture, and optimization governance. It predicts that early concentration of update energy should forecast later capability jaggedness; that scaling under a narrow objective need not eliminate anisotropy; and that explicitly funded auxiliary objectives can revive neglected capabilities. AJI is therefore not merely a descriptive label for uneven model behavior, but a testable theory of how finite optimization resources produce concentrated, delayed, and structurally uneven capability formation.

</details>


### [583] [SCALE-LoRA: Auditing Post-Retrieval LoRA Composition with Residual Merging and View Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01429)
*Shuaipeng Zhou,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCALE is a post-retrieval framework for reusing open-pool LoRA adapters, featuring LASRC for sparse composition and a reliability-analysis layer for uncertainty estimation, tested on FLAN-T5-Large and BBH benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LoRA adapter reuse methods (task-level composition, instance-level selection) are not automatic: retrieving relevant adapters doesn't guarantee parameter compatibility, and composing them doesn't guarantee reliable outputs.

Method: SCALE consists of (1) LASRC: preserves a linear anchor while residualizing block-wise adapter update directions to address merge interference, and (2) a reliability-analysis layer that treats disagreement among sparse composition views as an observable uncertainty signal.

Result: LASRC achieves directional single-view gains under fixed retrieval on FLAN-T5-Large and BBH with 97 LoRA adapters. SCALE-support is reported as a query-label-free reliability-analysis variant. Protocol-distinct BBH-8 validation shows same qualitative trend on three decoder-only backbones.

Conclusion: SCALE provides a usable (1.0×) merge path and a higher-cost reliability layer for open-pool LoRA reuse, though the reliability layer is presented as an analysis variant rather than a calibrated selector.

Abstract: Libraries of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters are becoming a practical by-product of parameter-efficient adaptation. Once such adapters accumulate, a natural question is no longer how to train one adapter for one task, but how to reuse an open pool of adapters for a new task given only a small support set. Prior work has shown that LoRA modules can be composed at the task level and dynamically selected at the instance level. However, open-pool LoRA reuse is not automatic: retrieving relevant adapters does not guarantee that their parameter updates are compatible, and composing adapters does not guarantee reliable outputs.
  We introduce the Sparse-Composition Agreement Layer (SCALE), a post-retrieval audit and composition framework for open-pool LoRA reuse. SCALE contains a deployable 1.0* merge path, Layer-Adaptive Sparse Residual Composition (LASRC), and a higher-cost reliability-analysis layer for multi-view disagreement. LASRC addresses merge interference by preserving a linear anchor while residualizing block-wise adapter update directions. The reliability layer treats disagreement among sparse composition views as an observable uncertainty signal and compares agreement, support-loss proxy selection, and oracle headroom under explicit path cost. In matched FLAN-T5-Large, BIG-Bench Hard (BBH), and 97-LoRA experiments, LASRC gives a directional single-view gain under fixed retrieval, while SCALE-support is reported as a query-label-free 3.0* reliability-analysis variant rather than as a calibrated or throughput-equivalent selector. Protocol-distinct BBH-8 validation shows the same qualitative trend on three decoder-only backbones. Detailed scores, paired audits, and path-cost records are reported in the experimental section.

</details>


### [584] [Rethinking Explanations: Formalizing Contrast in Description Logics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01442)
*Yasir Mahmood,Arnab Sharma,Axel-Cyrille Ngonga Ngomo,Balram Tiwari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes contrastive explanations for DL knowledge bases, answering why axiom P is true instead of another axiom Q, with formal foundations in description logics EL and ALC, plus implementation and evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing explanation formalisms (justifications and abductive reasoning) only point out reasoning steps behind entailments but lack user-centered approaches, ignoring an inquirer's needs, level of understanding, or prior knowledge. Users often are surprised because they anticipated a different similar event, and individual explanations for "why P" and "why not Q" are unsatisfactory alone.

Method: The authors define formal foundations of contrasting questions and contrastive explanations within description logics. Facts are ABox assertions C(x); foils can be C(y) (same concept, different individual) or D(x) (different concept, same individual). Properties are explored in DL EL and ALC. An implementation and experimental evaluation on KBs of varying sizes are provided.

Result: Formal definitions of contrastive explanations in DL are presented, covering two types of foils (different individual or different concept). Properties of these explanations are characterized in EL and ALC. An implementation demonstrates feasibility, with experiments on KBs of varying sizes.

Conclusion: Contrastive explanations provide a user-centered approach to explaining DL entailments by addressing the difference between a fact and an expected alternative, filling a gap left by traditional justification and abduction methods.

Abstract: There has been a growing interest in explaining entailments over description logic (DL) knowledge bases. The existing explanation formalisms focus on justifications to explain true axioms, and abductive reasoning to explain missing axioms in a knowledge base. However, these formalisms only point out the reasoning steps behind a (missing) entailment and lack a user-centered approach as they do not consider an inquirer's needs, level of understanding, or prior knowledge. We propose contrastive explanations, aiming at answering "why an axiom P (fact) is true instead of another axiom Q (foil)" over description logic knowledge bases. The motivation arises from the observation that when a user discovers that P has occurred, they are often surprised because they anticipated the occurrence of another similar event Q. Furthermore, individual explanations for "why P" and "why not Q" are unsatisfactory since a user expects to see the difference between P and Q. In this work, we first present formal foundations of contrasting questions and then define contrastive explanations within description logics. To this end, facts include ABox assertions of the form C(x) for a concept C and individual x. Possible foils for such facts are assertions C(y) (contrasting against an individual y), or D(x) (contrasting against a concept D). Additionally, we explore the properties of contrastive explanations in the DL EL and ALC. We also provide an implementation of our definition and an experimental evaluation on KBs of varying sizes.

</details>


### [585] [CoFlow: Coordinated Few-Step Flow for Offline Multi-Agent Decision Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01457)
*Guowei Zou,Haitao Wang,Beiwen Zhang,Boning Zhang,Hejun Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoFlow is a single-pass, few-step generative flow model for offline multi-agent reinforcement learning that maintains inter-agent coordination through joint-coupled velocity fields, achieving state-of-the-art results across 60 configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing few-step generative models for offline MARL sacrifice inter-agent coordination for inference speed. The authors aim to show this trade-off is unnecessary by designing a method that achieves both fast inference and preserved coordination.

Method: CoFlow combines Coordinated Velocity Attention (CVA) with Adaptive Coordination Gating to maintain joint-coupled velocity fields. It uses a finite-difference consistency surrogate to replace memory-heavy Jacobian-vector product backpropagation with two stop-gradient forward passes.

Result: Evaluated across 60 configurations (MPE, MA-MuJoCo, SMAC), CoFlow matches or surpasses Gaussian/value-based, transformer, diffusion, and prior flow baselines on episodic return. Three coordination probes confirm gains stem from coordination, not per-agent capacity. Single-pass inference suffices across all configurations.

Conclusion: CoFlow achieves state-of-the-art coordination quality in 1-3 denoising steps under both centralized and decentralized execution, proving that fast, few-step inference does not require sacrificing coordination.

Abstract: Generative models have emerged as a major paradigm for offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), but existing approaches require many iterative sampling steps. Recent few-step accelerations either distill a joint teacher into independent students or apply averaged velocities independently per agent, suggesting that few-step inference requires sacrificing inter-agent coordination. We show this trade-off is not necessary: single-pass multi-agent generation can preserve coordination when the velocity field is natively joint-coupled. We propose Coordinated few-step Flow (CoFlow), an architecture that combines Coordinated Velocity Attention (CVA) with Adaptive Coordination Gating. A finite-difference consistency surrogate further replaces memory-prohibitive Jacobian-vector product backpropagation through the averaged velocity field with two stop-gradient forward passes. Across 60 configurations spanning MPE, MA-MuJoCo, and SMAC, CoFlow matches or surpasses Gaussian / value-based, transformer, diffusion, and prior flow baselines on episodic return. Three independent coordination probes confirm that the gains flow through inter-agent coordination rather than per-agent capacity. A denoising-step sweep shows that single-pass inference suffices on every configuration. CoFlow reaches state-of-the-art coordination quality in 1-3 denoising steps under both centralized and decentralized execution. Project page: https://github.com/Guowei-Zou/coflow.

</details>


### [586] [Grounding Multi-Hop Reasoning in Structural Causal Models via Group Relative Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01482)
*Yunhan Bu,Quan Zhang,Huaping Zhang,Guotong Geng,Chunxiao Gao,Askar Hamdulla,Juan Wang,Qiuchi Li,Baohua Zhang,Shuai Lei,Yunbo Cao,Zhunchen Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes SCM-GRPO, a framework for Multi-Hop Fact Verification that uses Structural Causal Models to model evidence-claim dependencies and Rule-based Reinforcement Learning (GRPO) to dynamically optimize reasoning chain length, outperforming SOTA baselines on HoVer and EX-FEVER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer from hallucinations and fractured logical chains in MHFV; existing CoT methods lack explicit modeling of causal dependencies between evidence and claims.

Method: A framework grounding reasoning in a Structural Causal Model (SCM), treating verification as constructive causal inference, plus a Rule-based RL strategy using GRPO to optimize reasoning chain depth vs. conciseness.

Result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on HoVer and EX-FEVER datasets; identifies an "inverted U-shaped" correlation between reasoning chain length and accuracy.

Conclusion: SCM-GRPO offers a reliable and interpretable solution for complex fact verification by explicitly modeling causal dependencies and dynamically optimizing structural complexity.

Abstract: Multi-Hop Fact Verification (MHFV) necessitates complex reasoning across disparate evidence, posing significant challenges for Large Language Models (LLMs) which often suffer from hallucinations and fractured logical chains. Existing methods, while improving transparency via Chain-of-Thought (CoT), lack explicit modeling of the causal dependencies between evidence and claims. In this work, we introduce a novel framework that grounds reasoning in a Structural Causal Model (SCM), treating verification as a constructive causal inference process. We empirically identify an "inverted U-shaped" correlation between reasoning chain length and accuracy, revealing that excessive structural complexity degrades performance. To address this, we propose a Rule-based Reinforcement Learning strategy using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). This approach dynamically optimizes the trade-off between structural depth and conciseness. Extensive experiments on HoVer and EX-FEVER demonstrate that our SCM-GRPO framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, offering a reliable and interpretable solution for complex fact verification.

</details>


### [587] [MAP-Law: Coverage-Driven Retrieval Control for Multi-Turn Legal Consultation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01486)
*Qinchuan Cheng,Ruixuan Xie,Jiaqi Liu,Xiaoya Yuan,Yuxin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAP-Law is a coverage-driven framework for legal consultation that uses structured state tracking (issue, legal element, and evidence nodes) with dynamic retrieval control, achieving high element coverage (0.860) with far fewer retrieval rounds (2.9) and evidence pieces (5.8) compared to fixed-depth baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal consultation is high-stakes and requires authoritative support, but existing retrieval-augmented generation agents use fixed retrieval depth or coarse heuristics, leading to either insufficient evidence or excessive retrieval that increases context burden and weakens answer focus.

Method: MAP-Law models consultation as a controlled retrieval process over a joint structured state (issue, legal element, evidence nodes). After each retrieval round, it computes Element Coverage, Evidence Coverage, and Marginal Gain to decide whether to continue retrieval, redirect search, or generate the final response, turning stopping from a fixed hyperparameter into an interpretable decision.

Result: On 50 labor-law cases, MAP-Law with DeepSeek achieves 0.860 Element Coverage using 2.9 retrieval rounds and 5.8 evidence pieces. Compared to a fixed seven-round baseline, it reduces evidence volume by over 80% and retrieval rounds by 58%.

Conclusion: Coverage-driven stopping, joint graph representation, and LLM-based action selection all independently contribute to the framework's effectiveness, making retrieval control auditable and aligned with legal argumentative structure.

Abstract: Legal consultation is a high-stakes, knowledge-intensive task that requires agents to identify relevant legal issues, retrieve authoritative support, and determine when evidence is sufficient for a recommendation. Although retrieval-augmented generation has improved grounding in legal question answering, many multi-turn legal agents still rely on fixed retrieval depth or coarse heuristic control. This often leads to either insufficient support for key legal elements or excessive retrieval that increases context burden and weakens answer focus.
  We propose MAP-Law, a coverage-driven framework for retrieval control in multi-turn legal consultation. MAP-Law models consultation as a controlled retrieval process over a joint structured state consisting of issue nodes, legal element nodes, and evidence nodes. After each retrieval round, the agent computes Element Coverage, Evidence Coverage, and Marginal Gain, and uses these signals to decide whether to continue retrieval, redirect the search, or generate the final response. In this way, MAP-Law turns stopping from a fixed hyperparameter into an interpretable and auditable decision aligned with legal argumentative structure.
  Experiments on a self-constructed dataset of 50 cases across eight labor-law scenarios show that MAP-Law with DeepSeek as the action selector achieves an Element Coverage of 0.860 using only 2.9 retrieval rounds and 5.8 evidence pieces on average. Compared with a fixed seven-round baseline, it reduces evidence volume by over 80% and retrieval rounds by 58%. Ablation results further confirm the independent contributions of coverage-driven stopping, joint graph representation, and LLM-based action selection.

</details>


### [588] [SciResearcher: Scaling Deep Research Agents for Frontier Scientific Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01489)
*Tianshi Zheng,Rui Wang,Xiyun Li,Yangqiu Song,Tianqing Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciResearcher is a fully automated framework that synthesizes conceptual and computational tasks from sparse academic sources to create training data for scientific reasoning agents. Using supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, they built SciResearcher-8B, which achieves SOTA results (19.46% on HLE-Bio/Chem-Gold) and 13-15% absolute gains on biology/literature benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep research agents rely on knowledge graph construction or iterative web browsing, which fail in frontier science because domain knowledge is scattered across sparse, heterogeneous sources, and solving frontier problems requires sophisticated computation and reasoning beyond factual recall.

Method: SciResearcher is a fully automated agentic framework that synthesizes diverse conceptual and computational tasks grounded in academic evidence. It elicits information acquisition, tool-integrated reasoning, and long-horizon capabilities. The curated data is used for supervised fine-tuning and agentic reinforcement learning to produce the SciResearcher-8B agent model.

Result: SciResearcher-8B achieves 19.46% on HLE-Bio/Chem-Gold (new SOTA at its parameter scale, surpassing larger proprietary agents) and 13-15% absolute gains on SuperGPQA-Hard-Biology and TRQA-Literature benchmarks.

Conclusion: SciResearcher introduces a new paradigm for automated data construction for frontier scientific reasoning and offers a scalable path toward future scientific agents.

Abstract: Frontier scientific reasoning is rapidly emerging as a key foundation for advancing AI agents in automated scientific discovery. Deep research agents offer a promising approach to this challenge. These models develop robust problem-solving capabilities through post-training on information-seeking tasks, which are typically curated via knowledge graph construction or iterative web browsing. However, these strategies face inherent limitations in frontier science, where domain-specific knowledge is scattered across sparse and heterogeneous academic sources, and problem solving requires sophisticated computation and reasoning far beyond factual recall. To bridge this gap, we introduce SciResearcher, a fully automated agentic framework for frontier-science data construction. SciResearcher synthesizes diverse conceptual and computational tasks grounded in academic evidence, while eliciting information acquisition, tool-integrated reasoning, and long-horizon capabilities. Leveraging the curated data for supervised fine-tuning and agentic reinforcement learning, we develop SciResearcher-8B, an agent foundation model that achieves 19.46% on the HLE-Bio/Chem-Gold benchmark, establishing a new state of the art at its parameter scale and surpassing several larger proprietary agents. It further achieves 13-15% absolute gains on SuperGPQA-Hard-Biology and TRQA-Literature benchmarks. Overall, SciResearcher introduces a new paradigm for automated data construction for frontier scientific reasoning and offers a scalable path toward future scientific agents.

</details>


### [589] [MILD: Mediator Agent System with Bidirectional Perception and Multi-Layered Alignment for Human-Vehicle Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01507)
*Jiyao Wang,Yunbiao Wang,Yubo Jiao,Xiao Yang,Dengbo He,Sasan Jafarnejad,Luis Miranda-Moreno,Raphael Frank,Jiangbo Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces MILD (Mediator-in-the-Loop-Driving), an agentic system architecture that transforms human drivers from passive supervisors to active managers in partial driving automation. It uses a perception agent, a strategy agent with Evidence- and Constraint-weighted Policy Optimization (ECPO), and retrieval-augmented generation to align with safety regulations and driver preferences. Field experiments show MILD outperforms baselines in perception accuracy, strategy quality, and human-rated metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partial driving automation increases cognitive demands on human drivers due to bidirectional misalignment between the vehicle's intentions/decision logic and the driver's dynamic state/preferences, undermining shared situational awareness and causing coordination failures.

Method: The paper proposes MILD (Mediator-in-the-Loop-Driving) with three key components: (1) a perception agent for joint in-cabin and out-of-cabin understanding, (2) a lightweight strategy agent using Evidence- and Constraint-weighted Policy Optimization (ECPO) to generate compliant and explainable action suggestions, and (3) a retrieval-augmented generation module incorporating traffic regulations, speed recommendations, and driver preferences into the decision loop.

Result: Field experiments across three open datasets show MILD consistently outperforms baselines in perception accuracy and strategy quality under auditable offline metrics, and yields higher human-rated policy adequacy, comfort, and explanation.

Conclusion: This work offers a practical pathway for building auditable and aligned agents for human-vehicle collaborative driving by shifting the human role from passive supervisor to active manager.

Abstract: Prior studies report that partial driving automation can increase the cognitive demands on human drivers. This effect largely arises from human drivers' lack of transparent insight into the vehicle's intentions and decision logic, as well as from automated systems' limited awareness of the driver's dynamic state and preferences. This bidirectional misalignment undermines shared situational awareness and exacerbates coordination failures in human-vehicle interaction. To address these limitations, we argue for a paradigm shift that elevates the human role from passive supervisor to active manager. We introduce the Mediator-in-the-Loop-Driving (MILD) system, based on an agentic system architecture to facilitate synergistic human-vehicle collaboration. MILD integrates a perception agent for joint in-cabin and out-of-cabin understanding with a lightweight strategy agent that generates compliant and explainable action suggestions. To ensure these strategies are strictly aligned with safety regulations and human values, we develop Evidence- and Constraint-weighted Policy Optimization (ECPO). ECPO leverages automatic validators to steer the agent toward behaviors that are not only accurate but also structurally complete, substantiated by evidence, and free from constraint violations. Furthermore, a retrieval-augmented generation module dynamically incorporates constraints from traffic regulations, speed recommendations, and driver preferences into the decision loop. Field experiments across three open datasets demonstrate that MILD consistently outperforms baselines in both perception accuracy and strategy quality under auditable offline metrics, and yields higher human-rated policy adequacy, comfort, and explanation than baselines. This work offers a practical pathway for building auditable and aligned agents for human-vehicle collaborative driving.

</details>


### [590] [Multi-Agent Reasoning Improves Compute Efficiency: Pareto-Optimal Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01566)
*Florian Valentin Wunderlich,Lars Benedikt Kaesberg,Jan Philip Wahle,Terry Ruas,Bela Gipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper systematically analyzes inference scaling strategies (self-consistency, self-refinement, multi-agent debate, mixture-of-agents) for computational performance tradeoffs, finding that multi-agent methods outperform self-consistency at equal compute budgets, with mixture-of-agents being most efficient when parallel generations exceed sequential aggregations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference methods improve LM predictions without training but often neglect computational efficiency. The authors aim to systematically evaluate performance-cost tradeoffs of scaling strategies for real-world resource-constrained applications.

Method: Systematic analysis across 34 configurations and 100+ evaluations on MMLU-Pro and BBH benchmarks, computing Pareto-optimal fronts for methods including self-consistency, self-refinement, multi-agent debate, and mixture-of-agents across model sizes and parameter configurations.

Result: Inference scaling improves accuracy up to +7.1% points over CoT at 20x compute budget on MMLU-Pro. At equal compute, debate and mixture-of-agents outperform self-consistency by 1.3% and 2.7% points respectively. Self-consistency saturates earlier; multi-agent gains persist on harder tasks.

Conclusion: Mixture-of-agents is most efficient when parallel generations exceed sequential aggregations, providing a simple multi-agent design guideline for cost-effective inference scaling.

Abstract: Advances in inference methods have enabled language models to improve their predictions without additional training. These methods often prioritize raw performance over cost-effective compute usage. However, computational efficiency is key for real-world applications with resource constraints. We provide a systematic analysis of the inference scaling strategies self-consistency, self-refinement, multi-agent debate, and mixture-of-agents, to study their computational performance tradeoffs. We evaluate methods on two reasoning benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, BBH) and include extensive parameter configurations (e.g., scaling the number of parallel predictions, agents, and debate rounds) across different model sizes. Across 34 configurations and over 100 evaluations, we compute the Pareto-optimal front to select methods that achieve the best accuracy with the lowest computational budget. Notably, inference scaling improves accuracy by up to +7.1% points over chain-of-thought at the highest evaluated budgets (20x the CoT compute budget) on MMLU-Pro. With an equal computing budget, debate and mixture-of-agents outperform self-consistency by 1.3% and 2.7% points, respectively. While self-consistency saturates earlier, multi-agent gains persist, particularly on more complicated tasks. We identify a simple multi-agent design guideline: mixture-of-agents is most efficient when the number of parallel generations exceeds the number of sequential aggregations.

</details>


### [591] [Evaluating Agentic AI in the Wild: Failure Modes, Drift Patterns, and a Production Evaluation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01604)
*Mukund Pandey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper identifies seven failure modes in production agentic AI systems, demonstrates that standard evaluation metrics fail to detect most of them, and proposes a new continuous evaluation framework (PAEF) designed for production-scale monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM evaluation frameworks (HELM, MT-Bench, AgentBench, BIG-bench) are designed for controlled, single-session lab settings and cannot handle challenges that emerge when agentic AI systems operate continuously in production—such as compounding errors, tool failure cascades, non-deterministic drift, and lack of ground truth for long-horizon tasks.

Method: The authors present a taxonomy of seven failure modes from billion-event-scale production systems, empirically test where standard metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore, accuracy/AUC, agentic benchmarks) fail to detect each mode, and propose PAEF—a five-dimension evaluation framework with an open-source reference implementation designed for continuous evaluation on production traffic.

Result: Standard metrics fail to detect four of the seven failure modes entirely and detect the other three only after a multi-cycle lag.

Conclusion: Continuous, production-traffic-based evaluation frameworks like PAEF are necessary to reliably monitor agentic AI systems in deployment, as existing benchmarks and metrics are fundamentally insufficient.

Abstract: Existing evaluation frameworks for large language models -- including HELM, MT-Bench, AgentBench, and BIG-bench -- are designed for controlled, single-session, lab-scale settings. They do not address the evaluation challenges that emerge when agentic AI systems operate continuously in production: compounding decision errors, tool failure cascades, non-deterministic output drift, and the absence of ground truth for long-horizon tasks. This paper makes three contributions. First, we present a taxonomy of seven failure modes unique to production agentic systems, each grounded in observations from systems operating at billion-event scale. Second, we demonstrate empirically where standard metrics -- ROUGE, BERTScore, accuracy/AUC, and the agentic benchmarks above -- fail to detect each failure mode. Third, we propose PAEF (Production Agentic Evaluation Framework), a five-dimension evaluation framework with an open-source reference implementation, designed for continuous evaluation on production traffic rather than episodic benchmark runs. Our analysis shows that standard metrics fail to detect four of the seven failure modes entirely and detect three others only after a lag of multiple evaluation cycles.

</details>


### [592] [CP-SynC: Multi-Agent Zero-Shot Constraint Modeling in MiniZinc with Synthesized Checkers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01675)
*Yuliang Song,Eldan Cohen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CP-SynC is a multi-agent workflow using LLMs for zero-shot constraint programming modeling in MiniZinc, employing parallel modeling trajectories, synthesized semantic checkers, and evidence aggregation to outperform baselines on 100 CP problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translating natural language problem descriptions into executable CP models is a major bottleneck; LLMs struggle with subtle semantic errors without oracle validation at test time.

Method: CP-SynC uses a multi-agent workflow with modeling agents (generating/refining candidate models), validation agents (synthesizing semantic checkers for feedback), parallel exploration of multiple modeling trajectories, and selection agents that aggregate evidence via multi-agent voting to pick the final model.

Result: On a benchmark of 100 CP problems, CP-SynC substantially outperforms existing baselines in MiniZinc modeling.

Conclusion: CP-SynC effectively addresses the semantic error problem in LLM-based CP modeling through multi-agent coordination, parallel exploration, and evidence aggregation.

Abstract: Constraint Programming (CP) is a powerful paradigm for solving combinatorial problems, yet translating natural language problem descriptions into executable models remains a significant bottleneck. While Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise in automating this translation, they often struggle with subtle semantic errors in the absence of oracle validation at test time. To address this, we introduce CP-SynC (Constraint Programming modeling with Synthesized Checkers), a multi-agent workflow for zero-shot constraint modeling in MiniZinc. CP-SynC coordinates modeling agents that generate and refine candidate models and validation agents that synthesize semantic checkers to provide feedback on semantic correctness. To mitigate noise inherent in individual LLM outputs, CP-SynC explores multiple modeling trajectories in parallel and employs selection agents to select the final model via multi-agent evidence aggregation. Extensive experiments on a benchmark of 100 CP problems show that CP-SynC substantially outperforms existing baselines in MiniZinc modeling.

</details>


### [593] [Latent State Design for World Models under Sufficiency Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01694)
*Keon Woo Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a functional taxonomy for world models based on what their latent state is designed for (not architecture), and argues that actionable world models should match the state construction to the task rather than maximizing information preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world-model research is grouped by architecture or application, which hides critical distinctions (e.g., predictive sufficiency vs. control sufficiency). The authors aim to refocus the field on latent state design under sufficiency constraints.

Method: The paper introduces a functional taxonomy categorizing latent states by purpose (predictive embedding, recurrent belief state, object/causal structure, latent action interface, grounded planning interface, memory substrate). It provides an evaluation framework with seven axes and uses a diagnostic matrix to assess what each latent state preserves, discards, and enables.

Result: Seven evaluation axes are compared across methods: representation, prediction, planning, controllability, causal/counterfactual support, memory, and uncertainty. The resulting matrix reveals trade-offs between different sufficiency constraints.

Conclusion: An actionable world model is one whose state construction matches the task requirements, not one that preserves maximal information. The key insight is distinguishing predictive sufficiency from control sufficiency.

Abstract: A world model matters to an agent only through the state it constructs. That state must preserve some information, discard other information, and support some future function: prediction, control, planning, memory, grounding, or counterfactual reasoning. This paper treats world-model research as latent state design under sufficiency constraints.
  We propose a functional taxonomy that groups methods by what their latent state is for, rather than by architecture or application domain: predictive embedding, recurrent belief state, object/causal structure, latent action interface, grounded planning interface, and memory substrate. These roles expose distinctions that architecture-based groupings hide, including the gap between predictive sufficiency and control sufficiency, and the gap between passive video prediction and counterfactual action modeling.
  The taxonomy supports an evaluation framework that judges a model by the sufficiency constraint its latent state was built to satisfy. We compare methods along seven axes: representation, prediction, planning, controllability, causal/counterfactual support, memory, and uncertainty. We use the resulting matrix as a diagnostic for what a latent state preserves, discards, and enables.
  The conclusion that follows is that an actionable world model is the one whose state construction matches the task, not the one that preserves the most information.

</details>


### [594] [Model Routing as a Trust Problem: Route Receipts for Adaptive AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01710)
*Vincent Schmalbach*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes "route receipts" — compact, auditable records of the runtime path taken by an AI request (e.g., model alias, service tier, fallback rules, safety handling) — to improve transparency in adaptive AI systems. The authors argue that current platforms generate fragments of this information but lack a portable per-answer record, and they introduce a minimal schema and redaction model to fill this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Routing decisions (e.g., version aliases, service tiers, regional endpoints, fallback rules) affect cost, quality, and accountability of AI responses. Users cannot see what routing path was taken, which breaks trust when outcomes change unexpectedly. Answering "which model?" is insufficient — the full runtime path matters for auditability.

Method: The paper introduces the "route receipt" concept, proposes a minimal schema and redaction model for such receipts, and conducts a documentation-based survey of several AI platforms and serving stacks to identify existing transparency fragments.

Result: Route transparency fragments already exist across platforms but are not portable or per-answer. The proposed route receipt schema fills this gap. The survey reveals that current practices lack a standardized, compact record of runtime routing decisions.

Conclusion: Route receipts should become a standard part of model documentation alongside model cards, capturing runtime conditions. This enables users to reconstruct important routing decisions without exposing proprietary internals, thereby improving trust and auditability in adaptive AI systems.
