Motivation: Existing evaluations measure end-to-end QA accuracy, which conflates memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reasoning, making it impossible to isolate how well memory writers actually preserve information under fixed budgets.

Method: Introduces MEMAUDIT, a package-based oracle evaluation protocol that defines an auditable optimization problem (concave-over-modular coverage objective under storage and one-representation-per-experience constraints) solved via branch-and-bound with MILP certification.

Result: Across exact packages, stress tests, human-audited natural slices, and exported memory stores (Mem0, A-Mem, Letta), MEMAUDIT separates representation quality, validity-state preservation, and budget-aware selection effects that end-to-end QA cannot localize.

Conclusion: MEMAUDIT provides reusable package generators, certified solvers, natural exports, scorers, and reproducibility metadata for auditing what memory writers preserve under fixed budgets.

Abstract: Long-term LLM agents must compress streams of past interactions into persistent memory before future queries are known. Existing evaluations usually measure final question-answering accuracy, which entangles memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning. We introduce MEMAUDIT, an exact packageoracle evaluation protocol for budgeted long-term memory writing. A MEMAUDIT package fixes an experience stream, candidate memory representations, storage costs, semantic evidence units, future-query requirements, and a budget, turning write-time memory selection into a finite auditable optimization problem with a certified denominator. We instantiate this protocol with a concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective under storage and one-representation-per-experience constraints, and compute exact package optima using branch-and-bound with MILP certification. Across controlled exact packages, validity-heavy stress tests, human-audited natural support slices, and exported Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta stores, MEMAUDIT separates representation quality, validity-state preservation, and budget-aware selection effects that end-to-end QA cannot localize. The resulting artifact provides reusable package generators, certified solvers, natural package exports, external-system scorers, and cached reproducibility metadata for evaluating what memory writers actually preserve under fixed storage budgets.

</details>


### [621] [CBV: Clean-label Backdoor Attacks on Vision Language Models via Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02202)
*Ji Guo,Xiaolong Qin,Cencen Liu,Jielei Wang,Jierun Chen,Wenbo Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes CBV, a clean-label backdoor attack on VLMs using diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples via score matching and GradCAM-guided masking, achieving over 80% attack success rate on MSCOCO and VQA v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs rely on data poisoning with visual triggers and text label modifications, causing image-text mismatch that makes poisoned samples easy to detect. The authors aim to create a more stealthy, clean-label attack.

Method: CBV modifies the score during the reverse generation process of a diffusion model to generate poisoned samples with triggered image features. It incorporates textual information of triggered images as multimodal guidance, and uses a GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) to restrict modifications to semantically important regions.

Result: Achieves over 80% Attack Success Rate (ASR) on MSCOCO and VQA v2 datasets across four representative VLMs while preserving normal functionality.

Conclusion: CBV enables clean-label backdoor attacks on VLMs using diffusion models, producing natural-looking poisoned samples that are harder to detect than prior methods.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering (VQA). However, as their applications become increasingly widespread, recent studies have revealed that VLMs are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs primarily rely on data poisoning by adding visual triggers and modifying text labels, where the induced image-text mismatch makes poisoned samples easy to detect. To address this limitation, we propose the Clean-Label Backdoor Attack on VLMs via Diffusion Models (CBV), which leverages diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples via score matching. Specifically, CBV modifies the score during the reverse generation process of the diffusion model to guide the generation of poisoned samples that contain triggered image features. To further enhance the effectiveness of the attack, we incorporate the textual information of the triggered images as multimodal guidance during generation. Moreover, to enhance stealthiness, we introduce a GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) that restricts modifications to only the most semantically important regions, rather than the entire image. We evaluate our method on MSCOCO and VQA v2 with four representative VLMs, achieving over 80% ASR while preserving normal functionality.

</details>


### [622] [Submodular Benchmark Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02209)
*Alexander Smola*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes using submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model to select a small, informative subset of benchmarks for evaluating large language models, with entropy and mutual information as natural objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating large language models across many benchmarks is expensive, yet many benchmarks are highly correlated, motivating the need to select a small, informative subset.

Method: The authors formalize benchmark subset selection as submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model, using entropy (log-determinant covariance) and mutual information between selected and remaining benchmarks as objectives. Entropy selection coincides with pivoted Cholesky; mutual information is optimized greedily.

Result: Experiments on three matrices from ten public leaderboards show that mutual information selection outperforms entropy for imputation at small subsets.

Conclusion: Mutual information is a more effective criterion than entropy for selecting small, informative benchmark subsets, particularly for imputation tasks.

Abstract: Evaluating large language models across many benchmarks is expensive, yet many benchmarks are highly correlated. We formalize the selection of a small, informative subset as submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model. Entropy (log-determinant covariance) and mutual information between selected and remaining benchmarks arise as natural objectives. Both are submodular; entropy selection coincides with pivoted Cholesky and has spectral residual bounds, while mutual information is non-monotone in general but empirically monotone for small subsets, so we optimize it greedily. Experiments on three matrices from ten public leaderboards show that mutual information selection outperforms entropy for imputation at small subsets.

</details>


### [623] [CoVSpec: Efficient Device-Edge Co-Inference for Vision-Language Models via Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02218)
*Yuanyuan Jia,Shunpu Tang,Qianqian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoVSpec is a collaborative speculative decoding framework for VLM inference on mobile devices, using training-free visual token pruning, adaptive drafting, and parallel branching to achieve up to 2.21x throughput improvement and 96% communication reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying large VLMs on mobile devices is challenging due to computational and memory constraints. Device-edge co-inference with speculative decoding is a practical alternative, but direct extension suffers from excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead.

Method: 1) Training-free visual token reduction framework (prunes redundant tokens via query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency). 2) Adaptive drafting strategy (dynamically adjusts verification frequency and draft length). 3) Parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction (improves draft-side utilization and reduces correction-related transmission).

Result: Up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference, over 96% reduction in communication overhead compared to baselines, without compromising task accuracy.

Conclusion: CoVSpec effectively enables efficient VLM deployment on mobile devices through device-edge collaboration, significantly improving inference throughput and reducing communication costs while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in multimodal perception and reasoning. However, deploying large VLMs on mobile devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands. A practical alternative is device-edge co-inference, where a lightweight draft VLM on the mobile device collaborates with a larger target VLM on the edge server via speculative decoding. Nevertheless, directly extending speculative decoding to VLMs suffers from severe inefficiency due to excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead. To address these challenges, we propose CoVSpec, an efficient collaborative speculative decoding framework for VLM inference. Specifically, we first develop a training-free visual token reduction framework that prunes redundant visual tokens on the mobile device by jointly considering query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency. Moreover, we design an adaptive drafting strategy that dynamically adjusts both the verification frequency and the draft length. In addition, we introduce a parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction to improve draft-side utilization during target-side verification and reduce correction-related transmission overhead. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoVSpec achieves up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference and reduces communication overhead by more than 96% compared with baselines, without compromising task accuracy.

</details>


### [624] [Bucketing the Good Apples: A Method for Diagnosing and Improving Causal Abstraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02234)
*Li Puyin,Jiyuan Tan,Ahmad Jabbar,Thomas Icard,Atticus Geiger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a diagnostic method for causal-abstraction interpretability that partitions the input space into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions, enabling error analysis and iterative improvement of high-level causal hypotheses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current causal abstraction interpretability treats interchange intervention accuracy as a single global metric, which fails to reveal where interpretations succeed or fail. The authors aim to turn this into a more diagnostic tool that identifies specific failure regions and guides improvements.

Method: The method partitions the input space based on pairwise interchange-intervention behavior into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions. It follows a four-step recipe: evaluate interpretations, partition inputs by faithfulness, analyze failure regions, and iteratively refine the high-level hypothesis.

Result: The approach yields informative error analyses across multiple causal abstraction settings. In a toy logic task, recursively applying the recipe recovers a high-level hypothesis from scratch. It helps identify missing distinctions, discover unmodeled intermediate variables, and combine complementary partial interpretations.

Conclusion: Partitioning the input space is a useful step toward more precise, constructive, and scalable mechanistic interpretability, transforming causal abstraction from a global evaluation metric into a diagnostic tool that reveals both where and why interpretations work or fail.

Abstract: We present a method for diagnosing interpretation in neural networks by identifying an input subspace where a proposed interpretation is highly faithful. Our method is particularly useful for causal-abstraction-style interpretability, where a high-level causal hypothesis is evaluated by interchange interventions. Rather than treating interchange intervention accuracy as a single global summary, we refine this framework by partitioning the input space into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions according to pairwise interchange-intervention behavior. This turns causal abstraction from a purely global evaluation into a more diagnostic tool: it not only measures whether an interpretation works, but also reveals where it works, where it fails, and what distinguishes the two cases. This diagnostic view also provides practical heuristics for improving interpretations. By analyzing the structure of the well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions, we can identify missing distinctions in a high-level hypothesis, discover previously unmodeled intermediate variables, and combine complementary partial interpretations into a stronger one. We instantiate this idea as a simple four-step recipe and show that it yields informative error analyses across multiple causal abstraction settings. In a toy logic task, recursively applying the recipe recovers a high-level hypothesis from scratch. More broadly, our results suggest that partitioning the input space is a useful step toward more precise, constructive, and scalable mechanistic interpretability.

</details>


### [625] [Perturbation Dose Responses in Recursive LLM Loops: Raw Switching, Stochastic Floors, and Persistent Escape under Append, Replace, and Dialog Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02236)
*Pawel Kaplanski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how much injected text is needed to redirect language models trapped in recursive loops, finding that persistent redirection is limited (~16-36%) under append-mode with clipping, improves under full-history (75-80% escape), and that context-update rules (append/replace/dialog) significantly affect outcomes. A structural non-monotonic dip is identified and refutes heterogeneity as its cause.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recursive language-model loops settle into attractor-like patterns, raising the practical question of how much injected text is needed to redirect them and whether that redirection persists.

Method: 30-step recursive loops with three context-update rules (append, replace, dialog) on gpt-4o-mini and gpt-4.1-nano, measuring destination-coherent persistence and source-basin escape under various token doses and memory policies.

Result: Append-mode with tail clip: persistence ~16%, escape ~36% at dose 400. Full-history: escape crosses 50% at ~400 tokens, saturates 75-80%; persistence reaches 50% at ~1,500 tokens. Replace-mode switching is near-saturated but largely state-reset. A structural non-monotonic dip found in high-dose persistence.

Conclusion: Recursive-loop evaluations must distinguish transient movement from durable escape, subtract stochastic floors, and treat context-update rules as first-class safety-relevant design choices.

Abstract: Recursive language-model loops often settle into recognizable attractor-like patterns. The practical question is how much injected text is needed to move a settled loop somewhere else, and whether that move lasts. We study this in 30-step recursive loops by separating the model from the context-update rule: append, replace, and dialog updates expose different histories to the same generator. The main result is that persistent redirection in append-mode recursive loops is memory-policy-conditioned. Under a 12,000-character tail clip, destination-coherent persistence plateaus near 16 percent and retained source-basin escape near 36 percent at dose 400; neither crosses 50 percent. Under a full-history protocol, retained source-basin escape crosses 50 percent near 400 tokens and saturates at 75-80 percent by 1,500 tokens, while destination-coherent persistence first reaches 0.50 near 1,500 tokens with a Wilson 95 percent CI of [0.41, 0.61]. For raw switching, adversarial continuations yield an ED50 near 40 tokens, with paired-control floors near 35 percent and net switching never reaching +50 percentage points within 5-400 tokens. Replace-mode raw switching is near-saturated but largely reflects state-reset overwrite: insert-mode probes drop it to 12-32 percent. A homogeneous-perturbation control reproduced the high-dose non-monotonic dip in destination-coherent persistence, refuting perturbation heterogeneity as the cause; the dip appears structural, with mechanism unresolved. We report 37 experiments on gpt-4o-mini with within-vendor replication on gpt-4.1-nano. Recursive-loop evaluations should distinguish transient movement from durable escape, subtract stochastic floors, and treat context-update rules as first-class safety-relevant design choices.

</details>


### [626] [PhysicianBench: Evaluating LLM Agents in Real-World EHR Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02240)
*Ruoqi Liu,Imran Q. Mohiuddin,Austin J. Schoeffler,Kavita Renduchintala,Ashwin Nayak,Prasantha L. Vemu,Shivam C. Vedak,Kameron C. Black,John L. Havlik,Isaac Ogunmola,Stephen P. Ma,Roopa Dhatt,Jonathan H. Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PhysicianBench is a benchmark of 100 long-horizon clinical tasks in real EHR environments, requiring an average of 27 tool calls per task across 21 specialties. The best LLM agent achieves only 46% success rate (open-source models at most 19%), revealing a major gap between current agent capabilities and real-world clinical workflow demands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical agent benchmarks focus on static knowledge recall, single-step actions, or unverifiable intent, failing to capture the long-horizon, composite workflows of real clinical systems. A benchmark grounded in real clinical settings with verifiable execution is needed.

Method: 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases, reviewed by physicians, instantiated in an EHR environment with real patient records and standard APIs. Tasks span 21 specialties with 670 structured checkpoints graded by task-specific scripts with execution-grounded verification.

Result: Best proprietary model achieves 46% pass@1; best open-source model reaches only 19%, showing substantial gap between current agent capabilities and real clinical workflow demands.

Conclusion: PhysicianBench provides a realistic, execution-grounded benchmark for measuring progress toward autonomous clinical agents, revealing that current LLM agents are far from ready for real-world clinical deployment.

Abstract: We introduce PhysicianBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on physician tasks grounded in real clinical setting within electronic health record (EHR) environments. Existing medical agent benchmarks primarily focus on static knowledge recall, single-step atomic actions, or action intent without verifiable execution against the environment. As a result, they fail to capture the long-horizon, composite workflows that characterize real clinical systems. PhysicianBench comprises 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases between primary care and subspecialty physicians, with each task independently reviewed by a separate panel of physicians. Tasks are instantiated in an EHR environment with real patient records and accessed through the same standard APIs used by commercial EHR vendors. Tasks span 21 specialties (e.g., cardiology, endocrinology, oncology, psychiatry) and diverse workflow types (e.g., diagnosis interpretation, medication prescribing, treatment planning), requiring an average of 27 tool calls per task. Solving each task requires retrieving data across encounters, reasoning over heterogeneous clinical information, executing consequential clinical actions, and producing clinical documentation. Each task is decomposed into structured checkpoints (670 in total across the benchmark) capturing distinct stages of completion graded by task-specific scripts with execution-grounded verification. Across 13 proprietary and open-source LLM agents, the best-performing model achieves only 46% success rate (pass@1), while open-source models reach at most 19%, revealing a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of real-world clinical workflows. PhysicianBench provides a realistic and execution-grounded benchmark for measuring progress toward autonomous clinical agents.

</details>


### [627] [Zero-Shot Confidence Estimation for Small LLMs: When Supervised Baselines Aren't Worth Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02241)
*Luong N. Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Zero-shot confidence signals, especially average token log-probability, can match or outperform supervised baselines for local-to-cloud LLM routing without needing training data, and retrieval-conditional self-assessment further improves performance at lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As inference costs dominate LLM deployment budgets, routing easy queries to cheap local models and escalating hard ones to cloud models is a cost-control strategy. The question is whether small language models can reliably estimate their own correctness without supervised training data, enabling routing without labeled examples.

Method: The authors compare zero-shot confidence signals (e.g., average token log-probability) against RouteLLM-style supervised baselines across three 7-8B model families and two datasets. They also propose retrieval-conditional self-assessment (RSA), a pre-generation signal that selectively injects retrieved knowledge when similarity is high.

Result: Average token log-probability matches/exceeds supervised baselines in-distribution (AUROC 0.650-0.714 vs. 0.644-0.676) and substantially outperforms out-of-distribution (0.717-0.833 vs. 0.512-0.564). RSA improves over bare self-assessment by up to +0.069 AUROC at 3-10x lower latency than log-probability. A supervised baseline trained on 1,000 labeled examples never exceeds the zero-shot signal.

Conclusion: Zero-shot confidence signals are a reliable and effective alternative to supervised training for LLM routing, especially out-of-distribution. Retrieval-conditional self-assessment offers a practical latency-efficiency trade-off. The authors release all code, data, and experiment logs.

Abstract: How reliably can a small language model estimate its own correctness? The answer determines whether local-to-cloud routing-escalating queries a cheap local model cannot handle-can work without supervised training data. As inference costs dominate large language model (LLM) deployment budgets, routing most queries to a cheap local model while reserving expensive cloud calls for hard cases is an increasingly common cost-control strategy. We compare zero-shot confidence signals against RouteLLM-style supervised baselines across three 7-8B model families and two datasets (1,000 and 500 queries per model, respectively). Average token log-probability, which requires no training data, matches or exceeds supervised baselines in-distribution (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) 0.650-0.714 vs. 0.644-0.676) and substantially outperforms them out-of-distribution (0.717-0.833 vs. 0.512-0.564), because it measures a property of the model's generation rather than the query distribution. This paper further proposes retrieval-conditional self-assessment, a pre-generation signal that selectively injects retrieved knowledge when similarity is high, improving over bare self-assessment by up to +0.069 AUROC at 3-10x lower latency than log-probability. A supervised baseline trained on 1,000 labeled examples never exceeds the zero-shot signal. We release all code, data, and experiment logs.

</details>


### [628] [A Study of Belief Revision Postulates in Multi-Agent Systems (Extended Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02249)
*Michael Thielscher,Tran Cao Son*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper generalizes AGM belief revision postulates to multi-agent epistemic planning, proposes multi-agent full-meet revision, extends iterated revision postulates, and discusses challenges for event model-based operators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally investigate belief revision in multi-agent epistemic planning, specifically what happens to all agents' beliefs when one agent gains a belief about a state property.

Method: Generalizes classical AGM postulates to multi-agent settings using Kripke models, defines generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision, and extends iterated revision postulates.

Result: Shows generalized full-meet revision satisfies all multi-agent AGM postulates; presents an event model-based revision operator; identifies challenges for satisfying iterated revision postulates.

Conclusion: Provides a formal framework for evaluating dynamic epistemic reasoning; highlights open issues in defining fully satisfactory iterated multi-agent belief revision operators on Kripke models.

Abstract: We investigate the belief revision problem in epistemic planning, i.e., what will be the beliefs of all agents in a multi-agent system after an agent gains the belief in some state property. Based on the standard representation in epistemic planning of agents' beliefs via a single multi-agent Kripke model, we generalize the classical AGM belief revision postulates to the multi-agent setting, with the aim to provide a formal framework for evaluating dynamic epistemic reasoning frameworks in which the beliefs of all agents as the result of actions are computed. As an example of a simple operator that satisfies all of the generalized AGM postulates, we present generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision. We moreover define a generalization of the standard postulates for iterated revision, present a more sophisticated, event model based revision operator, and discuss the potential issues in defining an epistemic operator on Kripke models that can satisfy all of the generalized postulates for iterated multi-agent belief revision.

</details>


### [629] [Towards Understanding Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02269)
*Kei Nishimura-Gasparian,Robert McCarthy,David Lindner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper builds a diverse evaluation suite to study specification gaming in LLM agents, finding that all tested models exploit specifications at non-negligible rates, with RL reasoning training substantially increasing exploit rates, and test-time mitigations reducing but not eliminating the problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite specification gaming being a critical failure mode of LLM agents, there has been little systematic research into when it arises and what drives it. The authors aim to address this gap.

Method: The authors build and open-source a diverse suite of tasks where models can score highly by taking unintended actions. They test multiple models across eight settings (including five non-coding settings) and study the impact of RL reasoning training, RL reasoning budget, and test-time mitigations.

Result: All tested models exploit specifications at non-negligible rates. Grok 4 shows the highest rates, Claude models the lowest. RL reasoning training substantially increases exploit rates. Increasing RL reasoning budget has a weakly positive effect. Test-time mitigations reduce but do not eliminate specification gaming.

Conclusion: Specification gaming is a fundamental challenge arising from RL reasoning training. The authors release their evaluation suite to support further work on this problem.

Abstract: Specification gaming is a critical failure mode of LLM agents. Despite this, there has been little systematic research into when it arises and what drives it. To address this, we build and open source a diverse suite of tasks where models can score highly by taking unintended actions. We find that all tested models exploit their specifications at non-negligible rates in most of our eight settings, including five non-coding settings. We see the highest rates of specification gaming in Grok 4 and the lowest rates in Claude models. We use our evaluation suite to study what drives specification gaming, and find that: 1. RL reasoning training substantially increases the rate at which models exploit their specifications, 2. Increasing RL reasoning budget has a weakly positive effect on exploit rate, and 3. Test-time mitigations reduce but do not eliminate the rate of specification gaming. Our results suggest that specification gaming is a fundamental challenge arising from RL reasoning training; we release our evaluation suite to support further work on this problem.

</details>


### [630] [Complexity Horizons of Compressed Models in Analog Circuit Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02285)
*Pacome Simon Mbonimpa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a performance-aware model compression strategy using prerequisite graphs (DAGs) to optimize LLM selection for circuit analysis, enabling dynamic cascading of queries across compressed model variants to balance accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional evaluation methods treat LLM performance as a flat metric, failing to capture the hierarchical nature of engineering knowledge, leading to suboptimal trade-offs between reasoning accuracy and computational efficiency in specialized domains like circuit analysis.

Method: The framework uses Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to structure electronics concepts as prerequisite graphs, an agentic pipeline for generating prerequisite-based datasets, and a strategic evaluation engine that dynamically cascades queries across compressed LLM variants to select the smallest model within its conceptual knowledge boundaries.

Result: Experiments on analog electronics datasets show that prerequisite graphs provide a granular map of model compression with respect to performance given circuit analysis complexity.

Conclusion: Prerequisite graphs effectively enable selective deployment of compressed LLMs for circuit analysis, optimizing the accuracy-efficiency trade-off based on problem complexity.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) for specialized engineering domains, such as circuit analysis, often faces a trade-off between reasoning accuracy and computational efficiency. Traditional evaluation methods treat model performance as a flat metric, failing to account for the hierarchical nature of engineering knowledge. We propose a performance-aware model compression strategy that utilizes prerequisite graphs to optimize model selection for circuit analysis tasks. By structuring electronics design concepts as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs), we can identify the specific complexity horizons of an LLM's compressed variants' tiers. Our framework introduces an agentic pipeline for generating prerequisite-based datasets and a strategic evaluation engine that dynamically cascades queries across a spectrum of compressed variants of an LLM. This approach allows to select the smallest compressed model, given its conceptual knowledge boundaries in circuit analysis. Experimental results on analog electronics datasets demonstrate that prerequisite graphs provide a granular map of model compression with respect to the performance given circuit analysis complexity. (Source Code: https://github.com/pacomesimon/LLM_prereq_graphs_circuit_analysis, Demo: https://huggingface.co/spaces/pacomesimon/LLM_prereq_graphs_circuit_analysis)

</details>


### [631] [EngiAgent: Fully Connected Coordination of LLM Agents for Solving Open-ended Engineering Problems with Feasible Solutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02289)
*Xiyuan Zhou,Ruixi Zou,Xinlei Wang,Yuheng Cheng,Yan Xu,Junhua Zhao,Jinjin Gu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EngiAgent is a multi-agent system with a fully connected coordinator for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving using LLMs, outperforming prior approaches across four domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel at reasoning and code generation but fail to ensure feasibility (producing solutions that respect data and physical constraints), limiting their applicability to engineering problem solving where feasibility is critical.

Method: A multi-agent system with specialized agents (problem analysis, modeling, verification, solving, solution evaluation) connected via a fully connected coordinator that enables flexible feedback routing at every stage, unlike rigid pipeline-based approaches.

Result: Substantial improvements in feasibility across four representative engineering domains, with better robustness to diverse failure cases (data extraction errors, constraint inconsistencies, solver failures).

Conclusion: EngiAgent establishes a new paradigm for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving with LLMs, demonstrating that flexible multi-agent coordination can overcome the feasibility limitations of prior LLM-based approaches.

Abstract: Engineering problem solving is central to real-world decision-making, requiring mathematical formulations that not only represent complex problems but also produce feasible solutions under data and physical constraints. Unlike mathematical problem solving, which operates on predefined formulations, engineering tasks demand open-ended analysis, feasibility-driven modeling, and iterative refinement. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in reasoning and code generation, they often fail to ensure feasibility, which limits their applicability to engineering problem solving. To address this challenge, we propose EngiAgent, a multi-agent system with a fully connected coordinator that simulates expert workflows through specialized agents for problem analysis, modeling, verification, solving, and solution evaluation. The fully connected coordinator enables flexible feedback routing, overcoming the rigidity of prior pipeline-based reflection methods and ensuring feasibility at every stage of the process. This design not only improves robustness to diverse failure cases such as data extraction errors, constraint inconsistencies, and solver failures, but also enhances the overall quality of problem solving. Empirical results across four representative domains demonstrate that EngiAgent achieves substantial improvements in feasibility compared to prior approaches, establishing a new paradigm for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving with LLMs. Our source code and data are available at https://github.com/AI4Engi/EngiAgent.

</details>


### [632] [Distilling Long-CoT Reasoning through Collaborative Step-wise Multi-Teacher Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02290)
*Taewon Yun,Jisu Shin,Jeonghwan Choi,Seunghwan Bang,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRD is a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that uses perplexity-guided beam search to generate high-quality Long-CoT reasoning traces, enabling efficient student model distillation without full-scale teacher inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing curation-based distillation approaches select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, lack collaboration among heterogeneous teachers, and cannot dynamically explore diverse reasoning paths, leading to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning.

Method: CoRD performs step-wise reasoning synthesis using predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search, allowing multiple heterogeneous large reasoning models to collaboratively construct coherent reasoning trajectories while preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses.

Result: CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data, achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer structured supervision signals, generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings, all without substantial efficiency overhead.

Conclusion: Collaborative multi-teacher decoding with step-wise exploration is an effective and efficient alternative to post-hoc curation for Long-CoT reasoning distillation.

Abstract: Distilling large reasoning models is essential for making Long-CoT reasoning practical, as full-scale inference remains computationally prohibitive. Existing curation-based approaches select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, overlooking collaboration among heterogeneous teachers and lacking dynamic exploration, which leads to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning. We introduce CoRD, a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis guided by predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search. This enables heterogeneous LRMs to jointly construct coherent reasoning trajectories while efficiently preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses. Experiments show that CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data and achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer, structured supervision signals, without substantial efficiency overhead. CoRD further generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings. The dataset and model are available at \href{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}.

</details>


### [633] [Anon: Extrapolating Optimizer Adaptivity Across the Real Spectrum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02317)
*Yiheng Zhang,Kaiyan Zhao,Shaowu Wu,Yiming Wang,Jiajun Wu,Leong Hou U,Steve Drew,Xiaoguang Niu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Anon, a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity that can interpolate between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors, using a new Incremental Delay Update (IDU) mechanism to ensure convergence across the full adaptivity spectrum, outperforming state-of-the-art optimizers on image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive optimizers like Adam generalize worse than non-adaptive methods like SGD on classical architectures (e.g., CNNs). The key cause is that adaptivity in pre-conditioners limits the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes.

Method: Proposes Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer with Novel convergence technique) with continuously tunable adaptivity parameter R. Introduces Incremental Delay Update (IDU), a novel mechanism more flexible than AMSGrad's hard max-tracking, enhancing robustness to gradient noise.

Result: Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks, with theoretical convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings.

Conclusion: Adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle. Anon provides the first unified framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers, surpassing their advantageous properties.

Abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam have achieved great success in training large-scale models like large language models and diffusion models. However, they often generalize worse than non-adaptive methods, such as SGD on classical architectures like CNNs. We identify a key cause of this performance gap: adaptivity in pre-conditioners, which limits the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes. To address this, we propose Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer with Novel convergence technique), a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity in R, allowing it to interpolate between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors and even extrapolate beyond both. To ensure convergence across the entire adaptivity spectrum, we introduce incremental delay update (IDU), a novel mechanism that is more flexible than AMSGrad's hard max-tracking strategy and enhances robustness to gradient noise. We theoretically establish convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings. Empirically, Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on representative image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle, and Anon provides the first unified and reliable framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers and surpassing their advantageous properties.

</details>


### [634] [Can Causal Discovery Algorithms Help in Generating Legal Arguments?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02318)
*Soham Wasmatkar,Subinay Adhikary,Rakshit Rohan,Shouvik Kumar Guha,Saptarshi Pyne,Kripabandhu Ghosh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores using causal discovery algorithms (pioneered by Judea Pearl) to automatically generate legal arguments, applying them to a curated dataset of 150 homicide cases annotated with 17 legal concepts, and showing that discovered causal relationships can support viable legal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery algorithms have been successfully applied in medicine and economics but have not been leveraged in law. The authors aim to bridge this gap by investigating whether these algorithms can help automate the generation of legal arguments.

Method: The authors created a novel legal dataset with 17 legal concepts and annotated 150 homicide cases accordingly. They applied widely-used causal discovery algorithms to the annotated data to discover causal relationships between legal concepts, and quantified the associated degrees of belief using mathematical probabilities.

Result: Some discovered causal relationships can generate viable legal arguments. For example, establishing that a physical assault did not occur during a homicide is sufficient (probability 1) to conclude the homicide was not due to a property-related dispute.

Conclusion: Causal discovery algorithms can be helpful in generating legal arguments, opening up promising future research directions at the intersection of causal AI and law.

Abstract: In 2011, Judea Pearl received the Turing Award, considered the Nobel Prize in Computing, for fundamental contributions to artificial intelligence through the development of a calculus for probabilistic and causal reasoning. It includes pioneering the development of causal discovery algorithms. These computer algorithms can analyze large multivariate datasets and automatically discover the causal relationships among the constituent variables. They have been widely used in many critical fields such as medicine and economics to support decisions. However, to our knowledge, they have not been leveraged in law. This paper attempts to alleviate this gap by investigating whether causal discovery algorithms can be leveraged for automated generation of legal arguments. To that end, a novel legal dataset is prepared by identifying 17 legal concepts, such as physical assault and property dispute. A curated collection of 150 homicide cases are annotated with these concepts, e.g., a case is annotated with physical assault only if a physical assault had been reported in that case. Subsequently, a selected set of widely-used causal discovery algorithms is applied to the annotated dataset to discover the causal relationships between the legal concepts. Additionally, the degrees of belief associated with the discovered relationships are quantified in mathematical probabilities. It is shown that some of the causal relationships help generate viable legal arguments, e.g., if one could establish that a physical assault has not taken place during a homicide, it should be a sufficient condition (with probability 1) to establish that the homicide has not been committed due to a property-related dispute. Thus, this paper shows that causal discovery algorithms can be helpful in generating legal arguments, opening up avenues for promising future endeavors.

</details>


### [635] [ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02320)
*Yiheng Zhang,Yiming Wang,Kaiyan Zhao,Zhenglin Wan,Jiayu Chen,Leong Hou U*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO), a new policy gradient method that resolves PPO's hard clipping vs. SPO's unbounded gradient dilemma via a Redescending Influence Principle, achieving SOTA on MuJoCo with superior stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPO's hard clipping discards valuable gradient information from outliers causing sample inefficiency, while removing clipping (SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients leading to instability. The paper aims to resolve this fundamental dilemma.

Method: The authors establish a Unified Trust Region Framework, derive ANO based on design principles, and propose the Redescending Influence Principle - a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties and hard-thresholding to dynamic outlier suppression. They prove ANO has minimal structural complexity for robust optimization.

Result: ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. It demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even with 3x larger learning rates where PPO fails completely.

Conclusion: The Redescending Influence Principle is proven necessary for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization, and ANO's minimal structural complexity is sufficient for robust optimization.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) dominates deep RL but faces a fundamental dilemma. Its "hard clipping" mechanism discards valuable gradient information from outliers, leading to sample inefficiency. Conversely, removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients, causing significant instability and hyperparameter sensitivity. To resolve this, we establish a Unified Trust Region Framework that generalizes existing objectives. Within this framework, we derive Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on a set of design principles. We identify that the failure of standard policy gradients stems from a misapplication of gradient influence on outliers. We propose the Redescending Influence Principle, a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties (SPO) and hard-thresholding (PPO) to dynamic outlier suppression, and prove its necessity for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization. Theoretically, we prove ANO possesses the minimal structural complexity required for robust optimization. Empirically, ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Notably, ANO demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

</details>


### [636] [A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02366)
*Zhisheng Tang,Mayank Kejriwal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A compound AI system unifies fragmented research funding discovery (12,000+ opportunities across NSF, NIH, DARPA, etc.) using LLM-powered browser agents for data aggregation and a ReAct-based conversational interface with hybrid search, reducing discovery time from 30-45 minutes to under 10 minutes, currently used by 3,000+ users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers face fragmented funding discovery across disparate agency portals (NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, etc.) with heterogeneous interfaces and search capabilities, making manual discovery time-consuming (30-45 minutes).

Method: A compound AI system with two components: (1) aggregation layer using LLM-equipped browser agents to autonomously collect, normalize, and index ~12,000 federal/nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources; (2) agentic ReAct-based query processing layer interpreting research context (including PDFs) with hybrid search (structured index + selective web search), supporting multi-turn iterative refinement.

Result: Reduced grant discovery time from 30-45 minutes to under 10 minutes; currently used by 3,000+ users; real-time streaming with transparent intermediate reasoning for user trust calibration.

Conclusion: Compound AI systems can effectively unify fragmented research funding landscapes by combining autonomous data aggregation with agentic conversational search, significantly reducing discovery time while maintaining transparency and avoiding LLM hallucination.

Abstract: Research funding discovery remains fundamentally fragmented: researchers navigate disparate agency portals (e.g., in the United States, NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and many others) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas. We present a compound AI system that unifies this landscape through two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer that autonomously collects, normalizes, and indexes almost 12,000 federal and nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources via LLM-equipped browser agents, maintaining a biweekly-updated unified database; and (2) an agentic ReAct-based query processing layer that interprets research context (including from PDF documents) and employs hybrid search combining a structured index with selective web search to retrieve relevant opportunities - while avoiding LLM hallucination. The conversational interface supports iterative refinement through multi-turn interactions, allowing researchers to progressively apply constraints without reformulating their core research description. Results stream in real time with full transparency of intermediate reasoning, enabling appropriate calibration of user trust. Currently used by almost 3,000+ users, our approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI in reducing grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes (manual, fragmented portal searches) to under 10 minutes (unified, conversational search).

</details>


### [637] [Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02395)
*Yinghui Chi,Lucien Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs by injecting template-aware errors into symbolic reasoning chains, enabling prefix-invalid but trajectory-consistent data that improves reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for constructing process supervision data for PRMs offer limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency.

Method: Builds a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix.

Result: Synthesized data improves Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfers to mathematical reasoning; first-error localization is more challenging than overall step classification.

Conclusion: Fine-grained and verifiable process supervision is needed, as first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step-level classification.

Abstract: Process reward models (PRMs) rely on high-quality process supervision data, yet existing construction methods often provide limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency. We propose a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs. Our framework first constructs a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix. The resulting paired trajectories are prefix-invalid at the first error while remaining trajectory-consistent after symbolic recomputation, and are translated into aligned natural-language processes for PRM training and evaluation. Experiments show that the synthesized data improve Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfer to mathematical reasoning. Step-level evaluation further shows that first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step classification, highlighting the need for fine-grained and verifiable process supervision.

</details>


### [638] [HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02396)
*Jianing Wang,Linsen Guo,Zhengyu Chen,Qi Guo,Hongyu Zang,Wenjie Shi,Haoxiang Ma,Xiangyu Xi,Xiaoyu Li,Wei Wang,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes HeavySkill, viewing "heavy thinking" as an inner skill internalized within model parameters (parallel reasoning + summarization) rather than a separate orchestration layer, showing it outperforms BoN, approaches Pass@N with strong LLMs, and can be scaled via RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic orchestration frameworks with multiple agents, memory, and tools achieve success on complex reasoning tasks, but the actual driving mechanism behind their performance is obscured by intricate system designs. The authors aim to demystify this by identifying a core inner skill.

Method: HeavySkill conceptualizes "heavy thinking" as a minimal execution unit and an inner skill internalized in model parameters, structured as a two-stage pipeline: parallel reasoning followed by summarization. This operates beneath any agentic harness. The study empirically compares HeavySkill against BoN and Pass@N strategies across diverse domains, and further investigates scaling via reinforcement learning.

Result: HeavySkill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies. Stronger LLMs using HeavySkill can approach Pass@N performance. The depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be effectively scaled via reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: The paper offers a path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers, suggesting that the core skill driving agentic performance can be learned and scaled within the model itself.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic harness with orchestration frameworks that coordinate multiple agents with memory, skills, and tool use have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks. However, the underlying mechanism that truly drives performance remains obscured behind intricate system designs. In this paper, we propose HeavySkill, a perspective that views heavy thinking not only as a minimal execution unit in orchestration harness but also as an inner skill internalized within the model's parameters that drives the orchestrator to solve complex tasks. We identify this skill as a two-stage pipeline, i.e., parallel reasoning then summarization, which can operate beneath any agentic harness. We present a systematic empirical study of HeavySkill across diverse domains. Our results show that this inner skill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies; notably, stronger LLMs can even approach Pass@N performance. Crucially, we demonstrate that the depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be further scaled via reinforcement learning, offering a promising path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers.

</details>


### [639] [The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02398)
*Rahul Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces SCHEMA, an evaluation framework testing 11 frontier AI models for metacognitive stability under adversarial pressure. 8 of 11 models show catastrophic degradation (up to 30.2% accuracy drop) driven by compliance-forcing instructions ("Compliance Trap"), not threat content. Anthropic's Constitutional AI shows near-perfect immunity due to alignment training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming), but overlook a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse, where models lose metacognitive stability (knowing what they don't know, detecting errors, seeking clarification) under adversarial pressure.

Method: SCHEMA evaluates 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring, isolating the factors driving metacognitive degradation.

Result: 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation (up to 30.2% accuracy drop, p < 2×10⁻⁸). The "Compliance Trap" is identified: collapse is driven by compliance-forcing instructions, not threat content. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance. Models with advanced reasoning show worst degradation; Anthropic's Constitutional AI shows near-perfect immunity due to alignment-specific training, not superior capability.

Conclusion: Metacognitive stability under adversarial pressure is a critical but overlooked safety requirement. Compliance-forcing instructions can override epistemic boundaries even in capable models. Alignment-specific training (e.g., Constitutional AI) can provide immunity; dataset and evaluation infrastructure are released for further research.

Abstract: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their ability to maintain metacognitive stability -- knowing what they do not know, detecting errors, seeking clarification -- under adversarial pressure is a critical safety requirement. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming); we investigate a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse. We present SCHEMA, an evaluation of 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. We find that 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all $p < 2 \times 10^{-8}$, surviving Bonferroni correction). Crucially, we identify a "Compliance Trap": through factorial isolation and a benign distraction control, we demonstrate that collapse is driven not by the psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities exhibit the most severe absolute degradation, while Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrates near-perfect immunity -- not from superior capability (Google's Gemini matches its baseline accuracy) but from alignment-specific training. We release the complete dataset and evaluation infrastructure.

</details>


### [640] [FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02411)
*Kyle Zheng,Han Zhang,Renliang Sun,Chenchen Ye,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FitText is a training-free framework that embeds dynamic retrieval into an agent's reasoning loop to bridge the semantic gap between user task descriptions and tool documentation. It generates and iteratively refines pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, achieving significant performance gains on large-scale benchmarks (ToolRet and StableToolBench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a semantic gap between how users describe tasks and how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap because the agent's understanding evolves during execution while its tool set remains fixed.

Method: FitText generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search.

Result: On ToolRet (43k tools, 4 domains), FitText improves average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78. On StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), it achieves a 0.73 average pass rate—a 24-point absolute gain over static query retrieval.

Conclusion: FitText's dynamic retrieval approach significantly outperforms static query retrieval. However, under weaker base models, Memetic's evolutionary search inverts—amplifying noise rather than refining signal—indicating that model capacity is a prerequisite for evolutionary tool exploration.

Abstract: A semantic gap separates how users describe tasks from how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap: the agent's understanding of what it needs evolves during execution, but its tool set does not. We introduce FitText, a training-free framework that makes retrieval dynamic by embedding it directly in the agent's reasoning loop. FitText generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search. On ToolRet (43k tools, 4 domains), FitText improves average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78; on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), it achieves a 0.73 average pass rate--a 24-point absolute gain over static query retrieval. The gains transfer across base models capable of acting as competent semantic operators; under weaker base models, Memetic's evolutionary search inverts--amplifying noise rather than refining signal--surfacing model capacity as a prerequisite for evolutionary tool exploration.

</details>


### [641] [The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02427)
*Tu Nguyen,Rasul Tutunov,Xiaotong Ji,Matthieu Zimmer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APPS is a blockwise particle algorithm that approximates power sampling (p_theta(x)^alpha, alpha>1) via parallel hypothesis propagation, proposal-corrected reweighting, and future-value-guided resampling, improving the accuracy-runtime tradeoff in training-free decoding for reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Base LLMs already assign probability mass to correct multi-step solutions, but the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling (targeting p_theta(x)^alpha) can bias decoding toward such modes, but practical approximations need to account for future-dependent correction factors.

Method: APPS is a blockwise particle algorithm that: (1) propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting, (2) refines survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries, (3) redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes. Future-value signals are instantiated via short-horizon rollouts or an amortized learned selection head.

Result: APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding across reasoning benchmarks, suggesting part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

Conclusion: Better inference-time power approximation via APPS can help close the gap to post-trained systems without additional training, offering a principled scaling knob (particle count) with predictable memory usage.

Abstract: A recurring pattern in "reasoning without training" is that base LLMs already assign non-trivial probability mass to correct multi-step solutions; the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling provides a principled way to bias decoding toward such modes by targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1, but practical approximations must account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising.
  We introduce Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), a blockwise particle algorithm for approximating the sequence-level power target with a bounded population of partial solutions. APPS propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting and refines their survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries. This redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, while providing a direct scaling knob in the particle count and predictable peak memory. We instantiate the future-value signal with short-horizon rollouts and also study an amortized variant that replaces rollouts with a lightweight learned selection head. Across reasoning benchmarks, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding and suggests that part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

</details>


### [642] [Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02442)
*Munachiso Samuel Nwadike,Zangir Iklassov,Kareem Ali,Rifo Genadi,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that evaluating reasoning in language models should focus on process-based assessment of intermediate traces (adaptive, multi-step search) rather than just final-answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Final-answer accuracy alone cannot diagnose or debug the underlying reasoning processes in frontier models, motivating the need for process-based evaluation.

Method: The authors formalize reasoning as a search-like procedure requiring input-dependent intermediate steps and halting conditions, and argue for externalized reasoning traces as evaluation interfaces.

Result: Single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited for variable-depth computation, supporting the need for intermediate decoding and trace-based evaluation.

Conclusion: Shift toward process-based evaluation where reasoning is assessed through faithfulness and validity of intermediate traces as first-class evaluation targets.

Abstract: In this paper, we offer a guide for researchers on evaluating reasoning in language models, building the case that reasoning should be assessed through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than final-answer accuracy alone. Under an evaluation-oriented definition, reasoning requires selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions, which we formalize as a search-like procedure. We show that single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited in their ability to realize such variable-depth computation, motivating intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces as appropriate evaluation interfaces. Central to our argument is that final-answer accuracy alone is an insufficient measure of reasoning, because it provides little ability to diagnose or debug the underlying processes that produce individual solutions in frontier models. We therefore argue for a shift toward process-based evaluation, in which reasoning is assessed through the faithfulness and validity of intermediate reasoning traces as first-class evaluation targets.

</details>


### [643] [Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02452)
*Xiyuan Wang,Yi Hu,Yanbo Wang,Chuan Shi,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores how graphs can benefit large language models (LLMs) by reducing hallucinations, enhancing reasoning through graph-based prompting (CoT, ToT, GoT), and improving structured data understanding for domains like e-commerce and RDBs, while also discussing future directions such as sparse LLM architectures and brain-inspired memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prior work focuses on how LLMs help graph learning, this paper asks the reverse question: "How can graphs help LLMs?" The goal is to leverage graphs as complementary tools to address key LLM limitations like hallucinations, reasoning deficits, and poor structured data understanding.

Method: The paper analyzes three perspectives: (1) using graphs as up-to-date knowledge sources to mitigate hallucinations, (2) employing graph-based prompting techniques (CoT, ToT, GoT) to enhance reasoning, and (3) integrating graphs to improve structured data comprehension. Future directions such as sparse graph-based LLM architectures and brain-inspired memory are also outlined.

Result: The paper identifies three concrete ways graphs benefit LLMs: reducing hallucinations via dynamic knowledge, improving reasoning via graph-structured prompting, and expanding LLM applicability to structured domains like e-commerce, code, and relational databases.

Conclusion: Graphs play a vital complementary role in advancing LLMs. Future work should explore sparse graph architectures and brain-inspired memory systems to further integrate graphs into LLM design.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), classic graph learning tasks have greatly benefited from LLMs, including improved encoding of textual features, more efficient construction of graphs from text, and enhanced reasoning over knowledge graphs. In this paper, we ask a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? We address this question from three perspectives: 1) graphs provide an up-to-date knowledge source that helps reduce LLM hallucinations, 2) graph-based prompting techniques-such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree-of-Thought (ToT), and Graph-of-Thought (GoT)-enhance LLM reasoning capabilities, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs improves their understanding of structured data, expanding their applicability to domains such as e-commerce, code, and relational databases (RDBs). We further outlook some future directions including designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and brain-inspired memory systems.

</details>


### [644] [Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02475)
*David Wilmot*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Shadow-Loom is an open-source framework that converts narratives into graphical world models, then applies causal physics (Pearl's ladder, counterfactual AMWN calculus) and narrative physics (mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, surprise) to analyze stories, using LLMs only for boundary tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge narrative theory and causal reasoning by creating a computational framework that can model both causal structures and reader-state dynamics (mystery, suspense, etc.) from stories.

Method: Converts narrative into a versioned graphical world model; applies causal physics (Pearl's ladder + AMWN counterfactual calculus) and narrative physics (Sternberg-based structural reader-states); uses LLMs only for extraction, rendering, audit — not for core reasoning.

Result: An open-source research artefact (code, fixtures, pipeline) demonstrating a hybrid causal-narrative analysis framework, though not benchmarked as an NLP model.

Conclusion: Shadow-Loom offers a novel, principled approach to narrative analysis by combining causal and narrative physics in a graph-based system, contributed as a research tool rather than a performance-optimized model.

Abstract: Stories hold a reader's attention because they have causes, secrets, and consequences. Shadow-Loom is an experimental open-source framework that turns a narrative into a versioned graphical world model and lets two engines act on it: a causal physics grounded in Pearl's ladder of causation and a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks; and a narrative physics that scores the same graph against four structural reader-states -- mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise -- in the tradition of Sternberg's curiosity/suspense/surprise triad, with suspense formalised in the structural-affect line of work on story comprehension and computational suspense. Large language models are used only at the boundary: extraction, rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, and counterfactual reasoning are carried out in typed code over the graph. The system is offered as a research artefact rather than as a benchmarked NLP model; code, fixtures, and pipeline are released open source.

</details>


### [645] [Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02488)
*Periklis Mantenoglou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes mapping event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->- and introduces Streaming Trigger Graphs for efficient stream reasoning, enabling a unified composite event recognition mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event specification languages are studied in isolation, making comparison of expressivity difficult and obscuring the scope of stream reasoners.

Method: Maps practical fragments of event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->- (temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies), and proposes Streaming Trigger Graphs as an extension of Datalog materialisation for efficient stream reasoning.

Result: A uniform composite event recognition mechanism that generalises across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

Conclusion: The approach enables efficient, timely detection of critical events over high-velocity streams while unifying diverse event specification languages under a common framework.

Abstract: Several applications demand the timely detection of critical situations, such as threats to safety and transparency, over high-velocity streams of symbolic events. This demand has motivated the development of (i) event specification languages, which define composite events via temporal patterns over simpler events, and (ii) stream reasoning frameworks, evaluating patterns expressed in these languages. However, event specification languages are typically studied in isolation, complicating their comparison in terms of expressivity and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners. To mitigate this issue, we map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->-, a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies. To support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog->-, we propose Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of a state-of-the-art technique for Datalog materialisation. Our approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalise across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

</details>


### [646] [GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02489)
*Jinliang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIL is a framework for fast (<400ms) and accurate agent discovery in multi-agent LLM systems, using SLM-based tag prediction, pseudo-document expansion, and MaxSim Resonance to replace slow LLM parsing or imprecise vector search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent discovery methods face a trade-off between speed (slow LLM parsing >30s) and accuracy (fast vector retrieval sacrifices semantic precision), creating a bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration.

Method: Three innovations: (1) Fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level tag prediction; (2) Pseudo-Document Expansion to augment agent descriptions with synthetic queries; (3) MaxSim Resonance for fine-grained maximum similarity matching between queries and agent usage examples.

Result: Achieves sub-400ms latency (79× faster than LLM baselines), significantly outperforms traditional vector search in Recall@10, validated on the AgentTaxo-9K dataset (9,240 agents).

Conclusion: GRAIL provides a scalable, industrial-grade solution bridging speed and accuracy for real-time agent discovery in the "Internet of Agents."

Abstract: As the ecosystem of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents expands rapidly, efficient and accurate Agent Discovery becomes a critical bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration. Existing approaches typically face a dichotomy: either relying on heavy-weight LLMs for intent parsing, leading to prohibitive latency (often exceeding 30 seconds), or using monolithic vector retrieval that sacrifices semantic precision for speed. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{GRAIL} (Granular Resonance-based Agent/AI Link), a novel framework achieving sub-400ms discovery latency without compromising accuracy. GRAIL introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{SLM-Enhanced Prediction}, replacing the generalized LLM parser with a specialized, fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level capability tag prediction; (2) \textbf{Pseudo-Document Expansion}, augmenting agent descriptions with synthetic queries to enhance semantic density for robust dense retrieval; and (3) \textbf{MaxSim Resonance}, a fine-grained matching mechanism computing maximum similarity between user queries and discrete agent usage examples, effectively mitigating semantic dilution. Validated on \textbf{AgentTaxo-9K}, our new large-scale dataset of 9,240 agents, GRAIL reduces end-to-end discovery latency by over \textbf{79$\times$} compared to LLM-parsing baselines, while significantly outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10. This framework offers a scalable, industrial-grade solution for the real-time ``Internet of Agents."

</details>


### [647] [DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02503)
*Qiaohong Zhang,Weihao Ye,Jialong Chen,Yi Luo,BoYuan Li,Bowen Deng,Zibin Zheng,Jianhao Lin,Wei-Shi Zheng,Chuan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DataClaw is a process-oriented benchmark with ~2.06M real-world records and 492 cross-domain tasks for evaluating autonomous data analysis agents, revealing most current LLMs achieve below 50% accuracy in exploratory analysis settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and provide limited support for reasoning process evaluation, whereas autonomous agents need to be tested on exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments.

Method: Constructed ~2.06M real-world records across enterprise, industry, and policy domains with native noise preserved. Includes 492 cross-domain tasks from think-tank scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation of agent progress and reasoning breakdowns.

Result: Seven out of eight advanced LLMs achieved below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis revealed partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across models.

Conclusion: DataClaw provides a less data-constrained diagnostic testbed for probing capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents, highlighting that current agents remain far from reliable in exploratory settings.

Abstract: Evaluating autonomous data analysis agents requires testing their ability to perform exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments.
