TL;DR: JACTUS unifies compression and fine-tuning in a single framework by estimating input and gradient covariances, forming an orthogonal union with pretrained weight subspaces, and performing a projected low-rank approximation that allocates rank globally. It achieves strong results on vision (89.2% on ViT-Base) and language (80.9% on Llama2-7B) at 80% retained parameters, outperforming full-parameter PEFT baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT and low-rank compression are applied sequentially (compress first, then fine-tune), which misaligns the compressed subspace with downstream task objectives and wastes the global parameter budget.

Method: From a small calibration set, JACTUS estimates input and pre-activation gradient covariances, forms their orthogonal union with the pretrained weight subspace, performs a projected low-rank approximation inside this union, allocates rank globally by marginal gain per parameter, and trains only a compact core matrix.

Result: On vision: 89.2% average accuracy on ViT-Base across eight datasets at 80% retained parameters (surpassing DoRA 87.9%). On language: 80.9% average on Llama2-7B commonsense QA at 80% retained parameters (surpassing DoRA 79.7% and prior compress-then-finetune pipelines).

Conclusion: JACTUS demonstrates that jointly optimizing compression and adaptation, rather than decoupling them, yields superior performance at reduced parameter budgets, eliminating the need to retain full frozen weights while enabling fast and robust tuning.

Abstract: Adapting large pretrained models to diverse tasks is now routine, yet the two dominant strategies of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) and low-rank compression are typically composed in sequence. This decoupled practice first compresses and then fine-tunes adapters, potentially misaligning the compressed subspace with downstream objectives and squandering a global parameter budget. To overcome this limitation, we introduce JACTUS (Joint Adaptation and Compression with a Task-aware Union of Subspaces), a single framework that unifies compression and adaptation. From a small calibration set, JACTUS estimates input and pre-activation gradient covariances, forms their orthogonal union with the pretrained weight subspace, performs a projected low-rank approximation inside this union, allocates rank globally by marginal gain per parameter, and trains only a compact core matrix. This explicitly mitigates the potential misalignment between the compressed subspace and downstream objectives by coupling the directions preserved for compression with those required for adaptation, yielding a deployable low-rank model that avoids retaining full frozen weights while enabling fast and robust tuning. On vision, JACTUS attains an average 89.2% accuracy on ViT-Base across eight datasets at 80% retained parameters, surpassing strong 100% PEFT baselines (e.g., DoRA 87.9%). On language, JACTUS achieves an 80.9% average on Llama2-7B commonsense QA at the same 80% retained-parameter budget, outperforming 100% PEFT (e.g., DoRA 79.7%) and exceeding prior compress-then-finetune pipelines under the same ratained-parameter budget. We will release code.

</details>


### [676] [HAAS: A Policy-Aware Framework for Adaptive Task Allocation Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02832)
*Vicente Pelechanoa,Antoni Mestre,Manoli Albert,Miriam Gil*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HAAS is a framework for adaptive human-AI task allocation that uses rule-based governance plus contextual-bandit learning. Tests in software engineering and manufacturing show governance is tunable (not binary), can reduce fatigue in manufacturing, and no single governance setting works universally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches treat human-AI work distribution as a binary choice, but real operations involve shared tasks and complementary roles. Governing this distribution while balancing efficiency, oversight, and human capability remains unsolved.

Method: HAAS combines a rule-based expert system (enforcing governance constraints) with a contextual-bandit learner (selecting collaboration modes from feedback). Task-agent fit is modeled via five cognitive dimensions and a five-mode autonomy spectrum, tested on a cross-domain benchmark.

Result: Three findings: (1) governance is a tunable variable, not binary; (2) stronger governance in manufacturing can improve performance and reduce fatigue (workload-buffering effect); (3) no single governance setting dominates — moderate governance improves with learner experience.

Conclusion: HAAS provides a pre-deployment workbench for inspecting and comparing human-AI allocation policies before organizational commitment.

Abstract: Deciding how to distribute work between humans and AI systems is a central challenge in organisational design. Most approaches treat this as a binary choice, yet the operational reality is richer: humans and AI routinely share tasks or take complementary roles depending on context, fatigue, and the stakes involved. Governing that distribution -- balancing efficiency, oversight, and human capability -- remains an open problem. This paper presents Human-AI Adaptive Symbiosis (HAAS), an implemented framework for adaptive task allocation in software engineering and manufacturing. HAAS combines two coupled components: a rule-based expert system that enforces governance constraints before any learning occurs, and a contextual-bandit learner that selects among feasible collaboration modes from outcome feedback. Task-agent fit is represented through five auditable cognitive dimensions and a five-mode autonomy spectrum -- from human-only to fully autonomous -- embedded in a reproducible benchmark spanning both domains. Three empirical findings emerge. First, governance is not a binary switch but a tunable design variable: tighter constraints predictably convert autonomous AI assignments into supervised collaborations, with domain-specific costs and benefits. Second, in manufacturing, stronger governance can improve operational performance and reduce fatigue simultaneously -- a workload-buffering effect that contradicts the usual framing of governance as pure overhead. Third, no single governance setting dominates across all contexts; moderate governance becomes increasingly competitive as the learner accumulates experience within the governed action space. Together, these findings position HAAS as a pre-deployment workbench for comparing and inspecting human--AI allocation policies before organisational commitment.

</details>


### [677] [Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02860)
*Mohamad Khajezade,Fatemeh H. Fard,Mohamed Sami Shehata*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a knowledge distillation framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from large LLMs (DeepSeek-R1) into compact open-source models (Phi3, Qwen-Coder) for cross-language code clone detection, combined with response stabilization methods to improve reliability and reduce inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: X-CCD is challenging due to low surface similarity across languages. Using large LLMs as black-box systems raises concerns about cost, reproducibility, privacy, and unreliable output formatting. Compact open-source models struggle with reasoning-oriented prompts and consistent binary label mapping.

Method: 1) Knowledge distillation from DeepSeek-R1 into compact student models (Phi3, Qwen-Coder) using LoRA adapters. 2) Reasoning-oriented synthetic training data constructed from Project CodeNet. 3) Response stabilization methods: forced conclusion prompting, binary classification head, contrastive classification head.

Result: Distillation consistently improves reliability of compact models, often improves predictive performance especially under distribution shift. Classification-head variants substantially reduce inference time vs generation-based inference. Tested on Python-Java, Rust-Java, Rust-Python, Rust-Ruby.

Conclusion: Reasoning-oriented distillation combined with response stabilization makes compact open-source models more practical and reliable for X-CCD detection.

Abstract: Cross-language code clone detection (X-CCD) is challenging because semantically equivalent programs written in different languages often share little surface similarity. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for semantic clone detection, their use as black-box systems raises concerns about cost, reproducibility, privacy, and unreliable output formatting. In particular, compact open-source models often struggle to follow reasoning-oriented prompts and to produce outputs that can be consistently mapped to binary clone labels.
  To address these limitations, we propose a knowledge distillation framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from DeepSeek-R1 into compact open-source student models for X-CCD. Using cross-language code pairs derived from Project CodeNet, we construct reasoning-oriented synthetic training data and fine-tune Phi3 and Qwen-Coder with LoRA adapters. We further introduce response stabilization methods, including forced conclusion prompting, a binary classification head, and a contrastive classification head, and evaluate model behavior using both predictive metrics and response rate.
  Experiments on Python--Java, Rust--Java, Rust--Python, and Rust--Ruby show that knowledge distillation consistently improves the reliability of compact models and often improves predictive performance, especially under distribution shift. In addition, classification-head variants substantially reduce inference time compared to generation-based inference. Overall, our results show that reasoning-oriented distillation combined with response stabilization makes compact open-source models more practical and reliable for X-CCD detection.

</details>


### [678] [The Topology of Multimodal Fusion: Why Current Architectures Fail at Creative Cognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04465)
*Xiujiang Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that current multimodal AI has a topological limitation (contact topology / modal separability), and proposes a new framework based on the Chinese philosophical concept of xiang (operative schema), formalized via fiber bundles and Yang-Mills curvature, with proposed implementations including Neural ODEs, ANALOGY-MM benchmark, and META-TOP cross-civilizational test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and overcome a structural topological limitation in multimodal AI — the assumption of modal separability (contact topology) — that current architectures (CLIP, GPT-4V, diffusion models) all share.

Method: Three-pillar approach: (1) Philosophical: Reinterpret Wittgenstein's saying/showing via Chinese xiang (operative schema) as a cruciform framework; (2) Cognitive science: DMN/ECN/SN co-activation analyzed via overlap isomorphism vs superimposition collapse; (3) Mathematical: Formalization via fiber bundles and Yang-Mills curvature. Proposes UOO (Neural ODEs with topological regularization), ANALOGY-MM benchmark, and META-TOP benchmark.

Result: No empirical results presented. The paper offers a theoretical framework with proposed benchmarks (ANALOGY-MM with error-type-ratio metric, META-TOP with seven archetypes) and a phased experimental roadmap with falsification criteria.

Conclusion: The topological limitation of modal separability in current multimodal AI can be addressed by adopting a xiang-based cruciform framework, formalized topologically, with clear experimental falsification criteria.

Abstract: This paper identifies a structural limitation in current multimodal AI architectures that is topological rather than parametric. Contrastive alignment (CLIP), cross-attention fusion (GPT-4V/Gemini), and diffusion-based generation share a common geometric prior -- modal separability -- which we term contact topology. The argument rests on three pillars with philosophy as the generative center. The philosophical pillar reinterprets Wittgenstein's saying/showing distinction as a problem rather than a conclusion: where Wittgenstein chose silence, the Chinese craft epistemology tradition responded with xiang (operative schema) -- the third state emerging when saying and showing interpenetrate. A cruciform framework (dao/qi x saying/showing) positions xiang at the intersection, executing dual huacai (transformation-and-cutting) along both axes. This generates a dual-layer dynamics: chuanghua (creative transformation as spontaneous event) and huacai (its institutionalization into repeatable form). The cognitive science pillar reinterprets DMN/ECN/SN tripartite co-activation through the pathological mirror: overlap isomorphism vs. superimposition collapse in a 2D parameter space (coupling intensity x regulatory capacity). The mathematical pillar formalizes these via fiber bundles and Yang-Mills curvature, with the cruciform structure mapped to fiber bundle language. We propose UOO implementation via Neural ODEs with topological regularization, the ANALOGY-MM benchmark with error-type-ratio metric, and the META-TOP three-tier benchmark testing cross-civilizational topological isomorphism across seven archetypes. A phased experimental roadmap with explicit termination criteria ensures clean exit if falsified.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [679] [Information Accessibility Limits in Structured NP Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00953)
*Jing-Yuan Wei*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper shows that locating violating principal minors near the boundary of P-matrices is information-theoretically hard: polynomially many queries cannot identify the violating subset with constant success probability, even though the underlying structure is strong.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether strong structural properties of matrix families guarantee that violations can be efficiently located, or whether there exist fundamental information-theoretic barriers even in structured settings.

Method: The authors formulate violation search as an information acquisition problem, then use mutual information and Fano's inequality to derive a lower bound showing that each query reveals only vanishing information, so polynomially many queries are insufficient for reliable recovery.

Result: Any algorithm restricted to polynomially many queries cannot recover the violating subset with constant success probability, revealing an information-theoretic bottleneck.

Conclusion: Structure does not imply accessibility; even highly structured problems can be computationally intractable when the needed information cannot be accessed via available queries.

Abstract: We study the problem of locating violating principal minors in structured matrix families that lie near the boundary of P-matrices and admit sparse violations under perturbation. Viewing violation search as an information acquisition problem, we show that, despite strong underlying structure, the location of a violation is globally encoded and not accessible through local queries.
  This leads to an information-theoretic bottleneck: each query reveals only vanishing information about the violating subset, so that polynomially many queries accumulate insufficient information to identify it. Using mutual information and Fano's inequality, we show that any algorithm restricted to polynomially many queries cannot recover the violating subset with constant success probability.
  Our analysis highlights a fundamental distinction between structure and accessibility: even highly structured problems can be computationally intractable when the information required to locate a solution is not accessible through the available queries.

</details>


### [680] [Split and Aggregation Learning for Foundation Models Over Mobile Embodied AI Network (MEAN): A Comprehensive Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00970)
*Qianzhou Chen,Siqi Sun,Minrui Xu,Sijie Ji,Jiawen Kang,Yijie Mao,Zhouxiang Zhao,Zhaohui Yang,Dusit Niyato*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This survey comprehensively analyzes split learning (SL) and aggregation learning (AL) as key paradigms for distributed AI in 6G wireless communication, covering architectures, methodologies, privacy-preserving techniques, and applications across semantic communication, RIS, SAGINs, and quantum communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid advancement of foundation models and 6G systems demands efficient, scalable, and privacy-preserving machine learning approaches to address challenges in distributed AI, including communication efficiency, resource allocation, and data privacy.

Method: The paper conducts a comprehensive survey analyzing SL (partitioning neural networks for collaborative training with data isolation) and AL (aggregating intermediate results or model updates), examining their architectures, configurations, aggregation techniques, and integration with AI-native 6G technologies.

Result: SL is ideal for strict data isolation scenarios (e.g., vertical collaborations), while AL suits homogeneous horizontal data settings; they can be combined to balance privacy and communication efficiency. Applications span semantic communication, RIS, SAGINs, and quantum communication.

Conclusion: SL and AL play crucial roles in shaping distributed AI-driven 6G communication systems by addressing efficiency, privacy preservation, and scalability.

Abstract: The rapid advancements in foundation models and sixth-generation (6G) wireless communication systems necessitate the development of efficient, scalable, and privacy-preserving machine learning approaches. For foundation models in 6G, split learning (SL) and aggregation learning (AL) have emerged as promising paradigms that address key challenges in distributed artificial intelligence (AI), such as communication efficiency, resource allocation, and data privacy. SL enables multiple entities to collaboratively train deep learning models by partitioning neural networks, while AL focuses on aggregating intermediate results or model updates from multiple participants, improving robustness, optimizing resource utilization, and mitigating data leakage risks. Specifically, SL is ideal for scenarios requiring strict data isolation (e.g., vertical collaborations), whereas AL suits homogeneous horizontal data settings; they can be combined to balance privacy and communication efficiency. This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of SL and AL in 6G communication systems, exploring their architectures, technical methodologies, and integration with AI-native 6G communication technologies. We examine different SL configurations, aggregation techniques, and their roles in optimizing distributed foundation models. Furthermore, we discuss their applications in emerging wireless networks, including semantic communication, reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS), space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs), and quantum communication. By analyzing the impact of SL and AL, this survey provides insights into their role in shaping distributed AI-driven communication systems in the 6G era, focusing on efficiency, privacy preservation, and scalability.

</details>


### [681] [Improved Rate-versus-Distance Upper Bounds for LDPC Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01213)
*Chong Shangguan,Yulin Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new framework using coset-weight generating functions to estimate ball sizes in LDPC coset graphs, improving previous bounds on the rate-distance tradeoff for LDPC codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the rate-distance tradeoff of LDPC codes, which is a central problem in coding theory and error correction.

Method: Introduces the coset-weight generating function encoding minimum Hamming weights of all cosets, then upper-bounds this function via local growth analysis for codes spanned by low-weight vectors.

Result: Sharpens previous ball-size estimates by Iceland and Samorodnitsky, and improves upper bounds on the rate of LDPC codes for a significant range of relative distances.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides tighter rate-distance tradeoff bounds for LDPC codes, advancing theoretical understanding of their error-correction capabilities.

Abstract: LDPC codes play a vital role in coding theory and practical error correction. A central problem in this direction is to understand their rate--distance tradeoff. In this paper, we introduce a new framework for estimating ball sizes in the coset graphs of LDPC codes. The key new object is the coset-weight generating function, which encodes the minimum Hamming weights of all cosets of a linear code. Rather than estimating coset balls directly, we upper-bound this generating function through a local growth analysis for codes spanned by low-weight vectors. This framework sharpens the previous ball-size estimate of Iceland and Samorodnitsky. Combined with a general method of Friedman and Tillich that relates balls in coset graphs to sizes of error-correcting codes, it further improves the upper bounds on the rate of LDPC codes for a significant range of relative distances.

</details>


### [682] [RIS Optimization and Scaling Laws in Multi-Operator Systems: Is Quadratic Scaling Achievable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01389)
*Zheyu Wu,Matteo Nerini,Bruno Clerckx*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a practical RIS design framework for multi-operator wireless systems, showing that BD-RIS with sufficient interconnections achieves quadratic power scaling with RIS elements, while conventional RIS only achieves linear scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-operator RIS-aided systems, the RIS can unintentionally reflect signals from non-serving operators, causing inter-operator interference and rapid channel fluctuations. This issue needs to be addressed for practical deployment.

Method: The authors propose a framework maximizing received signal power of the serving operator while enforcing fixed RIS-reflected channels for non-serving operators. They derive closed-form solutions using a novel technique for coupled unitary and linear equality constraints, and provide scaling law analysis for various BD-RIS architectures.

Result: For two-operator systems, received signal power scales quadratically with RIS elements for group-connected BD-RIS with group size Gs>=2, but only linearly for conventional RIS. For L-operator systems, quadratic scaling occurs when Gs>=L. BD-RIS with Gs=2 achieves a 13dB gain over conventional RIS in a 128-element, two-operator system.

Conclusion: Quadratic scaling in multi-operator systems is achievable only with BD-RIS architectures having enough interconnections. BD-RIS significantly outperforms conventional RIS in multi-operator scenarios.

Abstract: This paper studies multi-operator wireless communication systems aided by general reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS), including both conventional single-connected RIS and beyond-diagonal RIS (BD-RIS). Specifically, we consider a system where multiple operators coexist in the same area over different frequency bands, each with a single-antenna base station, while one operator serves its single-antenna user with the aid of an RIS. In such a system, the RIS may unintentionally reflect signals from the non-serving operators, leading to inter-operator interference and rapid fluctuations of their effective channels. To address this issue, we propose a practical RIS design framework that maximizes the received signal power of the serving operator while enforcing fixed RIS-reflected channels of the non-serving operators. We derive closed-form solutions to the resulting optimization problem, based on a novel technique to deal with the coupled unitary and linear equality constraints. We further give scaling law analysis of the received signal power. For a two-operator system, the received signal power scales quadratically with the number of RIS elements for group-connected BD-RIS with group size Gs>=2, whereas for conventional single-connected RIS it scales only linearly. More generally, for an L-operator system with L-1 non-serving operators, the scaling-law transition occurs at Gs=L, where quadratic scaling is achieved when Gs>=L, and linear scaling otherwise. These results demonstrate that, in a multi-operator system, quadratic scaling is achievable only with BD-RIS architectures having enough interconnections. Simulation results validate the analysis and show the significant gain of BD-RIS over conventional RIS in multi-operator systems. In particular, group-connected BD-RIS with Gs=2 achieves a 13dB gain over conventional RIS in a two-operator system with a 128-element RIS.

</details>


### [683] [Neural Equalisers for Highly Compressed Faster-than-Nyquist Signalling: Design, Performance, Complexity and Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01570)
*Shubham Paul,Sheetal Kalyani,Nambi Sheshadri,R David Koilpillai*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a deep learning-based receiver framework for Faster-than-Nyquist (FTN) signalling that uses sliding window detection to handle intersymbol interference efficiently, achieving reliable performance at up to 75% spectral compression with low latency and low complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FTN signalling can enhance spectral efficiency by exceeding the Nyquist limit, but practical deployment is hindered by the need for low-complexity detection strategies that manage induced ISI while maintaining low latency and robust performance.

Method: A deep learning-based framework with: (1) a sliding window detection method leveraging temporal context for computational efficiency, (2) optimization for low latency and low complexity, and (3) robustness evaluation across varying channel conditions and noise profiles.

Result: Demonstrated viability of FTN systems with very low packing factors, achieving reliable performance under aggressive spectral compression up to 75%.

Conclusion: The proposed deep learning receivers are resilient to non-idealities and suitable for real-time applications and scalable deployment in FTN communication systems.

Abstract: Faster-than-Nyquist (FTN) signalling has emerged as a compelling technique for enhancing spectral efficiency in bandwidth-constrained communication systems. By intentionally introducing controlled intersymbol interference (ISI), FTN allows transmission at rates exceeding the traditional Nyquist limit, unlocking new potential in high-speed data communication. However, its practical deployment remains challenged by the need for low-complexity detection strategies that can cope with the induced ISI while maintaining low latency and robust performance. We propose deep learning receivers that are resilient to non-idealities.
  In this paper, we present a deep learning-based framework for FTN signalling that addresses these challenges through several novel contributions. First, we propose a sliding window detection method that leverages temporal context while preserving computational efficiency. Second, we demonstrate the viability of FTN systems with very low packing factors, showing that reliable performance can be achieved even under aggressive spectral compression (up to 75\%). Our architecture is optimised for low latency and low complexity, making it suitable for real-time applications and scalable deployment. In addition, we assess the robustness of our models across varying channel conditions and noise profiles, providing insights into their generalisability and resilience.

</details>


### [684] [Walsh Spectrum and Boomerang Properties of Locally-APN Niho Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01786)
*Yuehui Cui,Jinquan Luo,Can Xiang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper shows that Niho type power functions are locally-APN if and only if their Walsh spectrum takes four specific values, and studies their differential spectrum, Walsh spectrum, FBCT, and second-order zero differential spectra.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Locally-APN functions offer superior resistance against differential cryptanalysis, but only a small number have been studied. This paper aims to characterize Niho type power functions as locally-APN via Walsh spectrum conditions.

Method: The authors establish an equivalence between locally-APN property of Niho type power functions and a four-valued Walsh spectrum. They also analyze associated cyclic codes, differential spectrum, FBCT, and second-order zero differential spectra.

Result: A Niho type power function is locally-APN iff its Walsh spectrum takes values in {-p^m, 0, p^m, 2p^m}, corresponding to cyclic codes with four nonzero weights: p^{m-1}(p-1)(p^m + k) for k=0,1,-1,-2.

Conclusion: The paper provides a complete characterization of locally-APN Niho type power functions and analyzes their cryptographic properties.

Abstract: Recently, the Walsh spectrum and boomerang properties of special power functions have aroused widespread research interest, owing to their important applications in cryptography and information security. In particular, locally-APN functions may offer superior resistance against differential cryptanalysis compared to other functions of equivalent differential uniformity. Up till now only a small number of locally-APN functions have been studied. In this paper, we show that a Niho type power function is locally-APN if and only if its Walsh spectrum takes four values in \(\{-p^m, 0, p^m, 2p^m\}\). Equivalently, the associated cyclic codes have four nonzero weights: $p^{m-1}(p-1)(p^m + k)$ for $k = 0, 1, -1, -2$. Moreover, we also study properties of Niho type locally-APN power functions, including their differential spectrum, Walsh spectrum, Feistel Boomerang Connectivity Table(FBCT for short) and second-order zero differential spectra.

</details>


### [685] [Atomic Hybrid Sparse/Diffuse Channel Estimation and Cramér-Rao Bounds Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01825)
*Lei Lyu,Maxime Ferreira Da Costa,Urbashi Mitra*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid sparse/diffuse channel model and HALS algorithm for estimating channel parameters with atomic and L2 regularization, supported by CRB analysis and validated on synthetic/real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model and estimate wireless channels that contain both sparse (resolvable) components and diffuse (scattered) components, which existing approaches treat separately.

Method: Proposes aHSD frequency-domain channel model; designs HALS algorithm combining atomic norm and L2 regularization for joint sparse/diffuse estimation; provides Lagrange dual analysis for off-the-grid delay estimation; derives CRB bounds as functions of minimum frequency separations.

Result: Numerical simulations on synthetic and real data show HALS effectively estimates both components; the derived CRB bounds better predict HALS performance than previously considered bounds.

Conclusion: The proposed model and algorithm successfully handle hybrid sparse/diffuse channels with improved theoretical bounds and practical estimation performance.

Abstract: In this paper, an atomic hybrid sparse/diffuse (aHSD) channel model in the frequency domain is proposed. Based on a structural analysis of the resolvable paths and diffuse scattering statistics, the Hybrid Atomic-Least-Squares (HALS) algorithm is designed to estimate sparse/diffuse components with a combined atomic and $\ell_2$ regularization. A theoretical analysis of the Lagrange dual problem is conducted, and the conditions required for primal and dual solutions are provided, supporting an off-the-grid delay-time estimator. The Cramér--Rao Bound (CRB) analysis in this paper focuses on the estimation of the channel parameters, resulting in a bound on the aggregate channel. Lower and upper bounds for the CRB on parameters are derived as functions of the minimum separations between frequency parameters. Numerical results via simulations on synthetic and real data validate the efficacy of the HALS estimation strategy and show the improved predictive ability of the CRB analysis for the performance of HALS versus previously considered bounds.

</details>


### [686] [Remote Action Generation: Remote Control with Minimal Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01833)
*Szymon Kobus,Deniz Gündüz*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: GRASP enables efficient remote control over bandwidth-constrained channels by having the controller send minimal action guidance (via importance sampling) instead of full action specifications, while actors learn the controller's policy locally to progressively reduce communication needs—achieving up to 50x data reduction for continuous action spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable rate-efficient communication in remote control scenarios where a controller (with reward access) must steer actors over communication-constrained channels, especially when dealing with large or continuous action spaces that make direct action transmission expensive.

Method: The controller sends minimal information via importance-sampled action guidance (remote generation), and actors locally generate actions by sampling from the controller's evolving target policy. Actors concurrently use received guidance as supervised learning data to learn the controller's policy, improving local sampling and reducing future communication needs.

Result: Average 12-fold data reduction across all experiments; 50-fold reduction for continuous action spaces compared to direct action transmission; 41-fold reduction compared to reward transmission.

Conclusion: Remote generation with guided importance sampling is highly effective for communication-efficient remote control, with actor-side policy learning progressively reducing bandwidth requirements.

Abstract: We address the challenge of remote control where one or more actors, lacking direct reward access, are steered by a controller over a communication-constrained channel. The controller learns an optimal policy from observed rewards and communicates action guidance to the actors, which becomes demanding for large or continuous action spaces. To achieve rate-efficient communication throughout this interactive learning and control process, we introduce a novel framework leveraging remote generation. Instead of transmitting full action specifications, the controller sends minimal information, enabling the actors to locally generate actions by sampling from the controller's evolving target policy. This guided sampling is facilitated by an importance sampling approach. Concurrently, the actors use the received guidance as supervised learning data to learn the controller's policy. This actor-side learning improves their local sampling capabilities, progressively reducing future communication needs. Our solution, Guided Remote Action Sampling Policy (GRASP), demonstrates significant communication reduction, achieving an average 12-fold data reduction across all experiments (50-fold for continuous action spaces) compared to direct action transmission, and a 41-fold reduction compared to reward transmission.

</details>


### [687] [A Statistical-Physics Refinement of Soft Covering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01839)
*Neri Merhav*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses statistical physics to study the channel output distribution of random codes, deriving a two-branch free energy structure (bulk vs. sparse) that reveals phase transitions in the Rényi spectrum, with applications to guesswork, channel resolvability, and hypothesis testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the full Rényi spectrum of the channel output distribution induced by random codes, revealing the competition between typical and atypical codeword populations through the lens of statistical physics.

Method: The paper analyzes the annealed free energy (logarithm of the partition function Z_n) via a single-letter formula that decomposes into two branches: a bulk branch ψ_b(β,R) driven by typical codewords, and a sparse branch ψ_s(β,R) driven by atypical codewords. Phase boundaries (R^*(β), I^b(β), I^s(β)) are derived analytically.

Result: The phase diagram in the (β,R) plane has four regions separated by three boundaries, all meeting at (β,R)=(1,I(X;Y)). The bulk-sparse competition boundary R^*(β) has a closed form for β≥1. The Z-channel example illustrates the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: The two-branch decomposition of free energy provides a unified framework capturing the full Rényi spectrum of the output distribution, with direct applications to guesswork, channel resolvability, and hypothesis testing problems.

Abstract: We study the channel output distribution induced by a rate-$R$ random code via statistical physics. The partition function is $Z_n(β|\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{y^n}[P_{Y^n|\mathcal{C}}(y^n)]^β$, where $\mathcal{C}$ is the code and $β> 0$ is inverse temperature. Our focus is on the free energy which is the normalized logarithm of this quantity, which encodes the full Rényi spectrum of the output distribution. The single-letter formula derived for the annealed free energy decomposes into two branches which reflect a ``competition'' between two populations of codewords. One is the \emph{bulk branch}, $ψ_{\mbox{\tiny b}}(β,R)$, which is driven by typical codewords and the other one is the \emph{sparse branch} $ψ_{\mbox{\tiny s}}(β,R)$, which is driven by a-typical codewords, where the qualifiers `typical' and `atypical' are in a sense to become apparent later. We analyze the phase structure of each branch separately and characterize their competition. Both branches are derived for all $β> 0$. The phase boundary $R^\star(β)$, where the two branches are equal, is analyzed for $β\geq 1$, where it has an explicit closed-form expression. The phase diagram in the first quadrant of the $(β, R)$ plane has four regions separated by three boundaries: $R = I^{\mbox{\tiny b}}(β)$ (bulk branch transition), $R = R^\star(β)$ (bulk--sparse competition boundary), and $R = I^{\mbox{\tiny s}}(β)$ (sparse branch transition), all meeting at the point $(β, R) = (1, I(X;Y))$, where $I(X;Y)$ is the mutual information induced by the input type and the channel. Applications to guesswork, channel resolvability, and hypothesis testing are discussed, and all results are illustrated with a numerical example of a Z-channel.

</details>


### [688] [Optimal Communication Rate of Secure Aggregation over Ring Networks with Pairwise Keys](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01849)
*Xiang Zhang,Han Yu,Zhou Li,Yizhou Zhao,Giuseppe Caire*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies Information-theoretic Topological Secure Aggregation (TSA) over a ring topology using pairwise secret keys (instead of a trusted key server). It characterizes the minimum per-user communication rate: for K=3,4 users, each user must send at least 1 bit per sum bit; for K≥5, at least 2 bits. A linear pairwise-masking scheme achieves these rates, and optimality is proven via entropic converse bounds. Interestingly, only keys between users at ring distance 2 suffice for optimality when K≥4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TSA relies on a trusted key server with arbitrarily correlated keys, which creates a single point of failure. This paper aims to eliminate that vulnerability by using pairwise secret keys established via inter-user communication, thus improving robustness and decentralization.

Method: The paper considers a ring topology with K users and pairwise independent secret keys S_{i,j} shared between each user pair. A linear pairwise-masking scheme is proposed. The optimal communication rate is derived via tight entropic converse bounds that exploit the dependency structure of the keys.

Result: For a ring topology: K=3,4 require at least 1 bit per user per sum bit; K≥5 require at least 2 bits. Optimality is achieved by the proposed scheme. For K≥4, only keys at ring distance 2 are sufficient, showing topological sparsity matters.

Conclusion: Pairwise secret keys can replace a trusted key server for TSA, with a clear rate threshold depending on network size. The results highlight the role of topological structure in efficient and robust secure aggregation, with implications for decentralized federated learning.

Abstract: Information-theoretic topological secure aggregation (TSA)\cite{zhang2026information_regular} enables distributed users to compute neighborhood sums over arbitrary networks without revealing individual inputs, while remaining communication-efficient. It has broad applications, including secure model aggregation in decentralized federated learning (FL). Existing TSA formulations rely on arbitrarily correlated keys generated by a trusted key server, which introduces a single point of failure. In this paper, we instead study TSA with \tit{pairwise} secret keys, where each user pair $(i,j)$ shares an independent key $S_{i,j}$. Such keys can be established through inter-user communication, eliminating the need for a key server and improving robustness. Focusing on a ring topology with $K$ users, we characterize the minimum per-user communication rate: \tit{to securely compute one bit of the desired input sum, each user must send at least $1$ bit to its neighbors when $K=3,4$, and at least $2$ bits for all $K\ge 5$}. The higher rate in larger networks arises because each user must simultaneously satisfy two independent key-alignment constraints from its two neighborhoods, which cannot be resolved within a single broadcast symbol under pairwise key independence. We propose a linear pairwise-masking scheme that achieves these rates and prove its optimality via tight entropic converse bounds that exploit the dependency structure of the keys. Notably, for all $K\ge 4$, only a subset of the $\binom{K}{2}$ pairwise keys -- specifically, those between users at ring distance $2$ -- is sufficient to achieve optimality, revealing a nontrivial role of topological sparsity in secure aggregation.

</details>


### [689] [Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01869)
*Weixuan Chen,Qianqian Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an evolving semantic token communication system over MIMO fading channels that transmits only prefix portions of semantic tokens to reduce overhead, uses a parametric memory network (GPT-2-based) with kNN teacher distributions and online evolution to recover missing suffixes, achieving up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full semantic token transmission incurs considerable communication overhead; reducing transmission cost while maintaining reliable recovery is needed.

Method: Transmit only equal-length prefixes of semantic tokens; at receiver, use a parametric memory network (GPT-2-based) with kNN-distilled teacher distributions for suffix recovery; online evolution strategy periodically updates memory with new test samples.

Result: Outperforms existing evolving memory benchmark under various channel conditions and bandwidth ratios, with up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement.

Conclusion: Transmitting token prefixes with a learned parametric memory network and online adaptation effectively reduces communication overhead while maintaining high recovery quality.

Abstract: Token communication has emerged as a promising framework for efficient wireless transmission by representing source data as compact semantic tokens. However, transmitting full semantic tokens still incurs considerable communication overhead. In this paper, we propose an evolving semantic token communication system with a parametric memory network over MIMO fading channels. Specifically, only an equal-length prefix of each semantic token is transmitted, which reduces transmission cost while preserving a consistent token structure for receiver-side recovery. At the receiver, a parametric memory network is introduced to reconstruct the missing suffix information from the received token prefixes, where semantic memory is stored implicitly in the network parameters. To realize this design, full semantic tokens are first organized into a codebook, and truncated tokens are paired with the codeword labels of their corresponding full tokens. Based on these token-label pairs, kNN-based teacher distributions are constructed to fine-tune a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module, which learns to infer the codeword distribution of each incomplete token and recover the corresponding complete semantic token. In addition, an online evolution strategy is developed to periodically update the parametric memory network and the entire system using newly observed test samples, thereby improving adaptability under distribution shifts. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms the existing evolving memory benchmark under different channel conditions and channel bandwidth ratios, with up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement.

</details>


### [690] [SwiftChannel: Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Deep Learning-Based 5G Channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01931)
*Shengzhe Lyu,Yuhan She,Di Duan,Tao Ni,Yu Hin Chan,Chengwen Luo,Ray C. C. Cheung,Weitao Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: SwiftChannel is an algorithm-hardware co-design framework for 5G MIMO channel estimation, combining a lightweight CNN with attention mechanism and a dedicated FPGA accelerator that achieves sub-millisecond latency with 24x speedup and 33x energy efficiency over GPU solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Channel estimation in 5G networks faces challenges from MIMO and mmWave technologies, requiring accurate estimation under strict latency constraints on resource-limited hardware platforms.

Method: A CNN enhanced with parameter-free attention mechanism reconstructs full-resolution channel matrices from LS estimates. A multi-stage compression pipeline (knowledge distillation, re-parameterization, quantization-aware training) reduces model size. The compressed model and LS estimator are implemented on FPGA via HLS with fine-grained pipeline architecture.

Result: On Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC, achieves sub-millisecond latency, 24x speedup and 33x energy efficiency improvement over GPU solutions, with generalization across noise levels, user mobilities, and unseen channel profiles.

Conclusion: The algorithm-hardware co-design approach provides a future-proof, efficient channel estimation solution for 5G MIMO systems, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.

Abstract: Channel estimation is crucial in 5G communication networks for optimizing transmission parameters and ensuring reliable, high-speed communication. However, the use of multiple-input and multiple-output (MIMO) and millimeter-wave (mmWave) in 5G networks presents challenges in achieving accurate estimation under strict latency requirements on resource-limited hardware platforms. To address these challenges, we propose SwiftChannel, an algorithm-hardware co-design framework that integrates a hardware-friendly deep learning-based channel estimator with a dedicated accelerator. Our approach employs a convolutional neural network enhanced with a parameter-free attention mechanism, which effectively reconstructs full-resolution spatial-frequency domain channel matrices from low-resolution least squares (LS) estimates. We further develop a multi-stage model compression pipeline combining knowledge distillation, convolution re-parameterization, and quantization-aware training, resulting in substantial model size reduction with negligible accuracy loss. The hardware accelerator, implementing the compressed model and the LS estimator on FPGA platforms using High-level Synthesis (HLS), features a fine-grained pipeline architecture and optimized dataflow strategies. Tested on a Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC, the accelerator achieves sub-millisecond latency, providing up to 24x speed-up and over 33x improvement in energy efficiency compared to GPU-based solutions. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed design generalizes not only across various noise levels and user mobilities, but also to a variety of unseen channel profiles, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. By unifying algorithmic innovation with hardware-aware design, our work presents a future-proof channel estimation solution for 5G MIMO systems.

</details>


### [691] [Combinatorial Analysis of Dyadic and Quasi-Dyadic Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01942)
*Anthony Gómez-Fonseca,Gretchen L. Matthews,Kirsten D. Morris,Tefjol Pllaha*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper develops an algebraic framework for constructing QLDPC codes from dyadic matrices, enabling systematic enumeration and control of short cycles and absorbing sets to improve iterative decoding performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: QLDPC codes suffer performance degradation due to short cycles and harmful subgraphs in Tanner graphs; existing methods lack algebraic tools to systematically control these structures.

Method: Uses dyadic/quasi-dyadic matrices with translation-invariant block structure, relates cycles in lifted Tanner graphs to closed walks in protographs, introduces dyadic-aware PEG algorithms with forbidden shift sets, and characterizes absorbing sets in boundary cases.

Result: Achieves minimization of short-cycle multiplicity (4-, 6-, 8-cycles) and identifies absorbing set configurations; belief-propagation simulations show substantial decoding gains from reduced cycle multiplicity.

Conclusion: Dyadic algebraic structures provide a principled way to control harmful graph substructures in QLDPC codes, improving fault-tolerant quantum computation viability even when girth cannot be increased.

Abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes offer a promising route to scalable fault-tolerant quantum computation, but their performance under iterative decoding is strongly influenced by short-cycle structure and other harmful subgraphs in the associated Tanner graphs. This paper develops an algebraic framework for constructing and analyzing (Q)LDPC codes from dyadic and quasi-dyadic matrices-translation-invariant $2^\ell \times 2^\ell$ binary matrices specified compactly by a signature row and forming a commutative ring with recursive block structure. Leveraging this structure, we relate cycles in lifted Tanner graphs to tailless backtrackless closed walks in the protograph and derive efficient, implementable methods to enumerate and control short cycles (notably $4$-, $6$-, and $8$-cycles). We introduce dyadic-aware PEG-style construction algorithms that use forbidden sets of shifts to maximize attainable girth when possible and otherwise minimize the multiplicity of the shortest cycles at the target girth. Motivated by error-floor phenomena, we further characterize and explicitly enumerate absorbing sets in key dyadic layout boundary cases, identifying configurations that induce abundant $(a,0)$-absorbing sets. Finally, we propose CSS-oriented dyadic constructions that satisfy commutation constraints by design and demonstrate via belief-propagation simulations that reducing short-cycle multiplicity can yield substantial decoding gains even when girth cannot be increased.

</details>


### [692] [Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01991)
*Vishnu Teja Kunde,Jean-Francois Chamberland,Krishna R. Narayanan,Jamison Ebert*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the compression-delay tradeoff in real-time text transmission using causal language models as predictors within a predict-then-code architecture, comparing Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS, and gzip across GPT-2 and Llama 3.2 models, finding that Huffman is optimal for over-provisioned channels while arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression with decodability delay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the tradeoff between compression efficiency and delay (both algorithmic and computational) when using causal language models for real-time text transmission, particularly as model scale increases.

Method: The authors compare several coding schemes (Shannon ideal, Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at various block sizes, and gzip) within a predict-then-code architecture, evaluating algorithmic vs. computational delay across two language model scales: GPT-2 (124M parameters) and Llama 3.2 (3B parameters).

Result: Huffman coding emerges as the practical choice for over-provisioned channels with zero algorithmic delay. Scaling from GPT-2 to Llama 3.2 yields approximately 38% reduction in bits per character, effectively over-provisioning the channel and changing which coder is optimal. Arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression but incurs decodability delay.

Conclusion: The optimal coder depends on channel provisioning; better language models (larger scale) shift the tradeoff, making different coders optimal. Huffman is recommended for over-provisioned channels, while arithmetic coding is better when compression is prioritized over delay.

Abstract: Learning, prediction, and compression are intimately connected: a model that accurately predicts the next symbol in a sequence can be coupled with a source coder to compress that sequence near its information-theoretic limit. When tokenized characters arriving at a fixed reading pace are encoded into variable-length codewords and streamed over a fixed-rate channel, a queue forms whose per-token delay depends on the mean and variance of the bit lengths and on the coder's algorithmic latency. This paper investigates the compression--delay tradeoff that arises when a causal language model serves as the sequential predictor within a predict-then-code architecture for real-time text transmission. Several coding schemes are compared: Shannon (ideal), Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at various block sizes, and gzip. The analysis separates algorithmic delay, inherent to the coder, from computational delay, which shrinks as hardware improves. Huffman is the practical choice for over-provisioned channels, with zero algorithmic delay and modest compression overhead. Arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression at the cost of decodability delay. Findings are validated across two scales: GPT-2 (124M) and Llama~3.2 (3B), a twenty-five-fold parameter range. This scaling yields an approximately 38\% reduction in bits per character, effectively over-provisioning the channel and thereby changing which coder is optimal.

</details>


### [693] [Channel-coded Over-the-Air Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02025)
*Shudi Weng,Ming Xiao,Mikael Skoglund*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This letter proposes a novel channel coding scheme for over-the-air computation (AirComp) that preserves aggregation structure while mitigating channel distortions, showing that computation error decreases with coding rate and can asymptotically approach zero.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AirComp enables efficient wireless data aggregation, its computation accuracy is sensitive to channel impairments. The role of channel coding in AirComp has been largely unexplored, creating a critical gap in achieving reliable AirComp systems.

Method: A novel channel coding scheme tailored for AirComp that preserves the aggregation structure while mitigating channel distortions.

Result: The computation error decreases with the coding rate and can asymptotically approach zero.

Conclusion: Both theoretical and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly enhances computation performance for AirComp systems.

Abstract: This letter studies channel coding for over-the-air computation (AirComp). AirComp enables efficient wireless data aggregation, where computation accuracy is the key performance metric. However, this accuracy is sensitive to channel impairments. As a promising solution, the role of channel coding in AirComp has been largely unexplored, creating a critical gap in achieving reliable AirComp systems. To address this, we propose a novel channel coding scheme tailored for AirComp that preserves the aggregation structure while mitigating channel distortions. We show that the computation error decreases with the coding rate and can asymptotically approach zero. Both theoretical and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme significantly enhances computation performance.

</details>


### [694] [Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02045)
*Svitlana Matsenko,Amirhossein Ghazisaeidi,Marcin Jarzyna,Konrad Banaszek,Darko Zibar*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper uses reinforcement learning to optimize hardware-constrained communication systems, achieving performance gains under realistic conditions like limited filter taps, mean photon number constraints, and finite DAC/ADC resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address practical hardware limitations (limited FIR taps, mean photon number constraints, finite DAC/ADC resolution) that are often ignored in idealized communication system designs.

Method: Reinforcement learning is employed to jointly optimize transmitter and receiver parameters under realistic hardware constraints.

Result: Significant performance improvements are achieved under realistic hardware constraints compared to conventional approaches.

Conclusion: RL-based optimization is effective for practical communication systems with hardware limitations, bridging the gap between theoretical designs and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Using reinforcement learning, we optimize for practical hardware constraints, including limited FIR filter taps at the transmitter and receiver, mean photon number and finite DAC/ADC resolution. Under these realistic conditions, the proposed approach achieves significant performance improvements.

</details>


### [695] [Semantic Ordered Statistics Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02296)
*Chentao Yue,Branka Vucetic,Yonghui Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Sem-OSD is a soft decoder that integrates byte-level language model priors into ordered statistics decoding for short linear block codes, achieving significant coding gains over traditional decoders on both AWGN and burst-error channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve decoding performance for short linear block codes carrying byte-streamed sources (e.g., natural-language text) by leveraging semantic information from language models, which traditional decoders like OSD and Berlekamp-Massey do not exploit.

Method: Sem-OSD fuses byte-level language-model (LM) priors with channel reliability into a bit-level score for MRB selection and codeword scoring. It enumerates two TEP families: a bit-flip family (flips up to m bits) and an LM-driven family (up to ω byte substitutions), using a byte-level Transformer fine-tuned for byte-level denoising to compute the LM prior.

Result: On AWGN, sem-OSD achieves BLERs below the finite-blocklength normal-approximation bound on BCH(127,64) and shortened RS(16,8) over GF(256), outperforming Fossorier OSD by 1.5 dB coding gain. On Gilbert-Elliott burst-error channel, it provides 4 dB and 1 dB more coding gain than Berlekamp-Massey and OSD, respectively.

Conclusion: Sem-OSD demonstrates that semantic priors from language models can significantly enhance decoding performance for byte-streamed sources, surpassing traditional decoders and even information-theoretic bounds in certain regimes.

Abstract: We propose a Semantic Ordered Statistics Decoder (sem-OSD), a soft decoder for short linear block codes carrying byte-streamed sources such as natural-language text. Sem-OSD injects a byte-level language-model (LM) prior into ordered statistics decoding (OSD) through a fused bit-level score that combines channel reliability with the LM prior, and uses it for the most-reliable basis (MRB) selection and the codeword candidate scoring. Sem-OSD enumerates two complementary test-error-pattern (TEP) families: a bit-flip family that flips up to $m$ bits, and an LM-driven family of up to $ω$ byte substitutions that reaches error patterns the bit-flip family cannot. The LM prior is computed by a byte-level Transformer fine-tuned for byte-level denoising. Simulation results show that, on AWGN, sem-OSD achieves block error rates (BLERs) below the finite-blocklength normal-approximation bound for uniform sources on both binary BCH$(127,64)$ and shortened RS$(16,8)$ over GF(256), exceeding Fossorier OSD by a $1.5$ dB coding gain. On a Gilbert--Elliott burst-error channel, sem-OSD provides $4$ dB and $1$ dB of more coding gain than Berlekamp--Massey and OSD, respectively.

</details>


### [696] [Modal-Based Multi-Scatterer Channel Model for Localized Radiomap Extrapolation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02401)
*Wenli Li,Bin Wang,Guangxu Zhu,Haiyan Fan,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a physically interpretable channel model for radiomap reconstruction using spherical wave mode expansion, capturing EM wave propagation effects (especially for high-frequency communications). It formulates an inverse optimization problem to jointly learn scattering responses and scatterer locations from sparse measurements, with simulations showing accurate radiomap reconstruction in both spatial and beam domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency communications make previously negligible microscopic scatterers become mesoscopic with non-negligible EM effects, necessitating a wave-physics-based channel model that captures multi-path effects for accurate radiomap construction.

Method: Spherical wave mode expansion for source radiation, single/multiple scatterer interactions; iterative methods for scatterer coupling; inverse optimization for jointly learning scattering responses and scatterer locations; simplified low-order mode model for dense scatterers.

Result: The proposed model accurately reconstructs and extrapolates radiomaps in both spatial domain and beam domain.

Conclusion: The framework offers a physically interpretable, localized propagation modeling approach suitable for high-frequency wireless communications.

Abstract: A radiomap, representing the spatial distribution of wireless signal strength within a specific region, is fundamentally determined by the local propagation channel and finds extensive applications in network planning and optimization. The channel model is inherently linked to electromagnetic (EM) wave propagation, and the advent of high-frequency communications presents a new picture - microscopic (and thus negligible) scatterers in lower frequency bands become mesoscopic, rendering non-negligible EM effects. In this paper, we establish a channel model for multiple scatterers based on a spherical wave mode expansion. The source radiation, single scatterer response and multiple scatterer interactions are formed in the superposition of spherical-wave modes, capturing the multi-path effect in wave perspective. Iterative methods are used to handle the massive coupling between scatterers. This forward model is converted to an inverse optimization problem, where the scattering responses and the scatterer locations are jointly learned from sparse field measurements. A simplified approximate model is then introduced, employing fewer and simpler low-order modes while still allowing a larger number of more densely placed scatterers. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model accurately reconstructs and extrapolates radiomaps in both the spatial domain and the beam domain. Overall, the proposed framework offers a physically interpretable approach to localized propagation modeling.

</details>


### [697] [Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02416)
*Po-Heng Chou,Chiapin Wang,Chung-Chi Huang,Kuan-Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN) adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of handover decision-making in LEO satellite networks by enabling dynamic trade-off learning among multiple conflicting objectives (throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost) under time-varying conditions.

Method: A dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN)-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework that learns dynamic trade-offs among throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost.

Result: Outperforms conventional baselines with up to 10.3% throughput improvement and achieves near-zero blocking under typical operating conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed DDQN-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework is effective for LEO satellite networks, providing significant performance gains over traditional approaches.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN)-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks. The proposed method enables dynamic trade-off learning among throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost under time-varying network conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms conventional baselines, achieving up to 10.3% throughput improvement and near-zero blocking under typical operating conditions.

</details>


### [698] [Reduced-Feedback Hybrid Precoding for Wideband mmWave MIMO-OFDM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02418)
*Po-Heng Chou,Jia-Qing Lin,Wan-Jen Huang,Ronald Y. Chang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a feedback-efficient hybrid precoding framework for wideband mmWave MIMO-OFDM that reduces feedback overhead from O(K) to O(K/M + log M) using frequency-flat analog precoders and a binary-search-based hierarchical interpolation algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate the high cost of RF chains and CSI feedback overhead in large-scale antenna arrays for wideband mmWave MIMO-OFDM systems.

Method: 1) Constructs frequency-flat analog precoders by extracting dominant AoA/AoD directions from sparse frequency-domain channels. 2) Designs a quantized codebook using Lloyd algorithm. 3) Develops a binary-search-based hierarchical interpolation algorithm that adaptively assigns codewords based on subcarrier correlation.

Result: Achieves sub-linear feedback scaling from O(K) to O(K/M + log M). Comparable or superior spectral efficiency and BER performance with significantly reduced computational complexity and robustness under imperfect CSI.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively balances feedback overhead, performance, and complexity, making it suitable for practical wideband mmWave MIMO-OFDM systems.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a feedback-efficient hybrid precoding framework for wideband millimeter-wave (mmWave) multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) systems. To mitigate the high cost of radio frequency (RF) chains and channel state information (CSI) feedback in large-scale antenna arrays, we first construct frequency-flat analog precoders by extracting dominant angle-of-arrival (AoA) and angle-of-departure (AoD) directions from sparse frequency-domain channels. For digital precoding, we design a quantized codebook using the Lloyd algorithm and develop a binary-search-based hierarchical interpolation algorithm that adaptively assigns codewords according to subcarrier correlation. The proposed method achieves sub-linear feedback scaling by reducing the feedback overhead from O(K) to O(K/M + log M), where K is the number of subcarriers and M is the pilot spacing. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable or superior spectral efficiency and bit error rate (BER) performance to existing clustering and interpolation schemes, while significantly reducing computational complexity and exhibiting robustness under imperfect CSI.

</details>


### [699] [Computability Limits of Sequential Hypothesis Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02501)
*Amir Leshem*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper characterizes exactly which subsets of rational numbers (and effectively presented countable families) admit computable sequential hypothesis tests that make only finitely many errors almost surely, extending Cover's theorem with a complete necessary and sufficient condition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine which sets admit computable sequential decision procedures that eventually stabilize to correct answers, going beyond Cover's existential guarantee to understand the computational boundary of finite-error learning.

Method: The paper provides a complete characterization of subsets of Q that admit a computable finite-error sequential membership test, and extends this to effectively presented countable families of real means using concepts from computability theory (Δ^0_2 sets).

Result: A complete necessary and sufficient characterization of the subsets of Q that admit computable finite-error sequential membership tests, with extension to any effectively presented countable family of real means.

Conclusion: The results clarify what it means for inquiry to "converge to the truth" in probabilistic settings and formalize a fundamental limit to empirical methods even when only eventual stabilization is demanded, not fixed-time guarantees.

Abstract: Sequential hypothesis testing asks for decision rules that update as data arrive. A natural goal is \emph{eventual correctness}: the rule may change its mind early on, but it should make only finitely many wrong decisions almost surely. Starting from Cover's theorem, which guarantees such behavior for membership in a countable set of candidate means, we ask a sharper question: \emph{which sets actually admit computable sequential decision procedures with finitely many errors?} We answer this optimally by giving a complete characterization both necessary and sufficient of the subsets of $\Q$ that admit a computable finite-error sequential membership test. We further extend the characterization to any \emph{effectively presented} countable family of real means, exactly the setting in which Cover's identification rule can be implemented computably. Beyond the technical boundary, the results clarify within a precise probabilistic setting what it can mean for inquiry to ``converge to the truth,'' and they formalize a limit to which empirical methods can be expected to succeed when only eventual stabilization (rather than fixed-time guarantees) is demanded.
  keywords: Cover's theorem, sequential decision procedures, finite error learning, limit computability, $Δ^0_2$ sets.

</details>
